title: Composition of random functions and word reconstruction
body_tex:
\label{conj:Schanuel}
	Let $n\geq 1$ and let $x_1,\dots,x_n\in \mathbb{C}$ be $\mathbb{Q}$-linearly independent. Then
	$$
	\trdeg \mathbb{Q}(x_1,\dots,x_n,e^{x_1},\dots,e^{x_n})\geq n.
	$$
plain_text: Let $n >= 1$ and let $x_1,,x_n in C$ be $Q$-linearly independent. Then $$ Q(x_1,,x_n,e^ x_1 ,,e^ x_n ) >= n. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Schanuel's conjecture is a major open transcendence conjecture asserting broad algebraic independence of x_i and e^{x_i}; central in transcendence theory with very wide-reaching consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Schanuel's conjecture is a major, longstanding open problem in transcendental number theory with no clear imminent breakthrough; near-term resolution is very unlikely.
rationale: Schanuel's conjecture is a classical, major open problem in transcendence theory; the paper cites it as an assumption but does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Paper states: 'The following conjecture is a major (and wide open) problem in number theory' and then gives Schanuel's conjecture (lines 3309–3315).

title: Acyclic sets and colorings in digraphs under restrictions on degrees and cycle lengths
body_tex: \label{conj:EH}{\emph{\cite{EH1989}}} For every graph $H$, there is $\varepsilon_H>0$ such that any graph $G$ on $n$ vertices which does not have $H$ as an induced subgraph satisfies $\max\{\alpha(G),\omega(G)\}\geq n^{\varepsilon_H}$.
plain_text: For every graph $H$, there is $_H>0$ such that any graph $G$ on $n$ vertices which does not have $H$ as an induced subgraph satisfies $(G),(G) >= n^ _H $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The classical Erdős–Hajnal conjecture — a flagship open problem in extremal graph theory whose resolution would have deep, far-reaching structural and theoretical consequences.
viability_rationale: Erdős–Hajnal is a decades-old, central open conjecture with many partial special-case results but no general method; full resolution in the next five years is unlikely.
rationale: The Erdős–Hajnal conjecture is cited as a central open problem; the paper does not claim a proof or disproof of the general conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 145–146: Erdős–Hajnal conjecture stated. Line 142–143: described as one of the central questions in extremal graph theory (open).

title: Motivic Cohomology and K-groups of varieties over higher local fields
body_tex:
(Bass Conjecture)
	 The groups $K_{n}(X)$ are finitely generated for all integers $n \geq 0$.
plain_text: (Bass Conjecture) The groups $K_ n (X)$ are finitely generated for all integers $n >= 0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classical Bass conjecture in algebraic K-theory: finite generation of K-groups for varieties. Deep, central problem with broad structural consequences in arithmetic geometry and K-theory.
viability_rationale: Bass conjecture is a classic, long-standing deep K-theory problem; only low-dimensional cases settled. No concrete route here to finish the full statement within five years.
rationale: The paper cites Bass Conjecture as a classical open prediction and explicitly states both conjectures remain open except in low dimensions; authors do not prove it here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2160-2163: statement of Bass Conjecture; Lines 2171-2172: 'In general, both conjectures remain open, except in the case where dim(X) ≤ 1.'

title: Motivic Cohomology and K-groups of varieties over higher local fields
body_tex:
(Parshin Conjecture)
	 $K_{n}(X)$ are torsion for all integers $n \geq 1$.
plain_text: (Parshin Conjecture) $K_ n (X)$ are torsion for all integers $n >= 1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Parshin conjecture asserting torsionness of higher K-groups. A major complementary conjecture to Bass with substantial implications for finiteness and arithmetic K-theory.
viability_rationale: Parshin conjecture is a well-known hard conjecture in algebraic K-theory; despite partial progress, no indication of imminent complete resolution.
rationale: Parshin Conjecture is presented as a classical open conjecture; paper notes it remains open (except known low-dimensional cases) and does not resolve it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2165-2167: Parshin Conjecture statement; Lines 2171-2172: 'In general, both conjectures remain open, except in the case where dim(X) ≤ 1.'

title: Graham conjecture on small sets in abelian groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
Let $G$ be an abelian group. Then every subset $A \subseteq G \setminus\{0\}$ is sequenceable.
plain_text: Let $G$ be an abelian group. Then every subset $A subseteq G 0$ is sequenceable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Graham/Alspach sequenceability conjecture is a longstanding, central problem in additive combinatorics and design theory; resolving it would have wide combinatorial consequences.
viability_rationale: Graham/Alspach is a prominent, longstanding combinatorial conjecture; recent progress addresses special cases, but full general resolution remains unlikely imminently.
rationale: Graham/Alspach conjecture is a well-known open problem; paper notes recent progress for large primes but explicitly states it remains open for general abelian groups.
evidence_snippet: Lines 103-105 and 121-125: 'recently proved for sufficiently large primes ... it remains open for general abelian groups' and statement of conjecture.

title: Randomstrasse101: Open Problems of 2025
body_tex: For each dimension $d$, there exists an ETF in $\mathbb{C}^d$ with $n=d^2$ vectors.
plain_text: For each dimension $d$, there exists an ETF in $C^d$ with $n=d^2$ vectors.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Zauner's Conjecture (existence of SIC‑POVMs in every dimension) is a central, long-standing conjecture linking quantum information, frame theory, and number theory with wide implications.
viability_rationale: Zauner's Conjecture is a classic, long‑standing SIC existence problem; only conditional number‑theory reductions recently. Unconditional resolution within five years seems very unlikely.
rationale: Zauner's Conjecture (existence of SIC-POVMs / ETFs with n=d^2 for all d) is a longstanding, well-known open conjecture; paper notes no unconditional proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 576–582: describes ETFs and states Zauner's Conjecture; notes conditional number-theory progress but 'An unconditional proof ... remains unavailable.'

title: Weakening the Legendre Conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:cramer}As $n \rightarrow \infty$, we have $p_{n + 1} - p_n = O
  (\log^2 (p_n))$.
plain_text: As $n $, we have $p_ n + 1 - p_n = O (^2 (p_n))$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Cramér's conjecture is a classical, deep prediction on prime gaps; resolving it would fundamentally reshape analytic number theory and probabilistic models of primes.
viability_rationale: Cramér's conjecture is a classic, decades-old prime-gap flagship problem with no clear imminent route to resolution; extremely unlikely to be settled within five years.
rationale: Cramér's prime-gap conjecture is a famous, longstanding open problem; the paper cites it as unresolved and does not prove or refute it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 562-571: “This conjecture is another way to state ... While this is open, ... Specifically, Cramér conjectured ... p_{n+1}-p_n = O(\log^2(p_n)).”

title: Graham conjecture on small sets in abelian groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:main2}
Let $G$ be an abelian group. Then every subset $A \subseteq G \setminus\{0\}$ whose elements sum to zero and such that $\{x,-x\}\not\subset A$ for any $x\in G$, is sequenceable.
plain_text: Let $G$ be an abelian group. Then every subset $A subseteq G 0$ whose elements sum to zero and such that $ x,-x subset A$ for any $x in G$, is sequenceable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: CMPP conjecture for zero-sum subsets without inverse pairs: important for Heffter arrays and sequencing problems. Nontrivial and impactful within combinatorial design, though narrower than full Graham conjecture.
viability_rationale: CMPP variant is a strong combinatorial conjecture closely related to Graham; despite incremental advances, a full resolution appears unlikely within five years.
rationale: The CMPP conjecture is presented as a conjecture in the introduction; the paper advances small-case results but does not resolve the general conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 126-129: statement of CMPP conjecture; surrounding text discusses known partial results but not a full proof.

title: Cluster type varieties
body_tex:
\label{conj:geom-jordan}
There exists a constant $c(n)$, only depending on $n$, satisfying the following. 
Let $X$ be a $n$-dimensional rational variety. 
Let $G$ be a finite group acting on $X$. 
Then, there exists a normal abelian subgroup $A\leqslant G$ of index at most $c(n)$ with 
$A\leqslant \mathbb{G}_m^k \leqslant {\rm Bir}(X)$.
plain_text: There exists a constant $c(n)$, only depending on $n$, satisfying the following. Let $X$ be a $n$-dimensional rational variety. Let $G$ be a finite group acting on $X$. Then, there exists a normal abelian subgroup $A G$ of index at most $c(n)$ with $A G_m^k Bir (X)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A geometric Jordan property for the Cremona group would structurally constrain finite group actions on rational varieties in higher dimensions; important and central for birational group actions and classification problems.
viability_rationale: Proved in dimension two, open in dimension three+. There are partial geometric reductions (torus action on base) but known birationally rigid obstructions to lifting; substantial technical gaps remain, so near-term resolution seems unlikely.
rationale: The paper presents this as the 'geometric Jordan property' conjecture, notes it was proved in dimension two, and explicitly states it remains open in dimension three and higher.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1611–1616: conjecture statement. Lines 1620–1621: 'This conjecture was proved in dimension two... The conjecture remains open in dimension three and higher.'

title: A conditional bound for the least prime in an arithmetic progression
body_tex:
Let $\chi\pmod{q}$ be a Dirichlet character.
Then for any $\varepsilon>0$ and $s=\sigma+it$ satisfying $\sigma\geq 1/2$ and $|s-1|\geq 1/2$, we have
\[L(\sigma+it,\chi)\ll_{\varepsilon}(q(1+|t|))^{\varepsilon}.\]
plain_text: Let $$ be a Dirichlet character. Then for any $>0$ and $s=+it$ satisfying $ >= 1/2$ and $|s-1| >= 1/2$, we have \[L(+it,)_ (q(1+|t|))^ .\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The generalized Lindelöf hypothesis is a central analytic‑number‑theory conjecture with wide consequences for L‑functions and distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions; resolving it would be major.
viability_rationale: Generalized Lindelöf is a major analytic-number-theory conjecture (long-standing); resolution within five years is highly unlikely absent revolutionary breakthroughs.
rationale: GLH is a classical unproven hypothesis; the paper assumes GLH to derive conditional results but does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 75-86: "In its classical form, GLH states... [Conjecture stated]. Our main theorem shows that a bound nearly matching... holds under GLH."

title: Mapping properties of the $S$-operator
body_tex: Suppose that $d \geq 2$ is an even integer and $E \subset \mathbb{F}_q^d$. If $|E| \geq Cq^{d/2}$ for a sufficiently large constant $C$ independent of $q$, then $|\Delta(E)| \gtrsim q$.
plain_text: Suppose that $d >= 2$ is an even integer and $E subset F_q^d$. If $|E| >= Cq^ d/2 $ for a sufficiently large constant $C$ independent of $q$, then $|(E)| q$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Finite-field Erdős–Falconer improvement in even dimensions (d/2 exponent). A longstanding, central discrete-geometric problem whose resolution would significantly advance restriction and distance set theory.
viability_rationale: Open for all even d≥2; resolving needs an ε-improvement in spherical L^2 restriction (tied to hard Kloosterman sum bounds). No viable methods known for d≥4, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: This finite-field Erdős–Falconer distance conjecture for even dimensions is explicitly stated and the text says it remains open for all even dimensions.
evidence_snippet: Lines 209-211: "Conjecture: Suppose d≥2 even and E⊂F_q^d. If |E| ≥ C q^{d/2}... then |Δ(E)| ≳ q. This conjecture remains open for all even dimensions."

title: Modularity of special cycles on Shimura varieties: a survey
body_tex:
There exists a smooth toroidal compactification $X\hookrightarrow \overline{X}$, a finite collection $(Y_i)_{i\in I}$ of codimension $g$ cycles supported in the boundary $\overline X\smallsetminus X$, and corrected special cycles $\widetilde{\cZ}(M)=\overline{\cZ}(M)+\sum_ia_i(M)Y_i$ for $a_i(M)\in \Q$, such that the generating series: \[\sum_{M\geq 0}[\widetilde{\cZ}(M)]q^M\in\mathrm{CH}^g\left(\overline X\right)\llbracket q \rrbracket\]
is a holomorphic Siegel modular form of weight $\frac{n+2}{2}$ with respect to $\mathrm{Sp}_{2g}(\Z)$.
plain_text: There exists a smooth toroidal compactification $X X$, a finite collection $(Y_i)_ i in I $ of codimension $g$ cycles supported in the boundary $ X X$, and corrected special cycles $(M)=(M)+_ia_i(M)Y_i$ for $a_i(M) in $, such that the generating series: \[_ M >= 0 [(M)]q^M in CH^g( X) q \] is a holomorphic Siegel modular form of weight $n+2 2 $ with respect to $Sp_ 2g ()$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Core Kudla conjecture extending modularity of special-cycle generating series to toroidal compactifications; divisors and zero-cycles solved but general higher-codimension case remains open, with deep consequences for arithmetic geometry and automorphic–cycle relations.
viability_rationale: Kudla's compactification modularity has recent breakthroughs (divisors, zero-cycles, cohomology), but higher-codimension Chow-valued statement remains broad and technically deep—moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Paper explicitly states the Kudla toroidal compactification conjecture remains open in general, with only divisor and zero-cycle cases recently solved.
evidence_snippet: “This conjecture is still open in general, but the case of divisors was solved recently ..., as well as the case of zero-cycles.”

title: Modularity of special cycles on Shimura varieties: a survey
body_tex: There exists a smooth toroidal compactification $X\hookrightarrow \overline X$, a finite collection $(Y_i)_{i\in I}$ of codimension $gb$ cycles supported in the boundary $\overline X\smallsetminus X$, and corrected special cycles $\widetilde{\cZ}(M)=\overline{\cZ}(M)+\sum_ia_i(M)Y_i$ for $a_i(M)\in \Q$, such that the generating series: \[\sum_{M\geq 0}[\widetilde{\cZ}(M)]q^M\in\mathrm{CH}^{gb}(\overline X)\llbracket q\rrbracket\] is a holomorphic Hermitian modular form of weight $a+b$ with respect to $\mathrm{U}_{g,g}(\Z)$.
plain_text: There exists a smooth toroidal compactification $X X$, a finite collection $(Y_i)_ i in I $ of codimension $gb$ cycles supported in the boundary $ X X$, and corrected special cycles $(M)=(M)+_ia_i(M)Y_i$ for $a_i(M) in $, such that the generating series: \[_ M >= 0 [(M)]q^M in CH^ gb ( X) q\] is a holomorphic Hermitian modular form of weight $a+b$ with respect to $U_ g,g ()$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Unitary analogue of Kudla’s compactification modularity conjecture; several low-dimensional cases settled, but general gb-codimension modularity is open. Resolving it would significantly advance modularity of cycles on unitary Shimura varieties within the Kudla program.
viability_rationale: Unitary analogue sees notable recent partial resolutions (several low-rank cases, cohomological advances), yet the full Chow-valued compactification conjecture is still wide and unlikely to be finished imminently.
rationale: Authors state modularity of generating series valued in Chow is a major open problem; only special cases (e.g. g=b=1, some others) have been settled.
evidence_snippet: “The modularity of the generating series ... valued in the Chow group is still a major open problem; ... For g=b=1, this conjecture was settled ... and for g=a, b=1, it follows from recent work.”

title: Modularity of special cycles on Shimura varieties: a survey
body_tex:
\label{conj_An}
    The generating series $\Theta_{\cA_n}(q)$ is a holomorphic modular form of weight $2n$ with respect to $\mathrm{SL}_2(\Z)$, valued in $\CH^{n-1}(\cA_n)$.
plain_text: The generating series $_ _n (q)$ is a holomorphic modular form of weight $2n$ with respect to $SL_2()$, valued in $^ n-1 (_n)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Modularity prediction for generating series of elliptic-factor special cycles on Siegel moduli A_n; partial cohomological evidence for small n exists. Important and nontrivial, but narrower in scope than the general Kudla compactification conjectures.
viability_rationale: Siegel-cycle modularity has concrete partial results for small genera and cohomology; but the general Chow-valued statement for all n remains difficult, giving a slightly higher but still modest near-term chance.
rationale: The Siegel-variety generating-series conjecture is presented as open in general; the paper records partial/cohomological results and proofs for low genus cases only.
evidence_snippet: “The following conjecture ... The generating series ... is a holomorphic modular form ... Partial progress on the cohomological version ... and ... it is proved for g≤5 (and g≤3 by other methods).”

title: Untranscendable order types
plain_text: For every untranscendable linear order $$ at least one of the following three statements applies: $$ is strongly indecomposable, $$ contains an uncountable real type, $ = 2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural trichotomy for untranscendable linear orders would be a strong classification result in order theory, clarifying Baumgartner types and interacting with PFA—central within the subfield with clear structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Specialized set-theoretic/ordering problem; authors cite lack of structure theorem and reliance on PFA. Likely to remain independent or require deep consistency arguments, so low near-term solvability.
rationale: The paper explicitly poses this Trichotomy Conjecture in the 'Open Problems' section and does not prove it; it is presented as a still-open question (possibly assuming PFA).
evidence_snippet: Lines 1485–1492: '\begin{conj}[The Trichotomy Conjecture (possibly assuming \PFA)] ... For every untranscendable linear order ... either strongly indecomposable, contains an uncountable real type, or =2.'

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{gre} Let $s$ be a Gale-Robinson sequence with nonzero terms, of a primitive type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then the matrix $s \times s$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ be a Gale-Robinson sequence with nonzero terms, of a primitive type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then the matrix $s x s$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A general finite-rank bound for Gale–Robinson (even) orders extends low-order proofs and ties to integrability/cluster-algebra phenomena; meaningful to specialists but relatively specialized.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence and proofs for small n (≤7); authors outline invariant-based approach, so a concrete path exists though technically challenging for general n.
rationale: The author states Conjecture \ref{gre} for higher even orders after proving low-order cases (6,7); it is presented as an unproven generalisation.
evidence_snippet: Lines 101–105,103: 'Below, we put forward two conjectures... Conjecture \label{gre} Let s be a Gale-Robinson sequence... matrix s×s is of diamond rank at most 2^m.'

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{gro} Let $s$ be a Gale-Robinson sequence with nonzero terms, of a primitive type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then the matrix $s \times s$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ be a Gale-Robinson sequence with nonzero terms, of a primitive type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then the matrix $s x s$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The odd-order analogue gives a half-diamond rank bound for Gale–Robinson sequences; similarly valuable for Somos sequence theory and integrability, but technical and domain-specific.
viability_rationale: Analogous status to the even-order conjecture: proven for small n with experiments supporting general case; plausible but requires nontrivial extension of current methods.
rationale: The odd-order analogue Conjecture \ref{gro} is explicitly stated as a conjecture for higher orders; only small orders are proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 101–106,105: 'Conjecture \label{gro} Let s be a Gale-Robinson sequence... odd order n=2m+3. Then s×s is of half-diamond rank at most 2^m.'

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{cinv} For every proper type $\mathbf{n}$ of order $n$, it holds that $\dim \Omega_\boxtimes = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$.
plain_text: For every proper type $n$ of order $n$, it holds that $ _ = n/2 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicting dim Omega_box = floor(n/2) is a structural statement about invariants underpinning the rank conjectures; important for the program but mostly technical within the subject.
viability_rationale: Structural linear-algebraic invariant dimension conjecture, verified computationally for many small types; appears more accessible and likely attackable with current techniques.
rationale: Conjecture \ref{cinv} is posed in the 'Higher Orders and Further Work' section; verified experimentally for small n but not proven generally in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 527–529: '\begin{conjecture} \label{cinv} For every proper type n of order n, it holds that dim Omega_\boxtimes = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor. ... We already know that Conjecture \ref{cinv} is true of all 3 ≤ n ≤ 7.'

title: Cluster type varieties
plain_text: Let $(X,B)$ be a pair of dimension three. Assume that $X$ is a rational variety; $K_X+B sim 0$; and $D(X,B)_ PL S^2$. Then, the pair $(X,B)$ is log rational.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects dual-complex topology (S^2) to log rationality for Calabi–Yau log threefolds; resolving would meaningfully advance birational classification of cluster-type/log Calabi–Yau threefolds within threefold birational geometry.
viability_rationale: Concrete dimension-3 setting with partial proofs (P^3, many smooth Fano threefolds). Active research and recent progress make a resolution plausible but nontrivial; not yet a narrowly reduced finite gap.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture (attributed to Ducat) and only records partial verifications (P^3 and some smooth Fano threefold cases); the general statement is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1312–1321: 'Ducat proposed the following conjecture.' Conjecture stated. Lines 1340–1341 and 1326–1332: known for X≅P^3 and some smooth Fano threefold cases only.

title: Spanning tight components in 4-uniform hypergraphs
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
    Every $n$-vertex $k$-uniform hypergraph with minimum codegree at least $n/k$ has a spanning tight component.
plain_text: Every $n$-vertex $k$-uniform hypergraph with minimum codegree at least $n/k$ has a spanning tight component.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural threshold conjecture for tight connectivity in k-uniform hypergraphs; central to hypergraph connectivity and spanning-sphere problems, now resolved for k≤4 and tight examples suggest broad structural consequences if proved.
viability_rationale: Resolved for k=2,3 and proved here for k=4; recent progress raises plausibility, but extending methods to all k faces growing technical obstacles—moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The paper states the general k-uniform conjecture (k arbitrary) and proves only the k=4 case; thus the exact general statement remains open for k>4.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 294-296). Paper notes k=2,3 known and proves k=4 in Theorem (lines 298-303).

title: Distribution of sums involving Dirichlet characters over the $k$-free integers
body_tex:
\label{keating-hughes-oconell-conject}
        For all $r\in\mathbb{C}$ such that 
        $\Re(r)>-3/2$, we have
            \begin{align*}
                J_{r}(T)\sim \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{G^2(r+2)}{G(2r+3)}\alpha(r)T(\log T)^{(r+1)^2},
            \end{align*}
        where $G$ is Barnes' $G$-function defined by
            \[
                G(s+1)=(2\pi)^{\frac{s}{2}}\exp\bigg(-\frac{1}{2}(s^2+\gamma s^2+s)\bigg)\prod_{n=1}^\infty \bigg(1+\frac{s}{n}\bigg)^n\exp\bigg(-s+\frac{s^2}{2n}\bigg),
            \]
        with $\gamma$ being the Euler-Mascheroni constant and $s\in\mathbb{C}$, and
            \[
                \alpha(r)=\prod_p\bigg(1-\frac{1}{p}\bigg)^{r^2}\bigg(\sum_{m= 0}^\infty\bigg(\frac{\Gamma(m+r)}{m!\Gamma(r)}\bigg)^2\frac{1}{p^m}\bigg).
            \]
        The product above ranges over all prime numbers $p$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Precise RMT-predicted asymptotic for discrete moments of the zeta derivative; central to analytic number theory and connects random-matrix models with zero/value-distribution, yielding strong structural consequences if established.
viability_rationale: Precise RMT moment conjecture for zeta-derivative zeros; decades-old, only partial upper/lower bounds and special cases. Full asymptotic resolution is very unlikely within five years.
rationale: The Hughes–Keating–O'Connell precise asymptotic for discrete zeta-derivative moments is a well-known conjecture and remains unresolved; only limited partial results exist.

title: Distribution of sums involving Dirichlet characters over the $k$-free integers
body_tex: The set $\{\gamma \in\mathbb{R}_{>0} : \zeta(\beta+i\gamma)=0, \beta\in(0,1)\}$ is linearly independent over $\mathbb{Q}$.
plain_text: The set $ in R_ >0 : (+i)=0, in (0,1) $ is linearly independent over $Q$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Linear Independence Conjecture for zero ordinates is a strong structural statement with wide consequences for explicit formulas and distribution results; central to zero-spacing heuristics though extremely strong and unproven.
viability_rationale: Classical Linear Independence of zeta/L-function ordinates — a strong, longstanding unproven hypothesis assumed here with no concrete route to proof; resolution within five years is highly unlikely.
rationale: The Linear Independence (LI) conjecture for positive imaginary parts of nontrivial zeta zeros being Q-linearly independent is a standard open hypothesis.
evidence_snippet: Lines 838-840: Conjecture (LI): the set {γ>0 : ζ(β+iγ)=0, β∈(0,1)} is linearly independent over Q.

title: Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups
body_tex:
\label{conj: excluded minors finite case}
If $\Gamma$ is finite, then $\cM(\Gamma, \Gamma_1, \cA)$ has finitely many excluded minors.
plain_text: If $$ is finite, then $(, _1, )$ has finitely many excluded minors.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Finiteness of excluded minors impacts classification of a large minor-closed matroid family and ties to Geelen–Gerards structural program; resolving it would have broad structural consequences but appears very difficult.
viability_rationale: Authors state this is "likely to be extremely difficult"; depends on deep excluded-minor theory and unresolved Geelen–Gerards-type conjectures, so near-term resolution is unlikely.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture and provides no proof; it cites related unresolved conjectures but does not resolve the finite-group case itself.
evidence_snippet: We make the optimistic conjecture that if Γ is finite, then M(Γ, Γ_1, A) has finitely many excluded minors. (Conjecture statement lines 1447–1449)

title: Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups
plain_text: Let $$ be a field, let $G$ be a loopless graph with orientation $D$, and let $$ and $'$ be $(^+ ^ x )$-gain functions for $G$ so that $M(^+ ^ x , ^+, , G, ) = M(^+ ^ x , ^+, , G, ')$, and this matroid is $3$-connected and has rank at least $3$. Then $A(D, )$ and $A(D, ')$ are projectively equivalent if and only if $$ and $'$ are switching-and-scaling equivalent.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalizes known uniqueness results (Funk–Pivotto–Slilaty) to semidirect-product gains; valuable for representation classification but is a focused, specialist extension.
viability_rationale: Special cases already proved and authors note techniques from recent work likely apply, making a near-term resolution plausible but nontrivial.
rationale: Authors pose this equivalence as an explicit conjecture; they prove only implications in special cases and do not prove the full iff statement in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Then A(D, ψ) and A(D, ψ') are projectively equivalent if and only if ψ and ψ' are switching-and-scaling equivalent. (Conjecture lines 2009–2012)

title: Matroids from gain graphs over quotient groups
plain_text: Let $(G, )$ be a biased graph, let $M$ be an elementary lift of the frame matroid $F(G, )$ so that $M$ is $3$-connected, and let $$ be a field. If $A$ is a matrix representing $M$ over $$, then there is an $(^+ ^ x )$-gain function $$ for $G$ so that $F(G, ) = F(G, /^+)$, $M = M(^+ ^ x , ^+, , G, )$, and $A$ is projectively equivalent to $A(D, )$ for any orientation $D$ of $G$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts every representation of a 3‑connected elementary lift comes from a gain-function incidence matrix; key to a one-to-one correspondence between representations and gain classes, central in representability theory.
viability_rationale: Stronger representability classification related to Conjecture 122; authors expect approaches from recent literature to help, so moderately plausible within five years.
rationale: This is stated as an open conjecture (representations arise from gain-function incidence matrices); the paper does not prove it and treats it as future work.
evidence_snippet: If A is a matrix representing M over F, then there is an (F^+ ⋊ F^×)-gain function ψ for G so that ... and A is projectively equivalent to A(D, ψ). (Conjecture lines 2024–2026)

title: Weakening the Legendre Conjecture
body_tex:
There is a prime between $n^2$ and $(n + 1)^2$ for all positive integers
  $n$.
plain_text: There is a prime between $n^2$ and $(n + 1)^2$ for all positive integers $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Legendre's conjecture is a classical, longstanding prime-gap problem (primes between consecutive squares); its resolution would be a major advance in analytic number theory.
viability_rationale: Classical Legendre conjecture is a century-old prime-gap problem with no known approach likely to close it imminently; near-term resolution is very unlikely.
rationale: Legendre's conjecture (a prime between n^2 and (n+1)^2 for all n) is a classical open problem; the paper discusses it but does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 72-75: "Conjecture [Legendre] There is a prime between n^2 and (n+1)^2 for all positive integers n." Lines 83-84 note it has withstood proof.

title: The Lens of Abelian Embeddings
body_tex:
\label{conj:general_inverse}
    For all $k\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists $k'\in\mathbb{N}$, such that for all $\eps,\alpha>0$
    there are $d\in\mathbb{N}$ and $\delta>0$ such that the following holds.
    Suppose $\mu$ is a pairwise-connected distribution 
    over $\Sigma^k$ in which the mass of each atom is at least $\alpha$. If 
    $f_1,\ldots,f_k\colon \Sigma^n$ are $1$-bounded
    functions such that 
    $\card{\E_{(x_1,\ldots,x_k)\sim\mu^{\otimes n}}[f_1(x_1)\cdots f_k(x_k)]}\geq \eps$, 
    then there is a combinatorial degree-$k'$ function $P\colon \Sigma^n\to\mathbb{C}$ with 
    $2$-norm equal to $1$, and $L\colon\Sigma^n\to\mathbb{C}$
    with $2$-norm equal to $1$ and ${\sf deg}(L)\leq d$ such that 
    $\card{\inner{f_1}{L\cdot P}}\geq \delta$.
plain_text: For all $k in N$ there exists $k' in N$, such that for all $,>0$ there are $d in N$ and $>0$ such that the following holds. Suppose $$ is a pairwise-connected distribution over $^k$ in which the mass of each atom is at least $$. If $f_1,,f_k ^n$ are $1$-bounded functions such that $ _ (x_1,,x_k) sim ^ n [f_1(x_1) f_k(x_k)] >= $, then there is a combinatorial degree-$k'$ function $P ^n to C$ with $2$-norm equal to $1$, and $L^n to C$ with $2$-norm equal to $1$ and $ deg (L) <= d$ such that $ f_1 L * P >= $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad inverse theorem for k-wise correlations; resolving it would develop new combinatorial/additive tools and have substantial structural consequences across discrete mathematics and TCS.
viability_rationale: Open for k≥4, explicitly called very challenging; only partial results and new combinatorial tools seemingly required, so full resolution in five years looks unlikely but not impossible.
rationale: The paper states the conjecture as an open problem and explicitly invites resolving it; no proof or counterexample is provided.
evidence_snippet: Lines 753–756, 778–781: 'At present time, Question ... is open for all k≥4' and 'It would be very interesting to resolve Conjecture ... either in the positive or in the negative.'

title: Enumeration of Nondegenerate $2 \times (k+1) \times k$ Hypermatrices
plain_text: Fix a format $2 x (k+1) x k$ and let $P = , $ be a plane partition such that $P subseteq $. The number of nondegenerate $2 x (k+1) x k$ hypermatrices over $_q$ which respect $P$ is \[q^ k^2 (q-1)^ 2k [k+1 - _1]_q[k - _2]_q [2 - _ k ]_q * [k - _1]_q[(k-1) - _2]_q _q.\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An appealing explicit product formula enumerating nondegenerate hypermatrices; valuable within algebraic combinatorics but relatively specialized and many cases are already proven.
viability_rationale: Paper proves many cases and gives structural approaches; remaining instances appear approachable via enumerative/algebraic techniques, making a near-term resolution moderately plausible.
rationale: Authors pose this main enumeration conjecture and explicitly state they cannot prove it for all plane partitions P, proving only many cases.
evidence_snippet: Lines 348–351, 353–354: Conjecture stated; 'Although we will not be able to prove this conjecture for all P, there are large families of P for which we will show it holds.'

title: Quadratic Equations over Graph Products and the Exponent of Periodicity
body_tex:
\label{conj:PR98}
  The satisfiability of word equations in free monoids is \textsc{\small NP}-complete.
plain_text: The satisfiability of word equations in free monoids is NP-complete.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Longstanding Plandowski–Rytter conjecture on complexity of word equations; proving NP-completeness would settle a central classification question in string equation complexity.
viability_rationale: Longstanding, canonical NP-completeness conjecture for word equations; decades of partial progress but no clear path to NP-hardness, so near-term resolution is highly unlikely.
rationale: The NP-completeness conjecture for word equations in free monoids is cited as an open problem; the paper notes it 'remains wide open.'
evidence_snippet: Lines 875–886: Plandowski and Rytter conjectured NP-completeness; '... attempts to prove the analogue for quadratic equations over free monoids failed, and Conjecture PR98 remains wide open.'

title: Goldfeld conjecture for non-hyperelliptic direction
body_tex: The average analytic rank of $\lcrc{C_d}$ for positive square-free integers $d$ is $\frac{1}{4}$.
plain_text: The average analytic rank of $C_d$ for positive square-free integers $d$ is $1 4 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A Goldfeld-type prediction (average analytic rank 1/4) for a specific CM twist family; aligns with Katz–Sarnak and is meaningful in arithmetic statistics but family-specific.
viability_rationale: Conditional results (GRH) and Katz–Sarnak heuristics support the 1/4 constant, but proving the exact average requires deep analytic number theory beyond current unconditional techniques.
rationale: Authors propose the average analytic rank = 1/4 as a conjecture based on Katz–Sarnak; they provide conditional upper bounds but do not prove the conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 279–283: 'Following the philosophy, we conjecture the following. The average analytic rank of {C_d} for positive square-free integers d is 1/4.'

title: Concentration for random Euclidean combinatorial optimization
body_tex:
\label{conj:ptoq}
Fix $d\ge 3$ and $p\ge 1$. For every $q>p$ there exists $c=c(d,p,q)<\infty$ such that for every
$p$--optimal matching $\sigma_p$,
\[
\EE\Big[\sum_{i=1}^n |X_i-Y_{\sigma_p(i)}|^{q}\Big]
\;\le\; c\, n^{1-q/d}.
\]
plain_text: Fix $d >= 3$ and $p >= 1$. For every $q>p$ there exists $c=c(d,p,q)<$ such that for every $p$--optimal matching $_p$, \[ \; <= \; c\, n^ 1-q/d . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A p→q transfer controlling higher moments of p-optimal matchings; would extend concentration results across p and impact matching/TSP fluctuation theory—central in random Euclidean optimization.
viability_rationale: Authors already prove up to p<d^2/2 and numerics suggest extension; plausible via higher-moment/local-edge control but requires new geometric-probabilistic ideas, so moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: The p→q transfer principle is explicitly stated as Conjecture~\ref{conj:ptoq} and is not proved in the paper; authors note implications if true.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture~\ref{conj:ptoq} (lines 117–124) states E[sum |X_i-Y_{σ_p(i)}|^q] ≤ c n^{1-q/d}; the paper treats it as conjectural and uses it to discuss potential extensions.

title: Ribbon concordance and fibered predecessors, II: the general case
body_tex:
\label{conj:pred}
For each knot $K\subset S^3$, there are only finitely many knots $J\leq K$.
plain_text: For each knot $K subset S^3$, there are only finitely many knots $J <= K$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A central structural conjecture in knot concordance: finiteness of ribbon predecessors would settle deep ordering properties of ribbon concordance and have broad consequences in low-dimensional topology.
viability_rationale: Strong recent progress: paper proves finiteness for all fibered predecessors and introduces new Floer-homology tools. Remaining non-fibered case still broad; plausible progress but nontrivial gaps remain.
rationale: The paper proves finiteness for all fibered predecessors but does not prove the full conjecture for arbitrary knots; authors state the conjecture explicitly and it remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: They prove: 'For each knot K⊂S^3, there are only finitely many fibered knots J≤K' and state Conjecture (for all knots) separately and still open (Introduction, Conjecture and Theorem).

title: Distributions of unramified extensions of global fields
body_tex:
\label{intro-moments-conjecture} Let $k$ be a number field that lacks any nontrivial everywhere unramified field extension, $\Gamma$ a finite group, and $(\gamma_v)_{v \in\operatorname{Spec}(k \otimes\mathbb R)}  $ an assignment of a conjugacy classes $\gamma_v$ as above. Let $n$ be the number of roots of unity of order prime to $\abs{\Gamma}$ contained in $k$. Let $\mathbf H$ be a finite $n$-oriented $\Gamma$-group. 

Then 
\[ \lim_{ B \to \infty} \frac{  \sum_{ D\leq B} \sum_{K \in E_\Gamma (D, k ,\overline{\gamma})}   \Sur ( \bGal ( K^{ \operatorname{un}, \abs{\Gamma}'} / K), \mathbf H) }{ \sum_{D\leq B}  \abs{ E_\Gamma (D, k ,\overline{\gamma})} }=  b_{\mathbf H}. \]

Let $n$ be the number of roots of unity of order prime to $$ contained in $k$. Let $ H$ be a finite $n$-oriented $$-group. Then \[ _ B to _ D <= B _ K in E_ (D, k ,) ( ( K^ un, ' / K), H) _ D <= B E_ (D, k ,) = b_ H . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad Cohen–Lenstra–style moments conjecture predicting distributions of unramified Galois groups (refined by homology classes and roots of unity) is central in arithmetic statistics with wide structural consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Deep number-field generalization of proven function-field results; akin to nonabelian Cohen–Lenstra heuristics. Authors give supporting nonexistence cases, but the broad general conjecture seems out of reach in five years.
rationale: The authors state this as the main number-field conjecture and prove function-field analogues; they do not prove the number-field limit, so the exact statement remains open.

title: Rational points on modular curves: parameterization and geometric explanations
plain_text: There exist finitely many explicit open subgroups $G_1,, G_n$ of $_2()$ with the following property. If $E$ is a non-CM elliptic curve over $$, then there is an index $i$ and a point $x in X_ G_i^ag ()$ with $j$-invariant $j(x) = j(E)$ such that $G_E$ is conjugate to $G^_i$, where $ _ to T_ G_i $ is a lift of the character $_x _ to T_ G_i $ describing the $T_ G_i $-action on $_ G_i ^ -1 (x)$.

interestingness_rationale: An explicit finite parameterization of all non-CM elliptic Galois images (Mazur Program B refined) directly targets Serre uniformity and the classification of adelic images—central and highly consequential in arithmetic geometry.
viability_rationale: Conjecture is equivalent to Serre uniformity. Strong conditional progress (Zywina; this paper's refinement and explicit lists) exists, but unconditional Serre uniformity remains open and unlikely resolved within five years.
rationale: The paper formulates this explicit parameterization (Mazur Program B revised) as a conjecture; they discuss equivalence to Serre uniformity and give conditional results, so the exact statement remains open.

title: Projective bundles that admit coupled Kähler-Einstein metrics but no Kähler-Einstein metrics
plain_text: For every $n >= 4$, there exists a toric Fano manifold of dimension $n$ that admits a two-coupled KE metric but does not admit an ordinary KE metric. More precisely, for any integer $r >= 2$, consider the projective bundles align* X_r&=P_ P^r x P^ r-1 ( O O(-1,1)), 0.35cm X_r=2r, Y_r&=P_ P^ r+1 x P^ r-1 ( O O(-1,1)), Y_r=2r+1. align* These toric Fano manifolds $X_r$ and $Y_r$ form families of non-KE toric Fano manifolds that nevertheless admit two-coupled decompositions of $c_1(X_r)$ and $c_1(Y_r)$ supporting cKE metrics.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a systematic, dimension-uniform phenomenon distinguishing coupled Kähler–Einstein from ordinary KE metrics on toric Fano manifolds; solving it would give substantive structural insight in Kähler geometry beyond isolated examples.
viability_rationale: Explicit toric/projective-bundle families and polytope criteria with several proven low-r cases suggest a plausible, technical extension to all dimensions, but nontrivial analytic/combinatorial work remains.
rationale: The paper explicitly poses this as a conjecture and proves only special cases (X_r for r=2,3 and Y_r for r=2); the general statement for all n≥4 is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For every n≥4 there exists a toric Fano manifold of dimension n that admits a two-coupled KE metric but does not admit an ordinary KE metric. The conjecture holds for X_r with r=2,3 and Y_r with r=2.

title: The largest 5th pivot may be the root of a 61st degree polynomial
body_tex:
\label{g5conjecture}
    The largest possible growth factor for a $5\times 5$ real matrix under complete pivoting is exactly the lower bound proven in this paper, i.e., the  unique real root $g$ in the interval $[4,5]$ of the $61^{st}$ degree polynomial $P_5(g)$ in Equation (\ref{eq:61}).
plain_text: The largest possible growth factor for a $5 x 5$ real matrix under complete pivoting is exactly the lower bound proven in this paper, i.e., the unique real root $g$ in the interval $[4,5]$ of the $61^ st $ degree polynomial $P_5(g)$ in Equation ().
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A finite, small-n optimization: an exact conjectured maximum for 5×5 complete-pivoting growth factor, algebraically characterized and numerically supported—interesting historically but narrow in scope.
viability_rationale: Finite-dimensional, explicit candidate with strong numerical, symbolic, and improved upper-bound evidence; remaining task is a (likely doable) exhaustive algebraic/interval verification to rule out alternatives.
rationale: Authors present an exact algebraic candidate (root of a degree-61 polynomial) and state it as a conjecture that this lower bound is the global maximum; they do not prove it is the absolute maximum over all combinatorial cases.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: The largest possible growth factor for a 5×5 real matrix under complete pivoting is exactly the unique real root g in [4,5] of the 61st degree polynomial P_5(g). (Authors 'pose the conjecture'.)

title: A note on the affine plank conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj}
		If the planks $P_1,\dots,P_m \subset \R^d$ cover a convex body $K\subset\mathbb{R}^d$ then
		\[
		\sum_{i=1}^m \rw_K(P_i)
		 \ge 1.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Bang's affine plank conjecture is a long‑standing, foundational problem in convex geometry; resolving it would close a 70+ year open question with broad implications for covering and width inequalities.
viability_rationale: Bang's affine plank conjecture is a classic decades-old open problem; recent incremental bounds exist but no clear route to full resolution within five years.
rationale: This is Bang's affine plank conjecture, a classical open problem; the paper explicitly states "This conjecture remains open." and gives improved lower bounds but not a proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 111–118 and 120: Conjecture statement for affine plank; "This conjecture remains open. In fact, it is open even in the plane."

title: Monodromy rank and the semisimple Mumford-Tate conjecture for hyper-Kähler varieties
body_tex:
\label{conj:maximality of Galois action}
	For a $\QQ$-compatible system $\{\rho_{\ell} \colon G_K \to \GL(\rH_{\et}(X_{\overline{K}},\QQ_{\ell}))\}$ of Galois representations arising from a smooth projective variety $X$ over a finitely generated field $K$, the $\ell$-adic Lie group $\Gamma_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}$ is a maximal compact subgroup of $\bG_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}(\QQ_{\ell})$ for all sufficiently large primes $\ell$. Moreover, $\Gamma_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}$ is \emph{hyperspecial}; that is, there exists a smooth affine group scheme $\mathscr{G}_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}$ over $\ZZ_{\ell}$ such that $\mathscr{G}_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}(\ZZ_{\ell}) = \Gamma_{\ell}^{\simplyconnected}$.
plain_text: For a $$-compatible system $ _ ell G_K to (_ (X_ K ,_ ell )) $ of Galois representations arising from a smooth projective variety $X$ over a finitely generated field $K$, the $ ell $-adic Lie group $_ ell ^ $ is a maximal compact subgroup of $_ ell ^ (_ ell )$ for all sufficiently large primes $ ell $. Moreover, $_ ell ^ $ is hyperspecial; that is, there exists a smooth affine group scheme $G_ ell ^ $ over $_ ell $ such that $G_ ell ^ (_ ell ) = _ ell ^ $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts hyperspecial maximal compactness of ℓ-adic Galois images for compatible systems from varieties—central to the arithmetic of motives and with strong consequences for Mumford–Tate, Tate, and automorphy questions.
viability_rationale: A broad Serre/Larsen-style maximality conjecture; paper proves substantial hyper-Kähler cases but the general statement for arbitrary smooth projective varieties is a deep, long-standing problem—unlikely to be settled in 5 years.
rationale: The paper states the general maximality/hyperspecial conjecture for arbitrary smooth projective varieties but only proves it for hyper-Kähler varieties with b2>=4 (special case). The full general statement remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 456–458). Paper proves it for hyper-Kähler varieties with b2>=4 (MainThm lines 462–464), i.e. a special-case verification only.

title: Stacky Batyrev-Manin conjecture and modular curves
plain_text: dardayasudabm ] Assume that $ F$ is Zariski-dense in $$. Let $(L,x) in _ ()$ and let $H: F to _ >0 $ be the corresponding height. There exists a thin set $T subset F $ and $C>0$ such that $$\# F -T|0,1cmH(x) <= B _ B to C B^ a(L,x) (B)^ b(L,x)-1 .$$

interestingness_rationale: A stacky generalization of the Batyrev–Manin counting conjecture predicting precise asymptotics for rational points on stacks; a significant bridge in Diophantine geometry with broad structural impact.
viability_rationale: The stacky Batyrev--Manin conjecture is a broad analogue of a deep counting conjecture; authors resolve many modular-curve cases, but the full, general conjecture remains ambitious and unlikely to be fully solved soon.

title: A Note on Grünbaum's Conjecture about Longest Cycles and Paths
body_tex:
{\rm (Gr\"{u}nbaum, 1974)}\label{conj1}
		For any integer $k\geq 2$, $\Gamma(k,k)$ is empty.
plain_text: (Gr\" u nbaum, 1974) For any integer $k >= 2$, $(k,k)$ is empty.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Grünbaum's long-standing conjecture on nonexistence of Γ(k,k) for k≥2 is a deep structural claim in Hamiltonicity and longest-cycle theory, with enduring open status and meaningful subfield consequences.
viability_rationale: Grünbaum's conjecture is a decades-old combinatorial problem; this paper gives useful bounds and evidence, but the general conjecture remains hard with no apparent imminent complete resolution.
rationale: Grünbaum's conjecture (Γ(k,k) empty for k≥2) is posed and noted as long-open. The paper obtains partial bounds and emptiness in restricted ranges but does not resolve the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated as open (lines 141–144). Paper proves emptiness for n<k^2+2k+3 and related bounds (lines 160–163), not the general claim.

title: Discrete homotopy hypothesis for n-types
body_tex:
\label{conj:nerve}
    The nerve functor $\gnerve[\infty] \from \Graph \to \cSet$ is a weak equivalence of fibration categories.
plain_text: The nerve functor $ to $ is a weak equivalence of fibration categories.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts an ∞-categorical equivalence between graph-based discrete homotopy and classical spaces; a central structural bridge whose resolution would yield broad discrete analogues of major topological theorems.
viability_rationale: Paper gives concrete partial progress, but proving an equivalence of fibration categories (full discrete homotopy hypothesis) is a deep categorical/homotopical task with remaining conceptual obstacles.
rationale: The paper states the Discrete Homotopy Hypothesis as a fundamental open problem and does not prove it, only making progress towards it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1412-1414: 'The nerve functor gnerve[∞]: Graph → cSet is a weak equivalence of fibration categories.' Line 1421: 'This paper represents a step towards proving this conjecture.'

title: Existence of Riemannian invariants for integrable systems of hydrodynamic type
body_tex: Let $K_1, \dots, K_n$ be $n$-dimensional mutual symmetries that are linearly independent at every point.  Assume that there exists a linear combination $\sum c_iK_i$ which is $\mathrm{gl}$-regular. Then the Haantjes torsion of all $K_i$ vanishes.
plain_text: Let $K_1, , K_n$ be $n$-dimensional mutual symmetries that are linearly independent at every point. Assume that there exists a linear combination $ c_iK_i$ which is $gl$-regular. Then the Haantjes torsion of all $K_i$ vanishes.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural conjecture: vanishing Haantjes torsion under a gl-regular linear combination would yield broad diagonalizability/coordinate results for integrable hydrodynamic systems, supported by symbolic checks in low dimensions.
viability_rationale: Algebraic statement verified by computer for many dimensions and special Jordan cases; an overarching algebraic proof seems reachable within a few years.
rationale: The authors verified the statement by computer for dimensions 3..10 and propose the general statement as a conjecture; no general proof is given in the paper.
evidence_snippet: This suggests the following natural conjecture: ... Then the Haantjes torsion of all K_i vanishes. (Verified by computer in dimensions n=3..10.)

title: A discrete view of Gromov's filling area conjecture
body_tex:
\label[conj]{gromovConjecture}
    If $M$ is a compact, orientable Riemannian surface which isometrically fills the Riemannian circle of length $2\pi$, then the surface area of $M$ is at least $2\pi$.
plain_text: If $M$ is a compact, orientable Riemannian surface which isometrically fills the Riemannian circle of length $2$, then the surface area of $M$ is at least $2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gromov's filling area conjecture is a long-standing, influential problem in global Riemannian geometry; a resolution would be a major result with broad geometric implications.
viability_rationale: Decades-old, high-profile geometric conjecture with only partial case resolutions; unlikely to be fully settled within five years absent a major breakthrough.
rationale: Gromov's filling area conjecture is classical and the paper explicitly states the full conjecture remains unresolved; the authors obtain a new lower bound but do not prove the conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Paper states 'the full conjecture remains unresolved' (lines 77–79) and records Gromov's conjecture: area at least 2π for isometric fillings of circle (lines 95–97).

title: Hensel minimality, $p$-adic exponentiation and Tate uniformization

title: Hensel minimality, $p$-adic exponentiation and Tate uniformization
body_tex: For every positive integer $n$, every subvariety $V\subset E_q^n$ contains only finitely many maximal (with respect to inclusion) atypical components.
plain_text: For every positive integer $n$, every subvariety $V subset E_q^n$ contains only finitely many maximal (with respect to inclusion) atypical components.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Equivalent atypical-components formulation of Zilber–Pink for powers of an elliptic curve; a core, far-reaching conjecture in unlikely intersections with major structural impact if resolved.
viability_rationale: The atypical-component formulation of Zilber–Pink for powers of elliptic curves is a major open problem; only partial/weak results are cited, so near-term full resolution is unlikely.
rationale: This is the Zilber–Pink atypical-components formulation, equivalent to the unlikely version; the paper presents it as an open conjecture (not proved here).
evidence_snippet: Lines 1413–1415: "For every positive integer n, every subvariety V⊂E_q^n contains only finitely many maximal ... atypical components." Paper treats it as open.

title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
body_tex: There exists an $\eps >0$ and infinitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph of order $n$, whose perimeter gap is at least $\eps n$.
plain_text: There exists an $ >0$ and infinitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph of order $n$, whose perimeter gap is at least $ n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A linear perimeter gap would greatly improve the paper's logarithmic bound and resolve a strong variant of Alspach's question; significant for vertex-transitive digraph theory and tied to the unresolved undirected case.
viability_rationale: Authors state the undirected case is still open and their current bound is only logarithmic; the linear (eps n) gap appears significantly stronger and far out of reach presently.
rationale: The statement is posed explicitly as a conjecture in the paper's concluding remarks and is not proved or resolved within the manuscript.
evidence_snippet: Section Concluding remarks (lines 429–431): "\begin{conj} There exists an \eps >0 and infinitely many values of n for which there exists a connected vertex transitive digraph... perimeter gap is at least \eps n. \end{conj}"

title: Long cycles in vertex transitive digraphs
body_tex: Let $c(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive graph on $n$ vertices. Let $d(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive digraph on $n$ vertices. Then $d(n)= \Theta(c(n))$.
plain_text: Let $c(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive graph on $n$ vertices. Let $d(n)$ denote the minimum circumference of a connected vertex transitive digraph on $n$ vertices. Then $d(n)= (c(n))$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Showing d(n)=Θ(c(n)) would unify directed and undirected asymptotics for circumferences, a meaningful structural bridge within the vertex-transitive cycle problem but primarily impactful within this subfield.
viability_rationale: Equating directed and undirected asymptotics reduces to closing gaps in the undirected case; progress depends on matching difficult known bounds, so moderate-low near-term solvability.
rationale: The paper states this d(n)=Θ(c(n)) relationship as a conjecture in the concluding remarks and does not prove it; it's presented as an open question.
evidence_snippet: Section Concluding remarks (lines 437–439): "\begin{conj} Let c(n)... Let d(n)... Then d(n)= \Theta(c(n)). \end{conj}"

title: Hypercontractivity for a family of quantum Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroups
body_tex:
For $2\leq p<\8$, the optimal time $t'_p$ satisfies
		\[
		\wt{c}_2(\beta) (p - 1)^2 \leq e^{2\tau t'_p} \leq \wt{C}_2(\beta) (p - 1)^2,
		\]
		where $\wt{c}_2(\beta)$ and $\wt{C}_2(\beta)$ are positive constants depending only on $\beta$.
plain_text: For $2 <= p<\8$, the optimal time $t'_p$ satisfies \[ c_2() (p - 1)^2 <= e^ 2 t'_p <= C_2() (p - 1)^2, \] where $c_2()$ and $C_2()$ are positive constants depending only on $$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts sharp (p-1)^2 scaling for optimal hypercontractivity time of a quantum Ornstein–Uhlenbeck family; important for quantum functional inequalities but focused on a specific semigroup family.
viability_rationale: Authors obtain partial/related bounds and conjecture a (p-1)^2 scaling; active analytic techniques in quantum hypercontractivity make technical progress plausible though nontrivial.
rationale: Authors end the paper by explicitly stating this scaling of the optimal time as a conjecture; no proof or resolution appears in the manuscript.
evidence_snippet: Section 'Estimate of Optimal time t_p' (lines 1437–1444): "We end this paper with the following conjecture. For 2\le p<\infty, the optimal time t'_p satisfies \wt{c}_2(\beta)(p-1)^2 \le e^{2\tau t'_p} \le \wt{C}_2(\beta)(p-1)^2."

title: Randomized Zero Forcing
body_tex:
Given a bidirectional complete bipartite graph $K_{a,b}$ with all edges of equal weight where $a > b$, let $A$ denote the partite set with $a$ vertices, and $B$ denote the partite set with $b$ vertices. Given vertices $u \in A$ and $v \in B$,
$$\eptrzf{(K_{a,b}, \{v\})} < \eptrzf{(K_{a,b}, \{u\})}$$
plain_text: Given a bidirectional complete bipartite graph $K_ a,b $ with all edges of equal weight where $a > b$, let $A$ denote the partite set with $a$ vertices, and $B$ denote the partite set with $b$ vertices. Given vertices $u in A$ and $v in B$, $$ (K_ a,b , v ) < (K_ a,b , u ) $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A narrow, empirically supported monotonicity statement about expected propagation on complete bipartite graphs; a local structural conjecture with limited broader consequences.
viability_rationale: Concrete finite family with strong numerical evidence and accessible probabilistic structure; likely resolvable via elementary/monotonicity arguments within a few years.
rationale: The inequality for expected propagation times on K_{a,b} is presented as a conjecture in the paper; numerical data are given but no proof is provided.
evidence_snippet: Section 'Other families' (lines 1255–1258): "\begin{conjecture} Given a bidirectional complete bipartite graph K_{a,b}... with a>b... EPT(K_{a,b},\{v\}) < EPT(K_{a,b},\{u\}) \end{conjecture}"

title: Powers of binary forms and derived Hermite reciprocity
plain_text: For $1 <= j <= b$, we have equation* reg(I_ a,b ^j) = b+j+1 2 a- b-j+1 2 , equation* and the minimal generators of $I_ a,b ^j$ form a Gr\"obner basis.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gives an explicit Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity formula and Gröbner-basis statement for powers of a plethysm-generated ideal; valuable for resolutions and Rees algebra, but fairly specialized to commutative algebra/representation plethysm.
viability_rationale: Authors proved j=1, j=b-1, and j=b cases and develop strong representation-theoretic tools; remaining cases look technical and localized, making a full resolution plausibly achievable within five years.
rationale: The paper states this as a conjecture and only proves special cases (j=1, j=b, j=b-1); the full formula and Gröbner basis claim remain unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 308–314) states reg(I_{a,b}^j)=... and generators form a Gröbner basis. Lines 316–316 note proofs only for j=1, j=b, and j=b-1.

title: Random geometric graphs with smooth kernels: sharp detection threshold and a spectral conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:trace}
The sharp threshold for distinguishing RGG from \ER
                                            
is given by 
                                 
$n^3 \tr^2(\kappa^3) \gg 1$ (detectable)
and $n^3 \tr^2(\kappa^3) \ll 1$ (undetectable).
plain_text: The sharp threshold for distinguishing RGG from is given by $n^3 ^2(^3) 1$ (detectable) and $n^3 ^2(^3) 1$ (undetectable).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a unifying spectral criterion n^3 tr^2(κ^3) for sharp detection in geometric random graphs, bridging spectral operator theory and hypothesis testing with substantial cross-disciplinary impact if true.
viability_rationale: Conjecture is broad spectral universality for detection thresholds; current paper gives strong evidence but full universality likely demands new techniques, so near-term resolution is uncertain.
rationale: The authors present this spectral threshold as a conjecture, provide supporting evidence, but do not prove the universal sharp threshold claim here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (1174–1181): 'n^3 tr^2(κ^3)≫1 (detectable) and n^3 tr^2(κ^3)≪1 (undetectable).' Presented as a conjecture with supporting evidence.

title: Fully Leafed Induced Subtrees in Penrose P2 Tilings
body_tex:
\label{conj_g}
 The sea grafting (g) of Figure \ref{fig:greffages marins} can be extended into a bi-infinite fully leafed caterpillar.
plain_text: The sea grafting (g) of Figure can be extended into a bi-infinite fully leafed caterpillar.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A narrow existence claim about a specific bi-infinite fully leafed caterpillar in Penrose P2 tilings; useful for classifying such objects but largely technical and confined to this tiling subfield.
viability_rationale: Concrete, finite combinatorial construction; authors already built analogous bi-infinite caterpillars and propose a plausible reflection-based method. Likely resolvable with further constructive work in the near term.
rationale: The authors explicitly state belief the sea grafting (g) can be extended but give no proof; they pose it as an open conjecture to be resolved later.
evidence_snippet: “We think that this sea grafting can be extended... We also believe... proving this requires a non-trivial construction.” (lines 1405–1411)

title: Cycle tilings and $H$-factors in directed graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj1}
    Given any $\eta>0$, there exists $n_0=n_0(\eta)\in \mathbb N$ such that the following holds for all $n \geq n_0$ divisible by $4$.
If $G$ is an $n$-vertex digraph with 
$$\delta^0(G)\geq (1/2+\eta)n,$$
then $G$ contains a $C_4$-factor.
plain_text: Given any $>0$, there exists $n_0=n_0() in N$ such that the following holds for all $n >= n_0$ divisible by $4$. If $G$ is an $n$-vertex digraph with $$^0(G) >= (1/2+)n,$$ then $G$ contains a $C_4$-factor.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asymptotically sharp semidegree condition for C4-factors is a central extremal digraph problem; resolving it would advance even-cycle tiling theory and digraph factor results.
viability_rationale: A nontrivial extremal digraph problem: asymptotic and absorption approaches reduce it, but even-cycle exact thresholds remain challenging; possible progress but resolution within five years is uncertain.
rationale: The authors list this C4-factor minimum semi-degree statement explicitly in their 'Open problems' section as a conjecture and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet:
“
Given any η>0, there exists n0... If G is an n-vertex digraph with δ^0(G) ≥ (1/2+η)n, then G contains a C4-factor.” (lines 1579–1584)

title: Between proper and square coloring of planar graphs, hardness and extremal graphs
plain_text: For every planar graph $G$, align* & _2(G) <= 7 & if (G) = 3, & _2(G) <= (G) + 5 & if 4 <= (G) <= 7, & _2(G) <= 3(G)/2 + 1 & otherwise. align*
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Wegner's long-standing conjecture on planar graphs' distance-2 chromatic number is a central, classical problem in graph coloring with tight bounds and wide structural and algorithmic impact if resolved.
viability_rationale: Decades-old Wegner conjecture with only asymptotic and partial progress (special cases, (1+o(1))3Δ/2 bounds). No clear finite-case closure indicated; unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: This is Wegner's 1977 conjecture about chi_2 for planar graphs; it is a well-known open conjecture and is presented here without resolution.
evidence_snippet: Lines 64–71: "\begin{conjecture}[Wegner... ] For every planar graph G, • χ_2(G) ≤7 if Δ(G)=3, • χ_2(G) ≤ Δ(G)+5 if 4≤Δ(G)≤7, • χ_2(G) ≤ ⌊3Δ(G)/2⌋+1 otherwise.\end{conjecture}"

title: A Complex Analogue of Spencer's Six Standard Deviations Theorem and the Complex Banach-Mazur Distance
body_tex:
\label{conj1}
Let $n \geq 1$ be an integer and let $a_1, \ldots, a_n \in \mathbb{C}^n$ be vectors such that $\|a_i\|_{\infty} \leq 1$ for every $i=1, \ldots, n$. Then, there exists a unimodular vector $x \in \mathbb{C}^n$ such that $|\langle x, a_i \rangle| \leq \sqrt{n}$ for every $i=1, \ldots, n$.
plain_text: Let $n >= 1$ be an integer and let $a_1, , a_n in C^n$ be vectors such that $\|a_i\|_ <= 1$ for every $i=1, , n$. Then, there exists a unimodular vector $x in C^n$ such that $| x, a_i | <= n$ for every $i=1, , n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A sharp, dimension-uniform complex analogue of Spencer's theorem; tight via complex Hadamard matrices and implies exact Banach–Mazur distances. Connects discrepancy theory with complex Hadamard/SIC-POVM problems, giving broad structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Exact sharp constant for all n is a strong global statement; only n=2,3 resolved here. Related hard problems (Zauner, Hadamard classification) and lack of a clear general method make a 5-year resolution plausible but uncertain.
rationale: Conjecture 1 asserts existence of a unimodular vector with |⟨x,a_i⟩| ≤ √n for all n; paper proves only n=2,3, so the general statement remains open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture 1 stated (lines 133-136). Paper proves Conjecture 1 for n=2,3 (see theorem summary lines 186-187).

title: A Complex Analogue of Spencer's Six Standard Deviations Theorem and the Complex Banach-Mazur Distance
plain_text: For every integer $n >= 1$, we have equation d_ BM (_ 1 ^n(C), _ ^n(C)) = n. equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Determines the exact Banach–Mazur distance d_{BM}(ell_1^n(C),ell_\infty^n(C)), implied by Conjecture 1. Significant within Banach space geometry though narrower than the original vector-phase conjecture.
viability_rationale: Proved for n=2,3; upper bound follows from complex Hadamard matrices (exist for all n). Only the lower bound remains. Concrete finite-dimensional target and structure give moderate chance, but little progress beyond small n.
rationale: Conjecture 2 claims d_BM(ℓ_1^n(ℂ),ℓ_∞^n(ℂ)) = √n for every n; authors prove it only for n=2,3, so the general claim remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture 2 stated (lines 151-156). Paper notes Conjecture 2 holds for n=2,3 as a corollary (lines 186-187).

title: A Complex Analogue of Spencer's Six Standard Deviations Theorem and the Complex Banach-Mazur Distance
plain_text: For every integer $n >= 1$ and all $1 <= q <= 2 <= p <= $ we have equation d_ BM (_ p ^n(C), _ q ^n(C)) = n^ , equation where $ = 1 2 -1 p , 1 q -1 2 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gives an exact, dimension-dependent formula for d_{BM}(ell_p^n(C),ell_q^n(C)) when p,q straddle 2. Upper bounds are known; proving the matching lower bound would resolve a family of central Banach-space questions.
viability_rationale: Finite-dimensional but a complex analogue of Spencer/Komlós-style vector-balancing; proved only for n≤3 and upper bounds known. Adapting deep discrepancy/vector-balancing methods seems nontrivial; moderate chance of resolution in five years.
rationale: Conjecture 3 asserts d_BM(ℓ_p^n(ℂ),ℓ_q^n(ℂ)) = n^α for all n and 1≤q≤2≤p; the paper proves the conjecture only for n=2, leaving general case open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture 3 stated (lines 173-179). Paper states Conjecture 3 holds for n=2 (summary lines 186-187).

title: Khovanov concordance minima and the (4,5) torus knot
body_tex:
\label{conj:sliceribbon}
	The concordance class of the unknot has a global minimum (the unknot itself) with respect to $\leq$.
plain_text: The concordance class of the unknot has a global minimum (the unknot itself) with respect to $ <= $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The classical slice–ribbon conjecture (unknot as global minimum under ribbon concordance) is a flagship problem in low-dimensional topology with wide structural and classification consequences.
viability_rationale: Classic slice–ribbon conjecture (unknot global minimum). Long-standing, widely open problem with no concrete imminent route in the excerpt—resolution within five years is very unlikely.
rationale: This is the classical slice–ribbon conjecture (unknot is global minimum); the paper states it as the outstanding conjecture and does not resolve it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 210-214: "Slice-ribbon conjecture. The concordance class of the unknot has a global minimum (the unknot itself) with respect to \le."

title: How fast does the range of simple random walk grow?
body_tex:
\label{conj:linear}
If $G$ is vertex-nonamenable, then (\ref{def:linear}) holds for some $c=c(G)>0$.
plain_text: If $G$ is vertex-nonamenable, then () holds for some $c=c(G)>0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects vertex-isoperimetry (nonamenability) to linear range growth of simple random walk on possibly unbounded-degree graphs — a central, influential question in probability on graphs with clear structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Known for bounded-degree/non-amenable graphs; main barrier is unbounded degrees. Probabilistic/spectral techniques may bridge the gap, so a 5-year resolution is reasonably plausible.
rationale: The authors explicitly conjecture that vertex-nonamenability implies linear range and discuss known special cases (bounded-degree non-amenable, uniformly transient) but do not prove the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 299–313 define linear range and vertex-nonamenability and state Conjecture  (lines 311–313): 'If G is vertex-nonamenable, then (def:linear) holds...'.

title: A proof of Dolbeault geometric Langlands for $\mathrm{GL}_2$ with reduced spectral curves
