label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Identifying the Whittaker-normalized object in the Dolbeault geometric Langlands equivalence for GL2; a structural compatibility check for Phi and limit-category methods with consequences inside categorical Langlands/DT theory.
viability_rationale: Concrete categorical isomorphism tied to results in the same paper; specific, technical, and likely reachable by extending the authors' methods within a few years.
rationale: The paper states this as an explicit conjecture (Whittaker normalization) and does not prove it; presented as an open property of the functor Φ.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2651-2656: "We conjecture the following... There is an isomorphism Φ(O_{H(w)^{ss}}) ≅ s_! O_B ..."

title: Quadratic irrational analogues of Ramanujan's series for $1/π$
body_tex:
\label{C:1.1}
The series $N\in\left\{8,12,16,28\right\}$ in Table~\ref{T:1.1} are related to the series $N\in\left\{2,3,4,7\right\}$ in Table~\ref{T:1.1}.
plain_text: The series $N in 8,12,16,28 $ in Table are related to the series $N in 2,3,4,7 $ in Table .
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A table-level relation among quadratic-irrational 1/π series via modular/hypergeometric transformations; useful for classification but primarily a finite, technical pattern identification.
viability_rationale: Finite explicit cases at low modular levels, amenable to hypergeometric/modular transformations; authors proved similar conjectures and remark some cases are ‘‘very easy’’, so a resolution is likely with current methods.
rationale: Conjecture C1.1 is stated in the paper without a proof or a remark claiming it is resolved, hence remains an open conjecture in this work.
evidence_snippet: Lines 6572-6576: "Conjecture C: The series N∈{8,12,16,28} in Table 1.1 are related to the series N∈{2,3,4,7} in Table 1.1."

title: Quadratic irrational analogues of Ramanujan's series for $1/π$
body_tex:
\label{C:1.2}
The series $N\in\left\{6,10,18,22,58\right\}$ in Table~\ref{T:1.1} are related to the series $N\in\left\{3,5,9,11,29\right\}$ in Table~\ref{T:2.1}.
plain_text: The series $N in 6,10,18,22,58 $ in Table are related to the series $N in 3,5,9,11,29 $ in Table .
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Similar table-matching conjecture linking specific quadratic 1/π series by transformations; meaningful for completing the classification but a narrow, finite-scope statement.
viability_rationale: Finite explicit identities between listed series; several analogous cases are proved in the paper and authors note some cases are easy. Likely resolvable by routine modular/hypergeometric transformations within a few years.
rationale: Conjecture C1.2 is presented as a conjecture in the paper and is not annotated as proved; it remains an open relation asserted by the authors.
evidence_snippet: Lines 6578-6581: "Conjecture C1.2: The series N∈{6,10,18,22,58} in Table 1.1 are related to the series N∈{3,5,9,11,29} in Table 2.1."

title: Total positivity in twisted flag varieties
plain_text: Let $ P(^_ _1 _ _2 )_ 0 $ be the set of lines spanned by a vector whose coordinates with respect to $ B(^_ _1 _ _2 )$ are all nonnegative. Then $$(G/B^+ x G/B^-)_ 0 = (G/B^+ x G/B^-) P(^_ _1 _ _2 )_ 0 .$$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural representation-theoretic characterization of the totally nonnegative double flag via canonical bases; generalizes Lusztig/Bao–He results and links positivity, tensor products, and Kac–Moody theory with clear geometric consequences.
viability_rationale: One inclusion is proved here and recent canonical-basis positivity results shrink the gap. Active community and concrete representation‑theoretic tools make a full proof plausible within five years.
rationale: The paper formulates the equality as a conjecture and only proves one inclusion; the full equality remains unproven in this work.
evidence_snippet: Lines 692–695 state the conjecture. Lines 697–699: “Using these results, we prove one inclusion of the conjecture ... (G/B^+×G/B^-)_{≥0} ⊆ P(^ωΛ⊗Λ)_{≥0}.”

title: Homomorphism counting for immersion-closed classes is not isomorphism
body_tex: Let $\mathcal{M}_{1}$ and $\mathcal{M}_{2}$ be two distinct immersion-closed and union-closed classes. Then for some $i,j \in \{1,2\}$ with $i \neq j$, there are graphs $G$ and $H$ such that $G \equal_{\mathcal{M}_{i}} H$ and $G  \neq_{\mathcal{M}_{j}} H$.
plain_text: Let $M_ 1 $ and $M_ 2 $ be two distinct immersion-closed and union-closed classes. Then for some $i,j in 1,2 $ with $i != j$, there are graphs $G$ and $H$ such that $G _ M_ i H$ and $G _ M_ j H$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural strengthening of the main theorem distinguishing immersion-closed union-closed classes; nontrivial but scoped to homomorphism-indistinguishability theory rather than a field-changing bridge.
viability_rationale: Natural strengthening of a newly proved theorem; recent techniques likely applicable but nontrivial new construction required. Plausible within a few years but not an immediate corollary of the paper.
rationale: The paper presents this strengthening as an open conjecture, explicitly leaving it as an open problem and not proving it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 208-212: "The following further strengthening may be possible and we leave it as an open problem." then the conjecture statement.

title: Homomorphism counting for immersion-closed classes is not isomorphism
body_tex: There exists an absolute constant $c$ such that if $G\odd K_{ct}$, then $G$ contains a $K_{t}$-immersion.
plain_text: There exists an absolute constant $c$ such that if $G K_ ct $, then $G$ contains a $K_ t $-immersion.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts a linear (possibly c=1) relationship between oddomorphism size and K_t-immersions; central to the paper’s structural approach and has clear consequences for immersion/colouring theory within the subfield.
viability_rationale: Authors prove a version with an explicit (nonoptimal) bound and reduce to cases giving linear-degree immersions; concrete structural progress and small-t proofs make a constant c plausible, though optimizing constants remains technical.
rationale: The authors state the stronger constant-bound version as a conjecture after proving a non-optimal bound, explicitly marking it as conjectural and open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 241-245: "The bound in Theorem... is not necessarily optimal, and we make the following conjecture. There exists an absolute constant c such that..."

title: Almost-primes in Sun's $x^2+ny^2$ conjecture
plain_text: Sun2017 ] Every integer $n > 1$ has a decomposition $n = x + y$ with integers $x,y > 0$, such that $x + ny$ and $x^2 + ny^2$ are simultaneously prime.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Sun's simultaneous-prime decomposition conjecture is a strong prime-value-of-polynomial/additive problem; resolving it would have substantial impact in analytic number theory and sieve methods.
viability_rationale: Strong simultaneous-primality statement akin to Schinzel/twin-prime problems; only almost-prime sieve progress so far, making a full resolution within five years very unlikely.
rationale: This is Sun's original conjecture cited by the authors; the paper obtains almost-prime partial results but does not prove the exact statement that both values are simultaneously prime.
evidence_snippet: Lines 127-130: "Every integer n>1 has a decomposition n=x+y... such that x+ny and x^2+ny^2 are simultaneously prime."

title: Existence and non-existence of rational elliptic curves with prescribed torsion subgroups over quadratic fields
plain_text: Let $p 11,1920$ be a prime. Then, there exists infinitely many elliptic curves $E/$, satisfying equation* E(Q(-p))_ tors /2 x /10. equation*
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts infinitely many rational elliptic curves giving torsion Z/2×Z/10 over Q(√-p) for p≡11,19 mod20; a meaningful refinement in the classification of which quadratic fields realize specific torsion.
viability_rationale: Question about infinitely many rational curves realizing a torsion over quadratic fields reduces to ranks of quadratic twists of genus-1 modular curves; established tools and parity heuristics give plausible routes but not immediate.
rationale: The paper lists this as an open question/conjecture ('We also leave the following questions open') and does not prove existence of infinitely many such curves for the stated congruence classes.
evidence_snippet: Lines 210–216: 'We also leave the following questions open: (Conjecture) Let p≡11,19 (mod 20). Then there exists infinitely many elliptic curves E/ℚ with E(ℚ(√−p))_tors ≅ Z/2×Z/10.'

title: Existence and non-existence of rational elliptic curves with prescribed torsion subgroups over quadratic fields
plain_text: Let $p -124$ be a prime. Then, there exists infinitely many elliptic curves $E/$, satisfying equation* E(Q(-p))_ tors /2 x /12. equation*
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts infinitely many rational elliptic curves with torsion Z/2×Z/12 over Q(√-p) for p≡−1 mod24; impacts torsion-realizability questions via ranks of quadratic twists of modular curves.
viability_rationale: Similar to Conjecture 565: a genus-1 modular-curve/twist rank problem with existing descent/rank techniques and conditional parity tools, offering reasonable but not assured five-year solvability.
rationale: Paper explicitly poses this as an open conjecture and does not establish the infinitude of such elliptic curves for p≡−1 (mod 24); only related evidence and negative results for other congruence classes are given.
evidence_snippet: Lines 219–225: 'Conjecture: Let p≡−1 (mod 24) be a prime. Then there exists infinitely many elliptic curves E/ℚ with E(ℚ(√−p))_tors ≅ Z/2×Z/12.'

title: Ekedahl-Oort Types and Newton Polygons of Abelian Covers of $\mathbf{P}^1$ Branched at Three Points
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:dickson}
    \cite[Conjecture 1.3]{caldwell} If $m \geq 1$, $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_m$ are integers, $b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_m$ are all integers greater than or equal to $1$, and there is no prime that divides all of $a_1+b_1n, a_2+b_2n,\ldots, a_m+b_mn$ for every $n$, then there are infinitely many $n$ such that all of $a_1+b_1n, a_2+b_2n,\ldots, a_m+b_mn$ are prime.
plain_text: If $m >= 1$, $a_1,a_2,,a_m$ are integers, $b_1,b_2,,b_m$ are all integers greater than or equal to $1$, and there is no prime that divides all of $a_1+b_1n, a_2+b_2n,, a_m+b_mn$ for every $n$, then there are infinitely many $n$ such that all of $a_1+b_1n, a_2+b_2n,, a_m+b_mn$ are prime.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Dickson's conjecture (prime values of linear forms) is a fundamental, classical conjecture in analytic number theory (generalized prime k-tuples); resolving it would have major consequences across number theory.
viability_rationale: Dickson's conjecture is a far-reaching general prime-producing conjecture (generalizing many classical hard problems); a near-term proof is extremely unlikely.
rationale: Dickson's conjecture (linear forms prime infinitely often) is a classical unproved conjecture; the paper cites and assumes it but does not resolve it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 682-684: Conjecture (Dickson) stated; lines 686-690: paper deduces consequences assuming Dickson, not proving it.

viability_rationale: Kollár's Kodaira-dimension-zero fundamental-group conjecture is classic and solved only in low dimensions; remaining high-dimensional cases are longstanding and unlikely to be closed within five years.
rationale: This is Kollár's longstanding conjecture (kappa=0 implies almost-abelian pi_1). The paper notes it is proved in dimensions 3 and 4 but remains open in higher dimensions.

title: Towards Lang--Vojta via Degeneration
body_tex: \label{conjecture:Lang--Vojta} Let $k$ be a number field and $X/k$ a smooth projective variety, with canonical sheaf $\mathcal{K}$. Let $D$ be an effective, reduced, normal crossings divisor on $X$ and let $S$ be any finite set of primes in $\mathcal{O}_k$. If $\mathcal{K}\otimes\mathcal{O}_X(D)$ is big, then any set of $(D,S)$-integral points in $X$ is Zariski degenerate (i.e. not  dense in the Zariski topology).
plain_text: Let $k$ be a number field and $X/k$ a smooth projective variety, with canonical sheaf $K$. Let $D$ be an effective, reduced, normal crossings divisor on $X$ and let $S$ be any finite set of primes in $O_k$. If $K_X(D)$ is big, then any set of $(D,S)$-integral points in $X$ is Zariski degenerate (i.e. not dense in the Zariski topology).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Lang–Vojta conjecture is a central, field-level Diophantine statement predicting Zariski non-density of integral points for log-general-type pairs; resolving it would have broad, deep consequences across arithmetic geometry.
viability_rationale: This is the broad Lang–Vojta (Vojta/Bombieri–Lang) conjecture — a long-standing, deep Diophantine problem; unlikely to be fully resolved within five years despite local progress.
rationale: The Lang--Vojta conjecture is stated as the central open conjecture in Diophantine geometry; the paper treats cases but does not claim a proof of the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: "\begin{conjecture}[The Lang--Vojta conjecture]... If K_X(D) is big, then any set of (D,S)-integral points in X is Zariski degenerate." "for the most part, they remain open." (introduction).

title: Remarks on relative categoricity

title: Multivariate and quantitative Erdős-Kac laws for Beatty sequences
plain_text: Let $f$ be a polynomial with at least one irrational non-constant coefficient. For $n sim U[N]$, the random variable equation* (f(n)) - N N equation* converges in distribution to $N(0, 1)$ as $N$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends Erdős–Kac to generalised polynomials with irrational coefficients; a meaningful specialist conjecture unifying Beatty and integer polynomial cases, with partial results for many coefficient types.
viability_rationale: Weyl+Fourier methods already prove the conjecture for almost all leading coefficients (finite-type, algebraic). Remaining pathological (Liouville-like) irrationals need new quantitative equidistribution controls, so resolution is plausible but nontrivial in 5 years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as an open conjecture about Erdős–Kac for floor(f(n)) with an irrational coefficient; only special cases (finite type leading coefficient) are known.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: for polynomial f with an irrational non-constant coefficient, (ω(⌊f(n)⌋)-loglog N)/√(loglog N) → N(0,1). Paper notes special cases known but general case remains open (lines 147–156).

title: Multivariate and quantitative Erdős-Kac laws for Beatty sequences

title: Structured sunflowers and Ramsey properties
plain_text: Let $K$ be a relational class with strong amalgamation. We conjecture: center $()$ $K$ has the canonical vertex-Ramsey property $$ $K$ has the finite sunflower property. center We also conjecture that $K$ has the finite $2$-sunflower property if and only if $K$ has the finite sunflower property: note that $()$ would imply this.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes equivalence between canonical vertex‑Ramsey and finite sunflower properties for strong‑amalgamation Fraïssé classes. A natural connection in structural Ramsey theory with meaningful but specialized impact.
viability_rationale: Combinatorial/model-theoretic equivalence is concrete, authors proved one direction and reductions exist; community techniques make a resolution within five years reasonably plausible.
rationale: The authors pose this equivalence between the canonical vertex-Ramsey property and the finite sunflower property as an explicit conjecture in the 'Further questions' section, not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 664–669: 'Let K be a relational Fréchet class with strong amalgamation. We conjecture: (∗) K has the canonical vertex-Ramsey property ⇔ K has the finite sunflower property.'

title: On cyclically covering subspaces of $\mathbb{F}^n_q$
plain_text: Let \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) be two \(\)-invariant subspaces of \(F_ q^n \) satisfying \(F_ q^n = W_1 W_2\), where \(^n = id\). Then equation h_q(n) = h_ (F_ q^n ) = \,h_ (W_1),\,h_ (W_2)\, . equation

title: Recent advances in Brill--Noether theory and the geometry of Brill--Noether curves

Then the locus of line bundles \(L in W^r_dC\) for which \(^k_L\) is not of maximal rank has dimension equation (g, r, d) - 1 - | r + k k - (dk+1 - g) |. equation In particular, \(^k_L\) is of maximal rank for every \(L in W^r_dC\) when this quantity is negative.

title: Phase transitions with bounded index: Parallels to De Giorgi's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:indclasnogrowth}
    Let $4\leq n\leq 7$, and let $u:\R^n\to[-1,1]$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ is one-dimensional.
plain_text: Let $4 <= n <= 7$, and let $u:^n to [-1,1]$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ is one-dimensional.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A finite‑index analogue of De Giorgi for 4≤n≤7: if true, it yields a strong rigidity classification across higher dimensions, impacting PDE, geometric measure theory, and phase transition theory.
viability_rationale: Significant recent progress (full result in R^4 with growth, conditional higher-dim results) yields a concrete path; removing growth/conditional hypotheses is plausible but nontrivial within five years.
rationale: The finite-index De Giorgi conjecture for 4≤n≤7 is stated, but the paper proves only the n=4 case with energy-growth assumption and a conditional result for 4≤n≤7 assuming a stable-classification hypothesis.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 478–481). Theorem: classification in R^4 requires E(u,B_R) ≤ C R^3; conditional classification for 4≤n≤7 assumes (★) stable-classification.

title: Equilibrium measures for higher dimensional rotationally symmetric Riesz gases
body_tex:
\label{Cj1}
The equilibrium measure is supported in $\R^d$ if and only if $a \ge a_{ \rm cri }$.
plain_text: The equilibrium measure is supported in $^d$ if and only if $a >= a_ cri $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A threshold criterion for full support of the Coulomb half‑space equilibrium measure. It addresses a natural phase‑transition in Riesz/Coulomb gases with clear potential‑theoretic and probabilistic implications.
viability_rationale: Variational/analytic statement with proofs for low dimensions and a concrete reduction; accessible to current techniques and likely resolvable in the moderate term by experts.
rationale: Authors state the support-if-and-only-if claim for all d as a conjecture and explicitly note no rigorous proof in full generality, proving only the special case d=3 in the appendix.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: equilibrium measure supported in R^d iff a ≥ a_cri. Authors: 'At present, we are not aware of a rigorous proof of this statement in full generality.' Appendix proves d=3.

title: Decidability of Interpretability

viability_rationale: Major, well-known Bodirsky–Pinsker dichotomy: long-standing, widely studied with many partial results but still far from a general resolution; unlikely within five years.
rationale: The Bodirsky–Pinsker conjecture is stated and noted in the paper as still wide-open in general despite many special-case confirmations.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 152–154). Paper remarks it has been confirmed in many instances but remains "wide-open in general" (line 156).

title: Totally $Δ$-Modular Tree Decompositions of Graphic Matrices for Integer Programming
body_tex:
\label{conj:IPonaTree}
    Fix a positive integer $d$. Let $A$ be a matrix with exactly two nonzero entries per row such that $G(A)$ is a tree and $\|A\|_\infty \leq d$. Then the integer program
    \[\max\{w^Tx : Ax \leq b, x \in [\ell,u] \cap \mathbb{Z}^n\}\]
    can be solved in polynomial time for constant $d$.
plain_text: Fix a positive integer $d$. Let $A$ be a matrix with exactly two nonzero entries per row such that $G(A)$ is a tree and $\|A\|_ <= d$. Then the integer program \[ w^Tx : Ax <= b, x in [ ell ,u] cap Z^n \] can be solved in polynomial time for constant $d$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Polynomial-time solvability for two-nonzero-per-row matrices whose support graph is a tree (bounded entries) is a focused, technical open question; interesting for the subfield but narrowly scoped.
viability_rationale: Specialized, narrower case (two nonzeros per row, tree support, bounded entries). Unknown even for paths, but concentrated structure and active techniques give moderate chance of near-term resolution.
rationale: The paper explicitly labels this tree/path integer-program solvability statement as an open conjecture and remarks it is unknown even for a path.
evidence_snippet: Lines 896–903: conjecture stated and authors note it is not known even when G(A) is a path.

title: Acyclic sets and colorings in digraphs under restrictions on degrees and cycle lengths

For every tournament $H$, there is $\varepsilon_H > 0$ such that 
any $H$-free tournament $T$ on $n$ vertices satisfies $\vec{\alpha}(T) \geq n^{\varepsilon_H}$.

rationale: The tournament version is presented as a conjecture and is noted equivalent to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture, which remains open in general; the paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 136–139: tournament conjecture stated. Lines 141–143: Alon, Pach and Solymosi proved equivalence to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture (central open question).

title: Cohen-Macaulayness of squarefree powers of edge ideals of whisker graphs
body_tex:
Let $G = W(C_n)$ be the whisker graph of a cycle of length $n$. Then
\[
\depth\!\bigl(R/I(G)^{[q]}\bigr)
=
\begin{cases}
n+q-1, & \text{ if } 1\le q\le \lfloor n/2\rfloor,\\
2q-1, & \text{ if } \lfloor n/2\rfloor+1\le q\le n.
\end{cases}
\]
plain_text: Let $G = W(C_n)$ be the whisker graph of a cycle of length $n$. Then \[ \!(R/I(G)^ [q] ) = cases n+q-1, & if 1 <= q <= n/2, 2q-1, & if n/2+1 <= q <= n. cases \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A concrete depth formula for whisker cycles; resolving it completes the depth classification for this natural family in combinatorial commutative algebra—important to specialists but limited broader consequences.
viability_rationale: Paper verifies the formula for 1≤q≤⌊n/2⌋ and one midpoint case; remaining finite explicit range q>n/2 seems amenable to targeted combinatorial/algebraic completion within a few years.
rationale: The paper states the conjecture from DRS24 and only verifies it in a substantial but restricted range (1 ≤ q ≤ ⌊n/2⌋, and an extra case when n odd), not for all q up to n.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 298–307). Paper: 'As a consequence of our main results, we verify this conjecture in a substantial range, namely for 1 ≤ q ≤ n/2, and additionally for q = ⌈n/2⌉ when n is odd' (lines 310–311).

title: Cohen-Macaulayness of squarefree powers of edge ideals of whisker graphs
plain_text: Let $G=W(C_n)$ be the whisker graph of the cycle $C_n$. Then \[ \!(R/I(G)^ [q] ) = cases n+q-1, & if 1 <= q <= n/2, 2q-1, & if n/2<q <= n. cases \]

viability_rationale: Significant partial results in this work (and prior), with an explicit remaining range. Techniques appear likely to be extendable to settle remaining q values soon.
rationale: The identical DRS24 conjecture is presented and the authors prove it only for certain ranges (Corollary shows 1≤q≤⌊n/2⌋ and one extra odd case), not the full q-range 1..n.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture statement (lines 1921–1933). Corollary verifies partial cases: 'depth = n+q-1 for 1≤q≤⌊n/2⌋; 2q-1 for n odd and q=⌈n/2⌉' (lines 1935–1947).

title: On the maximum number of tangencies among $1$-intersecting curves
body_tex:

	Every family of $n$ precisely $1$-intersecting planar curves in which no three curves share a common point admits $O(n)$ tangencies.

interestingness_rationale: Pach's longstanding conjecture about O(n) tangencies is a central open problem in combinatorial geometry; its resolution would significantly advance understanding of curve arrangements.
viability_rationale: Pach's conjecture is a long-standing, widely known hard problem; current paper improves bounds but gives no decisive new route, so full O(n) resolution in five years seems unlikely.
rationale: Pach's conjecture is cited as open. The paper improves bounds but does not resolve the O(n) claim, so the exact conjecture remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 159-162: Pach conjecture stated. Lines 175-181: authors improve bounds to O(n^{5/3}) and O(n^{3/2}) but do not prove O(n).

title: On Bipartite-Almost Bipartite Graphs and the Determinantal Factorization
body_tex:
For every graph $G$, 
	\[
	\left|\textnormal{corona}(G)\right|+\left|\textnormal{ker}(G)\right|\le 2\alpha(G)+k,
	\]
	where $k$ is the number of odd cycles in $G$. Alternatively, $k$ could
	be the number of odd cycles in $D(G)$, or the maximum number of
	disjoint odd cycles.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural structural inequality linking corona, ker, independence number and odd-cycle count; meaningful within matching/independence theory but limited to that subfield rather than a broad breakthrough.
viability_rationale: Specialized combinatorial inequality with explicit structural decompositions in the paper; concrete expressions and related equalities suggest a plausible finite-route to a proof within a few years.
rationale: The inequality is listed in the paper's 'Open Problems' section and is not proved in the text; it is posed as an open conjecture.

title: The Kobayashi-Hitchin correspondence for nef and big classes
body_tex: Let $X$ be a compact K\"{a}hler manifold and $\alpha$ be a nef and big class on $X$. Let $T=\theta+dd^c\varphi$ be an adapted closed positive $(1,1)$-current with minimal singularities in $\alpha$ where $\theta$ is a smooth representative in $\alpha$.  Let $E$ be a holomorphic vector bundle on $E$ with a smooth hermitian metric $h_0$. Assume that $E$ admits a $T$-adapted HYM metric $h=h_0e^{2S}$ with an $h_0$-hermitian endomorphism $S$. Then $S$ is equisingular with $\varphi$, that is, $|S|_{h_0\otimes h_0^*}+\varphi\in L^{\infty}(X)$.

title: Matchings in hypergraphs via Ore-degree conditions

\label{con1}
Let $n>rs$. If ${\cal H}$ is an $r$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices such that $\sigma_r({\cal H})>r\left({n-1 \choose r-1}-{n-s \choose r-1}\right)$, then ${\cal H}$ contains a matching of size $s$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural, sharp Ore-degree analogue of the Erdős Matching Conjecture; resolving it clarifies extremal hypergraph matching thresholds and structural extremal examples.
viability_rationale: Closely related to long-standing matching problems (Erdős Matching Conjecture). Authors proved the statement for large n; lowering to the optimal n>rs remains a substantial combinatorial barrier, so near-term resolution is unlikely.

title: Abstract twisted Brin--Thompson groups

viability_rationale: Boone–Higman is a classic, long-standing grand conjecture; full resolution in five years is very unlikely absent major breakthroughs.

title: Energy-Morawetz estimates for Teukolsky equations in perturbations of Kerr
body_tex: The maximal Cauchy development of any initial data set for Einstein vacuum equations, that is sufficiently close to a subextremal Kerr initial data in a suitable sense, has a complete future null infinity and a domain of outer communication\footnote{The domain of outer communication is the causal past of future null infinity.} which is asymptotic to a nearby member of the subextremal Kerr family.
plain_text: The maximal Cauchy development of any initial data set for Einstein vacuum equations, that is sufficiently close to a subextremal Kerr initial data in a suitable sense, has a complete future null infinity and a domain of outer communication which is asymptotic to a nearby member of the subextremal Kerr family.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Kerr stability conjecture is a flagship open problem in general relativity; resolving it yields fundamental understanding of black hole dynamics and spacetime stability with very broad consequences.
viability_rationale: Recent major progress: nonlinear stability proven for small |a|, and this work (with companion) establishes key linear energy/Morawetz estimates for full subextremal range. However substantial nonlinear GCM/closure for general a remains to be carried out.
rationale: The paper states the conjecture is proved only in the slowly rotating case; the full subextremal (all |a|<m) nonlinear stability remains unresolved and the paper does not claim a complete proof.
evidence_snippet: “The most recent breakthrough... is its proof in the slowly rotating case (|a|/m << 1). A complete resolution... requires establishing energy and Morawetz estimates... in suitable perturbations of any subextremal Kerr spacetime.”

title: Energy-Morawetz estimates for the wave equation in perturbations of Kerr
body_tex: The maximal Cauchy development of any initial data set for EVE, that is sufficiently close to a subextremal Kerr initial data in a suitable sense, has a complete future null infinity and a domain of outer communication\footnote{The domain of outer communication is the complement of the black hole region.} which is asymptotic to a nearby member of the subextremal Kerr family.
plain_text: The maximal Cauchy development of any initial data set for EVE, that is sufficiently close to a subextremal Kerr initial data in a suitable sense, has a complete future null infinity and a domain of outer communication which is asymptotic to a nearby member of the subextremal Kerr family.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Same flagship Kerr stability conjecture: central to nonlinear GR, its resolution would be a major breakthrough with extensive implications across mathematical relativity and physics.
viability_rationale: Resolved for slowly rotating Kerr; key missing step is uniform energy-Morawetz/Teukolsky control for all subextremal a. Active substantial progress exists, but major technical obstacles remain, so modest chance of full resolution in 5 years.
rationale: The conjecture (nonlinear stability of the entire subextremal Kerr family) remains unresolved; only the slowly rotating case has been proved. This paper treats a partial step toward the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: “The state of the art on this conjecture is its resolution in the slowly rotating case... A complete resolution of the Kerr stability conjecture will require in particular to prove energy-Morawetz estimates... for any subextremal Kerr background.”

title: Orbit-Level Stretching in Cubic Fourier-Galerkin Navier-Stokes: Sharp Incidence, Spectral Decay, and a Continuation Criterion
body_tex:
\label{conj:sign-cancel}
There exists a constant $\delta>0$ such that
\[
  \frac{\E\bigl[\rh(V_N)\bigr]} 
       {\E\bigl[\rh\!\bigl(\abs{V_N}\bigr)\bigr]}
  \;\sim\; N^{-\delta}
  \qquad (N\to\infty),
\]
and consequently
\[
  \E\bigl[\rh(V_N)\bigr] \;=\; \Theta\!\bigl(N^{-3/2-\delta}\bigr).
\]
The numerical evidence suggests $\delta\approx 0.67$.
A proof would require quantifying the spectral effect of the skew
structure inherited from the energy identity
$\langle B(u,u),u\rangle=0$ on the dominant eigenvector of $V_N$.

A proof would require quantifying the spectral effect of the skew structure inherited from the energy identity $ B(u,u),u=0$ on the dominant eigenvector of $V_N$.

title: The Unitary Conjugation Groupoid as a Universal Mediator of the Baum--Connes Assembly Map
body_tex:
\label{conj:descent-identity}
For any invertible element $u \in M_n(A_\theta)$, the class obtained by the
composition
\[
[u] \in K_1(A_\theta) \xrightarrow{\text{lift}} KK^1_{\mathcal{G}_{A_\theta}}(A_\theta,\mathbb C)
\xrightarrow{\text{desc}} K_1(C^*(\mathcal{G}_{A_\theta})) \xrightarrow{\cong} K_1(A_\theta)
\]
coincides with the original class $[u]$. In particular, the lift map is injective
and its image consists of exactly those equivariant $KK$-classes that are fixed
by the natural automorphisms.
plain_text: For any invertible element $u in M_n(A_)$, the class obtained by the composition \[ [u] in K_1(A_) lift KK^1_ G_ A_ (A_, C) desc K_1(C^*(G_ A_ )) K_1(A_) \] coincides with the original class $[u]$. In particular, the lift map is injective and its image consists of exactly those equivariant $KK$-classes that are fixed by the natural automorphisms.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An identity conjecture tying a geometric descent construction to K1 of the irrational rotation algebra: significant for Baum–Connes/dynamics and index theory, but focused within noncommutative geometry/operator algebras.
viability_rationale: Explicit constructions for generators U,V, plus descent and Morita-equivalence framework, reduce the problem to technical verifications for general unitaries in A_theta; narrow, concrete target makes near-term resolution plausible.
rationale: This descent-identity conjecture for the irrational rotation algebra is posed as a programmatic outlook and is not proved in the paper.

title: The Unitary Conjugation Groupoid as a Universal Mediator of the Baum--Connes Assembly Map

\mu_{\mathcal{G}_A} : K^*_{\mathcal{G}_A}(\mathcal{G}_A^{(0)}) \longrightarrow K_*(C^*(\mathcal{G}_A))
\]
which is an isomorphism for a large class of $C^*$-algebras, including all nuclear $C^*$-algebras with Cartan subalgebras and all crossed products $C(X) \rtimes \Gamma$ with $\Gamma$ satisfying the Baum–Connes conjecture.

interestingness_rationale: A Baum–Connes–style isomorphism for unitary conjugation groupoids would subsume classical Baum–Connes instances and conceptually link intrinsic symmetry groupoids to K-theory, a deep, broadly consequential conjecture in noncommutative geometry.

title: A Wasserstein metric approach to generalized Skewes' numbers. I. Prime number races
body_tex:
\label{conj: ELI_A}
 Let $q \in \Zz_{\geq 1}$, $A>1$. If $\mathcal X_q$ is a set of Dirichlet characters modulo $q$, we let $(\gamma_n)_{n \geqslant 1}$ be an enumeration in non-decreasing order of the positive imaginary parts of zeros, counted with multiplicities, of Dirichlet $L$-functions associated with the characters $\chi \in \mathcal X_q$. For all $T$ sufficiently large, one has $$\left|\sum_{j=1}^{N(T)} m_j \gamma_j\right| \gg_A N(T)^{-N(T)^A}$$ for any integers $m_1, \dots, m_{N(T)}$, not all zero, satisfying $|m_j| \leq N(T)$, where $N(T)$ is the number of such imaginary parts up to height $T$, \emph{i.e.} $N(T) = \sum_{\gamma_n \leq T} 1$.
plain_text: Let $q in _ >= 1 $, $A>1$. If $ X_q$ is a set of Dirichlet characters modulo $q$, we let $(_n)_ n 1 $ be an enumeration in non-decreasing order of the positive imaginary parts of zeros, counted with multiplicities, of Dirichlet $L$-functions associated with the characters $ in X_q$. For all $T$ sufficiently large, one has $$|_ j=1 ^ N(T) m_j _j| _A N(T)^ -N(T)^A $$ for any integers $m_1, , m_ N(T) $, not all zero, satisfying $|m_j| <= N(T)$, where $N(T)$ is the number of such imaginary parts up to height $T$, i.e. $N(T) = _ _n <= T 1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A quantitative linear-independence for Dirichlet L-zeros would be a deep analytic-number-theory breakthrough, with strong consequences for prime races and error-term control in classical problems.
viability_rationale: An effective linear independence for L-function zeros is a deep analytic-number-theory statement; authors note LI is out of reach—very unlikely in five years.
rationale: ELI_A is formulated as an effective linear-independence conjecture for Dirichlet L-function zeros and is assumed (not proved) in later conditional results, so it remains open.
evidence_snippet: Section 'An effective linear independence conjecture' formulates Conjecture [ELI_A] giving explicit lower bounds for nontrivial integer combinations of zeros' imaginary parts.

title: Induced Ramsey numbers for fans
body_tex:
{\upshape \cite{KPR98}}\label{con-1}
For any graph $G$, there is a constant $f=f(G)$ that depends only on $G$ such that, for any graph $H$ on $n$ vertices, we have
$$
r_{\mathrm{ind}}(G, H) \leq n^f.
$$
plain_text: For any graph $G$, there is a constant $f=f(G)$ that depends only on $G$ such that, for any graph $H$ on $n$ vertices, we have $$ r_ ind (G, H) <= n^f. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The KPR polynomial upper bound for induced Ramsey numbers is a longstanding, influential conjecture in extremal graph theory; resolving it would have broad consequences across Ramsey-type problems.
viability_rationale: KPR conjecture is longstanding and broad; despite partial progress, a full proof remains a major open problem and unlikely within five years.
rationale: The Kohayakawa–Prömel–Rödl conjecture is cited (1998) and remains an open general conjecture; the paper discusses partial results but does not resolve it.
evidence_snippet: The paper cites Conjecture (KPR98): for any G there is f(G) with r_ind(G,H) \le n^{f}, and discusses partial/verifying results.

viability_rationale: Targeted Nakayama-algebra statement with several partial results in the paper; finite-dimensional, structural techniques likely to close the remaining gap within a few years.
rationale: The authors explicitly pose this as a conjecture and do not prove it in the paper; they state it would imply stronger properties and leave it for future work.

title: Inversion monotonicity in subclasses of the 1324-avoiders
body_tex:
\label{conj:invomono}
  All patterns, except for the identity patterns, are inversion monotone.
plain_text: All patterns, except for the identity patterns, are inversion monotone.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad enumerative-combinatorics conjecture whose resolution would control inversion distributions for pattern-avoiding permutations and improve bounds for the Stanley–Wilf limit of 1324, a central subfield problem.
viability_rationale: Open since 2012; only sporadic special-case and conditional progress (few collections, 1324 for n≥(k+7)/2). No clear route reducing the universal all-pattern claim to a finite/technical closure, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: The inversion-monotonicity conjecture (all nontrivial patterns are inversion monotone) is stated as open; the paper proves only special cases.
evidence_snippet: Lines 60-66: "Conjecture 20 ... All patterns, except for the identity patterns, are inversion monotone." and "Conjecture ... is open for all nontrivial patterns."

title: Edge-Isoperimetric Inequalities in Chamber Graphs of Hyperplane Arrangements
body_tex:
\label{conj:1}
    Let $S$ be an arbitrary subset of at most half of the chambers of a hyperplane arrangement $\mathcal{H}$ in
    general position in $\R^d$. Let $|S| = \sum_{i=0}^d {k \choose  i}$
    for some $k \ge d-1$. Then
    \[
        |\partial S| \geq \sum_{i=0}^{d-1} {k \choose  i}.
    \]
plain_text: Let $S$ be an arbitrary subset of at most half of the chambers of a hyperplane arrangement $H$ in general position in $^d$. Let $|S| = _ i=0 ^d k i $ for some $k >= d-1$. Then \[ | S| >= _ i=0 ^ d-1 k i . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural isoperimetric conjecture generalizing grid results to chamber graphs of hyperplane arrangements; proving it would give structural geometric-combinatorial insight though its impact is largely within this subfield.
viability_rationale: Concrete low-dimensional proofs (d=2) and a 3D asymptotic result plus explicit topological approach give a plausible path to extend methods; nontrivial but reasonably approachable within five years.
rationale: Authors state the conjecture explicitly and only prove it in dimension 2 and an asymptotic 3D result; the general d case remains unproven in the paper.
evidence_snippet: "We prove Conjecture~\ref{conj:1} in \R^2, and our main low-dimensional result is that the asymptotic conjecture holds in \R^3." (lines 1232-1233)

title: Maximum spectral sum of graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj:spectral_sum_extremal_graphs}
Let $n\geq 5$. Then for all graphs $G\in \mathcal{G}(n)$, we have
\[
\lambda_1(G)+\lambda_2(G)\leq \lambda_1(K(n,p,q))+\lambda_2(K(n,p,q)),
\]
where if $n=7k+r$ with $0\leq r\le 6$, then 
\[
(p,q)= 
\begin{cases}
(2k,2k) & \text{if }r=0,1;\\
(2k+1,2k) & \text{if }r=2;\\
(2k+1,2k+1) & \text{if }r=3, 4;\\
(2k+2,2k+1) &\text{if }r=5;\\
(2k+2,2k+2) &\text{if }r=6.
\end{cases} 
\] 
Moreover, equality holds if and only if $G\cong K(n,p,q)$.
plain_text: Let $n >= 5$. Then for all graphs $G in G(n)$, we have \[ _1(G)+_2(G) <= _1(K(n,p,q))+_2(K(n,p,q)), \] where if $n=7k+r$ with $0 <= r <= 6$, then \[ (p,q)= cases (2k,2k) & if r=0,1; (2k+1,2k) & if r=2; (2k+1,2k+1) & if r=3, 4; (2k+2,2k+1) &if r=5; (2k+2,2k+2) &if r=6. cases \] Moreover, equality holds if and only if $G K(n,p,q)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Characterizing extremal graphs attaining the proven spectral-sum bound is a natural extremal-spectral problem with clear combinatorial significance, though focused within spectral graph theory.
viability_rationale: Paper proves the tight 8/7 upper bound; only characterization of extremal graphs remains, hinging on a bypassable technical assumption. Explicit structure and developed tools make resolution plausible within five years.
rationale: The paper proves the 8/7 upper bound but explicitly states that characterizing extremal graphs (the stated conjecture) remains open and is proposed for readers.
evidence_snippet: "The problem of characterizing graphs which attain the bound remains open (see Conjecture \ref{conj:spectral_sum_extremal_graphs} below)." and the conjecture statement. (lines 1200-1203,1210-1216)

title: Maximum spectral sum of graphs
body_tex: For sufficiently large $n$, the path uniquely minimizes the spectral sum among all connected graphs of order $n$.
plain_text: For sufficiently large $n$, the path uniquely minimizes the spectral sum among all connected graphs of order $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extremal spectral-graph conjecture: the path minimizing λ1+λ2 would give a clean characterization in spectral extremal theory. Nontrivial and meaningful within spectral graph theory but scope is subfield-limited.
viability_rationale: Authors nearly characterized extremals via graphons, note a clear technical gap they expect to bypass; related tree results recently resolved—concrete local progress makes a 5-year resolution plausible.
rationale: The paper explicitly poses this as an open conjecture in the concluding remarks and does not prove it; they proved the upper bound but not this minimization characterization.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1241-1244: 'The minimization of the spectral sum of graphs remains an open problem. We propose the following conjecture... For sufficiently large n, the path uniquely minimizes the spectral sum...'

title: The $\infty$-Oreo$^{^\circledR}$
body_tex:
\label{conj:quiver-topology}
The topology of the food quiver determines the convergence properties and
limit compositions of its $\infty$-foods.  In particular, the presence
of additional cycles passing through a vertex alters the limit
compositions in a predictable way.
plain_text: The topology of the food quiver determines the convergence properties and limit compositions of its $$-foods. In particular, the presence of additional cycles passing through a vertex alters the limit compositions in a predictable way.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Combinatorial/topological prediction for a playful, model-specific framework. Connects to algebraic topology/representation ideas but is primarily specific to the paper's construction with limited broader mathematical impact.
viability_rationale: Conjecture is natural, finite-dimensional and supported by examples; however it is stated vaguely and requires development of topology/representation machinery, so solvable but not immediate.
rationale: The authors state this topology-determines-convergence claim as a conjecture and leave it as an invitation; no proof is given in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1752-1756: 'The topology of the food quiver determines the convergence properties and limit compositions of its ∞-foods. In particular, the presence of additional cycles...'

title: Adjacent vertices of small degree in minimal matching covered graphs

viability_rationale: Specific combinatorial graph statement with strong existing structural results (minimum degree, cubic vertex counts); accessible techniques suggest a reasonably high near-term chance.
rationale: The authors state this as a conjecture in a dedicated section; prior results give related bounds but do not resolve this exact statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 683-685: 'We surmise the following: Every minimal brace with at least 6 vertices has a 3-line.'

rationale: The authors obtain rates of order 1/2 up to logarithms and explicitly conjecture these rates are optimal; no proof of optimality is given.

title: Seymour-tight orientations
body_tex: Every oriented graph contains at least one vertex such that  $|N^+_2(v)| \geq |N^{-}_1(v)|$.

interestingness_rationale: A natural variant of Seymour's second‑neighbourhood conjecture; central to directed graph theory with structural consequences for tournaments and Eulerian digraphs, making it a significant subfield problem.
viability_rationale: Nontrivial partial results (tournaments, 'almost all' graphs) exist but no clear route to a full proof; intimately tied to Seymour's harder conjecture.
rationale: Stated as Sullivan's conjecture in the paper; authors cite known partial results but do not prove it, indicating it remains unresolved.

viability_rationale: MUB(6)<7 is a well-known, stubborn small‑dimensional problem with substantial partial work and SOS attempts but no clear finishing route; modest short‑term chance.
rationale: The statement 'MUB(6) < 7' is posed as an open conjecture; as of known literature (pre-2024) the full 7 MUBs in dimension 6 is unresolved.

title: Lattice homology of integrally closed submodules and Artin algebras
body_tex:
\label{conj:elso-intro}
    Suppose that $\mathcal{I} \triangleleft k[x_1, x_2]$ is a finite codimensional, integrally closed ideal. If $\mathbb{SH}_{\geq 1}(\mathcal{I} \triangleleft k[x_1, x_2])=0$, then $\mathcal{I}$ can be realized by a single (semi-)valuation.
plain_text: Suppose that $I k[x_1, x_2]$ is a finite codimensional, integrally closed ideal. If $SH_ >= 1 (I k[x_1, x_2])=0$, then $I$ can be realized by a single (semi-)valuation.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects vanishing positive lattice homology to realization by a single valuation for integrally closed plane ideals; confirmed for monomial case. Significant within singularity/valuation theory but specialized to two variables.
viability_rationale: Plane (two-variable) case; monomial case proved and extensive computations reported. Specific, low-dimensional setting with concrete structural tools—remaining gap appears technical and approachable by experts.
rationale: Paper states this as a conjecture and only proves the monomial case; the general two-variable integrally closed ideal statement is not resolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 1168-1170); monomial case proved later (line 1172 onwards).

title: Lattice homology of integrally closed submodules and Artin algebras
body_tex:
\label{conj:elso}
    Let us suppose that $\mathcal{I} \triangleleft \mathcal{O}_2=k[x_1, x_2]$ is a finite codimensional, integrally closed ideal. We conjecture that if ${\rm homdim}(\mathcal{I} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O})=0$, then $\mathcal{I}$ can be realized by a single valuation, i.e., $\mathcal{I}=\{ f \in \mathcal{O}_2\,:\, \mathfrak{v}(f) \geq d_{\mathcal{I}}\}$ for some discrete valuation $\mathfrak{v}$ and integer $d_{\mathcal{I}}$.
plain_text: Let us suppose that $I O_2=k[x_1, x_2]$ is a finite codimensional, integrally closed ideal. We conjecture that if $ homdim (I O)=0$, then $I$ can be realized by a single valuation, i.e., $I= f in O_2\,:\, v(f) >= d_ I $ for some discrete valuation $v$ and integer $d_ I $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Same statement as 1499: links homological vanishing to valuation ideals in k[x1,x2]. Nontrivial within algebraic geometry/commutative algebra but narrowly scoped to plane integrally closed ideals.
viability_rationale: Same conjecture as 1499: integrally closed ideals in k[x,y]. Monomial theorem and computations give realistic routes; likely solvable with further technical advances.
rationale: Authors pose this as Conjecture \ref{conj:elso}; they prove it for monomial ideals but do not prove the general homdim=0 implies single valuation statement.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (lines 2391-2393); theorem proves monomial-case converse (lines 2395-2402).

title: A note on Bondal's conjecture
body_tex: (\cite{bondal1993non}) If $(X, \pi)$ is a Fano Poisson complex manifold of rank $r$ then $D_{2k}$ has an irreducible component of dimension at least $2k+1$ for all $k\in \{0, 1, \dots, r-1\}.$
plain_text: () If $(X, )$ is a Fano Poisson complex manifold of rank $r$ then $D_ 2k $ has an irreducible component of dimension at least $2k+1$ for all $k in 0, 1, , r-1 .$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Bondal's conjecture is a longstanding, structural statement in Poisson/Fano geometry about degeneracy‑locus dimensions; resolving it has broad algebraic‑geometric implications and is a central open problem in the field.
viability_rationale: Bondal's conjecture is a decades-old foundational algebraic/Poisson geometry problem with deep obstructions; no clear imminent route to full resolution.
rationale: Bondal's 1993 conjecture is stated as a classical open conjecture; the paper provides supporting results but does not prove it in full generality (noted open cases remain).
evidence_snippet: Lines 171–176 and 182–187: 'Bondal made the following conjecture... If (X,π) is Fano Poisson... D_{2k} has an irreducible component of dimension at least 2k+1.' Some low-dimensional cases resolved; others remain open.

title: Greedy sparsifications of sums of positive semidefinite matrices
body_tex:
\label{conj:fixed-rank}
For every integer \(r\ge 2\) and every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists a constant \(C_{r}\) such that the following holds. Let
\(
P_1,\dots,P_m
\)
be orthogonal projections of rank \(r\) in \(\R^d\), and assume that
\[
\sum_{i\in[m]} \lambda_i P_i=\frac{r}{d}\id_d,
\qquad
\sum_{i\in[m]} \lambda_i=1, \quad
 \lambda_i \ge 0 \
\quad\text{for all }i\in[m].
\]
Then there exist indices \(i_1,\dots,i_N\in[m]\) with
\[
N\le C_{r} \frac{d}{\e^2}
\]
such that
\[
\norm{
\frac{d}{rN}\sum_{j\in[N]} P_{i_j}-\id_d
}
\le \epsilon.
\]

title: Helly Theorems for Generalized Turán Problems
body_tex:
\label{optimal path conjecture}
		Let $k,t$ be integers with $k\ge 2$ and $t\ge 2k+1$.  If $0\le r\le k-2$ is the unique integer such that $t-3\equiv r \mod k-1$, then every family of graphs $\c{F}$ either satisfies
		\[\ex(n,P_t,\c{F})=O(\ex(n,\c{F})^{r+2}n^{k-2-r}),\]
		or $\ex(n,P_t,\c{F})=\Omega(n^{k+1})$.
plain_text: Let $k,t$ be integers with $k >= 2$ and $t >= 2k+1$. If $0 <= r <= k-2$ is the unique integer such that $t-3 r k-1$, then every family of graphs $F$ either satisfies \[(n,P_t,F)=O((n,F)^ r+2 n^ k-2-r ),\] or $(n,P_t,F)=(n^ k+1 )$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Sharp exponent prediction for paths in generalized Turán problems; tight in many cases and would refine Helly-type combinatorial tools, yet its significance is focused on extremal/path-specific questions.
viability_rationale: Paper settles several cases (r=k-2,k-3), provides tightness constructions, and pinpoints a concrete Helly-type obstruction; remaining gap is technical and approachable, so a resolution within ~5 years is plausible but uncertain.
rationale: The authors state the 'optimal path conjecture' in concluding remarks as a believed refinement and note only special cases are proved (r=k-2,k-3), leaving the general conjecture open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1498–1505: "In general we believe the following refinement for paths should be true... \begin{conjecture}...\end{conjecture}." They note main results imply it for cases r=k-2,k-3.

title: Rational Exponents for General Graphs
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:maxDegree}
		If $H$ is a graph of maximum degree $\Del(H)$, then every rational in the interval
		\[
		\left[v(H)-\frac{e(H)}{\Del(H)},v(H)\right]
		\]
		is realizable for $H$.
plain_text: If $H$ is a graph of maximum degree $(H)$, then every rational in the interval \[ [v(H)-e(H) (H) ,v(H)] \] is realizable for $H$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad uniform interval of realizable exponents tied to maximum degree; proving it would substantially advance the classification of generalized Turán exponents for arbitrary graphs, a central subfield problem.
viability_rationale: Paper proves an approximate version and the tree case; available methods (random polynomial, reductions) seem adaptable. Closing the constant gap appears technical, making a resolution within five years moderately plausible.
rationale: The maximum-degree realizability conjecture is proposed in the concluding remarks; the paper proves it for trees or approximately for general graphs but does not resolve the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1306–1312: "\begin{conjecture}\label{conjecture:maxDegree} If H is a graph of maximum degree ... every rational ... is realizable for H. \end{conjecture}" Presented as open.

title: The Galois Alperin weight conjecture for finite category algebras

\label{conj:AWC for finite groups}
For any finite group $G$, there exists a bijection between $\S(kG)$ and $\S(W(k\O_G))$.
plain_text: For any finite group $G$, there exists a bijection between $(kG)$ and $(W(k_G))$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Alperin's weight conjecture is a flagship conjecture in modular representation theory; its resolution would have deep, wide-ranging structural consequences for blocks and character theory.
viability_rationale: Equivalent to Navarro's Galois Alperin weight conjecture. Significant reduction-to-simple-groups progress exists, but many deep simple-group/Galois cases remain; full resolution within five years unlikely.
rationale: Alperin's weight conjecture is stated (classical open problem); the paper discusses extensions and equivalences but does not prove it in general.
evidence_snippet: Lines 231–233 and 235–239: "\begin{conjecture}[Alperin]... For any finite group G, there exists a bijection between S(kG) and S(W(kO_G))." Presented as conjecture; equivalence to category version noted.

title: p-adic Hodge theory of de Rham local systems, I: Newton polygon and monodromy
body_tex:
\label{conj: RpMT1}
Let $X$ be a smooth rigid-analytic space over a $p$-adic field $K$, and let $\mathbb{L}$ be a de Rham $\mathbb{Q}_p$-local system on $X$. Every rank-$1$ point $x \in X$ admits an open neighborhood $V \subset X$ and a \emph{finite} \'etale covering $V' \to V$ such that the restriction $\mathbb{L}|_{V'}$ is log-crystalline at all classical points of $V'$.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a smooth rigid-analytic space over a $p$-adic field $K$, and let $L$ be a de Rham $Q_p$-local system on $X$. Every rank-$1$ point $x in X$ admits an open neighborhood $V subset X$ and a finite \'etale covering $V' to V$ such that the restriction $L|_ V' $ is log-crystalline at all classical points of $V'$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The local relative p-adic monodromy conjecture at rank-1 points is central in p-adic Hodge theory; its resolution would strongly link Newton polygon constancy and log-crystalline coverings with major consequences.
viability_rationale: Rank-1 restriction makes the relative p-adic monodromy claim more tractable; recent partial results and proposed strategies (Kedlaya, Shimizu references) make a near-term resolution plausible but not certain.
rationale: RpMT_1 is presented as a conjecture (local relative p‑adic monodromy around rank‑1 points); the paper proves equivalences but does not establish the conjecture itself.
evidence_snippet: Lines 420-428: Theorem states equivalence; Conjecture RpMT_1 is then stated (each rank‑1 point admits finite étale V' with L|_{V'} log‑crystalline).

title: The record statistic and forward stability of Schubert products
body_tex:
\label{conj:fs-rdense}
As $n\to\infty$, the following hold.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item (Boolean, refined constant term.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\mathcal B_n}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}\;=\;n+3+o(1).
\]

\item (CoGrassmannian.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\CoGr_n}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}\;=\;n+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\sqrt{n}+O(1).
\]

\item ($321$-avoiding.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Av_n(321)}$, then there exists a constant $c_{321}\in[0.92,0.94]$ such that
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}\;=\;n+c_{321}\sqrt{n}+O(1).
\]

\end{enumerate}
plain_text: As $n to $, the following hold. [label=()] (Boolean, refined constant term.) If $u,v sim B_n$, then \[ (u,v)\;=\;n+3+o(1). \] (CoGrassmannian.) If $u,v sim _n$, then \[ (u,v)\;=\;n+1 n+O(1). \] ($321$-avoiding.) If $u,v sim _n(321)$, then there exists a constant $c_ 321 in [0.92,0.94]$ such that \[ (u,v)\;=\;n+c_ 321 n+O(1). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asymptotic predictions for forward stability in three permutation classes; mathematically substantive within algebraic combinatorics but largely class-specific rather than field-transforming.
viability_rationale: Concrete asymptotic claims for specific permutation classes with supporting computations and partial results in the paper; technical but standard probabilistic/combinatorial refinements likely within reach in a few years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this multi-part asymptotic conjecture (record-dense regime) and does not prove these precise asymptotic formulas or constants within the text.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Record-dense regime): As n→∞, (a) E[FS]=n+3+o(1) for Boolean; (b) E[FS]=n+1/√π √n+O(1) for CoGrassmannian; (c) E[FS]=n+c_{321}√n+O(1).

title: The record statistic and forward stability of Schubert products
body_tex:
\label{conj:fs-rint}
As $n\to\infty$, the following hold.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item (Smooth.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Sm_n}$, then there exists a constant $c_{\mathrm{sm}}\in[1.35,1.40]$ such that
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)} \;=\; c_{\mathrm{sm}}\,n + o(n).
\]

\item (Maximal Levi-spherical.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Lev_n}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)} \;=\; \frac{5}{4}\,n + O(1).
\]
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: As $n to $, the following hold. [label=()] (Smooth.) If $u,v sim _n$, then there exists a constant $c_ sm in [1.35,1.40]$ such that \[ (u,v) \;=\; c_ sm \,n + o(n). \] (Maximal Levi-spherical.) If $u,v sim _n$, then \[ (u,v) \;=\; 5 4 \,n + O(1). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Linear-order constants for smooth and maximal Levi-spherical classes give concrete structural predictions for FS; useful to specialists but narrow in scope.
viability_rationale: Mid-regime linear constants and a 5/4 prediction are specific, tested numerically, and supported by structure; further rigorous averaging/limit arguments could resolve these within five years.
rationale: The intermediate-regime conjecture (specific linear constants for Sm_n and Lev_n) is posed in the paper and is not proved there; authors report supporting computations only.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Intermediate record regime): (a) exists c_sm∈[1.35,1.40] with E[FS]=c_sm n+o(n); (b) for Lev_n, E[FS]=5/4 n+O(1).

title: The record statistic and forward stability of Schubert products
body_tex:
\label{conj:fs-rsparse}
As $n\to\infty$, the following hold.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item ($231$-avoiding.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Av_n(231)}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}\;=\;2n-5+o(1).
\]

\item ($132$-avoiding.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Av_n(132)}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}\;=\;2n-5+o(1).
\]

\item (Fireworks.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\mathcal F_n}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)}
\;=\;
2n-\frac{2n}{\log n-\log\log n}
+o\!\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right).
\]

\item (Vexillary.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\Vex_n}$, then
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)} \;=\; 2n - O(\sqrt{n}).
\]

\item (Covexillary.) If $u,v\sim \Unif{\CoVex_n}$, then there exists a constant $c_{\mathrm{cov}}\in[6,9]$ such that
\[
\E{\FS(u,v)} \;=\; 2n - c_{\mathrm{cov}} - o(1).
\]
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: As $n to $, the following hold. [label=()] ($231$-avoiding.) If $u,v sim _n(231)$, then \[ (u,v)\;=\;2n-5+o(1). \] ($132$-avoiding.) If $u,v sim _n(132)$, then \[ (u,v)\;=\;2n-5+o(1). \] (Fireworks.) If $u,v sim F_n$, then \[ (u,v) \;=\; 2n-2n n- n +o\!(n n ). \] (Vexillary.) If $u,v sim _n$, then \[ (u,v) \;=\; 2n - O(n). \] (Covexillary.) If $u,v sim _n$, then there exists a constant $c_ cov in [6,9]$ such that \[ (u,v) \;=\; 2n - c_ cov - o(1). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Multiple detailed asymptotic claims across Catalan and broader permutation families (including delicate fireworks term); informative for combinatorics though largely class-local.
viability_rationale: Multiple precise asymptotics (some delicate log/constant terms) for various pattern classes; some parts look accessible, others (fireworks/log corrections) are more intricate, so mixed solvability.
rationale: The paper proposes several record-sparse asymptotic claims (multiple permutation classes) and does not prove these precise asymptotics; some related equidistribution results are proved but not these formulas.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Record-sparse regime): e.g. Av_n(231) and Av_n(132) have E[FS]=2n-5+o(1); fireworks, vexillary, covexillary asymptotics also conjectured.

title: The record statistic and forward stability of Schubert products
body_tex:
\label{conj:record-equivalence-recursive}
Fix $k\ge 2$ and let $\pi,\sigma\in S_k$. Then $\pi$ and $\sigma$ are record-equivalent if and only if all of the following hold:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textnormal{(\arabic*)}]
\item \textbf{Record-set agreement at rank $k$:}
\[
\Rec(\pi)=\Rec(\sigma).
\]

\item \textbf{Wilf-equivalence at rank $k$:}
\[
|\Av_n(\pi)|=|\Av_n(\sigma)|\qquad\text{for all }n\ge k.
\]

\item \textbf{Non-max deletion minors are record-equivalent:} if $m_\pi=m_\sigma$, then for every $i\in[k]$ with $i\neq m_\pi$,
\[
\pi^{(i)}\ \text{and}\ \sigma^{(i)}\ \text{are record-equivalent in }S_{k-1}.
\]

\item \textbf{Terminal step when the maximum sits at $k-1$:} if $k\ge 4$ and
\[
\Rec(\pi)=\Rec(\sigma)=[k-1]
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
m_\pi=m_\sigma=k-1,
\]
then
\[
\pi(k)=\sigma(k).
\]
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Fix $k >= 2$ and let $, in S_k$. Then $$ and $$ are record-equivalent if and only if all of the following hold: [label=()] Record-set agreement at rank $k$: \[ ()=(). \] Wilf-equivalence at rank $k$: \[ |_n()|=|_n()| all n >= k. \] Non-max deletion minors are record-equivalent: if $m_=m_$, then for every $i in [k]$ with $i != m_$, \[ ^ (i) \ and\ ^ (i) \ are record-equivalent in S_ k-1 . \] Terminal step when the maximum sits at $k-1$: if $k >= 4$ and \[ ()=()=[k-1] m_=m_=k-1, \] then \[ (k)=(k). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A proposed iff recursive classification for record-equivalence of patterns is structural and potentially widely useful in pattern-avoidance theory, making it a central subfield problem.
viability_rationale: A recursive classification supported by exhaustive computations up to k<=8; the conjecture is structural and seems amenable to inductive/constructive proof, making near-term resolution plausible.
rationale: Authors state this recursive criterion for record-equivalence as a conjecture, supported by computations up to k≤8, but do not prove the if-and-only-if criterion in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Recursive criterion): π,σ∈S_k are record-equivalent iff four listed conditions (record-set agreement, Wilf-equivalence, deletion-minor equivalences, terminal step) hold.

title: A Note on Generalized Erdős-Rogers Problems
plain_text: For fixed integers $4 <= k<s$, it holds that $r_k(s,n) >= twr_ k-1 ((n)).$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Erdős–Hajnal tower lower-bound conjecture for hypergraph Ramsey numbers is a classic, flagship problem with deep, wide-ranging implications across Ramsey theory.
viability_rationale: Erdős–Hajnal tower-type lower bound is a long-standing flagship conjecture with only partial range results; unlikely to be settled generally within five years absent a major breakthrough.
rationale: The Erdős–Hajnal tower-growth conjecture for r_k(s,n) is stated and discussed; partial cases are known but the full conjecture (for all 4≤k<s) remains open and is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Erdős and Hajnal): For fixed 4≤k<s, r_k(s,n)≥twr_{k-1}(Ω(n)). Paper notes partial verifications but does not settle it in full.

title: Quantum Query Complexity of the Hyperoctahedral Group
body_tex:
\label{conj:bridge-detail}
Let $V=V_{((N-1),(1))}$ be the natural representation of\/ $B_N$.
Then:
\begin{enumerate}
\item[\textup{(i)}] \textbf{\textup{[Proven]}}
  The cumulative spectral reach satisfies
  \[
    R_{\le t}(V)
    = \bigl\{(\alpha,\beta)\vdash N :
       d_{T(B_N,V)}\bigl((\alpha,\beta),\,I(V)\bigr)\le t-1\bigr\}.
  \]
\item[\textup{(ii)}] \textbf{\textup{[Proven]}}
  The tensor product graph $T(B_N,V)$ is bipartite with respect to
  $\varepsilon$-parity: irreps split into $\varepsilon$-even
  \textup{(}$|\beta|$ even\textup{)} and $\varepsilon$-odd
  \textup{(}$|\beta|$ odd\textup{)} classes, and tensoring with~$V$
  maps each class to the other.
\item[\textup{(iii)}] \textbf{\textup{[Proven for all $N\ge 2$]}}
  A bottleneck irrep is $V_{((1^N),\varnothing)}=\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)$
  \textup{(}unique for $N\ge 3$; one of four co-bottlenecks for
  $N=2$\textup{)}, and
  \[
    \gamma_{\mathrm{graph}} \;:=\;
    \ecc_{T(B_N,V)}\bigl(I(V)\bigr) \;=\; 2N-3.
  \]
\item[\textup{(iv)}] \textbf{\textup{[Conjectured]}}
  The adversary quantity is tight:
  \[
    \gamma_{\mathrm{adv}}
    \;=\; \gamma_{\mathrm{graph}}
    \;=\; 2N-3,
  \]
  which, combined with the proven formula
  $Q_{LV}(B_N)=\gamma_{\mathrm{graph}}+1=2(N{-}1)$, gives
  $Q_{LV}(B_N)=\gamma_{\mathrm{adv}}+1$.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Let $V=V_ ((N-1),(1)) $ be the natural representation of\/ $B_N$. Then: [(i)] [Proven] The cumulative spectral reach satisfies \[ R_ <= t (V) = (,) N : d_ T(B_N,V) ((,),\,I(V)) <= t-1 . \] [(ii)] [Proven] The tensor product graph $T(B_N,V)$ is bipartite with respect to $$-parity: irreps split into $$-even ($||$ even) and $$-odd ($||$ odd) classes, and tensoring with $V$ maps each class to the other. [(iii)] [Proven for all $N >= 2$] A bottleneck irrep is $V_ ((1^N),) =sgn()$ (unique for $N >= 3$; one of four co-bottlenecks for $N=2$), and \[ _ graph \;:=\; _ T(B_N,V) (I(V)) \;=\; 2N-3. \] [(iv)] [Conjectured] The adversary quantity is tight: \[ _ adv \;=\; _ graph \;=\; 2N-3, \] which, combined with the proven formula $Q_ LV (B_N)=_ graph +1=2(N - 1)$, gives $Q_ LV (B_N)=_ adv +1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A meaningful conjectured link between the adversary bound and tensor‑product graph eccentricity for B_N; technically neat and relevant to quantum query complexity but narrowly scoped to this group.
viability_rationale: Highly specific, narrow claim: graph eccentricity and exact Q_LV are already proved here, leaving a concrete adversary-vs-graph identity to verify. Technical but approachable with current adversary techniques within five years.
rationale: Part (iv) is explicitly labeled '[Conjectured]' in the paper and is not proved; other parts are proved but this exact adversary-eccentricity equality remains conjectural.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture text: item (iv) marked '[Conjectured]' asserting γ_adv = γ_graph = 2N-3 (adversary quantity tight).

title: FlowBoost Reveals Phase Transitions and Spectral Structure in Finite Free Information Inequalities
body_tex:
\label{conj:phase}
  For all $n \ge 3$, the $p$-Stam inequality~\eqref{eq:pstam} holds for all monic real-rooted pairs if and only if $p \in (1, 2]$. That is, the critical exponent is $p^* = 2$, independent of the degree $n$.
plain_text: For all $n >= 3$, the $p$-Stam inequality holds for all monic real-rooted pairs if and only if $p in (1, 2]$. That is, the critical exponent is $p^* = 2$, independent of the degree $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a universal critical exponent for p-Stam inequalities with implications for finite free CLT and spectral structure; significant within finite-free/information-inequality theory.
viability_rationale: Computational evidence and conditional reductions provided; central spectral claim is crisp but nontrivial. Moderate chance of resolution if spectral conjecture yields to analysis.
rationale: The p-Stam phase-transition (critical exponent p^*=2 independent of degree) is explicitly conjectured and supported numerically; the paper does not prove the if-and-only-if statement.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For all n\ge 3 the p-Stam inequality holds for all monic real-rooted pairs iff p\in(1,2], i.e. p^*=2.

title: A twosided linear estimate and a dyadic reduction of the UMD Conjecture
body_tex:
There exist constants $c,C>0$ such that for any Banach space $X$, there holds
\[
	c \sup_{\alpha}\| \mathcal{T}_{\alpha} \|_{L_X^p \mapsto L_X^p} \leqslant 
	 \| \mathcal{H}\|_{L_X^p \rightarrow L_X^p} \leqslant 
	C \sup_{\alpha}\| \mathcal{T}_{\alpha} \|_{L_X^p \mapsto L_X^p}.
\]
plain_text: There exist constants $c,C>0$ such that for any Banach space $X$, there holds \[ c _ \| T_ \|_ L_X^p maps to L_X^p \| H\|_ L_X^p L_X^p C _ \| T_ \|_ L_X^p maps to L_X^p . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A formulation of the UMD conjecture reducing Hilbert-transform bounds to dyadic operators; resolving it would settle a major open problem in harmonic analysis and Banach space theory.
viability_rationale: Paper reduces UMD conjecture to checking two simple dyadic operators, substantially narrowing the problem. This makes a near-term resolution plausible but still requires nontrivial Banach-space/operator-norm work, so uncertain.
rationale: This is the well-known UMD conjecture reduced in the paper; the authors reduce it to dyadic operators but do not resolve the conjecture itself.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (UMD Conjecture) states two-sided comparability of ||H|| and sup ||T_α|| for all Banach spaces X; paper gives a reduction but does not prove it.

    where $\mathbf{B}_{p-1}$ is the even Bernoulli number.

title: Sampleability transport, nonlinear regularization, and the porous medium flow
body_tex:
\label{conj:mesa}
Let $\mathcal U$ denote the class of compactly supported uniform measures
\[
\mathcal U=\left\{\vol(A)^{-1}\mathcal L^D|_A:\ A\subset\R^D\text{ compact},\ 0<\vol(A)<\infty\right\}.
\]
After replacing the density-ratio objective by a density-cap constraint, the $m\to\infty$ limit of the Wasserstein gradient flows of the R\'enyi entropies $\mathcal E_m$ should converge to a Hele-Shaw or mesa-type free-boundary evolution whose terminal state is the Wasserstein projection of $\mu$ onto $\mathcal U$.
The classical convergence results of Gil-Quir\'os~\cite{GilQuiros2001} and the free-boundary theory summarized in V\'azquez~\cite{Vazquez2007} provide the main evidence for this picture, but the full projection statement has not been proved in the present sampleability setting.
plain_text: Let $ U$ denote the class of compactly supported uniform measures \[ U= (A)^ -1 L^D|_A:\ A subset ^D compact,\ 0<(A)< . \] After replacing the density-ratio objective by a density-cap constraint, the $m to $ limit of the Wasserstein gradient flows of the R\'enyi entropies $ E_m$ should converge to a Hele-Shaw or mesa-type free-boundary evolution whose terminal state is the Wasserstein projection of $$ onto $ U$. The classical convergence results of Gil-Quir\'os and the free-boundary theory summarized in V\'azquez provide the main evidence for this picture, but the full projection statement has not been proved in the present sampleability setting.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts a deep variational limit (m→∞) linking porous-medium Wasserstein flows to a Hele–Shaw free-boundary and exact projection onto uniform measures — a significant bridge between PDE, optimal transport, and generative modeling.
viability_rationale: Mesa/Hele-Shaw projection requires deep free-boundary and variational analysis; classical evidence exists but the full projection statement is technically challenging and unlikely imminently.
rationale: Authors explicitly pose the mesa/Hele-Shaw projection convergence as a conjecture and state the full projection claim has not been proved in this setting.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: "... the m→∞ limit ... should converge to a Hele-Shaw or mesa-type ... whose terminal state is the Wasserstein projection ..." and: "the full projection statement has not been proved in the present sampleability setting."

title: CayleyPy-4: AI-Holography. Towards analogs of holographic string dualities for AI tasks
body_tex:
Let $D_k(n)$ be the diameter of $S_n$  Cayley graphs generated by consecutive cycles
                                                                     
of length $k$ and their inverses. They are given by quadratic quasi-polynomials in $n$ (for $n$ large enough) and the following holds:
\begin{enumerate}

    \item All the statements below  are valid for $ n \ge 2k. $ (The starting point for quasi-polynomials to be valid.)
                                                 

    \item The periods of  quasi-polynomials  are:
    \[
    \tau =
    \begin{cases}
    k - 1, & \text{if } k \text{ is even}, \\[4pt]
    2(k - 1), & \text{if } k \text{ is odd}.
    \end{cases}
    \]
    
                                                          
        
                             
        
        
    \item The general form of the diameter:
        
                                                           
        
    \[
    D_k(n) =  \frac{n(n-1)}{2(k-1)} + Q_0(n),
    \]    
    where $Q_0(n)$ is quasi-polynomial of degree zero, i.e. just the periodic function of $n$. 
    Moreover in many cases $Q_0(n)$ is just the correction to make the first term expression  to be the  integer 
                                   
    (floor/ceil), however it is not always the case.  

    
                                                          
    \item The last layer always contains either the full reversal
    $
    R = (n{-}1,\, n{-}2,\, \dots,\, 1,\, 0),
    $
    or its modified form with one adjacent inversion:
    $
    R' = R \circ (i,\, i{+}1).
    $
    
    \item The alternation between $R$ and $R'$ in the last layers has a period
    $
    T = k - 1.
    $ I.e.  the sequence $S(n) =$ $R$ or $R'$ whether the last layer contains $R$ or $R'$ will be periodic with the period $k-1$. 

                                                                               
    
    
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Let $D_k(n)$ be the diameter of $S_n$ Cayley graphs generated by consecutive cycles of length $k$ and their inverses. They are given by quadratic quasi-polynomials in $n$ (for $n$ large enough) and the following holds: All the statements below are valid for $ n >= 2k. $ (The starting point for quasi-polynomials to be valid.) The periods of quasi-polynomials are: \[ = cases k - 1, & if k is even, [4pt] 2(k - 1), & if k is odd. cases \] The general form of the diameter: \[ D_k(n) = n(n-1) 2(k-1) + Q_0(n), \] where $Q_0(n)$ is quasi-polynomial of degree zero, i.e. just the periodic function of $n$. Moreover in many cases $Q_0(n)$ is just the correction to make the first term expression to be the integer (floor/ceil), however it is not always the case. The last layer always contains either the full reversal $ R = (n - 1,\, n - 2,\, ,\, 1,\, 0), $ or its modified form with one adjacent inversion: $ R' = R (i,\, i + 1). $ The alternation between $R$ and $R'$ in the last layers has a period $ T = k - 1. $ I.e. the sequence $S(n) =$ $R$ or $R'$ whether the last layer contains $R$ or $R'$ will be periodic with the period $k-1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad structural conjecture that diameters D_k(n) are quadratic quasi-polynomials with specified periods and layer behavior; if true, it organizes diameter growth across all k.
viability_rationale: General structural conjecture (quadratic quasi-polynomials, periods, last-layer behavior). Supported by data and partial proofs for small k; proving all listed structural items for all k is plausible but moderately challenging.
rationale: A general conjecture that D_k(n) are quadratic quasi-polynomials with listed properties is proposed but not proven; paper provides empirical support only.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: 'D_k(n) = n(n-1)/(2(k-1)) + Q_0(n)' and period statements, valid for n >= 2k. (Conj lines 5011-5014, 4990-4999)

title: Obstructions to Smooth Full-Holonomy Cayley Fibrations

    A torsion-free $\m{Spin}(7)$-manifold with full holonomy does not admit a smooth Cayley fibration.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Conjectures no smooth Cayley fibrations on full-holonomy torsion-free Spin(7) manifolds; a central geometric/topological constraint in exceptional holonomy, supported by analogies and strong partial results.
viability_rationale: Paper reduces the Spin(7) nonexistence claim to a single 4‑manifold mapping-class conjecture and already excludes one case; plausible within five years if the boundary Dehn‑twist conjecture is resolved, but nontrivial.
rationale: Stated as an open conjecture in the Spin(7) paper; authors provide evidence and partial reductions but do not prove non-existence of smooth Cayley fibrations for full holonomy.
evidence_snippet: Lines 260-264: "\begin{con} A torsion-free \m{Spin}(7)-manifold with full holonomy does not admit a smooth Cayley fibration. \end{con}." Theorem reduces possibilities but does not prove the conjecture.
