title: The $G$-Noncommutative Minimal Model Program
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:1}
Let $\pi:X\to Y$ be a $G$-contraction of a smooth projective variety $X$. 
\begin{itemize}
    \item[(A)] One can associate to $\pi$ a canonical family of quasi-convergent paths $\{\sigma_t^{\pi,\psi}\}$ in $\Stab(X_G)/\mathbb C$. Generic values of the parameter $\psi$ yield a $G$-linear semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$, and different generic values give mutation-equivalent decompositions.
    \item[(B)] For a generic $\psi$, the semiorthogonal factors of $D^b_G(X)$ are closed under tensor product with complexes of the form $\pi^{\ast}(E)$ for $E\in\mathrm{Perf}_G(Y)$.
    \item[(C)] Given a further $G$-contraction $Y\to Y'$, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ associated with $X\to Y'$ refines the decomposition associated with $X\to Y$.
    \item[(D)] If $\pi:X\to X'$ is a $G$-equivariant morphism of smooth varieties with $R\pi_{\ast}(\cO_X)=\cO_{X'}$, then for suitable parameters, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ asscoiated to $X\to Y$ refines the decomposition obtained by combining the semiorthogonal decomposition of 
    \[\pi^{\ast}(D^b_G(X'))\cong D^b_G(X')\]
    associated to $X'\to Y$ with the decomposition 
    \[
    D^b_G(X)=\langle\mathrm{ker}(\pi_{\ast}),\pi^{\ast}(D^b_G(X'))\rangle.
    \]
\end{itemize}
plain_text: Let $:X to Y$ be a $G$-contraction of a smooth projective variety $X$. [(A)] One can associate to $$ a canonical family of quasi-convergent paths $ _t^ , $ in $(X_G)/ C$. Generic values of the parameter $$ yield a $G$-linear semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$, and different generic values give mutation-equivalent decompositions. [(B)] For a generic $$, the semiorthogonal factors of $D^b_G(X)$ are closed under tensor product with complexes of the form $^ (E)$ for $E in Perf_G(Y)$. [(C)] Given a further $G$-contraction $Y to Y'$, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ associated with $X to Y'$ refines the decomposition associated with $X to Y$. [(D)] If $:X to X'$ is a $G$-equivariant morphism of smooth varieties with $R_ (_X)=_ X' $, then for suitable parameters, the semiorthogonal decomposition of $D^b_G(X)$ asscoiated to $X to Y$ refines the decomposition obtained by combining the semiorthogonal decomposition of \[^ (D^b_G(X')) D^b_G(X')\] associated to $X' to Y$ with the decomposition \[ D^b_G(X)=(_ ),^ (D^b_G(X')). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad structural proposal linking equivariant Bridgeland stability, canonical quasi-convergent paths, and G-linear semiorthogonal decompositions; confirming it would have significant consequences across equivariant/noncommutative algebraic geometry.
viability_rationale: A broad equivariant extension of a deep noncommutative MMP/Bridgeland-stability program; only low-dimensional support so far and substantial conceptual obstacles remain in higher dimensions.
rationale: The paper formulates this G-equivariant NMMP proposal (items A–D) as a conjecture and provides partial evidence (e.g. support in two-dimensional case) but does not prove the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: Let π:X→Y be a G-contraction... (A)–(D) give canonical quasi-convergent paths and SOD/refinement properties. 'A recent work [ESS2025] provides strong support for this conjecture in the two-dimensional case.'

title: A very robust Ramsey theorem for matchings
body_tex:
\label{conj:hyper}
  Let $q,s\ge 1$ and $r\ge 2$ be integers.

  such that the following holds:
  For all $\vt\in\NN_+^q$ and $n\ge(r-1)\tmax+\Lambda_\vt+1+n\phi(s/n)$,
  if $H$ is an $r$-uniform $n$-vertex $s$-connector
  then $H\to \vt K_r^r$.

viability_rationale: Natural but ambitious hypergraph generalisation of known hard Ramsey/AF L theorem; no decisive local reductions given. Likely difficult and less probable to be settled within five years.

as $x \to \infty$, where $\Cco \geq 0$ is an explicit constant, defined in \eqref{E:Ccoprime}.

title: Towards Pósa's Conjecture for $3$-graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj}
For all $\alpha > 0$ there exists $n_0 \in \N$ such that every $3$-graph~$H$ on $n \ge n_0$ vertices with $\delta_2(H) \ge (3/4 + \alpha)n$ contains the square of a tight Hamilton cycle.
plain_text: For all $ > 0$ there exists $n_0 in $ such that every $3$-graph $H$ on $n >= n_0$ vertices with $_2(H) >= (3/4 + )n$ contains the square of a tight Hamilton cycle.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Determining the sharp 3/4 minimum codegree threshold for the square of a tight Hamilton cycle is a central, well‑motivated extremal hypergraph problem with significant subfield impact.
viability_rationale: Significant progress from 4/5 to 7/9; remaining gap to 3/4 is small but authors stress substantial new ideas needed, so moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Paper proves the 7/9 + o(n) bound but explicitly conjectures the (3/4 + o(n)) threshold; that optimal statement remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 101-103: main theorem gives 7/9 + α. Lines 111-113: conjecture states (3/4 + α)n suffices; posed as open.

title: Cyclic sieving for a class of rectangular domino tableaux
body_tex:
The set of rectangular domino tableaux $\DT(n^{2k})$ exhibits the cyclic sieving phenomenon with function
$$f(q)= 
\dfrac{[k \lceil n/2\rceil]!_q \hspace{1 mm} \cdot \hspace{1 mm} [k \lfloor n/2\rfloor]!_q}{ \displaystyle \prod_{i=1}^k [i]_q[i+1]_q\cdots \left[i+\left\lceil \frac{n}{2}\right\rceil -1\right]_q \cdot [i]_q[i+1]_q\cdots \left[i +\left\lfloor \frac{n}{2}\right\rfloor-1\right]_q} \cdot \displaystyle\qbin{kn}{k \lfloor n/2 \rfloor}_q.
$$.

interestingness_rationale: A natural CSP extension for rectangular domino tableaux; of clear interest in algebraic combinatorics and representation theory but limited in scope and broader mathematical consequences.
viability_rationale: Authors have exact enumerations and analogy to the proven rectangular SYT CSP; plausible but requires new structural/representation-theoretic understanding of domino promotion—nontrivial, so moderate near-term solvability.

viability_rationale: Classic strong monodromy problem; known for n=2 and special n=3 cases only. Wide open in general for n≥3; limited class-specific progress doesn’t make full conjecture likely within five years.

title: Remarks on the inverse Littlewood conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj-1}
Let $A\subset \bbz$ be a finite set of size $N$. If $\anorm{1_A}_1\leq K\log N$ then for some $m,r\ll_K 1$ the following holds. There are finite arithmetic progressions $P_1,\ldots,P_m\subset \mathbb{Z}$, each of length at most $N$, and finite sets $X_1,\ldots,X_r\subset \bbz$ such that $\big| \bigcup_{j}X_j\big|=o_K(N)$,
together with $\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_m,\eta_1,\ldots,\eta_r\in \{-1,1\}$ such that
\[1_A=\sum_{1\leq i\leq m}\epsilon_i 1_{P_i}+\sum_{1\leq j\leq r}\eta_j 1_{X_j}.\]
plain_text: Let $A subset $ be a finite set of size $N$. If $1_A_1 <= K N$ then for some $m,r_K 1$ the following holds. There are finite arithmetic progressions $P_1,,P_m subset Z$, each of length at most $N$, and finite sets $X_1,,X_r subset $ such that $| _ j X_j|=o_K(N)$, together with $_1,,_m,_1,,_r in -1,1 $ such that \[1_A=_ 1 <= i <= m _i 1_ P_i +_ 1 <= j <= r _j 1_ X_j .\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A strong inverse-structure statement implying A is essentially a bounded signed union of few progressions (up to o(N)). Central in additive combinatorics with finite-field analogues; resolving it would have substantial structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Finite-field analogues and partial structure exist, but the strong integer decomposition into bounded many arithmetic progressions with only o_K(N) error is a substantial strengthening and unlikely to be resolved very soon.
rationale: Authors pose Conjecture 1 as a new speculation about structure and explicitly state they know of no counterexample; the paper does not prove or refute it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 388–394: "We do not know of a counterexample to the following conjecture. \begin{conjecture}\label{conj-1} ... If \|1_A\|_1\le K\log N then ... 1_A=\sum_{i}\epsilon_i1_{P_i}+\sum_j\eta_j1_{X_j}.\end{conjecture}"

title: Remarks on the inverse Littlewood conjecture
body_tex: Let $K>0$ and $N$ be sufficiently large depending only on $K$. Let $A\subset \bbz$ be a finite set of size $N$. If $\anorm{1_A}_1\leq K\log N$ then $\dim A\ll_K (\log N)^2$. Furthermore, $A$ contains a subset of size $\gg_K N$ and dimension $\ll_K \log N$.
plain_text: Let $K>0$ and $N$ be sufficiently large depending only on $K$. Let $A subset $ be a finite set of size $N$. If $1_A_1 <= K N$ then $ A_K ( N)^2$. Furthermore, $A$ contains a subset of size $_K N$ and dimension $_K N$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Sharpens Pichorides' dim bound from O((log N)^3) to O((log N)^2); examples already show (log N)^2 is attainable, so this is a natural, meaningful specialist conjecture within inverse Fourier/additive theory.
viability_rationale: Sharpens the known (log N)^3 bound to (log N)^2 with concrete examples suggesting optimality; quantitative nature and recent techniques make moderate near-term progress plausible but nontrivial to complete.
rationale: The authors state this dimension-optimisation statement as a conjecture they 'conjecture is the best possible' and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 399–406: "We conjecture that this is the best possible. \begin{conjecture} Let K>0... If \|1_A\|_1\le K\log N then \dim A\ll_K (\log N)^2. Furthermore, A contains a subset of size \gg_K N and dimension \ll_K \log N. \end{conjecture}"

title: Generic flatness of the cohomology of thickenings
body_tex:
\label{our_conj}
Let $\ba\colonequals\left\{a_{ij} \mid 1\le i\le m,\, 0\le j \le n\right\}$ be indeterminates over a field $\kk$. Set $A\colonequals\kk[\ba]$, and let $R$ be the polynomial ring $A[x_0,\dots,x_n]$. Consider the ideal 
\[
I \colonequals \frakA_1 \cap \dots \cap \frakA_m
\]
of $R$, where $\frakA_i$ is the ideal of size $2$ minors
\[
\frakA_i \colonequals I_2 \begin{pmatrix}
x_0 & x_1 & \cdots & x_n\\
a_{i0} & a_{i1} & \cdots & a_{in}
\end{pmatrix}
\quad \text{ for } 1\le i\le m.
\] 
In other words, $I$ is the defining ideal of a \emph{generic} set of $m$ points in projective space. Set $\Rees(I)$ to be the Rees algebra
$\bigoplus_{t\ge 0} I^tT^t \subset R[T]$. Then there exists a nonzero element $a \in A$ such that 
\[
\HH^i_{(x_0,\dots,x_n)}\left(\Rees(I)\right)\otimes_A A_a
\]
is a free $A_a$-module for each $i\ge 0$.
plain_text: Let $ a_ ij 1 <= i <= m,\, 0 <= j <= n $ be indeterminates over a field $$. Set $A$, and let $R$ be the polynomial ring $A[x_0,,x_n]$. Consider the ideal \[ I _1 cap cap _m \] of $R$, where $_i$ is the ideal of size $2$ minors \[ _i I_2 pmatrix x_0 & x_1 & & x_n a_ i0 & a_ i1 & & a_ in pmatrix for 1 <= i <= m. \] In other words, $I$ is the defining ideal of a generic set of $m$ points in projective space. Set $(I)$ to be the Rees algebra $_ t >= 0 I^tT^t subset R[T]$. Then there exists a nonzero element $a in A$ such that \[ ^i_ (x_0,,x_n) ((I))_A A_a \] is a free $A_a$-module for each $i >= 0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Addresses generic constancy of symbolic-power degrees via Rees-algebra local cohomology; a structurally meaningful question in commutative algebra/algebraic geometry with clear consequences for general sets of points.
viability_rationale: Nontrivial partial result (proved for m≤n+2) and concrete algebraic framework give plausible progress, but the general case likely needs new ideas—moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture and only proves the statement for m ≤ n+2 (special case). The full general statement remains unresolved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 219–239: Conjecture states generic freeness of local cohomology of the Rees algebra; Lines 243–246: authors prove the conjecture only when m ≤ n+2.

title: Maximally probable tree topologies with $r$-furcation
plain_text: Consider the set of rooted $r$-furcating unlabeled topologies with $n$ leaves, permitting simultaneous $r$-furcations. [label = ()] The unlabeled topology whose labelings have the largest number of tie-permitting labeled histories takes the form in Theorem . This topology is unique in having the maximal value. Further, this same unlabeled topology has the largest number of tie-permitting labeled histories with exactly $z$ events, $ _r n <= z <= (n-1)/(r-1)$. This topology is not necessarily unique in having the maximal value.

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
body_tex:
\label{Transbpf}
Let $(X, B+\bbeta)$ be a generalized klt K\"ahler pair, where $X$ is a normal $\mathbb{Q}$-factorial compact K\"ahler variety, and $\alpha \in H_{\mathrm{BC}}^{1,1}(X,\mathbb{R})$ a nef class on $X$. If $\alpha-\left(K_X+B+ \bbeta_X\right)$ is nef and big, then there exists a proper surjective morphism with connected fibers $f: X \rightarrow Y$ to a normal compact K\"ahler variety $Y$ with rational singularities and a K\"ahler class $\omega_Y \in H_{\mathrm{BC}}^{1,1}(Y,\mathbb{R})$ such that $\alpha=f^* \omega_Y$.
plain_text: Let $(X, B+)$ be a generalized klt K\"ahler pair, where $X$ is a normal $Q$-factorial compact K\"ahler variety, and $ in H_ BC ^ 1,1 (X,R)$ a nef class on $X$. If $-(K_X+B+ _X)$ is nef and big, then there exists a proper surjective morphism with connected fibers $f: X Y$ to a normal compact K\"ahler variety $Y$ with rational singularities and a K\"ahler class $_Y in H_ BC ^ 1,1 (Y,R)$ such that $=f^* _Y$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Transcendental basepoint-free for generalized klt Kähler pairs; non-projective analogue of BCHM, central to the Kähler MMP and deformation/structural results in complex geometry.
viability_rationale: Transcendental BPF is proved for projective X and Kähler threefolds; higher-dimensional Kähler case remains open but is active — partial progress suggests modest chance within five years.
rationale: The paper states the transcendental base point free conjecture for Kähler varieties and only records it is known in the projective case; the full Kähler statement remains unresolved in general.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Transcendental base point free). If α−(K_X+B+β_X) is nef and big then α=f^*ω_Y. (Paper: 'holds when X is projective' by DH24MMP, but general Kähler case not proved.)

title: Lower bounds for multivariate independence polynomials and their generalisations

title: On the Graphical $r$-Stirling Numbers of the First Kind for Specific Graph Families
body_tex:
\label{conj:unimodal-planar}
For any planar graph $G$ of order $n$, the sequence of graphical Stirling numbers
\[
\{\st{G}{k}\}_{k=1}^n
\]
is \emph{unimodal}, i.e., there exists an index $k_0$ such that
\[
\st{G}{1} \le \cdots \le \st{G}{k_0} \ge \cdots \ge \st{G}{n}.
\]
This property is known for paths, cycles, trees, lollipop, and Tadpole graphs.
plain_text: For any planar graph $G$ of order $n$, the sequence of graphical Stirling numbers \[ G k _ k=1 ^n \] is unimodal, i.e., there exists an index $k_0$ such that \[ G 1 <= <= G k_0 >= >= G n . \] This property is known for paths, cycles, trees, lollipop, and Tadpole graphs.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Unimodality for all planar graphs is a natural, nontrivial enumerative-combinatorics conjecture over a large graph class; meaningful within combinatorics but not a cross-field flagship problem.
viability_rationale: Broad unimodality for all planar graphs is a wide generalization; known for special families but no clear finite reduction, so resolution within five years seems unlikely.
rationale: The unimodality for arbitrary planar graphs is posed as an open conjecture in the paper's 'Further Work and Open Problems' and is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1789–1796: '\begin{conjecture} For any planar graph $G$ ... the sequence of graphical Stirling numbers \{${G\brack k}$\} is unimodal ... \end{conjecture}.'

title: On the Graphical $r$-Stirling Numbers of the First Kind for Specific Graph Families
body_tex:
\label{conj:real-rooted}
If $G$ is a chordal graph, then the graphical cycle polynomial of the first kind
\[
\mathcal{C}(G, x) = \sum_{k=1}^n \st{G}{k} x^k
\]
has only real roots. Real-rootedness would imply log-concavity and unimodality of the sequence $\{\st{G}{k}\}$.
plain_text: If $G$ is a chordal graph, then the graphical cycle polynomial of the first kind \[ C(G, x) = _ k=1 ^n G k x^k \] has only real roots. Real-rootedness would imply log-concavity and unimodality of the sequence $ G k $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Real-rootedness for chordal graphs' cycle polynomials would imply log-concavity/unimodality and links algebraic and structural graph properties—a significant specialist conjecture with clear structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Real-rootedness for chordal graphs is plausible and aligns with known techniques (induction on elimination), but nontrivial; moderate chance of progress but not imminent certainty.
rationale: The paper proposes real-rootedness for chordal graphs' graphical cycle polynomials as a conjecture without proof; it is presented as an open problem.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1802–1808: '\begin{conjecture} If $G$ is a chordal graph, then the graphical cycle polynomial ... has only real roots. Real-rootedness would imply log-concavity and unimodality. \end{conjecture}.'

title: A relation between the HOMFLY-PT and Kauffman polynomials via characters
body_tex:
If a knot $\mathcal{K}$ with braid index at least $m$, with $ m\geq4$, satisfies the HOMFLY--PT/Kauffman relation, then the HZ transform of its HOMFLY--PT polynomial is factorisable, i.e.
     \be\label{conj4}
 { H (\mathcal{K}) = \widehat {KF}(\mathcal{K}) \implies  Z(\mathcal{K}) = \frac{\lambda \prod_{i=0}^{m-2}(1-\lambda q^{\alpha_i})}{\prod_{i=0}^m (1-\lambda q^{\beta_i})} }.
 \ee
plain_text: If a knot $K$ with braid index at least $m$, with $ m4$, satisfies the HOMFLY--PT/Kauffman relation, then the HZ transform of its HOMFLY--PT polynomial is factorisable, i.e. H (K) = KF (K) Z(K) = _ i=0 ^ m-2 (1- q^ _i ) _ i=0 ^m (1- q^ _i ) .
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates two major knot polynomials via BMW algebra characters and HZ-factorisability; nontrivial structural link in knot invariants with consequences in representation theory and topological string contexts, but primarily subfield-level impact.
viability_rationale: Authors give explicit BMW-character conditions, computational evidence, and families proved; but a full uniform proof over all knots is nontrivial. Moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper states the corrected conjecture explicitly (for m≥4) and presents counterexamples to a previous version but does not prove the corrected claim; it is posed as an open expectation.

title: Hasse-Witt invariants of Calabi-Yau varieties
body_tex:
\rm
\label{17112025ymsc}
For all primes $p\not| N$,
the Hasse-Witt invariant ${\rm HW}(X_z,\alpha_z)$ up to sign  is the truncation of the holomorphic period at degree $p-1$  and  the quantity
$$
A_p(z):={\rm HP}(X_z,\alpha_z)^{p-1}
{\rm HW}(X_z,\alpha_z)
$$
after inserting the mirror map $A_p(z(q))$ has $p$-integral coefficients and modulo $p$ it is a  constant independent of $z$, that is, $A_p(z(q))\equiv_p 1$ (up to sign).
plain_text: For all primes $p| N$, the Hasse-Witt invariant $ HW (X_z,_z)$ up to sign is the truncation of the holomorphic period at degree $p-1$ and the quantity $$ A_p(z):= HP (X_z,_z)^ p-1 HW (X_z,_z) $$ after inserting the mirror map $A_p(z(q))$ has $p$-integral coefficients and modulo $p$ it is a constant independent of $z$, that is, $A_p(z(q))_p 1$ (up to sign).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates Hasse–Witt invariants, truncated periods and p‑adic integrality after the mirror map; meaningful within arithmetic mirror symmetry and could have significant structural consequences in the subfield.
viability_rationale: Broad arithmetic/mirror-symmetry congruence; p-integrality of the mirror map is still unresolved except for hypergeometric cases. Partial cases likely progress, but full general resolution seems unlikely within five years.
rationale: Authors pose this Hasse–Witt vs period congruence as a conjecture and only provide numerical/experimental evidence; they explicitly note related p-integrality is an unsolved conjecture.

plain_text: For all $n >= 0$, we have align a_ 5,1 (n) >= a_ 5,3 (n) >= a_ 5,6 (n). align
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A numerically observed inequality among hook-counts in 5-core partitions; a narrow combinatorial pattern with limited broader implications beyond partition enumerative theory.
viability_rationale: Empirically supported combinatorial inequalities about hook counts; methods are elementary/combinatorial in nature, making a rigorous proof or counterexamples reasonably attainable soon.
rationale: Numerical exploration suggests this inequality for 5-core hook counts; it is presented as a conjecture and not proven in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Numerical exploration in Mathematica suggests the following conjecture. \begin{conjecture} For all n\ge0, a_{5,1}(n)\ge a_{5,3}(n)\ge a_{5,6}(n). \end{conjecture}

title: Infinite families of non-fibered twisted torus knots
body_tex: A twisted torus knot is fibered if and only if its Alexander polynomial is monic.
plain_text: A twisted torus knot is fibered if and only if its Alexander polynomial is monic.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects monicity of the Alexander polynomial to fiberedness for twisted torus knots; computational evidence is promising but pattern-based and counterexamples exist in broader knot classes.
viability_rationale: Concrete, restricted knot family with computational evidence and multiple analytic tools (Alexander/twisted Alexander, knot Floer homology). Progress seems plausible, but full iff classification may still be challenging.
rationale: The equivalence is proposed as a new conjecture in the Introduction; the paper provides evidence but does not prove the if-and-only-if statement.
evidence_snippet: "A twisted torus knot is fibered if and only if its Alexander polynomial is monic." (Conjecture in Introduction, lines 161-165)

title: Thresholds for colouring the random Borsuk graph
body_tex:
For every $d\geq 2$ and $3 \leq k \leq d+1$, the property of having chromatic number $>k$ 
has a sharp threshold.
plain_text: For every $d >= 2$ and $3 <= k <= d+1$, the property of having chromatic number $>k$ has a sharp threshold.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Sharp-threshold refinement for chromatic transitions in random Borsuk graphs; central to probabilistic topology/graph-colouring work and removes the paper's ‘almost all n’ caveat.
viability_rationale: Paper proves semi-sharpness and sharpness for k=2; closing 'almost all n' to all n seems a plausible technical refinement in random-graph/percolation methods within a few years.
rationale: Authors conjecture removal of the 'almost all n' qualifier; their theorem does not resolve the exact stated sharp-threshold claim.
evidence_snippet: Lines 3559-3566: 'Theorem showed ... for almost all n. Naturally we conjecture ... the property ... has a sharp threshold.'

title: Thresholds for colouring the random Borsuk graph
plain_text: For every $d >= 2$ there exists a constant $c=c(d)$ such that, for every fixed $>0$ and sequence $=(n)$: $$ _ n to ( (G(n,)) > d+1 ) = cases 1 & if $ > (1+) * c * ( n /n)^ 1/d $, 0 & if $ < (1-) * c * ( n /n)^ 1/d $. cases. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts precise ln(n)/n scaling for the (d+2)-colour transition, linking chromatic thresholds to percolation/coverage phenomena — important for random geometric/topological graph theory.
viability_rationale: A precise logarithmic-scale threshold for >d+1 colours aligns with existing work but requires delicate coverage/percolation analysis; achievable but technically challenging.
rationale: Detailed conjecture (Conjecture 3.2) is presented as an expectation about the d+2 threshold; paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 3578-3586: 'For every d>=2 there exists a constant c(d) ... lim_{n->\infty} P( chi > d+1 ) = 1 if alpha > (1+eps)c(ln n / n)^{1/d}, 0 if ...' (stated as conjecture).

title: Thresholds for colouring the random Borsuk graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:conjk}
For each $d\geq 2$, there exist constants $c_3=c_3(d),\dots,c_{d}=c_{d}(d)$ such that 

$$ c_2 < c_3 < \dots < c_{d}, $$ 

\noindent 
where $c_2$ is as provided by Theorem~\ref{bt1} and, for every fixed $\eps>0$
and $3 \leq k \leq d$, and every sequence $\alpha=\alpha(n)$:

$$ 
\lim_{n\to\infty}\Pee(  \chi(G(n,\alpha)) > k ) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{ if $\alpha > (1+\eps)\cdot c_k\cdot n^{-1/d}$, } \\
0 & \text{ if $\alpha < (1-\eps)\cdot c_k\cdot n^{-1/d}$. }
\end{cases}. $$
plain_text: For each $d >= 2$, there exist constants $c_3=c_3(d),,c_ d =c_ d (d)$ such that $$ c_2 < c_3 < < c_ d , $$ where $c_2$ is as provided by Theorem and, for every fixed $>0$ and $3 <= k <= d$, and every sequence $=(n)$: $$ _ n to ( (G(n,)) > k ) = cases 1 & if $ > (1+) * c_k * n^ -1/d $, 0 & if $ < (1-) * c_k * n^ -1/d $. cases. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Family of sharp thresholds c_k n^{-1/d} with strict ordering c_2<...<c_d; a natural, meaningful extension within the same framework but largely specialist and technical.
viability_rationale: Existence and strict ordering of constants c_k tied to 'higher-order percolation' lacks a clear characterization; substantial conceptual advances likely needed.
rationale: Authors state Conjecture 3.3 about constants c_k and thresholds for 3<=k<=d; no proof given in paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 3590-3604: 'For each d>=2, there exist constants c_3,...,c_d such that c_2 < c_3 < ... and lim_{n->\infty} P( chi > k ) = 1/0 depending on alpha ...' (conjecture).

title: Impact of local girth on the S-packing coloring of k-saturated subcubic graphs
body_tex: Every claw-free subcubic graph is $(1,1,2,3)$-packing colorable.
plain_text: Every claw-free subcubic graph is $(1,1,2,3)$-packing colorable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extension of proved girth-3 results; settles a concrete packing-coloring question for claw-free subcubic graphs. Valuable to graph theorists but limited impact outside packing-coloring specialists.
viability_rationale: Active subcubic packing-coloring research with partial sharp results in this paper increases plausibility, but remaining cases are unspecified and combinatorial obstacles likely remain; modest chance of resolution in 5 years.
rationale: The authors pose this as an open problem in the 'Sharpness and Open Problems' section and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1365–1367: "We also conjecture the following. Every claw-free subcubic graph is (1,1,2,3)-packing colorable."

title: Impact of local girth on the S-packing coloring of k-saturated subcubic graphs
body_tex: Every claw-free subcubic graph is $(2,2,2,2,2,2,2)$-packing colorable.
plain_text: Every claw-free subcubic graph is $(2,2,2,2,2,2,2)$-packing colorable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A finite-distance-2 color bound for claw-free subcubic graphs; a modest, primarily combinatorial bound-building conjecture with limited broader mathematical consequences.
viability_rationale: Active research area with recent partial progress on claw-free subcubic packings, but the conjecture (equivalently chi(G^2)≤7) is a stronger, nontrivial global claim without a clear finite reduction. Moderate but not high near-term solvability.
rationale: Stated explicitly among the open problems and not resolved in the paper; only partial/related results are proved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1368–1370: "Every claw-free subcubic graph is (2,2,2,2,2,2,2)-packing colorable."

title: Stein degree of proper morphisms
body_tex:
Let \((X,B)\to Y\) be a log Calabi--Yau fibration of dimension \(d\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(0\), and let \(S\subset B\) be a horizontal component with
\[
\mu_S B\ge t>0.
\]
Then
\[
\sdeg(S/Y)
\]
is bounded in terms of \(d\) and \(t\).
plain_text: Let \((X,B) to Y\) be a log Calabi--Yau fibration of dimension \(d\) over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(0\), and let \(S subset B\) be a horizontal component with \[ _S B >= t>0. \] Then \[ (S/Y) \] is bounded in terms of \(d\) and \(t\).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A boundedness statement linking Stein degree, log Calabi–Yau fibrations, and singularity theory; proving it would control arithmetic complexity and influence birational/moduli theory.
viability_rationale: Birkar's Stein-degree boundedness has recent progress (Birkar--Qu result cited); boundedness problems in birational geometry are active and often reachable with current techniques, making a resolution plausible within 5 years.
rationale: Birkar's boundedness conjecture for Stein degree is stated as a conjecture in this exposition; the paper notes partial results but does not claim a complete resolution.
evidence_snippet: "Then sdeg(S/Y) is bounded in terms of d and t." presented as a conjecture (lines 589-599) and followed by remarks and recent partial progress (lines 615-619).

title: On the paucity of lattice triangles
body_tex:
The known list of obtuse rational lattice triangles is complete.
Equivalently, every obtuse rational lattice triangle is either a member of one
of the two known infinite families or is Hooper's triangle.
plain_text: The known list of obtuse rational lattice triangles is complete. Equivalently, every obtuse rational lattice triangle is either a member of one of the two known infinite families or is Hooper's triangle.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A concrete classification problem in Teichmüller dynamics/translation surfaces; completing the obtuse lattice-triangle list is important in the niche but has limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Significant progress: the paper rules out a density-1 subset in the hard obtuse window using effective arithmetic/MW rank obstructions and formalization; remaining sporadic low-genus cases look finite and verifiable soon.
rationale: The completeness of the known list of obtuse rational lattice triangles is posed as an open conjecture; the paper proves strong partial (density-zero) results but not the full classification.
evidence_snippet: "The known list of obtuse rational lattice triangles is complete..." (conjecture stated lines 225-229) and paper rules out all but a density 0 subset in the hard window (abstract).

title: Linear spaces in Hessian loci of cubic hypersurfaces
body_tex:
\label{CONJ:CO}
    The hessian map $h_{d,n}$ is birational onto its image for every $d\geq3$ and for every $n\geq2$, except for the case where $(d,n)=(3,2)$.
plain_text: The hessian map $h_ d,n $ is birational onto its image for every $d3$ and for every $n2$, except for the case where $(d,n)=(3,2)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Ciliberto–Ottaviani conjecture on birationality of the Hessian map is a central, longstanding problem in projective algebraic geometry with notable consequences for reconstruction and moduli questions.
viability_rationale: Generically finite and proved in key cases (cubic surfaces, Waring-rank loci); recent progress on cubic threefolds gives evidence, but full uniform birationality across all d,n is broad and likely not resolved imminently.
rationale: This is the Ciliberto–Ottaviani conjecture stated here; the paper proves special cases (evidence for cubic threefolds) but does not claim the full conjecture is resolved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Ciliberto-Ottaviani): The hessian map h_{d,n} is birational onto its image for every d≥3, n≥2, except (d,n)=(3,2). Authors note prior partial results and give evidence in special cubic cases but do not prove the full conjecture.

title: Support of Continuous Smeary Measures on Spheres
body_tex:
\label{conj:FSS-conjecture}
Let $X$ be a random variable supported on a set $A\subset \mathbb{S}^m$ whose convex closure has nonzero volume and which has a unique Fr\'echet mean $\mu$. Then $\mu$ is Type\,I finite sample smeary.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a random variable supported on a set $A subset S^m$ whose convex closure has nonzero volume and which has a unique Fr\'echet mean $$. Then $$ is Type\,I finite sample smeary.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Finite-sample smeariness conjecture for Fréchet means on spheres. Relevant to statistics on manifolds and inference, a meaningful specialist problem within geometric statistics.
viability_rationale: Geometric-statistics conjecture close to proven special cases (hemispherical/support thresholds); extension to full generality seems approachable but not immediate.
rationale: The Type I finite-sample smeariness conjecture is stated (citing earlier work) and the paper proves only special/hemispherical cases, not the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 3210-3214: present Conjecture 7; subsequent theorem (3219+) proves a special hemispherical-support case, not the general statement.

title: CayleyPy-4: AI-Holography. Towards analogs of holographic string dualities for AI tasks
body_tex:
For $D \ge 2$, the conjecture was found to be:
\[
D(n,d)=\frac{d-1}{2}\,n-\delta(n,d),
\]
where the offset term $\delta(n,d)$ is given by
\[
\delta(n,d)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{d(d-2)}{4}, & \text{if $d$ is even and $n$ is even},\\[8pt]
\dfrac{d(d-2)}{4}+\dfrac12, & \text{if $d$ is even and $n$ is odd},\\[8pt]
\left(\dfrac{d-1}{2}\right)^2, & \text{if $d$ is odd}.
\end{cases}
\]
plain_text: For $D >= 2$, the conjecture was found to be: \[ D(n,d)=d-1 2 \,n-(n,d), \] where the offset term $(n,d)$ is given by \[ (n,d)= cases d(d-2) 4 , & if $d$ is even and $n$ is even, [8pt] d(d-2) 4 +12, & if $d$ is even and $n$ is odd, [8pt] (d-1 2 )^2, & if $d$ is odd. cases \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An explicit linear-in-n diameter formula with parity-dependent offset for a specific family of Schreier graphs: useful and testable but narrowly focused combinatorial statement.
viability_rationale: Concrete combinatorial diameter formula with extensive experimental data and parity cases. Methods from combinatorics/graph theory likely to close remaining gaps for fixed d, making a proof plausible within 5 years.
rationale: Authors present the explicit D(n,d) formula as a conjecture supported by experimental data but do not prove it; remains posed as open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: 'For D >= 2, D(n,d)=((d-1)/2)n - δ(n,d)' with casewise δ(n,d). (Conj lines 4543-4555)

interestingness_rationale: Concrete quasi-polynomial/periodic formulas for Cayley graph diameters across small k give substantive structural insight into diameters of permutation-generator graphs, relevant in algebraic combinatorics.
viability_rationale: Many explicit quasi-polynomial formulas for small k with BFS verification and OGF structure. Extending proofs to each fixed k seems tractable; full generality for all k less certain but achievable incrementally.
rationale: The quasi-polynomial formulas for D_k(n) for k=4,5,6,7 are listed as conjectural with checks for small n, not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: explicit formulas for D_4(n), D_5(n), D_6(n), D_7(n) and OGFs presented as 'Conj'. (Section lines 4838-4848 and 4868-4912)

title: CayleyPy-4: AI-Holography. Towards analogs of holographic string dualities for AI tasks
body_tex:
Diameters of the Schreier coset graphs $S_n / ( S_{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \times S_{n-\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} )$
with consecutive $k$-cycles, not inverse closed case are given by :
\[
\begin{aligned}
n &\equiv 0 \!\pmod{(2k-2)} \;:& \!\!\!
D_k(n) &= \frac{n^2 + 4(k-1)}{4(k-1)}, \\[6pt]
n &\equiv -1 \!\pmod{(2k-2)} \;:& \!\!\!
D_k(n) &=  \frac{n^2 + 4(k-1)-1}{4(k-1)},
\end{aligned}
\]
and else, denoting $p = n \pmod{(2k-2)} $,
\[
\begin{aligned}
 D_k(n)= &  \frac{n(n+2k-p-2)}{4(k-1)}, & p  {\rm \ is \ even \ }
\\[6pt]
 D_k(n)= &   \frac{(n-1)(n+2k-p-2)}{4(k-1)}, & p  {\rm \ is \ odd \ }.
\end{aligned}
\]
plain_text: Diameters of the Schreier coset graphs $S_n / ( S_ n/2 x S_ n- n/2 )$ with consecutive $k$-cycles, not inverse closed case are given by : \[ aligned n & 0 \!(2k-2) \;:& \!\!\! D_k(n) &= n^2 + 4(k-1) 4(k-1) , [6pt] n & -1 \!(2k-2) \;:& \!\!\! D_k(n) &= n^2 + 4(k-1)-1 4(k-1) , aligned \] and else, denoting $p = n (2k-2) $, \[ aligned D_k(n)= & n(n+2k-p-2) 4(k-1) , & p \ is \ even \ [6pt] D_k(n)= & (n-1)(n+2k-p-2) 4(k-1) , & p \ is \ odd \ . aligned \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gives explicit modular (p-period) closed formulas for diameters of a natural family of Schreier coset graphs; concrete and potentially widely applicable within combinatorial group theory.
viability_rationale: Explicit modular formulas for Schreier coset diameters with concrete checks for many k. Reasonable chance to be proved for wide ranges, though full uniform bounds and exact start thresholds may delay complete resolution.
rationale: Authors give an explicit formula for Schreier coset graph diameters as a conjecture (expected valid for large n) without proof.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: closed-form piecewise formulas for D_k(n) of S_n/(S_{floor n/2} x S_{...}) given; expected valid for large n. (Conj lines 5099-5116)

title: Tree-independence number and forbidden induced subgraphs: excluding a $6$-vertex path and a $(2,t)$-biclique
body_tex:
\label{conj:PrKtt}
    For any two positive integers~$r$ and~$t$, the class of~${\{P_r, K_{t,t}\}}$-free graphs has bounded tree-independence number.
plain_text: For any two positive integers $r$ and $t$, the class of $ P_r, K_ t,t $-free graphs has bounded tree-independence number.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad structural conjecture linking forbidden induced subgraphs to bounded tree‑independence number with algorithmic consequences; central in structural/algorithmic graph theory and widely impactful if resolved.
viability_rationale: Very general r,t statement in structural graph theory; only partial progress so far and broad scope make full resolution within five years unlikely.
rationale: The conjecture is the general statement for all r,t; the paper proves only partial cases (r ≤ 6), leaving the full general conjecture unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 107–110 and 121–123: Conjecture stated for all r,t; paper: "we confirm the validity of this conjecture for r ≤ 6 and arbitrary t" and leave larger r open.

title: Tree-independence number and forbidden induced subgraphs: excluding a $6$-vertex path and a $(2,t)$-biclique
body_tex:
\label{conj:PrK2t}
    For any two positive integers~$r$ and~$t$, the class of ${\{P_r, K_{2,t}\}}$-free graphs has bounded tree-independence number.
plain_text: For any two positive integers $r$ and $t$, the class of $ P_r, K_ 2,t $-free graphs has bounded tree-independence number.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A focused instance (excluding K_{2,t}) of the general P_r,K_{t,t} conjecture; important as a stepping stone and already provably hard for larger r, but narrower than the full statement.
viability_rationale: Restricted K_{2,t} variant already settled for r≤6 here; remaining cases r≥7 form an infinite family—progress plausible but nontrivial to finish in five years.
rationale: Authors pose this general {P_r,K_{2,t}} conjecture and prove it only for r ≤ 6; cases r ≥ 7 remain open, so the exact general statement is unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 116–119 and 121–126: Conjecture stated; paper proves "For every integer t ≥ 2 ... every {P_6,K_{2,t}}-free graph ..." and notes remaining open cases r ≥ 7.

title: The relativistic $p$-adic sunscreen conjecture
body_tex:
Let $F(x,y) \in \mathbb{C}_p[[x,y]]$ be a power series with constant term $0$ and non-vanishing gradient (i.e. $F(x,y)=0+ ax + by + \ldots$ with $(a,b)\neq (0,0)$). Then, in any disk $D=\{(x,y) \in \mathbb{C}_p^2, |x| \leq \delta, |y| \leq \delta)$ where $F$ converges, the set
$\{ (x,y) \in \BC(1/2) \cap D\, |\, F(x,y)=0 \}$
is a profinite set of cardinality $2^\mathbb{N}$.
plain_text: Let $F(x,y) in C_p[[x,y]]$ be a power series with constant term $0$ and non-vanishing gradient (i.e. $F(x,y)=0+ ax + by + $ with $(a,b) != (0,0)$). Then, in any disk $D= (x,y) in C_p^2, |x| <= , |y| <= )$ where $F$ converges, the set $ (x,y) in (1/2) cap D\, |\, F(x,y)=0 $ is a profinite set of cardinality $2^N$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Deep p-adic Hodge-theoretic conjecture about transverse intersections of BC(1/2) with analytic curves; significant within Banach–Colmez/Fargues–Fontaine theory and likely to have structural consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Deep p-adic Hodge/Banach–Colmez intersection problem; author notes even simple nonlinear cases are open. Highly technical modern area—low near-term solvability probability.
rationale: The 'relativistic p-adic sunscreen' statement is presented as a conjecture; the author notes it is open for non-linear F and offers examples/bounty.
evidence_snippet: Lines 201–206 and 211: '\begin{conjecture*}... the set { (x,y) in BC(1/2) ∩ D | F(x,y)=0 } is a profinite set of cardinality 2^N.\end{conjecture*}' and 'To our knowledge, this conjecture is open for any non-linear convergent F.'

title: Randomstrasse101: Open Problems of 2025
plain_text: Let \(G\) be distributed as a random dense circulant graph. Then, equation E (G) = (1 + o(1))n. equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Analogous asymptotic for random dense circulant graphs links structured pseudorandom models to Erdős–Rényi behaviour; interesting but narrower than the fully random case.
viability_rationale: Authors already have partial bounds (upper O(sqrt{n log log n})); numerical evidence and structure of circulants make full asymptotics reasonably attainable.
rationale: Paper poses analogous conjecture for random dense circulant graphs; authors note only partial results and no resolution.
evidence_snippet: Lines 461–469: 'We conjecture... Let G be distributed as a random dense circulant graph. Then, E θ(G) = (1+o(1)) sqrt(n).' Line 471 notes a partial result only.

title: Randomstrasse101: Open Problems of 2025
body_tex: The Paley Equiangular Tight Frame construction satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property beyond the square-root bottleneck. In other words, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for $m \sim p^{1/2+\varepsilon}$, any matrix formed by picking $m$ (distinct) vectors in the Paley ETF has condition number bounded by a constant independent of $p$.
plain_text: The Paley Equiangular Tight Frame construction satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property beyond the square-root bottleneck. In other words, there exists $>0$ such that, for $m sim p^ 1/2+ $, any matrix formed by picking $m$ (distinct) vectors in the Paley ETF has condition number bounded by a constant independent of $p$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Paley ETF satisfying RIP beyond the square-root barrier would break a major deterministic bottleneck in compressed sensing, linking number theory, frame constructions, and sparse recovery.
viability_rationale: Paley ETF overcoming the square‑root RIP bottleneck is a deep deterministic RIP/number‑theory problem; substantial barriers remain, making near‑term resolution unlikely.
rationale: Paley ETF satisfying RIP beyond square-root bottleneck is posed as a conjecture linked to Paley constructions; no proof given and remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 624–628: describes Paley ETF construction and conjectures it satisfies RIP beyond square-root bottleneck (existence of ε>0 for m∼p^{1/2+ε}).

title: Irreducible Characteristic Cycles for Orbit Closures of a Symmetric Subgroup
body_tex:
\label{conj:positive}
Let $Y$ be the closure of a $K$-orbit in $X$.  When
\(c_M^T(Y) \in H^T_*(X) \) is expressed in terms of the Schubert basis $[Y_w]_T$,
each coefficient is a sum of monomials in the positive simple roots of $G$, restricted to
$T$, with nonnegative integer coefficients.

interestingness_rationale: Extends equivariant positivity to Chern–Mather classes of K-orbit closures; meaningful in Schubert calculus and representation theory but focused on a special family of orbit closures.

rationale: Paper states the conjecture and only verifies it in an example; no general proof or disproof is given.

title: Simplicial shells and thickness in the partition graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:rear-central-nucleation}
For all sufficiently large $n$, the first genuinely higher simplicial behaviour of the
partition graph is nucleated in the rear-central part of $G_n$.
Equivalently, the earliest tetrahedral and higher-order thick zones are asymptotically
separated from the front extremes and are organized around the central rear body of the
graph rather than around its antennas.
plain_text: For all sufficiently large $n$, the first genuinely higher simplicial behaviour of the partition graph is nucleated in the rear-central part of $G_n$. Equivalently, the earliest tetrahedral and higher-order thick zones are asymptotically separated from the front extremes and are organized around the central rear body of the graph rather than around its antennas.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Meaningful structural conjecture about where higher simplicial thickening appears in the partition graph; computationally supported and geometrically descriptive but largely confined to this combinatorial-topological setting.
viability_rationale: Specific combinatorial-geometric claim with computational evidence (n≤30) and structural partial results; tractable by further asymptotic analysis/computation, but nontrivial geometry keeps certainty moderate.
rationale: The paper states this as a conjectural strengthening supported by computations but does not prove it; authors explicitly mark it conjectural.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1186-1194: "The computational evidence suggests a stronger statement, which we formulate separately... For all sufficiently large n, the first genuinely higher simplicial behaviour..."

title: Size conditions for admissible or consecutive even cycles in graphs
body_tex:
\label{c1}
	If $G$ is an $n$-vertex graph not containing $k$ cycles of consecutive even lengths, 
	then $e(G)\leq (2k+1)(n-1)/2$, with equality only if every block of $G$ 
	is a clique of order $2k+1$.
plain_text: If $G$ is an $n$-vertex graph not containing $k$ cycles of consecutive even lengths, then $e(G) <= (2k+1)(n-1)/2$, with equality only if every block of $G$ is a clique of order $2k+1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A well-known extremal-graph conjecture restricting edge counts when k consecutive even cycles are forbidden; central to extremal graph theory with clear structural and extremal-characterization consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Extremal graph conjecture with partial range proofs and incremental improvements; active field so solvable progress likely, but full general statement remains challenging.
rationale: The Verstraëte conjecture is stated and partially resolved in this paper for a specific n-range; the full conjecture remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 133-141 and 141-142: "Verstraëte posed the following conjecture... The remaining cases are still open. As a byproduct... we verify Conjecture \\ref{c1} for 2k+2 <= n <= 4k+1."

title: On canonicity for integral models of Shimura varieties with hyperspecial level
body_tex:
[Tate conjecture in characteristic $p$]
\label{conj:tate_char_p}
Suppose that $\kappa$ is a perfect field over $k(v)$ and fix a point $x\in \Ss_K(\kappa)$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item There exists a reductive group $I_x$ over $\Rat$ with realization maps $\alpha_\ell\colon I_x\otimes_{\Rat}\Rat_\ell\to I_{\ell,x}$ for all $\ell$ such that 
    \[
    I_x(\Int_{(p)})\defn I_x(\Rat)\cap I_{\Int_p,x}(\Int_p) =\Aut^p(x),
    \]
    and such that, for all $\ell$, the map $I_x(\Int_{(p)})\to I_{\ell,x}(\Rat_\ell)$ is the realization map from \emph{Remark~\ref{rem:realizations_quasi-isogenies}}.
    \item If $\kappa$ is finite, then, for all $\ell$, the realization map $I_x\otimes\Rat_\ell\to I_{\ell,x}$ is an isomorphism.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: [Tate conjecture in characteristic $p$] Suppose that $$ is a perfect field over $k(v)$ and fix a point $x in _K()$. Then: There exists a reductive group $I_x$ over $$ with realization maps $_ ell I_x_ _ ell to I_ ell ,x $ for all $ ell $ such that \[ I_x(_ (p) ) I_x() cap I_ _p,x (_p) =^p(x), \] and such that, for all $ ell $, the map $I_x(_ (p) ) to I_ ell ,x (_ ell )$ is the realization map from Remark . If $$ is finite, then, for all $ ell $, the realization map $I_x_ ell to I_ ell ,x $ is an isomorphism.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Characteristic-p variant asserting existence and l-adic compatibility of reductive I_x; closely tied to Kisin/Faltings results and central to integral models and p-adic arithmetic geometry.
viability_rationale: Tate-type assertion in characteristic p is deep; deducible in several important cases but full generality remains difficult, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: The characteristic-p Tate statement is given as a conjecture; authors note deductions in special (Kisin/pre-abelian) cases and future work, but do not prove the full conjecture here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture [Tate conjecture in characteristic p]: existence of reductive I_x with realization maps and isomorphism for finite kappa. Remarks say it can be deduced in pre-abelian type or from Kisin, but remains conjectural.

title: The Galois Alperin weight conjecture for finite category algebras

{H24}}]\label{conj:BGAWC for finite groups}
For any finite group $G$, and any central idempotent $b$ of $kG$, there exists a bijection $\S(kGb)\to \S(W(k\O_G,b))$ commuting with the action of $\Gamma_b$.
plain_text: H24 ] For any finite group $G$, and any central idempotent $b$ of $kG$, there exists a bijection $(kGb) to (W(k_G,b))$ commuting with the action of $_b$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The blockwise Galois Alperin weight conjecture is a strong, influential refinement in modular representation theory with important structural implications for blockwise local-global relationships.
viability_rationale: This is a blockwise Galois variant of the long‑standing Alperin weight conjecture. Despite active reductions and some case verifications (e.g. symmetric groups), no general closure is evident, so full resolution in 5 years is unlikely.
rationale: The blockwise GAWC for finite groups is stated (Navarro) and cited; the paper does not establish it in full generality, only relates it to the category version.

title: The Galois Alperin weight conjecture for finite category algebras

	For any finite EI-category $\C$ and any central idempotent $b$ of $k\C$, there exists a bijection $\S(k\C b)\to \S(W(k\O_\C,b))$ commuting with the action of $\Gamma_b$.

interestingness_rationale: Blockwise categorical analogue for EI-categories is a useful generalization but its impact is tempered by equivalence to the group/block versions and its more specialized scope.
viability_rationale: Equivalent to the blockwise GAWC for finite groups; this is a deep, long-standing open problem in modular representation theory with only partial case results (e.g., symmetric groups). No concrete path to a general proof is given.
rationale: The blockwise category-version is posed and proven equivalent to the group blockwise conjecture, but the paper does not prove the conjecture itself.

title: Bicompact torsion classes and conjectures on brick infinite algebras

interestingness_rationale: A natural structural statement in representation theory: characterizes brick-infinite algebras via infinite semibricks. Important inside the subfield (classification of bricks/semibricks), nontrivial but narrowly scoped.
viability_rationale: Active representation-theory area with concrete partial results (hereditary, semistable) and reductions; still a nontrivial general case but plausible progress within five years.

evidence_snippet: Lines 1168-1174: 'Enomoto proposed that the converse also holds.' Conjecture: 'Let A be brick infinite. Then there exists an infinite semibrick in mod A.'

title: Diameter bounds for arbitrary finite groups and applications
body_tex:
\label{conj:folk}
        If $G \le S_n$ is transitive then $\diam(G) \ll n^{O(1)}$.
plain_text: If $G <= S_n$ is transitive then $(G) n^ O(1) $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A longstanding folklore diameter bound for transitive permutation groups; central to permutation-group expansion and algorithmic group theory with broad consequences if resolved.
viability_rationale: Longstanding folklore conjecture; significant quasi-polynomial progress exists (Helfgott–Seress). Still needs new uniform ingredients or Babai; plausible but not likely resolved within 5 years.
rationale: The paper states the folklore transitive-permutation-group polynomial-diameter conjecture and does not prove it; best known bounds remain far from the conjectured polynomial bound.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If G ≤ S_n is transitive then diam(G) ≪ n^{O(1)} (lines 170–173). Helfgott–Seress bound diam(G) ≤ n^{O((log n)^3 log log n)}; conjecture remains open (lines 185–189,190).

title: Diameter bounds for arbitrary finite groups and applications

interestingness_rationale: Grigorchuk's gap conjecture is a major structural question in geometric group theory distinguishing intermediate from polynomial growth; resolving it would be a landmark result.
viability_rationale: Decades-old major open problem; only partial reductions and conditional results (residually finite case relies on Babai) exist, with reductions to simple groups still unresolved and no clear imminent route to full proof.
rationale: Grigorchuk's gap conjecture is stated in full generality; the paper gives reductions and proves special/residually-finite cases but does not resolve the full conjecture.

title: Growth in noncommutative algebras and entropy in derived categories
body_tex:
\label{conj: exp}
\label{conj: pol}
Suppose that the category $\mathbf{D}^b (\mathsf{qgr} A)$ for a graded coherent algebra $A$ admits a classical generator. 

(a) The entropy of the Serre twist functor on this category satisfies the inequality
$$
\mathsf{h}_t (S) \le  \entropy (A) . 
$$

(b) If the algebra $A$ has subexponential growth, then the polynomial entropy of this functor satisfies the inequality 
$$
\hpol_t (S) \le   \gkd A  -1. 
$$
plain_text: Suppose that the category $D^b (qgr A)$ for a graded coherent algebra $A$ admits a classical generator. (a) The entropy of the Serre twist functor on this category satisfies the inequality $$ h_t (S) <= (A) . $$ (b) If the algebra $A$ has subexponential growth, then the polynomial entropy of this functor satisfies the inequality $$ _t (S) <= A -1. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a conceptual bridge between categorical/polynomial entropy of the Serre twist and algebraic growth (entropy, GK‑dimension), with broad implications in noncommutative geometry and derived-category dynamics.
viability_rationale: Significant partial results (finite global dimension, regular algebras, coordinate rings) exist. Problem is specialized but active; general case likely needs new categorical/growth techniques, plausible but not highly likely in five years.
rationale: The paper proves the entropy inequalities in several important cases (finite global dimension, regular algebras) but does not establish the general conjectured inequalities for all graded coherent algebras.
evidence_snippet: Abstract: 'For algebras of finite global dimension, the entropies are bounded above by the growth entropy ... However, the polynomial entropy is zero for monomial algebras of polynomial growth, so inequality can be strict.'

title: Coarse Balanced Separators in Fat-Minor-Free Graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj:fatminor-sep}
For every graph $H$ and $d\in \N$, there exist constants $c,r\in \N$ such that every $n$-vertex weighted graph $G$ that excludes $H$ as a~$d$-fat minor has a~$(c\sqrt{n},r)$-coverable balanced separator.
plain_text: For every graph $H$ and $d in $, there exist constants $c,r in $ such that every $n$-vertex weighted graph $G$ that excludes $H$ as a $d$-fat minor has a $(cn,r)$-coverable balanced separator.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Sharp coarse-separator statement generalizing the Robertson–Seymour sqrt(n) separator to fat minors; central in structural graph theory with significant structural and algorithmic consequences.
viability_rationale: Authors already prove a cn^{1/2+ε} coverable separator and give an effective algorithm; the remaining ε appears a technical gap likely addressable but nontrivial, so plausible within five years.
rationale: The paper proves a coarse separator with c n^{1/2+ε} balls for every ε>0, and explicitly states the stronger c√n (no ε) version as a conjecture; it is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Abstract: we prove existence of separators coverable by c n^{1/2+ε} balls. Conjecture: there exist c,r such that every graph excluding H as a d-fat minor has a (c√n,r)-coverable balanced separator.

title: CayleyPy-4: AI-Holography. Towards analogs of holographic string dualities for AI tasks
body_tex:
(Not inverse closed case).
For consecutive $k$-cycle generators not inverse closed, the diameters of the 
Schreier coset graph: $S_n / \bigl(S_{l} \times S_{n-l}\bigr)$ (``$k$-shrunken''  Grassmannian $Gr(l,n,k)$)
are given by quasi-polynomials in two variables $n$ and $t = n-(l+1)$.

For $k=3$ with  period~$2$:
\[
D_l(n) = l\!\left(\left\lfloor \frac{t}{2} \right\rfloor + 1\right) + (t \bmod 2),
\qquad
t = n - (l+1), \quad n \ge l+1.
\]
Under the condition:  $l \ge 3$. For $l=2$, the formula is: 
$D_2(n) = n - 1, \qquad n \ge 4.$

For $k=4$ with  period~$3$ (again $t = n - (l+1)$):
\[
D_l(n) =
\begin{cases}
l, & t = 0, \\[2pt]
l+1, & t = 1, \\[2pt]
l+2, & t = 2, \\[6pt]
l\!\left( 2 + \left\lfloor \dfrac{t-3}{3} \right\rfloor \right),
& t \ge 3,\; t \equiv 0,1 \pmod{3}, \\[10pt]
l\!\left( 2 + \left\lfloor \dfrac{t-3}{3} \right\rfloor \right) + 1,
& t \ge 3,\; t \equiv 2 \pmod{3}.
\end{cases}
\]

For $k=5$ with  period~$4$ (again $t = n - (l+1)$,
for all $L \ge 4$, starting from
$ t+1 \ge 9 \qquad \text{that is,} \qquad N \ge L + 9$):
                                                                                      
\[
D(L,N) = L \left\lfloor \frac{t+1}{4} \right\rfloor + \mathbf{1}_{(t+1) \equiv 0 \; (\mathrm{mod}\ 4)} .
\]
plain_text: (Not inverse closed case). For consecutive $k$-cycle generators not inverse closed, the diameters of the Schreier coset graph: $S_n / (S_ l x S_ n-l )$ (``$k$-shrunken'' Grassmannian $Gr(l,n,k)$) are given by quasi-polynomials in two variables $n$ and $t = n-(l+1)$. For $k=3$ with period $2$: \[ D_l(n) = l\!( t 2 + 1) + (t 2), t = n - (l+1), n >= l+1. \] Under the condition: $l >= 3$. For $l=2$, the formula is: $D_2(n) = n - 1, n >= 4.$ For $k=4$ with period $3$ (again $t = n - (l+1)$): \[ D_l(n) = cases l, & t = 0, [2pt] l+1, & t = 1, [2pt] l+2, & t = 2, [6pt] l\!( 2 + t-3 3 ), & t >= 3,\; t 0,1 3, [10pt] l\!( 2 + t-3 3 ) + 1, & t >= 3,\; t 2 3. cases \] For $k=5$ with period $4$ (again $t = n - (l+1)$, for all $L >= 4$, starting from $ t+1 >= 9 that is, N >= L + 9$): \[ D(L,N) = L t+1 4 + 1_ (t+1) 0 \; (mod\ 4) . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Bi-variable quasi-polynomiality for diameters of k-shrunken Grassmannian-like Schreier graphs (with explicit k=3,4,5 formulas) is a notable structural phenomenon in combinatorial geometry.
viability_rationale: Bi-variable quasi-polynomiality is a striking but more ambitious generalization. Verified for several small-k cases; general two-variable proofs look harder and less certain within five years.
rationale: Multi-part conjecture giving quasi-polynomial formulas (not inverse-closed case) for various k and l; presented as conjectural with conditions, not proved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Not inverse closed case): diameters of S_n/(S_l x S_{n-l}) are bi-variable quasi-polynomials; explicit k=3,4,5 formulas given. (Conj lines 5650-5660, 5664-5676, 5678-5684)

title: CayleyPy-4: AI-Holography. Towards analogs of holographic string dualities for AI tasks
body_tex:
(Inverse closed case). Same setup as above , but inverse closed case.

For $k=3$ from $L\ge 2$(?): 
\[
D(L, N) = 
\left\lceil \frac{L (N - L)}{2} \right\rceil
\]

For $k=4$ from $L\ge 2$: 
\[
D(L, N) = 
\begin{cases} 
\frac{L}{3}(N - L), & 3 \mid L, \\[10pt]
\left\lfloor \frac{L(N - L) + 2}{3} \right\rfloor, & 3 \nmid L.

For $k=5$ for $N,L,N-L$ large enough:

\]

For $k=3$ from $L >= 2$(?): \[ D(L, N) = L (N - L) 2 \] For $k=4$ from $L >= 2$: \[ D(L, N) = cases L 3 (N - L), & 3 L, [10pt] L(N - L) + 2 3 , & 3 L. cases \] For $k=5$ for $N,L,N-L$ large enough: \[ D(L, N) = LN + 2 4 - 2 L^2 8 - 1_ L 2 4 \, 1_ N 2 4 \]

viability_rationale: Explicit inverse-closed formulas for small k (k=3,4,5) with clear combinatorial form. These specific cases look provable; extending to wider parameter ranges is plausible in the near term.
rationale: Inverse-closed case formulas for D(L,N) (k=3,4,5) are stated as conjectures; paper does not prove them, only asserts ranges and evidence.

title: The Exact Saturation Number for the Diamond
body_tex: Let $\mathcal F$ be a diamond-saturated family. If $|\mathcal F|=n+1$, then $\mathcal F$ is either a maximal chain, the empty set together with all singletons, or the full set together with all complements of singletons. Moreover, if $\emptyset,[n]\notin\mathcal F$, then $|\mathcal F|\geq 2n-c$, for some universal constant $c$.
plain_text: Let $ F$ be a diamond-saturated family. If $| F|=n+1$, then $ F$ is either a maximal chain, the empty set together with all singletons, or the full set together with all complements of singletons. Moreover, if $,[n] F$, then $| F| >= 2n-c$, for some universal constant $c$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural classification of extremal diamond-saturated families after establishing the minimum size. Important for understanding extremal configurations but concentrated on one poset.
viability_rationale: Structure question for extremal diamond-saturated families builds on a complete size characterization; combinatorial classification seems approachable with further case analysis.
rationale: The authors prove sat^*(n,D2)=n+1 but do not classify all extremal families; they explicitly conjecture the structural classification remains open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If F is diamond-saturated with |F|=n+1 then F is either a maximal chain, empty set+singletons, or full set+complements; paper proves size n+1 but not this structural uniqueness.

title: Counting Theorems for Algebraic Relations
plain_text: Let $X subseteq R^n$ be definable in a -minimal structure with sharp derivatives and let $k=0,,n- X-1$. Then, for every $g,h in N$, equation \# X^ trans(k) (k,g,h) = poly_X(g,h). equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A strong polynomial-counting generalization of Wilkie's conjecture for o-minimal sets; meaningful for Diophantine applications though focused within the o-minimal/Diophantine community.
viability_rationale: Generalizes Wilkie's conjecture; k=0 proved only in special Pfaffian cases and authors obtain only weakened/special-case results (trajectories, k<√n−1). Broad o-minimal scope suggests full resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: The strong poly-counting conjecture in sharply o-minimal structures is posed as a main open conjecture; the paper proves only special/related results, not the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: #X^{trans(k)}(k,g,h) = poly_X(g,h) for definable X in a sharp o-minimal structure (lines 201–207). Paper notes this is a main conjecture and not proved (lines 198–204,214–221).

title: Algebraic statistics of Hüsler-Reiss graphical models in multivariate extremes

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates two ML-degree notions in algebraic statistics/matrix completion; equality known for chordal graphs and strict gaps occur. Resolving it would clarify structural links between Gaussian and extremal models.
viability_rationale: Substantial structural evidence and several resolved families (chordal, suspension, K_{2,n}, cycles) reduce the gap; remaining general cases likely technical but non-grand. Moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper states the inequality as an explicit conjecture and does not prove it; only special cases and examples are discussed.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1105-1111: "The examples above suggest the following conjecture... For any graph G, the extremal ML degree is at most its Gaussian ML degree: emld(G) ≤ mld(G)."

title: Algebraic statistics of Hüsler-Reiss graphical models in multivariate extremes

interestingness_rationale: A simple tight relation between extremal MLT and Gaussian MLT; if true it gives a clear bridge between inference thresholds for two model classes, with combinatorial and rigidity implications.
viability_rationale: Authors prove partial results (inequality, C4 case) and suggest matroid/rigidity methods, but an all-graph equality is broad and combinatorially challenging; plausible progress but not imminent within five years.
rationale: Authors pose equality emlt(G)=MLT(G)-1 as a conjecture and provide evidence (C4, inequalities) but do not prove it in general.

title: Coexact $1$-form spectral gaps of hyperbolic rational homology spheres
body_tex: There exists infinitely many hyperbolic rational homology spheres with $\lambda_1^*>2$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts infinitely many hyperbolic rational homology spheres with spectral gap above 2; has direct consequences for Floer theory (L-spaces) and 3-manifold spectral geometry, a central active problem.
viability_rationale: Authors produce infinite families with limits ≈0.82 and arithmetic examples, but current methods do not approach 2 and no concrete route to >2 is given; large remaining gap makes near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: Paper proves families with limit points ≈0.82 and poses existence of infinitely many with λ1^*>2 as an explicit conjecture; not resolved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 141-143: "We conjecture: There exists infinitely many hyperbolic rational homology spheres with λ1^* > 2."

viability_rationale: Recent 2025 conjecture with multiple partial verifications (P5-free, 4K1-free, 2P3-/claw-free cases) and structural reductions suggest moderate near-term solvability, but the general case remains broad and nontrivial.
rationale: The conjecture (attributed to Hoàng) is stated; the paper verifies it for several restricted graph classes but does not prove the general statement.

title: The Poincaré polynomial of the type B analogue of $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{0,n+1}$

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Real-rootedness of the type B Poincaré polynomials would imply gamma-positivity and strong log-concavity; verified computationally to n≤300, important in algebraic/combinatorial geometry.
viability_rationale: Gamma-positivity, a concrete quadratic recursion and verification to n≤300 give substantial progress and plausible routes (stability/interlacing), but a full general stability argument is nontrivial and not yet exhibited.
rationale: Authors propose B_n real-rooted for all n, verify up to n≤300 computationally, but do not prove the general claim.
evidence_snippet: Lines 887-892: "Conjecture: For all n≥1 the polynomials B_n are real-rooted. With the help of a computer we have verified that B_n is real-rooted for every n≤300."

title: On the expected value of energy in groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:EarthboundZero}
Let $G$ be a finitely generated group of exponential growth. Let $k \colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ be a nondecreasing function with $k \ll \sqrt{|\B_S(n)|}$, and let $\A$ be a $k$-subset of $F_n$ sampled uniformly at random in the word-balls $\B_S(n)$. Then,
\[
\lim_{n} \E[E(\A,\A)] = k^2 \,.
\]
plain_text: Let $G$ be a finitely generated group of exponential growth. Let $k N to N$ be a nondecreasing function with $k |_S(n)|$, and let $$ be a $k$-subset of $F_n$ sampled uniformly at random in the word-balls $_S(n)$. Then, \[ _ n = k^2 \,. \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts vanishing correction terms for expected energy in exponential-growth finitely generated groups, linking group growth with typical multiplicative behavior; meaningful in probabilistic group theory.
viability_rationale: Statement reduces to proving commuting and square-incidence probabilities vanish for all exponential-growth finitely generated groups. Proven for several classes (hyperbolic, RAAGs); a uniform general proof is nontrivial, so modest near-term solvability.
rationale: The limit statement for finitely generated groups of exponential growth is posed as a conjecture; the authors prove it only for certain classes.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1151-1157: "We thus propose the following conjecture... Let G be finitely generated of exponential growth... lim_n E[E(A,A)] = k^2."

title: On the expected value of energy in groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:Jeff}
Let $G$ be an infinite group and let $S$ be a finite generating set for $G$. Then, 
\begin{enumerate}[(i)]
\item $\iota_S(G)$ is positive if, and only if, $G$ is virtually abelian.
\item for every function $k \colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $k \ll \sqrt[4]{|{\bf B}_S(n)|}$, and for every $\A$, $k$-subset of $G$ sampled uniformly at random in $\B_S(n)$, we have 
$$\E[E(\A, \A^{-1})] = 2  k^2 - k \,. $$
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Let $G$ be an infinite group and let $S$ be a finite generating set for $G$. Then, [(i)] $_S(G)$ is positive if, and only if, $G$ is virtually abelian. for every function $k N to N$ such that $k | B _S(n)| $, and for every $$, $k$-subset of $G$ sampled uniformly at random in $_S(n)$, we have $$ = 2 k^2 - k \,. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Two-part conjecture: involution density characterizes virtual abelianness and gives a universal expectation formula for E(A,A^{-1}). Both statements connect algebraic group structure with probabilistic invariants.
viability_rationale: Partial proofs for residually finite/subexponential, hyperbolic, lamplighter and finite-involution cases; residual-finiteness seems technical. Broad scope (all finitely generated groups including exotic examples) leaves nontrivial obstacles—plausible but not imminent.
rationale: The two-part conjecture for finitely generated groups is explicitly posed; paper proves parts in many cases but not in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1536-1543: "Conjecture: (i) ι_S(G) positive iff G virtually abelian. (ii) For k≪|B_S(n)|^{1/4}, E[E(A,A^{-1})]=2k^2-k."

title: A lift of the colored Jones polynomial of a knot
body_tex:
\label{conj.1g} 
There is a map
\be
\label{Jg}
\{ \text{Knots }K\text{ in} \,\, S^3\}
  \to \calH_{\BZ[t^{\pm 1}, \Delta_K^{\mathfrak{g}}(t)^{-1}]/\BZ[t^{\pm 1}]},
\qquad K \mapsto J^\mathfrak{g}_K(t,q) 
\ee
satisfying
\be
\label{eg}
J^{\mathfrak{g}}_K(q^\lambda,q)
= J^{\mathfrak{g}}_{K,\lambda}(q) \in \BZ[q^{\pm 1}]
\subset \calH_\BZ \hookrightarrow \BZ[\![q-1]\!],
\qquad (\text{for all dominant $\lambda$}) \,.
\ee

interestingness_rationale: Proposes a Habiro-ring lift of colored Jones polynomials for all simple Lie algebras. Deeply bridges quantum invariants, integrality and Dehn-filling functoriality—high-impact in quantum topology.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the sl2 case and MMR holds for all simple g, so methods may extend. Nontrivial representation-theoretic/integrality obstacles remain; plausible progress in five years but requires substantial technical work.

title: KLR-Schur algebra of coherent sheaves on the projective line: Tilting and PBW bases

title: Modularity of special cycles on Shimura varieties: a survey
body_tex: The classes $[\cZ(m)]$ are in fact defined in the $L^2$-cohomology of $X$, and their generating series is a modular form.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts modularity of special-cycle classes in L^2/intersection cohomology of general Shimura varieties; a broadly relevant structural statement linking compactifications, L^2-cohomology and Kudla-type modularity.
viability_rationale: Concrete L2/intersection-cohomology framework with existing proofs in key cases (Bruinier–Zuffetti, Getz–Goresky) gives a plausible path to wider resolution within several years.
rationale: The paper explicitly poses this as a conjecture and only cites partial results (orthogonal up to middle degree; Hilbert modular surfaces). No general proof or refutation is given.

title: Modularity of special cycles on Shimura varieties: a survey
body_tex: Let $X$ be a Shimura variety for $\SU(2,2)\sim \SO(4,2)$ that is simultaneously unitary and orthogonal type. The generating series $\Phi_2(q)\in \CH^2(X)\otimes \Mod_3(\Sp_4(\Z))$ of codimension 2 orthogonal special cycles is the Saito-Kurokawa lift of the generating series $\Phi_1(q)\in \CH^2(X) \otimes \Mod_4(\SL_2(\Z))$ of codimension 2 unitary special cycles.
