The generating series $_2(q) in ^2(X) _3(_4())$ of codimension 2 orthogonal special cycles is the Saito-Kurokawa lift of the generating series $_1(q) in ^2(X) _4(_2())$ of codimension 2 unitary special cycles.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts a concrete Saito–Kurokawa lift relating orthogonal and unitary generating series for the SU(2,2)/SO(4,2) case—an interesting, specialized bridge between lift theory and cycle-generating series.
viability_rationale: Low-dimensional coincidence with known lifting maps (Saito–Kurokawa) and recent modular interpretations suggest a feasible, targeted proof strategy for this specific isogeny case.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture in a survey and offers no proof; the exact lifting relation for these Chow-valued generating series is not established in the literature cited.
evidence_snippet: "Let X be a Shimura variety for SU(2,2) ~ SO(4,2). The generating series Φ_2(q) ... of codimension 2 orthogonal special cycles is the Saito-Kurokawa lift of the generating series Φ_1(q) ... of codimension 2 unitary special cycles."

title: Beyond singular value gaps in randomized subspace approximation
plain_text: Assume the hypotheses of thm:cdf. Then with probability $1-$, equation _1 <= _k C_ N,k,p, 1+_k^2C_ N,k,p, ^2 , equation where $C_ N,k,p, $ is the constant in cor:scalar-bd and $_k$ is the Frobenius singular value ratio from eq:Fgap.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Tightens a probabilistic RRF error constant from δ^{-1/2} to δ^{-1/(p+1)} for p>1 using the Frobenius singular value ratio; a meaningful technical advance in randomized numerical linear algebra but limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Proven for p=0,1 with heuristic justification and strong numerics; the gap reduces to quantifying “almost quasiconvex” for a beta II density when N≫p — a focused technical task likely resolvable in five years.
rationale: The authors explicitly state Conjecture 5.1 as an open conjecture; it is proved only for p=0,1 in the paper and remains unproven for general p>1.
evidence_snippet: Section: “We state our conjecture formally... observe that thm:Fgap-bound is exactly the conjecture when p=0,1.” Conjecture 5.1 and inequality (1055) are presented as conjectural (lines 1050–1056).

title: On the list version of a conjecture of Erdős and Neumann-Lara
body_tex:
\label{conj:ENL} 
For every integer $k$ there is an integer $f(k)$ such that, for every graph $G$, $\chi(G)\geq f(k)$ implies $\vec\chi(G)\geq k$.
plain_text: For every integer $k$ there is an integer $f(k)$ such that, for every graph $G$, $(G) >= f(k)$ implies $(G) >= k$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A longstanding Erdős–Neumann–Lara conjecture linking graph chromatic number and orientations' dichromatic number; central in graph colouring/orientation theory with broad structural implications if resolved.
viability_rationale: Classic 1979 Erdős–Neumann–Lara conjecture; only fractional and list variants settled and f(3) remains open. Longstanding, widely resistant—unlikely to be solved within five years.
rationale: The original Erdős–Neumann–Lara conjecture is described as a famous open problem; the paper settles only the list version, not the original statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 238-244: "A famous open problem of Erdős and Neumann-Lara... In the present paper, we address and settle the list version of the conjecture."

title: Borel and shifted category O
body_tex:
\label{conj:qq shifted}

\cite[Conjecture 6.11]{FH3} For any $i\in I$, $a\in\mathbb{C}^*$, $w\in W$ we have
$$
\mathcal{Q}_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a} = [L^{\esh}(\bpsi_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a})]
$$
in $K_0(\mathcal{O}^{\esh})$.
plain_text: For any $i in I$, $a in C^*$, $w in W$ we have $$ Q_ w(_i^),a = [L^ (_ w(_i^),a )] $$ in $K_0(O^ )$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes identification of QQ-system variables with shifted prefundamental simple classes in K0(O^sh); gives an explicit representation-theoretic solution to QQ-relations, important within quantum affine/cluster frameworks.
viability_rationale: Concrete finite-type identification of QQ-variables with shifted simple modules; some cases proved and equivalent to Conjecture 44, but nontrivial global matching remains, giving moderate near-term solvability chance.
rationale: Conjecture 6.11 of FH3 is restated (equality in K_0(\mathcal{O}^{\esh})); the authors note equivalence to another conjecture but do not prove it here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2108-2116: 'For any i,a,w we have \mathcal{Q}_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a} = [L^{\esh}(\bpsi_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a})] in K_0(\mathcal{O}^{\esh}).'

title: Borel and shifted category O
body_tex:
\label{conj:qq borel}

For any $i\in I$, $a\in\mathbb{C}^*$, $w\in W$ we have the relation 
$$
\mathcal{Q}_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a} = (\chi^{w(\omega_i^\vee)})^{-1}[L(\bpsi_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a})]
$$
in $\overline{K}_0(\mathcal{O})$.
plain_text: For any $i in I$, $a in C^*$, $w in W$ we have the relation $$ Q_ w(_i^),a = (^ w(_i^) )^ -1 [L(_ w(_i^),a )] $$ in $K_0(O)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Equivalent Borel-formulation of the QQ identification in the renormalized Grothendieck ring: connects QQ-systems to quantum affine Borel representations and explicit character formulas, with notable structural consequences in the subfield.
viability_rationale: Equivalent precise Borel formulation of the QQ-system solution; finite-type and some simple cases are known, but full verification across Weyl group elements is a substantial technical task.
rationale: The paper presents this Borel-formulation conjecture (equivalent to Conjecture 43) and only verifies special cases (e.g. w=s_i), not the full statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2120-2127: 'For any i,a,w we have the relation \mathcal{Q}_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a} = (\chi^{w(\omega_i^\vee)})^{-1}[L(\bpsi_{w(\omega_i^\vee),a})] in \overline{K}_0(\mathcal{O}).'

title: Almost prime numbers
body_tex:
\label{ExistAlmostPrime}
Are there any composite weakly almost- primes greater than four or any composite almost-primes ?
plain_text: Are there any composite weakly almost- primes greater than four or any composite almost-primes ?
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An existence question for a new subtype of Carmichael numbers—nontrivial within the paper's framework but narrow in scope; meaningful to specialists though not a broad field-changing statement.
viability_rationale: Specific existence of composite almost-primes (a constrained subset of Carmichael numbers). Authors obtain strong restrictions but no constructive path; plausible progress but nontrivial—moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper poses existence of composite weakly almost-primes/almost-primes as a question and does not produce such composite examples; it only derives restrictions (e.g. Theorem: any almost-prime is prime or Carmichael).
evidence_snippet: Lines 69-72: conjecture posed. Lines 90-92: "Any almost-prime number is either a prime or a Carmichael number." No existence proof provided.

title: Almost prime numbers
body_tex: There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers.
plain_text: There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Infinitude of composite almost-primes would produce a special infinite subset of Carmichael numbers and bears on primes with ω(p−1)≤2; a deep, subfield-central problem with notable consequences.
viability_rationale: Asks infinitude of a restricted subset of Carmichael numbers. AGP-type constructions might be adaptable, but strong uniform congruence constraints and no explicit construction/evidence here make a near-term resolution uncertain.
rationale: The authors explicitly conjecture infinitude of composite almost-prime numbers and do not prove it; results in the paper impose constraints but stop short of proving infinitude.
evidence_snippet: Lines 96-98: "There are infinitely many composite almost-prime numbers." (stated as a conjecture, no proof follows).

title: Almost prime numbers
body_tex:
\label{SmallPortionOfPrimes}
There exists an $\epsilon<1$ such that the inequality $$\# \left\{p|n\ |\ \omega(p-1)>2\right\}<\varepsilon \omega(n)$$ is satisfied for infinitely many composite almost-primes $n$.
plain_text: There exists an $<1$ such that the inequality $$\# p|n\ |\ (p-1)>2 < (n)$$ is satisfied for infinitely many composite almost-primes $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical density statement about prime factors of composite almost-primes that is specialist in nature but implies a significant corollary about primes with few prime divisors of p−1.
viability_rationale: Implies infinitude of primes with ω(p−1)≤2 (covers safe/Sophie Germain primes). That's a deep unsolved distribution problem; paper gives restrictive lemmas but no clear finite reduction—near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: The paper formulates this density-type conjecture for prime divisors of composite almost-primes and uses it conditionally; it is not proved within the article.
evidence_snippet: Lines 156-158: conjecture stated. Lines 160-166: authors show this conjecture would imply infinitely many primes with ω(p-1)≤2, indicating it is conditional and unproven.

title: On semimonotone matrices of exact order two
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:1}
		Let \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \cap \mathbf{Z} \) be an \( \mathbf{E_{0_2}} \)-matrix. Then, the leading principal diagonal block of $A^{-1}:$
		\[
		A_{\alpha\alpha}^{-1} + A_{\alpha\alpha}^{-1} A_{\alpha \bar{\alpha}} (A / A_{\alpha\alpha})^{-1} A_{\bar{\alpha} \alpha} A_{\alpha\alpha}^{-1},
		\]
		as described in the proof of Theorem~\ref{theorem:4.11}, is a \( \mathbf{Z} \)-matrix. Moreover, \(A\) has exactly one negative eigenvalue.
plain_text: Let \( A in R^ n x n cap Z \) be an \( E_ 0_2 \)-matrix. Then, the leading principal diagonal block of $A^ -1 :$ \[ A_ ^ -1 + A_ ^ -1 A_ (A / A_ )^ -1 A_ A_ ^ -1 , \] as described in the proof of Theorem , is a \( Z \)-matrix. Moreover, \(A\) has exactly one negative eigenvalue.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects E_{0_2} semimonotone matrices with Z-property of an inverse principal block and predicts inertia (exactly one negative eigenvalue). Structurally meaningful within matrix classification but limited to a specialized class.
viability_rationale: Narrow linear-algebra conjecture with explicit examples and partial results; likely approachable by matrix-analytic techniques, giving a moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper explicitly formulates this as a conjecture (Conjecture 1) and does not prove it; no counterexample or proof is given in the text.
evidence_snippet: Lines 564-572: 'Motivated by numerous examples ... we propose the following conjectures: \begin{conjecture} ... the leading principal diagonal block of A^{-1} ... is a Z-matrix. Moreover, A has exactly one negative eigenvalue.'

title: On semimonotone matrices of exact order two
body_tex: Let \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \) be an \( \mathbf{E_{0_2}} \)-matrix. Then, \( \det(A) < 0 \), \( A^{-1} \) exists and is a \( \mathbf{Z} \)-matrix.
plain_text: Let \( A in R^ n x n \) be an \( E_ 0_2 \)-matrix. Then, \( (A) < 0 \), \( A^ -1 \) exists and is a \( Z \)-matrix.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Claims global properties (negative determinant, invertibility, inverse a Z-matrix) for all E_{0_2} matrices. A strong classification result within a niche area with clear structural consequences, but limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Concrete determinant/inverse claim supported by examples and proved invertibility for the Z-subclass; tractable linear-algebra methods make near-term resolution reasonably plausible.
rationale: This statement is presented immediately after as an explicit conjecture and the paper does not prove it; it remains posed as an open question within the article.
evidence_snippet: Lines 575-577: '\begin{conjecture} Let A\in R^{n\times n} be an E_{0_2}-matrix. Then, det(A)<0, A^{-1} exists and is a Z-matrix. \end{conjecture}'

title: Integral equations for flexural scattering problems with periodic boundaries
plain_text: Let $$ denote the portion of $$ lying in the strip $[-d/2,d/2) x R$ and equation H_() = _ _ () G_ ,L (-)()\, d and D_() = _ _ () G_ ,L (-)()\, d , equation be the quasi-periodic Hilbert transform and Laplace double layer potential respectively. If $$ is H\"older continuous and quasi-periodic, then equation 14H_^2[] = -4 + D_^2[]. equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends the classical Poincaré–Bertrand identity to quasi-periodic Hilbert and double-layer operators. Technically central for rigorous quasi-periodic boundary integral formulations and important for the paper's scattering methodology.
viability_rationale: Quasi-periodic analogue of a classical Poincaré–Bertrand identity with strong numerics; standard analytic/operator techniques likely extend, making a proof within five years fairly likely.
rationale: The authors state a 'Quasi-periodic Poincar\'e--Bertrand formula' as a conjecture, provide numerical evidence, but do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 382-392: 'We propose the following conjecture, which gives a quasi-periodic version ... \begin{conjecture}[Quasi-periodic Poincar\'e--Bertrand formula] ... If σ is H\"older continuous and quasi-periodic, then 1/4 H_ξ^2[σ] = -σ/4 + D_ξ^2[σ]. \end{conjecture}'

title: Aldous-type Spectral Gaps in Unitary Groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:main conj}
    Let $\Gamma=([n],w)$ be an arbitrary hypergraph with non-negative weights. Then the smallest non-trivial eigenvalue of the $\U(n)$-spectrum of $\Gamma$ is attained in one of the irreps $(1,0,\ldots,0,-1)$ or $(2,0,\ldots,0,-2)$.
plain_text: Let $=([n],w)$ be an arbitrary hypergraph with non-negative weights. Then the smallest non-trivial eigenvalue of the $(n)$-spectrum of $$ is attained in one of the irreps $(1,0,,0,-1)$ or $(2,0,,0,-2)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central Aldous-type analog: claims that only two specific irreps determine the U(n) spectral gap for any weighted hypergraph, bridging continuous U(n) Laplacians and discrete KMP processes with broad structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Authors prove several nontrivial cases and reduce possibilities; equivalent discrete reformulation exists. Significant progress but full arbitrary-hypergraph case remains broad and nontrivial, so moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: The paper states this as Conjecture 4.1 for arbitrary hypergraphs and only proves it in special cases (mean-field, n-1-supported); it is posed as an open conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 301-306: 'The following conjecture arises... Let Γ=([n],w) be an arbitrary hypergraph... Then the smallest non-trivial eigenvalue... is attained in one of the irreps (1,0,...,0,-1) or (2,0,...,0,-2).' (stated as Conjecture)

title: Aldous-type Spectral Gaps in Unitary Groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:main KMP style}
    Let $\Gamma=([n],w)$ be an arbitrary hypergraph with non-negative weights. Then $\lambda_{\min}^*(\Gamma,\kmp_2)$ is the smallest non-trivial eigenvalue in the $\U(n)$-spectrum of $\Gamma$. Namely,
    \[
        \lambda_{\min}^*(\Gamma,\kmp_2) = \inf_{\triv\ne\rho\in\irr(\U(n))}\lambda_{\min}(\Gamma,\rho) = \min_{\triv\ne\rho\in\irr(\U(n))}\lambda_{\min}(\Gamma,\rho).
    \]
plain_text: Let $=([n],w)$ be an arbitrary hypergraph with non-negative weights. Then $_ ^*(,_2)$ is the smallest non-trivial eigenvalue in the $(n)$-spectrum of $$. Namely, \[ _ ^*(,_2) = _ in ((n)) _ (,) = _ in ((n)) _ (,). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Equivalent KMP formulation of the main conjecture: asserts the two-particle discrete KMP always attains the U(n) spectral gap, directly linking finite discrete processes and infinite-dimensional unitary spectra with significant cross-field impact.
viability_rationale: Several nontrivial cases are proved, but the conjecture is open even for graphs. It is a broad U(n)-representation generalization of Aldous' hard problem; partial progress exists but full resolution seems unlikely within five years.
rationale: Conjecture 4.2 is presented as equivalent to Conjecture 4.1 and explicitly stated as a conjecture; the paper proves equivalence and special cases but not the general equality for all hypergraphs.
evidence_snippet: Lines 424-431: 'This shows that Conjecture 4.1 is equivalent to the following one: ... (Conjecture 4.2) ... λ_min^*(Γ,KMP_2) = inf_{triv≠ρ} λ_min(Γ,ρ) = min_{triv≠ρ} λ_min(Γ,ρ).' (stated as Conjecture)

title: Aldous-type Spectral Gaps in Unitary Groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:pure KMP}
    For any weighted hypergraph $\Gamma$ with non-negative weights and $k\in\mathbb{Z}_{\ge2}$ we have
    \[
        \lambda_{\min}^*(\Gamma,\kmp_k)=\lambda_{\min}^*(\Gamma,\kmp_2).
    \]
plain_text: For any weighted hypergraph $$ with non-negative weights and $k in Z_ 2 $ we have \[ _ ^*(,_k)=_ ^*(,_2). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Substantive discrete conjecture: all k-particle KMP processes share the same minimal torus-invariant eigenvalue as the two-particle case; important for KMP spectral theory and supportive of the main unitary conjecture.
viability_rationale: Nontrivial unitary-group analogue with several nontrivial cases proved, but still open even for graphs. Significant structural progress here helps, yet remaining generality and new representation-theoretic difficulties make a near-term full solution uncertain.
rationale: Conjecture 4.3 (pure KMP) is explicitly posed as open; authors note it holds in their special cases but 'not known even when Γ is a graph.'
evidence_snippet: Lines 437-442 and 450-451: 'Conjecture 4.3: ... λ_min^*(Γ,KMP_k)=λ_min^*(Γ,KMP_2).' '... to the best of our knowledge, Conjecture 4.3 is not known even when Γ is a graph.'

title: Aldous-type Spectral Gaps in Unitary Groups
body_tex:
\label{conj:order of omega_k}
    Let $\Gamma=([n],w)$ be a hypergraph with non-negative weights and for every $k\in\Z_{\ge1}$ let $\omega_k=\omega_k(\Gamma)$ denote the smallest new eigenvalue of $\L(\Gamma,\kmp_k)$. Then 
    \[
        \omega_1\le\omega_3\le\omega_5 \le\ldots~~~~~~~\mathrm{and}~~~~~~~\omega_2\le\omega_4\le\omega_6 \le\ldots
    \]
plain_text: Let $=([n],w)$ be a hypergraph with non-negative weights and for every $k in _ 1 $ let $_k=_k()$ denote the smallest new eigenvalue of $(,_k)$. Then \[ _1 <= _3 <= _5 <= and _2 <= _4 <= _6 <= \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Monotonicity conjecture for 'new' eigenvalues (odd/even k sequences): a structural ordering supported in special cases and simulations, useful but narrower and more technical within KMP spectral analysis.
viability_rationale: Proved for (n-1)-supported hypergraphs and supported by simulations; specialized conjecture with concrete partial results but no clear global method. Plausible extension of current techniques could resolve it within five years.
rationale: Conjecture 4.5 about ordering of ω_k is explicitly stated as a conjecture; authors prove it for n-1-supported hypergraphs but leave the general case open (supported by simulations).
evidence_snippet: Lines 1448-1453: 'Conjecture 4.5: Let Γ... and ω_k denote the smallest new eigenvalue... Then ω_1≤ω_3≤ω_5... and ω_2≤ω_4≤ω_6...'. Corollary proves it in special case; simulations support general conjecture.

title: Lower Bounds for the Pfaffian Number of Graphs
body_tex:
Let $G$ be a matchable graph.
	Let $H'$ be a non-spanning conformal subgraph of $G$ and let $H'' := G - V(H')$.
	If $H'$ and $H''$ are not pfaffian, then
		\[ \pf(H') + \pf(H'') - 1 < \pf(G).  \]
plain_text: Let $G$ be a matchable graph. Let $H'$ be a non-spanning conformal subgraph of $G$ and let $H'' := G - V(H')$. If $H'$ and $H''$ are not pfaffian, then \[ (H') + (H'') - 1 < (G). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A focused structural strengthening about pfaffian numbers; meaningful within matching/Pfaffian-orientation theory but narrow in scope and unlikely to have very broad consequences beyond the subfield.
viability_rationale: Paper proves related lower bounds and structural lemmas; conjecture refines a specific corollary. Narrow, approachable combinatorial target with concrete intermediate results, plausible but not certain within five years.
rationale: The paper states this inequality as an explicit conjecture in Conclusions and does not prove it; no evidence here that it has been resolved elsewhere.
evidence_snippet: Lines 895-901: 'we conjecture that the lower bound ... is not sharp if both conformal subgraphs are not pfaffian. Conjecture: Let G be matchable... If H' and H'' are not pfaffian, then pf(H')+pf(H'')-1 < pf(G).'

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex:
If $G$ is a connected graph on $n$ vertices, then
\[
b(G) \le \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil.
\]
plain_text: If $G$ is a connected graph on $n$ vertices, then \[ b(G) <= n . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Burning Number Conjecture is a central open problem in graph burning; asymptotic resolution exists, but exact √n bound is a prominent extremal question with clear significance in diffusion/influence models.
viability_rationale: Classic Burning Number Conjecture with substantial recent asymptotic progress but not resolved. Active area increases chance, yet final exact bound remains a hard, long-standing problem.
rationale: The Burning Number Conjecture b(G) ≤ ⌈√n⌉ is stated as a central open problem; the paper notes only asymptotic progress, not a full proof of the exact inequality.
evidence_snippet: Lines 232-238,249-252: 'A central open problem... BNC: If G connected on n vertices, b(G) ≤ ⌈√n⌉.' Norin and Turcotte showed asymptotically b(G) ≤ (1+o(1))√n.

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex:
If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices, then 
\[
b(T) \le \lceil \sqrt{n} \rceil.
\]
plain_text: If $T$ is a tree on $n$ vertices, then \[ b(T) <= n . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The tree-specialization of the Burning Number Conjecture encapsulates the core open case; resolving it would settle BNC and has substantial impact within graph burning and extremal graph theory.
viability_rationale: Tree-restricted BNC is narrower with many partial results but remains the central unresolved reduction of BNC. Progress exists but full resolution still seems moderately difficult soon.
rationale: The tree-restricted version of the BNC is presented as an equivalent open conjecture; the paper does not resolve it and cites related hardness and partial results.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2746-2752: 'Thus the BNC reduces to: Conjecture: If T is a tree on n vertices, then b(T) ≤ ⌈√n⌉.'

title: Burning rooted graph products
body_tex: If $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices, then $b(G) \le \lceil \sqrt{n}\rceil$.
plain_text: If $G$ is a connected graph with $n$ vertices, then $b(G) <= n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Burning Number Conjecture is a central, longstanding open problem in graph processes with sharp extremal examples and broad implications for influence propagation; resolving it would be a major advance in the area.
viability_rationale: Strong recent asymptotic progress (Norin–Turcotte) and many resolved classes raise plausibility, but closing the final exact sqrt(n) gap for all graphs remains nontrivial; moderate near-term solvability chance.
rationale: The paper presents the Burning Number Conjecture (BNC) as a central open problem; only asymptotic and special-case results are cited, not a full proof of the exact statement.
evidence_snippet: A major motivation... the Burning Number Conjecture (BNC): If G is a connected graph with n vertices, then b(G) ≤ ⌈√n⌉.

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{cinve} In the setting of Conjecture \ref{cinv}: (a) Every element $\Phi$ of $\Omega_\boxtimes$ exhibits the symmetry $\Phi(x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{n - 1}) = \Phi(x_{n - 1}, x_{n - 2}, \ldots, x_0)$; and (b) There exists a basis of $\Omega_\boxtimes$ which consists entirely of positive-coefficient polynomials when viewed over $\mathbb{Z}[\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \mathbf{x}]$.
plain_text: In the setting of Conjecture : (a) Every element $$ of $_$ exhibits the symmetry $(x_0, x_1, , x_ n - 1 ) = (x_ n - 1 , x_ n - 2 , , x_0)$; and (b) There exists a basis of $_$ which consists entirely of positive-coefficient polynomials when viewed over $Z[, x]$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts symmetry and positivity structure of invariant space for Gale–Robinson sequences; strengthens known positivity phenomena and is verified for small n, but is a specialist structural statement within a narrow subfield.
viability_rationale: Concrete, finite-dimensional algebraic conjecture with extensive computational verification for small n and some proofs up to n=7; plausible but requires new structural insights for general n.
rationale: The paper states Conjecture 5.2 (cinve) as an experimental supplement and does not prove it; only verified for small n by computation. It remains posed as an open hypothesis.
evidence_snippet: Lines 529-535: Conjecture \ref{cinve} is stated as a supplement to Conjecture \ref{cinv}; experiments cover small n, and the text frames it as a conjecture rather than a theorem.

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{grte} Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then, generically, the matrix $s \times t$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and even order $n$ with $n = 2m + 2$. Then, generically, the matrix $s x t$ is of diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Generalises proven finite-rank phenomena (orders 6,7) to even orders, giving a concrete bound 2^m; impactful for understanding matrix-rank structure of Gale–Robinson/Somos sequences, though largely subfield-specific.
viability_rationale: Supported by experiments for orders 8–9 and broader numerical data, but full general proof seems to need deeper theory beyond costly case computations.
rationale: Conjecture \ref{grte} is presented as a generalisation (even order) supported by experiments for small orders but not proved in the paper, thus remains open as stated.
evidence_snippet: Line 539 and 543: Conjecture \ref{grte} is stated (diamond rank ≤ 2^m) and the author reports experimental agreement for orders 8 and 9, without a proof.

title: Diamond Determinants and Somos Sequences
body_tex: \label{grto} Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then, generically, the matrix $s \times t$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
plain_text: Let $s$ and $t$ be two twinned Gale-Robinson sequences with nonzero terms, of a proper type and odd order $n$ with $n = 2m + 3$. Then, generically, the matrix $s x t$ is of half-diamond rank at most $2^m$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Odd-order analogue of the finite-rank conjecture, proposing half-diamond rank ≤2^m; similar significance to the even case, with experimental support but focused within the same specialised area.
viability_rationale: Analogous to the even-order statement with experimental backing for small orders; likely requires novel methods to handle all odd orders, so moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Conjecture \ref{grto} (odd order half-diamond rank ≤ 2^m) is likewise posed as an open conjecture supported by experiments but not established in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 541-543: Conjecture \ref{grto} is stated and the author notes experimental agreement for orders 8 and 9, with no proof provided.

title: Inversion diameter and 2-edge-colored homomorphisms
body_tex:
\label{conj:delta}
    Every graph $G$ satisfies $\diam(G) \leq \Delta(G)$.
plain_text: Every graph $G$ satisfies $(G) <= (G)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Major open bound asserting inversion diameter ≤ maximum degree; longstanding, central in the study of orientation reconfiguration and tied to several graph colouring parameters, with wide structural implications if resolved.
viability_rationale: Well-known conjecture (Havet et al.) with recent improvements but still a broad graph-theoretic statement; partial progress helps but full resolution seems unlikely within five years.
rationale: The paper cites Havet et al.'s Conjecture (diam(G) ≤ Δ(G)), discusses partial progress and improved bounds, and explicitly notes the general case remains an open problem.
evidence_snippet: Lines 115-120: Conjecture 6.4 (diam(G) ≤ Δ(G)) is stated; authors improved bounds but state the general case remains one of the main open problems.

title: The Airy line ensemble at the rough-smooth boundary
body_tex:
\label{conj:Gibbs}
		The two line ensembles $\{\cA_i^{n, \pm} : i \in \N\}$ converge in the uniform-on-compact topology to the  Airy line ensemble $\{\cA_i : i \in \N\}$.
plain_text: The two line ensembles $ _i^ n, : i in $ converge in the uniform-on-compact topology to the Airy line ensemble $ _i : i in $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Uniform convergence of undirected two-dimensional backbone paths to the Airy line ensemble would extend Brownian Gibbs techniques to 2D, resolving scaling limits at noisy boundaries and advancing integrable probability and random-tiling interface theory.
viability_rationale: Paper proves strong partial convergence and lists a plausible Gibbs approach; new ideas needed for 2D/undirected paths, but active literature and concrete ingredients make a near-term resolution moderately plausible.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture (uniform-on-compact convergence) and only proves weaker/partial convergence results; the exact uniform convergence is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: “Of course, the optimal result in this direction would be to show that the paths \cA_i^{n,\pm} actually converge uniformly to the Airy line ensemble. We state this as a conjecture.” (lines 553–563)

title: Variants of Merge-Width and Applications

Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded short merge-width.

interestingness_rationale: Clarifies robustness of a new graph parameter; resolving links structural, algorithmic and labelling consequences for merge-width, but impact is largely within a specialised area of graph structure theory.
viability_rationale: Authors prove many closure cases (bounded expansion, transductions) and develop tools here; the remaining general gap is nontrivial but focused, making resolution plausible within a few years.

evidence_snippet: Lines 932–935: 'We pose: Every class of bounded merge-width has bounded short merge-width.'

title: Colour diversity in spanning structures under Dirac-type conditions
body_tex:
\label{conj:PY}
   Every proper edge-colouring of an $n$-vertex graph with $\delta(G)\ge n/2$ contains a Hamilton cycle with at least $n/2-o(n)$ distinct colours.
plain_text: Every proper edge-colouring of an $n$-vertex graph with $(G) >= n/2$ contains a Hamilton cycle with at least $n/2-o(n)$ distinct colours.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural Dirac-threshold rainbow Hamiltonicity problem; settling the n/2 boundary would resolve a central question about colour diversity under minimum-degree constraints with clear structural consequences.
viability_rationale: Paper already proves the statement for every c>1/2 and highlights only the threshold case c=1/2; this narrow, well-studied Dirac-type gap is likely solvable with refinements.
rationale: The paper cites the conjecture (lines 211–213) and proves a near-case for c>1/2 (lines 216–223), but the exact δ(G)≥n/2 (c=1/2) case remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 211–213: conjecture states δ(G)≥n/2 implies a Hamilton cycle with ≥ n/2 - o(n) colours; lines 216–223: authors verify it for c>1/2 only.

title: Colour diversity in spanning structures under Dirac-type conditions
body_tex: Let $1/2<c\le1$. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with $\delta(G)\ge cn$, then every proper edge-colouring of $G$ contains at least $n/8$ Hamilton cycles, each using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.

interestingness_rationale: Extends uncoloured multiplicity results to the colourful Dirac setting; interesting within extremal graph theory but more specialized, hinging on technical multiplicity and colour-distribution issues.
viability_rationale: Strengthening to many (≈n/8) colourful Hamilton cycles adds substantial combinatorial difficulty beyond the single-cycle case, with no clear near-term route indicated.
rationale: Proposed in the concluding remarks (lines 925–927) as an open question; the paper does not prove the multiplicity result.
evidence_snippet: Lines 925–927: conjecture: for 1/2<c≤1, every proper colouring contains ≥ n/8 Hamilton cycles, each using ≥ cn - o(n) colours.

title: Colour diversity in spanning structures under Dirac-type conditions
body_tex:
Let $1/2<c\le1$. Suppose that $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree
$\delta(G)\ge cn$. Then there exists a constant $d>0$ such that for every tree $T$ on $n$ vertices with $\Delta(T)\le dn/\log n$, any proper edge-colouring of $G$
contains a copy of $T$ using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
plain_text: Let $1/2<c1$. Suppose that $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $(G) >= cn$. Then there exists a constant $d>0$ such that for every tree $T$ on $n$ vertices with $(T) <= dn/ n$, any proper edge-colouring of $G$ contains a copy of $T$ using at least $cn-o(n)$ distinct colours.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A colourful analogue of the Komlós–Sárközy–Szemerédi tree-embedding under Dirac conditions; significant within extremal graph theory and rainbow-structure study but mainly impactful inside the subfield.
viability_rationale: Paper proves Dirac Hamilton/matching cases and parallels KSS tree embedding; techniques (absorption/embedding/colour control) plausibly extensible, making a near-term resolution moderately likely.
rationale: The paper states this as an explicit conjecture in the concluding remarks and does not prove it; only Hamilton cycle/matching results are proved, not this tree-embedding colourful version.
evidence_snippet: Lines 936-940: "Let 1/2<c\le1... for every tree T on n vertices with Δ(T)≤ d n/log n, any proper edge-colouring of G contains a copy of T using at least cn-o(n) distinct colours." (posed as Conjecture)

title: Invertible Calabi-Yau Orbifolds over Finite Fields II
body_tex:
\label{conj:supertrace-counts-points}
Let $A$ be an $n\times n$ Berglund--H\"ubsch matrix over $\F_p$ with $n\ge 3$, and let $G$ be a subgroup of $G_A$ such that $J\in G\cap G^T$. If $Y_{A,G}$ is the orbifold $[X_A/(G/\langle J\rangle)]$ and $Z_{A,G}$ is any crepant resolution of $Y_{A,G}$, then for all $\nu\ge 1$,
\[
\#Z_{A,G}(\F_{p^\nu}) = {\rm ST}_{p^\nu}(A,G).
\]
At $\nu=1$, this is \cite[Conjecture~4.1]{AP2}.
plain_text: Let $A$ be an $n x n$ Berglund--H\"ubsch matrix over $_p$ with $n >= 3$, and let $G$ be a subgroup of $G_A$ such that $J in G cap G^T$. If $Y_ A,G $ is the orbifold $[X_A/(G/ J)]$ and $Z_ A,G $ is any crepant resolution of $Y_ A,G $, then for all $ >= 1$, \[ \#Z_ A,G (_ p^ ) = ST _ p^ (A,G). \] At $=1$, this is .
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts Frobenius supertrace equals point counts for crepant resolutions of Berglund–Hübsch orbifolds — a concrete arithmetic mirror-symmetry conjecture tying p-adic/cohomological data to zeta functions.
viability_rationale: Concrete arithmetic/mirror-symmetry conjecture with supporting nu=1 evidence and cohomological framework; technical but tractable incrementally, though full general proof remains challenging.
rationale: This is the paper's main conjecture asserting point counts equal a supertrace for crepant resolutions; the authors provide evidence but do not prove it, so it remains open.

title: Weakening the Legendre Conjecture

viability_rationale: Weaker, quantified prime-gap formulation has partial conditional results and computations, but unconditional general proof for all fixed α>1 still ties to deep prime-gap barriers; modest chance of progress in five years.

title: Weakening the Legendre Conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:legendre:mills}Define the sequence $q_1, q_2, q_3, \ldots$ by
  $q_1 = 2$ and by letting $q_{n + 1}$ be the least prime exceeding $q_n^2$.
  Then $q_{n + 1} < (q_n + 1)^2$ for all $n$.
plain_text: Define the sequence $q_1, q_2, q_3, $ by $q_1 = 2$ and by letting $q_ n + 1 $ be the least prime exceeding $q_n^2$. Then $q_ n + 1 < (q_n + 1)^2$ for all $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural Mills-related weakening of Legendre; significant for prime-generating constants but narrowly focused and likely comparable in difficulty to other prime-gap conjectures.
viability_rationale: Mills-type/Legendre-weak conjecture is explicitly noted as likely of comparable difficulty to Legendre's conjecture; authors deem it out of reach, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: The Mills-type sequence inequality is presented as a conjecture in the paper, noted weaker than Legendre but still out of reach; the paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 706-714: Conjecture states q_{n+1}< (q_n+1)^2 for all n, and the text: “Although Conjecture ... is weaker than Legendre's conjecture, it is likely of a similar difficulty and thus still out of reach.”

title: Euler characteristics of $SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$-orbit graphs of origamis
body_tex: Let $(\mathcal{G}_{n})_{n}$ be the family of $\SL(2,\Z)$-orbit graphs of primitive $n$-squared origamis in $\calH(2)$. Then $(\mathcal{G}_{n})_{n}$ is a family of expander graphs.

interestingness_rationale: McMullen's expander conjecture links dynamics on moduli spaces, spectral gaps, and arithmetic; proving it would have broad structural consequences for translation-surface dynamics and combinatorial/arithmetical graph families.
viability_rationale: McMullen's expansion conjecture is well-known and difficult; this paper gives indirect evidence but substantial spectral/control gaps remain, so full resolution in 5 years is unlikely.
rationale: McMullen's conjecture is stated and only supported by indirect evidence in this paper; the authors prove Euler characteristic growth but do not prove expansion.

title: Euler characteristics of $SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$-orbit graphs of origamis
body_tex:
{Zm}}]\label{conj:zmiaikou}
    For any $n\geq7$, there are exactly two orbits of primitive $n$-squared origamis in the stratum $\calH(1,1)$. Moreover, when $n$ is odd, the positive integers
    $$\mathcal{A}_n(1,1) = \frac{1}{24}n^2(n-3)(n-5)\prod_{\substack{p|n\\p\text{ prime}}}\left(1-\frac{1}{p^2}\right),$$
    and
    $$\mathcal{S}_n(1,1) = \frac{1}{8}n^2(n-1)(n-3)\prod_{\substack{p|n\\p\text{ prime}}}\left(1-\frac{1}{p^2}\right)$$
    are the cardinals of the orbits of $n$-squared origamis with monodromy group $\Alt(n)$ and $\Sym(n)$, respectively.
    When $n$ is even, the positive integers
    $$\mathcal{A}_n(1,1) = \frac{1}{24}n^3(n-2)\prod_{\substack{p|n\\p\text{ prime}}}\left(1-\frac{1}{p^2}\right),$$
    and
    $$\mathcal{S}_n(1,1) = \frac{1}{8}n^2(n-2)(n-4)\prod_{\substack{p|n\\p\text{ prime}}}\left(1-\frac{1}{p^2}\right)$$
    are the cardinals of the orbits of $n$-squared origamis with monodromy group $\Alt(n)$ and $\Sym(n)$, respectively.

Moreover, when $n$ is odd, the positive integers $$A_n(1,1) = 1 24 n^2(n-3)(n-5)_ p|n p prime (1-1 p^2 ),$$ and $$S_n(1,1) = 1 8 n^2(n-1)(n-3)_ p|n p prime (1-1 p^2 )$$ are the cardinals of the orbits of $n$-squared origamis with monodromy group $(n)$ and $(n)$, respectively. When $n$ is even, the positive integers $$A_n(1,1) = 1 24 n^3(n-2)_ p|n p prime (1-1 p^2 ),$$ and $$S_n(1,1) = 1 8 n^2(n-2)(n-4)_ p|n p prime (1-1 p^2 )$$ are the cardinals of the orbits of $n$-squared origamis with monodromy group $(n)$ and $(n)$, respectively.

title: Euler characteristics of $SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$-orbit graphs of origamis
body_tex:
\label{conj:wild}

plain_text: Given a family $(G_ n )_ n $ of $(2,)$-orbit graphs of primitive $n$-squared origamis in a connected component of a stratum $H:=_ g (k_ 1 ,,k_ s )$ not contained in a strictly smaller arithmetic subvariety, we have $|G_ n | = (n^ _ H-1 ) = (n^ 2g+s-2 )$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts polynomial growth of SL(2,Z)-orbit sizes in strata; central to Teichmüller dynamics, orbit counting, and has clear consequences for expansion and genera of arithmetic Teichmüller curves.
viability_rationale: Specialized orbit-growth statement with partial/conditional evidence in the paper. Active field and numeric data help, but remaining arithmetic/orbit-count obstructions make a full resolution in 5 years uncertain.
rationale: The statement is presented explicitly as Conjecture~\ref{conj:wild} and is used as an assumption elsewhere; the paper does not prove or refute it.

title: On the expansion formulas of cluster varieties from surfaces and their combinatorial properties
body_tex: Fix a non-$\cT$ diagonal $\gamma = AB$ in a general $n$-triangulated $m$-gon with $\cT$-triangulation of type $\mathcal{P}(m_1,n_1,\ldots,m_k,n_k)$. Then for $l = 1,2,...,n$ and $i,j \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $i+j \leq n$, every function $D^{[m_1,n_1,\ldots,m_k,n_k]}_l$ and $I^{[m_1,n_1,\ldots,m_k,n_k]}_{(i,j)}$ is a Laurent polynomial of variables $x^{(r)}_{(a,b,c)}$ where $r = 1,2,...,(m-2)$ and $(a,b,c) \in \Gamma_{n+1}$ with each coefficient being $1$.
plain_text: Fix a non-$$ diagonal $ = AB$ in a general $n$-triangulated $m$-gon with $$-triangulation of type $P(m_1,n_1,,m_k,n_k)$. Then for $l = 1,2,...,n$ and $i,j in N$ such that $i+j <= n$, every function $D^ [m_1,n_1,,m_k,n_k] _l$ and $I^ [m_1,n_1,,m_k,n_k] _ (i,j) $ is a Laurent polynomial of variables $x^ (r) _ (a,b,c) $ where $r = 1,2,...,(m-2)$ and $(a,b,c) in _ n+1 $ with each coefficient being $1$.
label: real_open_conjecture

viability_rationale: Verified for n=1,2; authors provide explicit recurrences, a ‘‘well‑triangulated’’ combinatorial framework and monomial counts, suggesting a concrete but technical extension to general n — plausible but not immediate.
rationale: The 'Multiplicity free' property is posed as a conjecture for general n; the paper verifies only n=1,2 and does not prove the general case.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 5796–5798) asserts coefficients equal 1 for general n; text notes the conjecture holds for n=1,2 but is left open in general.

title: Ours go to 211: Euler pseudoprimes to 47 prime bases (from Carmichael numbers)
body_tex:
If an odd squarefree composite integer $ n $ has
$\varphi(n)/2$
Euler liars in
$\mathbb{Z}_n^*$
then it is a Carmichael number,
and if it has
$\varphi(n)/4$
Euler liars in
$\mathbb{Z}_n^*$
then it is either a Carmichael number or a Sophie Germinus pseudoprime.
plain_text: If an odd squarefree composite integer $ n $ has $(n)/2$ Euler liars in $Z_n^*$ then it is a Carmichael number, and if it has $(n)/4$ Euler liars in $Z_n^*$ then it is either a Carmichael number or a Sophie Germinus pseudoprime.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classifies composites with unusually large proportions of Euler liars, linking such counts to Carmichael or Sophie Germain pseudoprimes; specialist but meaningful for understanding Euler pseudoprimes and primality-test failure modes.
viability_rationale: Concrete, mid-scale number-theory classification plus computational evidence; plausible progress but requires nontrivial structural arguments — moderately likely within five years.
rationale: The authors explicitly state this as a conjecture and leave it for future work; it is not proved or resolved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 4105–4116: "We conjecture the following... If an odd squarefree composite integer n has φ(n)/2 Euler liars ... then it is a Carmichael number, and if it has φ(n)/4 Euler liars ... then it is either a Carmichael number or a Sophie Germinus pseudoprime."

title: Non-commutative crepant resolutions for (almost) simplicial toric algebras

    A Gorenstein toric algebra always has an NCCR.

interestingness_rationale: A central conjecture in toric/noncommutative algebraic geometry asserting NCCRs exist for all Gorenstein toric algebras; resolving it yields broad structural and categorical consequences in the field.
viability_rationale: Long-standing open: many special cases (simplicial, almost-simplicial) now resolved; remaining general Gorenstein toric algebras are combinatorially diverse without a clear uniform method—moderate chance in five years.
rationale: The statement is presented as a named conjecture in the paper and the authors only prove special cases; the general claim remains unresolved here.

title: On the Maximum Number of Spanning Trees in $C_4$-Free Graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
Let $q$ be a prime power and $n=q^2+q+1$. Then
\[
\mathrm{st}(n,C_4)=\tau(ER_q)=n^{\frac{n-3}{2}},
\]
and the maximizers are precisely the orthogonal polarity graphs.
plain_text: Let $q$ be a prime power and $n=q^2+q+1$. Then \[ st(n,C_4)=(ER_q)=n^ n-3 2 , \] and the maximizers are precisely the orthogonal polarity graphs.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An extremal graph conjecture identifying orthogonal polarity graphs as unique maximizers of spanning trees at projective-plane orders; significant within extremal graph theory and links to finite geometry.
viability_rationale: Strong partial results: exact τ for ER_q and a matching leading-term upper bound, plus a concrete route via stability linking near-maximal τ to edge extremality. Main gap is proving the needed spectral/stability statement.
rationale: The authors pose this exact equality and uniqueness as a conjecture in the paper and do not prove it; they discuss plausible routes and leave it open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 284–289: "Conjecture: Let q be a prime power and n=q^2+q+1. Then st(n,C_4)=τ(ER_q)=n^{(n-3)/2}, and the maximizers are precisely the orthogonal polarity graphs."

title: Equivariant log concavity and the $\operatorname{FI^\sharp}$-module structure on $H^i(\operatorname{Conf}(n,\mathbb{R}^d))$
body_tex:
\label{mainConjecture}
    Given positive integers with $i<j\leq k <\ell$ and $i+\ell = j+k$, there is an inclusion of $\FIS$-modules:
    \[A^i\otimes A^\ell \hookrightarrow A^j\otimes A^k,\]
    and
    \[C^{i}\otimes C^{\ell}\hookrightarrow C^{j}\otimes C^{k}.\]
plain_text: Given positive integers with $i<j <= k < ell $ and $i+ ell = j+k$, there is an inclusion of $$-modules: \[A^i A^ ell A^j A^k,\] and \[C^ i C^ ell C^ j C^ k .\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Links FI^#-module structure to strong equivariant log-concavity for configuration-space cohomology; a structural representation-stability conjecture with genuine mathematical content but primarily impactful within a focused subfield.
viability_rationale: Proved up to degree 19; FI^sharp and representation-stability structure plus algorithmic methods give concrete progress, but extending to all degrees seems nontrivial and technically challenging.
rationale: The paper states the conjecture for all degrees but only proves the inclusion for degrees m ≤ 19, leaving the full conjecture open.

title: Topology of projective Tate-Shafarevich twists
body_tex:
\label{saccaconj}
    Total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations that are related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent.
plain_text: Total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations that are related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asks whether Tate–Shafarevich twists preserve deformation class of projective Lagrangian (hyper-Kähler) fibrations; significant for classification and moduli of irreducible hyper-Kähler varieties with clear structural consequences.
viability_rationale: This paper proves cohomology/Hodge invariance and establishes the conjecture under torsion/primitivity/smooth-base hypotheses via recent BKV results; removing those technical hypotheses remains the main obstacle.
rationale: The paper formulates Saccà's general conjecture and only proves results under additional hypotheses (torsion class, smooth base, primitivity), so the full general statement remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 68-70): total spaces of projective Lagrangian fibrations related by a Tate-Shafarevich twist are deformation-equivalent. Paper proves it under extra assumptions (abstract and lines 86-90, 48-49).

title: Structure and Spectrum of Nonergodic Nilsystems
body_tex:
\label{Conj}
   Let $(X=G/\Gamma,\mu,T_1,\ldots,T_d)$ be an ergodic $\Z^d$-nilsystem. Then the Koopman representation of $(T_1,\ldots,T_d)$ on $L^2(X,\mu)$ decomposes as
    \[
L^2(X,\mu)=E_{\mathrm{ds}}\oplus E_{\mathrm{Leb}},
\]
where $E_{\mathrm{ds}}\cong L^2(G/G_2\Gamma)$ has discrete spectrum and $E_{\mathrm{Leb}}$, if nontrivial, splits into a countable orthogonal sum of invariant subspaces, each having maximal spectral type absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure of a nontrivial subtorus of $\bS^d$.
plain_text: Let $(X=G/,,T_1,,T_d)$ be an ergodic $^d$-nilsystem. Then the Koopman representation of $(T_1,,T_d)$ on $L^2(X,)$ decomposes as \[ L^2(X,)=E_ ds E_ Leb , \] where $E_ ds L^2(G/G_2)$ has discrete spectrum and $E_ Leb $, if nontrivial, splits into a countable orthogonal sum of invariant subspaces, each having maximal spectral type absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure of a nontrivial subtorus of $^d$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural spectral decomposition for ergodic Zd-nilsystems (discrete plus Lebesgue pieces modeled by subtorus Haar measures) is a substantive contribution to nilsystem spectral theory with clear structural implications.
viability_rationale: Proved for single transformations and Z^2; conjecture reduces to understanding nonergodic Z^{d-1}-nilsystems. With concrete partial results and active research, moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The author explicitly poses this spectral decomposition for ergodic Z^d-nilsystems as a conjecture (motivated by proved special cases like Z^2), and does not prove it in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Lines 286-292: 'begin{conjecture}... L^2(X,μ)=E_{ds}⊕E_{Leb}, where E_{ds}≅L^2(G/G_2Γ) and E_{Leb} splits into countably many subspaces with maximal spectral type absolutely continuous w.r.t. Haar on a subtorus. end{conjecture}'

title: Rational points on modular curves: parameterization and geometric explanations
body_tex:
\label{SUC}
There exists a bound $C$ such that for every non-CM elliptic curve $E/\Q$ and every $\ell>C$, the mod $\ell$ representation $\rho_{E,\ell}$ is surjective.

interestingness_rationale: Serre's uniformity is a classical, central open problem about uniform surjectivity of mod-ℓ Galois representations for non-CM elliptic curves over Q, with deep structural consequences across arithmetic geometry.
viability_rationale: Serre's uniformity is a decades-old flagship problem with major partial results but no clear imminent route to a full proof; unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: This is Serre's uniformity question, a classical open conjecture about surjectivity of mod-ℓ Galois representations for non-CM elliptic curves; the paper assumes it rather than proving it.

viability_rationale: Explicit Serre uniformity (finite exceptional list) is concrete but rests on deep global Galois/image control; substantial progress exists but full resolution in 5 years seems unlikely.

title: Shifted multiplicative subgroups are not ratio sets
plain_text: Let $d >= 2$ be fixed. Then for all sufficiently large prime powers $q 1 d$, the multiplicative subgroup $G$ of $_q$ of index $d$ admits no nontrivial additive decomposition.
label: real_open_conjecture

viability_rationale: Recent breakthroughs (Kalmynin resolved the index-2 case; Hanson–Petridis and others advanced techniques) make progress plausible, but extending to all fixed index d and prime powers remains nontrivial.
rationale: The generalization to fixed index d over prime powers q is presented as a well-believed conjecture; the paper notes Kalmynin made substantial progress over prime fields but does not claim the full conjecture is proved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 139-142 state the general conjecture for index d. Line 149 says Kalmynin 'made substantial progress towards \cref{conj:additiveSarkozy2} over prime fields', not a full resolution.

title: Shifted multiplicative subgroups are not ratio sets
body_tex:
\label{conj:M_S}
Let $p$ be a large enough prime. Then for each $\lambda\in \F_p^*$, the set $(\mathcal{R}_p-\lambda)\setminus\{0\}$ has no nontrivial multiplicative decomposition, that is,  there are no two subsets $A,B$ of $\F_p$ with $|A|,|B|\geq 2$, such that \[AB=(\mathcal{R}_p-\lambda)\setminus\{0\}.\]
plain_text: Let $p$ be a large enough prime. Then for each $ in _p^*$, the set $(R_p-)0$ has no nontrivial multiplicative decomposition, that is, there are no two subsets $A,B$ of $_p$ with $|A|,|B| >= 2$, such that \[AB=(R_p-)0.\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural multiplicative analogue of Sárközy's conjecture; extends Shkredov/Kalmynin. Resolving would meaningfully advance structure theory of multiplicative subgroups and decomposition phenomena in finite fields.
viability_rationale: Significant recent progress (ratio-set case, many special primes/subgroups) and multiple partial results exist, but the full AB multiplicative-decomposition statement still has nontrivial gaps.

title: Projective bundles that admit coupled Kähler-Einstein metrics but no Kähler-Einstein metrics
body_tex:
For every $n\geq 4$, there exist toric Fano manifolds of dimension $n$ that admit two-coupled KE metrics but do not admit
ordinary KE metrics. In particular, foreach integer $r\geq 2$, the projective bundles
\[
\P_{\C P^r\times \C P^{r-1}}(\mathcal O\oplus \mathcal O(-1,1)), \qquad \P_{\C P^{r+1}\times \C P^{r-1}}(\mathcal O\oplus \mathcal O(-1,1))
\]
provide infinite families of such examples.

In particular, foreach integer $r >= 2$, the projective bundles \[ _ P^r x P^ r-1 ( O O(-1,1)), _ P^ r+1 x P^ r-1 ( O O(-1,1)) \] provide infinite families of such examples.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts a systematic geometric phenomenon separating coupled Kähler–Einstein and ordinary KE metrics in all dimensions; significant within Kähler/Fano geometry with structural implications and explicit candidate families.
viability_rationale: Author provides explicit constructions and low-dimensional proofs suggesting a systematic pattern; extending to all dimensions seems plausible but requires nontrivial geometric verification.
rationale: Paper constructs specific examples (e.g., six-dimensional) but poses existence for all n≥4 as a conjecture; no general proof is given.

title: On Weighted Twisted K-Energy and Its Applications
plain_text: )$-extremal Poincar\'e-type K\"ahler metrics ] Let $(X, )$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold and let $D$ be a smooth divisor. Let $T$ be a maximal compact torus in the reduced automorphism group $_ red (X, D)$. The manifold $X$ admits a $T$-invariant $(1,1,2)$-extremal metric in $[]$ if and only if the following conditions are satisfied: [(i)] Global Coercivity: The twisted K-energy $_ 1, ell ^ ext ^ 2 $ is coercive on the space of finite energy potentials $(X, )$ relative to $T^C$; [(ii)] Boundary Coercivity: The extremal K-energy $_ 1, ell ^ ext _ D $ is coercive on the space of finite energy potentials $(D, |_ D )$ relative to $T^C$; [(iii)] Boundary Stability Condition: On the divisor $D$, the extremal functions satisfy: \[ ell ^ ext _ D - ell ^ ext |_ D = c > 0 \] for some constant $c$.

viability_rationale: Active Kähler-geometry progress: paper gives convexity, openness and stability results narrowing gaps, but the full extremal/coercivity equivalence is broad and technically deep; modest near-term solvability plausible.

\label{conj:deterministic_rudelson}
Let $A_1,\dots,A_n\in \mathcal{M}_d$ be positive semidefinite and suppose that
\[
\lambda_1A_1+\cdots+\lambda_nA_n=\id_d,
\]
where the coefficients $\lambda_i$ are nonnegative and summing up to one.
Then for every $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ there exists a deterministic algorithm that selects indices
$i_1,\dots,i_k\in[n]$ with $k\le C(\varepsilon)\,d\ln d$ and outputs positive coefficients $\beta_1,\dots,\beta_k$
such that
\[
\Bigl\|\beta_1A_{i_1}+\cdots+\beta_kA_{i_k}-\id_d\Bigr\|_{S_\infty}\le \varepsilon.
\]

title: Betti numbers of ideals generated by $n+1$ powers of general linear forms
body_tex:
For a generic ideal $I = (f_1,\dots,f_r) \subset k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ with $\deg f_i = d_i$ we have
$$
\HS_{R/I}(T) = \left[\frac{\prod_{i=1}^r (1-T^{d_i})}{(1-T)^n}\right].
$$
plain_text: For a generic ideal $I = (f_1,,f_r) subset k[x_1,,x_n]$ with $ f_i = d_i$ we have $$ _ R/I (T) = [ _ i=1 ^r (1-T^ d_i ) (1-T)^n ]. $$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: This is Fröberg's conjecture on Hilbert series of generic ideals—a classical, central problem in commutative algebra with substantive structural consequences for Hilbert functions, semi-regular sequences, and Lefschetz-type properties.
viability_rationale: Fröberg's conjecture is a longstanding, widely studied open problem with many partial results but no known complete strategy; full resolution within five years appears unlikely from current progress.

title: Monodromy rank and the semisimple Mumford-Tate conjecture for hyper-Kähler varieties
body_tex:
\label{conj:Mumford--Tate conjecture}
Fix a field embedding $K \subseteq \CC$. Let $\overline{K}$ be the algebraic closure of $K$ inside $\CC$. For any prime $\ell$, the isomorphism $\GL(\rH^{i}_{\et}(X_{\overline{K}},\QQ_{\ell})) \allowbreak\cong \GL(\rH^{i}_{B}(X))_{\QQ_{\ell}}$ via the Artin comparison isomorphism gives an identification
	\[
	\bG_{\ell,i}(X)^{\circ} =\MT_{i}(X_{\CC})_{\QQ_{\ell}}\,.
	\]
	Here $\bG_{\ell,i}(X)$ is the \emph{$\ell$-adic algebraic monodromy group} of $\rH^i_{\et}(X_{\overline{K}},\QQ_{\ell})$; and $\MT_i(X_{\CC})$ is the \emph{Mumford--Tate group} of the Hodge structure on the Betti cohomology $\rH^i_B(X,\QQ)$.
plain_text: Fix a field embedding $K subseteq $. Let $K$ be the algebraic closure of $K$ inside $$. For any prime $ ell $, the isomorphism $(^ i _ (X_ K ,_ ell )) (^ i _ B (X))_ _ ell $ via the Artin comparison isomorphism gives an identification \[ _ ell ,i (X)^ =_ i (X_ )_ _ ell \,. \] Here $_ ell ,i (X)$ is the $ ell $-adic algebraic monodromy group of $^i_ (X_ K ,_ ell )$; and $_i(X_ )$ is the Mumford--Tate group of the Hodge structure on the Betti cohomology $^i_B(X,)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Mumford–Tate conjecture equating ℓ-adic algebraic monodromy and Mumford–Tate groups is a deep, flagship problem in arithmetic geometry linking Hodge and Galois theories with very broad consequences.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the semisimplified case for b2≥4 and deformation invariance—strong partial progress—but the full (non-semisimple/remaining cases) Mumford–Tate conjecture is deep and historically resistant, unlikely solved within 5 years.

title: Dualizable abelian fibrations
body_tex:
\label{conj3.1}
Any proper $f: X\to Y$ between nonsingular varieties admits a motivic decomposition.
plain_text: Any proper $f: X to Y$ between nonsingular varieties admits a motivic decomposition.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Corti–Hanamura motivic decomposition conjecture is foundational for relative Chow motives; resolving it would broadly impact motives, decomposition theorems, and deep structural links across algebraic geometry.
viability_rationale: The general Corti–Hanamura motivic decomposition is a broad, long-standing motive conjecture. Despite special-case progress, a complete proof for all proper morphisms is very unlikely in five years.
rationale: The motivic decomposition conjecture (Corti--Hanamura) is presented as an open conjecture; the paper proves it in special cases (abelian schemes) but not in general.
evidence_snippet: Lines 866-871: "The motivic decomposition conjecture of Corti--Hanamura predicts that a motivic decomposition always exists." Line 873: theorem shows it holds for abelian schemes.

title: Dualizable abelian fibrations
body_tex:
\label{conj}
Let $f: M \to B$ be a Lagrangian fibration. The following hold.
\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)]  The morphism $f: M \to B$ admits a multiplicative decomposition theorem
    \[
    Rf_* \BQ_M \simeq \bigoplus_{i} \CH_{(i)},\quad \CH_{(i)} := {^\Fp}H^{i + \dim B}(Rf_{*}\BQ_M)[-i - \dim B] \in D^b_c(B)    \]
    \item[(b)] Let 
    \[
    H^*(M, \BQ) = \bigoplus_{i} H_{(i)}^*(M, \BQ)
    \]
    be the cohomological decomposition given by an isomorphism in (a). Then 
    \[
    c_i(T_M) \in H^{2i}_{(i)}(M, \BQ).
    \]
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Let $f: M to B$ be a Lagrangian fibration. The following hold. [(a)] The morphism $f: M to B$ admits a multiplicative decomposition theorem \[ Rf_* _M _ i _ (i) , _ (i) := ^ H^ i + B (Rf_ * _M)[-i - B] in D^b_c(B) \] [(b)] Let \[ H^*(M, ) = _ i H_ (i) ^*(M, ) \] be the cohomological decomposition given by an isomorphism in (a). Then \[ c_i(T_M) in H^ 2i _ (i) (M, ). \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts a multiplicative perverse decomposition and placement of Chern classes for Lagrangian fibrations; a structurally meaningful conjecture in hyperkähler/perverse-Hodge theory with important but more focused consequences.
viability_rationale: Statement for Lagrangian fibrations is narrower with significant partial results (Hitchin, hyperkähler cases). Active techniques suggest plausible substantial progress or resolution in many cases within five years, though full generality remains nontrivial.
rationale: Conjecture for Lagrangian fibrations is stated as open; paper records known special cases (projective irreducible symplectic, Hitchin) but does not prove it generally.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1117-1132: Conjecture states multiplicative decomposition and Chern class membership for Lagrangian fibrations; line 1134 notes known special cases but not full proof.

title: Towards the Erdős matching conjecture for 4-uniform hypergraphs: stability and applications
plain_text: Let $H$ be an $n$-vertex $k$-graph with matching number at most $s$. Then equation e(H) <= n k -n-s k ,sk+k-1 k . equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Erdős Matching Conjecture is a longstanding flagship problem in extremal combinatorics; resolving it determines fundamental extremal structures for hypergraph matchings with wide implications across the field.
viability_rationale: The EMC is a classic, long-standing conjecture; strong recent advances (including k=4 progress here) help, but the full general statement is unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: This is the general Erdős Matching Conjecture for all k; the paper proves only the k=4 case for n≥5s (large n), so the full conjecture remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 138-143: EMC stated. Lines 158-163: paper proves EMC for k=4 and n≥5s (Theorem EMCK4), a partial result not resolving the general conjecture.

title: Towards the Erdős matching conjecture for 4-uniform hypergraphs: stability and applications

interestingness_rationale: Gives asymptotic minimum d-degree thresholds for matchings across parameters; influential within hypergraph matching theory and guides many degree-threshold results, though more specialist than EMC.
viability_rationale: The KOT asymptotic minimum-degree conjecture has many resolved cases and new stability tools; nonetheless its full generality remains broad and nontrivial.
rationale: The paper presents Conjecture KOT as an open conjecture, cites partial progress and special-case results, but does not claim a full proof of the general asymptotic formula.

title: Solving equations of signature $(p,p,2)$ with coefficients over number fields
body_tex:
\label{conj1}  Let $\overline{\rho}:G_K\rightarrow \GL_2(\overline{\mathbb{F}}_p)$ be an odd, irreducible, continuous representation with Serre conductor $\frakN$ (prime-to-$p$ part of its Artin conductor) 
	and such that $\det(\overline{\rho})=\chi_p$ is the mod $p$ cyclotomic character.   
	Assume that $p$ is unramified in $K$ and that $\overline{\rho}\mid _{G_{\Q_\frakp}}$ arises from a finite-flat group scheme over $\O_{\Q_{\frakp}}$ for every prime $\frakp\mid p$.  Then there is a weight two, mod $p$ eigenform $\theta$ over $K$ of level $\frakN$ such that for all primes $\frakq$ coprime to $p\frakN$, we have
	\[
	{\rm Tr}(\overline{\rho}({\rm Frob}_{\frakq}))=\theta(T_{\frakq}),
	\]
	where $T_{\frakq}$ denotes the Hecke operator at $\frakq$.

title: Modularity of a certain "rank-2 attractor" Calabi-Yau threefold

The (semi-simplification of the) $4$-dimensional representation of $G_ (17) $ on $H^3_ \'et (X_ _ , , _ ell )$ is isomorphic to the restriction from $G_ $ to $G_ (17) $ of $_ f, ell _ g, ell (-1)$, where $f$ and $g$ are cuspidal newforms of weights $4$ and $2$, respectively, for $_1(34)$, character $(17 * )$, labelled $34.4.b.a$ and $34.2.b.a$ in the LMFDB database .

title: On constructing small subgraphs in the budget-constrained random graph process
body_tex:
\label{conj:K5}
    For all $t\in[M]$, if
    \[t=o\left(n^{3/2}\right)\qquad\text{ or }\qquad b=o\left(\max\left\{\frac{n^{12}}{t^{7}},\left(\frac{n^{2}}{t}\right)^{5/3}\right\}\right),\]
    then for any $(t,b)$-strategy a.a.s.~$B_t$ does not contain a copy of~$K_5$.
plain_text: For all $t in [M]$, if \[t=o(n^ 3/2 ) or b=o( n^ 12 t^ 7 ,( n^ 2 t )^ 5/3 ),\] then for any $(t,b)$-strategy a.a.s. $B_t$ does not contain a copy of $K_5$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Tightening the budget threshold for K5 in the budget-constrained random graph process is a meaningful specialist question clarifying a phase transition; notable but focused on one small clique case.
viability_rationale: Authors already proved matching bounds for K4 and provide upper/lower bounds for K5 with a remaining gap; specialized probabilistic-combinatorics problem with concrete partial progress, reasonably likely resolvable in 5 years.
rationale: The paper states this as an explicit conjecture (gap remains for K5) and does not prove the stronger lower bound; authors remark a gap remains between bounds.
evidence_snippet: Lines 635–643: theorem gives nonmatching bounds for K5; 643: 'We believe that the upper bound should give the correct behaviour.' 645–649: Conjecture №K5 stated.

title: Bestvina metric and tree reduction for $K(π,1)$-conjecture

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A combinatorial/structural property for tree Coxeter-diagram Artin groups that is pivotal in reducing the K(π,1)-conjecture broadly; resolving it would yield substantial progress on K(π,1) for many Artin groups.
viability_rationale: Conjecture holds in many cases, but the hardest regime (all edge labels 3) reduces to delicate commuting-pair analysis; active work exists yet structural difficulties make a near-term full proof uncertain.
rationale: The paper explicitly proposes this conjecture as an open hypothesis about special 4-cycles; only partial cases are known and the general tree-diagram case remains unresolved here.

viability_rationale: Active field with many special-case/local proofs (zonoids, bodies of revolution) but the full local log-Brunn–Minkowski in general remains a broad open problem with significant gaps.
rationale: The paper formulates the local L^p-Brunn-Minkowski inequality as a conjecture and only proves it in special cases (bodies of revolution and related local results), not the full general statement.

title: A discrete view of Gromov's filling area conjecture

title: Dehn quandles of surfaces and their bounded cohomology

viability_rationale: Specialized algebraic/topological problem with partial results (length-three case proved); concrete structural methods may resolve it in the near term, but uncertainty remains.
rationale: After proving idempotent characterization for limited cases (e.g., length three), the authors explicitly conjecture the full classification of idempotents in Z[D_g] and do not prove the general statement.

title: On Sets of Monochromatic Objects in Bicolored Point Sets
body_tex: For any $\delta > 0$ there exists $\epsilon > 0$ such that in a non-collinear set of $n$ two-colored points, if the number of points on a line is at most $(1/2-\delta)n$, then the number of monochromatic lines is at least $\epsilon n^2$.
plain_text: For any $ > 0$ there exists $ > 0$ such that in a non-collinear set of $n$ two-colored points, if the number of points on a line is at most $(1/2-)n$, then the number of monochromatic lines is at least $ n^2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extremal combinatorial-geometry conjecture linking a line-density threshold to a quadratic lower bound on monochromatic lines; resolving it would clarify extremal structure beyond the near-pencil/Böröczky examples.
viability_rationale: Authors prove strong special cases and identify the Boroczky extremal example; incidence/stability methods look promising but a uniform δ→ε control remains nontrivial, so moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture (Böröczky example extreme) and does not prove or refute it; only related partial results are given.
evidence_snippet: Lines 416-419: "We conjecture... For any δ>0 there exists ε>0 such that... if the number of points on a line is at most (1/2−δ)n, then the number of monochromatic lines is at least ε n^2."

title: Towards direct $L^2$-bounds for maximal partial sums of Walsh--Fourier series: The case of dyadic partial sums
body_tex:
\label{claim:one_node} Suppose that $\mathcal{C}$ is a set of columns of a DTWH matrix $W_\Phi$ that has exactly one node in which each branch is complete above the node. Denote by $W_{\Phi,\mathcal{C}}$ the submatrix of $W_\Phi$ whose columns are those in $\mathcal{C}$. Let $\Phi'$ be the truncation that assigns to each column at or above the node its length with respect to a selected primary branch, and $\Phi'=\Phi$
for any column whose length is smaller than the nodal length.  Then $\|W_{\Phi',\mathcal{C}}\|\geq \|W_{\Phi,\mathcal{C}}\|$.
plain_text: Suppose that $C$ is a set of columns of a DTWH matrix $W_$ that has exactly one node in which each branch is complete above the node. Denote by $W_ ,C $ the submatrix of $W_$ whose columns are those in $C$. Let $'$ be the truncation that assigns to each column at or above the node its length with respect to a selected primary branch, and $'=$ for any column whose length is smaller than the nodal length. Then $\|W_ ',C \| >= \|W_ ,C \|$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A meaningful structural claim about operator norms of DTWH truncations that would imply optimality of the standard truncation; important for the paper's program but narrowly focused on Walsh truncation structure.
viability_rationale: Concrete finite matrix inequality; paper reduces to special cases and supplies evidence but no proof. Problem seems approachable by focused linear-algebra/combinatorial methods, though nontrivial technical work remains.
rationale: The paper explicitly states Conjecture~\ref{claim:one_node} and uses it as an assumption in Proposition~\ref{prop:strong_dyad} without proving or refuting it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 397–400: "\begin{conjecture} ... Then \|W_{\Phi',\mathcal{C}}\|\geq \|W_{\Phi,\mathcal{C}}\|.\end{conjecture}"; Prop. 404–407 assumes this conjecture.

$W_{N}^{\rm opt}$.

title: RLGT: A reinforcement learning framework for extremal graph theory

    For any $n \ge 3$, the graph $K_{\lfloor n / 3 \rfloor} \lor \overline{K_{\lceil 2n / 3 \rceil}}$ attains the maximum Mostar index among all connected simple graphs of order $n$.

viability_rationale: Specific extremal-graph conjecture with computational checks and an unsuccessful refutation attempt; finite combinatorial nature makes resolution plausible within years, but no clear closing route or strong local progress is given.
rationale: The authors describe the Mostar-index extremal claim as a natural conjecture that 'still seems to be open' and report an unsuccessful attempt to disprove it.
evidence_snippet: Despite being natural, the following conjecture on the extremality of the Mostar index still seems to be open. We end the section with one non-application, i.e., an unsuccessful attempt to refute a conjecture.

title: Spectrum, Tjurina spectrum, and Hertling conjecture for singularities of modality $\leq 3$

interestingness_rationale: The Hertling conjecture is a longstanding, central problem in singularity theory linking spectral variance and range; resolving it impacts Hodge-theoretic and F-manifold structures across the field.
viability_rationale: 25-year-old conjecture with partial progress (proved here for modality ≤3). Significant general-case gaps remain and the problem is technical; modest likelihood of full resolution within five years.
rationale: Hertling's conjecture is stated in full generality and the paper proves it only for singularities of modality 3; the full conjecture remains a longstanding open problem.

title: On rational chain connectedness of globally +-regular varieties
body_tex: \label{non-vanishing_conjecture} (Non-vanishing Conjecture) Let $X$ be a smooth projective variety over $\C$. If $K_X$ is pseudo-effective, then $\kappa(X)\geq 0$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The classical non-vanishing conjecture is a central open problem in the minimal model program; resolving it has major structural consequences (abundance, Kodaira dimension control) across birational geometry.
viability_rationale: Classical non-vanishing conjecture central to the MMP; decades-old major open problem with no local evidence here for imminent resolution.
rationale: This is the standard Non-vanishing Conjecture in MMP: open in general (known in low dimensions), and the paper cites it as a conjectural assumption rather than proving it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 277-280: 'Some of our results are contingent on the following conjecture: (Non-vanishing Conjecture) Let X be a smooth projective variety over C. If K_X is pseudo-effective, then κ(X) ≥ 0.'

title: On rational chain connectedness of globally +-regular varieties
body_tex: Let $(X,\Delta)$ be a strongly globally $+$-regular variety, projective over an open subset of $\mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})$.  Then $(X,\Delta)$ (and hence $(X_{\mathbb{Q}},\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}})$) is of log Fano type.
plain_text: Let $(X,)$ be a strongly globally $+$-regular variety, projective over an open subset of $Spec(Z)$. Then $(X,)$ (and hence $(X_ Q ,_ Q )$) is of log Fano type.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates strong global +regularity in mixed characteristic to log Fano type; a meaningful specialist conjecture that would clarify the link between singularity theory and Fano classification with geometric consequences.
viability_rationale: Significant open problem: recent partial progress (this paper, BMPSTWW1) yields rational chain connectedness in cases, but producing the log Fano boundary in mixed characteristic is a substantial unresolved hurdle; full resolution within 5 years unlikely.
rationale: Paper explicitly introduces this as a conjecture (the converse of a proposition) and notes the existence of the boundary making it log Fano is a 'major open question'.
evidence_snippet: Lines 421-426: 'Conjecturally, the converse of this proposition holds: Let (X,Δ) be a strongly globally + -regular variety... Then (X,Δ) is of log Fano type.' Followed by 'the existence of the boundary ... is major open question.'

title: On rational chain connectedness of globally +-regular varieties
body_tex: Let $X$ be a klt projective variety over $\C$. If $H^i(X,L)=0$ for $i>0$ and any semi-ample line bundle on $X$, then $K_X$ is not pseudo-effective.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical criterion tying vanishing for all semi-ample line bundles to negativity of K_X; potentially useful for MMP arguments but narrow in scope and application.
viability_rationale: Very general klt statement not resolved here. Paper gives related mixed-characteristic and special-case progress but provides no explicit reduction or finite check; full resolution within five years seems unlikely.
rationale: Paper states this vanishing-implies-non-pseudo-effective claim as a conjecture used to remove an assumption; it is not proved here and remains open within the paper's context.
evidence_snippet: Lines 543-546: 'It is clear that the same argument implies that if the following conjecture holds, then one can drop the assumption... [Conjecture] Let X be a klt projective variety over C. If H^i(X,L)=0 for i>0 and any semi-ample line bundle on X, then K_X is not pseudo-effective.'

When $1 r_1^2 +1 r_2^2 <= 2$, there exists an inner spectral boundary given by the circle centered at the origin with radius equation r_1^2+r_2^2-1-(r_1^2-r_2^2)^2+1 2 . equation The conjecture is that these constructions generate all spectral boundaries of $M$.

rationale: The authors state this spectral-boundary description as an explicit conjecture about their model; it is proposed (not proved) and illustrated numerically, so remains open here.
