title: Spectral boundaries of deterministic matrices deformed by rotationally invariant random non-Hermitian ensembles
body_tex:
\label{main_conjecture}
Let $\vb{A}$ be a large deterministic matrix and $\vb{B}$ a rotationally invariant random matrix. Then, for large $\omega$ and $z\in\C$, one expects
\begin{equation}\label{eq_conjecture}
    \mathcal{G}_{\vb{A} + \vb{B}}(\omega, z)
    \;=\; \mathcal{G}_{\vb{A}}\!\left(\omega - \mathcal{R}_{1, \vb{B}}(\g_1,\g_2),\, z - \mathcal{R}_{2, \vb{B}}(\g_1,\g_2)\right)
\end{equation}where $\g_1 = \g_{1, \vb{A} + \vb{B}}$ and $\g_2 = \g_{2, \vb{A} + \vb{B}}$. 
This identity can be analytically continued to all $(\omega,z)$, although one must carefully choose the appropriate branches of $\mathcal{R}_1$ and $\mathcal{R}_2$.
plain_text: Let $A$ be a large deterministic matrix and $B$ a rotationally invariant random matrix. Then, for large $$ and $z in $, one expects equation G_ A + B (, z) \;=\; G_ A \!( - R_ 1, B (_1,_2),\, z - R_ 2, B (_1,_2)) equationwhere $_1 = _ 1, A + B $ and $_2 = _ 2, A + B $. This identity can be analytically continued to all $(,z)$, although one must carefully choose the appropriate branches of $R_1$ and $R_2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes an additive subordination identity for non‑Hermitian rotationally invariant ensembles; resolving it would give structural control of spectra and edges in deformed nonnormal RMT, a central problem in that subfield.
viability_rationale: Concrete technical conjecture in active non-Hermitian random-matrix/free-probability research; authors give numerics and a framework. Progress likely but analytic/branch issues nontrivial, so plausible but not imminent.
rationale: The paper explicitly labels and uses this as a conjecture (an assumption) and does not prove or refute it; later arguments rely on it.
evidence_snippet: "We restate here: \begin{conjecture}...\end{conjecture}. We now aim to prove Theorem... by relying on the conjecture." (lines 764–772)

title: More Derived Models in PFA
plain_text: Suppose $$ is a limit of Woodin cardinals. Then the following hold. $^ D(V,) < ^+$. $D(V,) AD_ R .$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Links PFA to determinacy in derived models at limits of Woodin cardinals; proving it would strongly connect forcing axioms with inner model/determinacy theory, a major direction in modern set theory.
viability_rationale: Deep inner-model/determinacy conjecture linking PFA and derived models; partial special-case progress exists, but full resolution requires major inner-model advances—unlikely within five years.
rationale: The authors present progress and conditional/special-case results toward the PFA conjecture but do not establish the full statements as universally true.
evidence_snippet: "This paper makes significant progress towards resolving Conjecture \ref{conj:PFA}... The main theorem ... implies part (1) in the case \kappa has cofinality \omega..." (lines 81–106)

title: Disjoint Correspondence Colorings for $K_5$-Minor-free Graphs
plain_text: If $G$ is $K_5$-minor-free and $$ is a correspondence 6-cover, then $G$ has 6 disjoint $$-colorings. That is, $(_5)=6$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural combinatorial strengthening about disjoint correspondence colorings for K5‑minor‑free graphs; significant within graph coloring and list/correspondence theory but limited in scope to that community.
viability_rationale: Accessible combinatorial problem with concrete bounds (current upper bound 8) and supporting partial results; likely solvable by refinements of known graph-coloring/correspondence techniques in the near term.
rationale: The conjecture is posed as an open problem; the paper proves related weaker/partial results (upper bound 8, constructions for 3 colorings) but not the asserted equality chisc(K5)=6.
evidence_snippet: "So the first open case is \chisc(\K_5). The best known upper bound is \chisc(\K_5)\le 8... We cautiously believe that this upper bound can be improved as follows. \begin{conj} ... \chisc(\K_5)=6\end{conj}." (lines 142–149)

title: Cantor sets in higher dimensions II: Optimal dimension constraint for stable intersections
body_tex:
\label{conj:Falconer-regularCantor}
    Every regular Cantor set 
    \(K \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) with \(\dimHD(K) > d/2\) can be 
    $C^\infty$-approximated by a regular Cantor set $\tilde K$ such that the pair \((\tilde{K}, \tilde{K})\) has $C^r$-stable intersection for some $r>1$.
plain_text: Every regular Cantor set \(K subset R^d\) with \((K) > d/2\) can be $C^$-approximated by a regular Cantor set $ K$ such that the pair \((K, K)\) has $C^r$-stable intersection for some $r>1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A stable‑intersection analogue of Falconer’s conjecture for regular Cantor sets; resolving it would be a deep advance in fractal geometry and dynamical intersections, touching major open themes.
viability_rationale: Strengthens Falconer-type distance/set problems; related to long-standing hard questions. New methods help, but full conjecture seems as difficult as major open problems—unlikely soon.
rationale: The authors explicitly formulate this as a conjecture and note it is open, comparing it to Falconer's distance set conjecture which remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: "We formulate this in the following conjecture. \begin{conjecture}...\end{conjecture} This conjecture can be viewed as a 'stable' version of Falconer’s distance set conjecture... Falconer’s conjecture is still open." (lines 365–374)

title: On the Coupled Cluster Doubles Truncation Variety of Four Electrons
body_tex:
Consider the rational map $\gamma: \mathbb P^m \dashrightarrow \mathbb P^{m+s}$ such that 
\[ \begin{matrix}
\gamma: & \mathbb P^m \setminus \textrm{V}(z_0) & \longrightarrow & \mathbb P^{m+s}\\
& z & \mapsto & y 
\end{matrix} \quad \text{with} \quad y_i = \begin{cases}
    z_0^2 & \text{if }i=0,\\
    z_0z_i & \text{if } i \in \{1,\ldots,m\},\\
    \gamma_i(z) & \text{if } i \in \{ m+1, \ldots, m+s\},
\end{cases}
\]
where $\gamma_i(z)$ are general homogeneous quadratic polynomials for $i=m+1,\ldots, m+s$. Then the graph of $\gamma$ has degree
\[ \deg(\text{graph}(\gamma)) = \begin{cases}
(m - s + 2)2^s -1 & \text{if } s<m, \\
2^{m+1}-1 & \text{if } s \geq m.
\end{cases} \]
plain_text: Consider the rational map $: P^m P^ m+s $ such that \[ matrix : & P^m V(z_0) & & P^ m+s & z & maps to & y matrix with y_i = cases z_0^2 & if i=0, z_0z_i & if i in 1,,m , _i(z) & if i in m+1, , m+s , cases \] where $_i(z)$ are general homogeneous quadratic polynomials for $i=m+1,, m+s$. Then the graph of $$ has degree \[ (graph()) = cases (m - s + 2)2^s -1 & if s<m, 2^ m+1 -1 & if s >= m. cases \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A concrete degree formula for a class of quadratic-enriched rational maps has clear consequences for computing CC degrees and links geometry to quantum chemistry, but is a specialist algebro-geometric statement with limited cross-field reach.
viability_rationale: Explicit algebraic-degree formula with strong computational evidence; standard intersection-theoretic and enumerative methods plausibly can prove it, though technical work remains.
rationale: The authors present the degree formula as an explicit conjecture supported by computational evidence, but do not prove it for the general parameters (m,s).
evidence_snippet: Lines 1114-1126,1115-1129: 'Based on computations ... we conjecture the following: Consider the rational map ... Then the graph of \(\gamma\) has degree \(= (m-s+2)2^s-1\) if \(s<m\), else \(2^{m+1}-1\)'.

title: On the Coupled Cluster Doubles Truncation Variety of Four Electrons
body_tex:
\label{conj: CC degree}
    For $d=4$ and $n \leq 12$, the CC degree of the truncation variety $V_{\{2\}}$ is 
    $$ \CCdeg_{4,n}(\{2\}) =
    (m - s + 2)2^s -1,$$ where $m = 6{{n-4}\choose 2}$ and $s={{n-4}\choose 4}$.

viability_rationale: Finite-n statement (n≤12) with confirming computations for small n and linked to the generic-degree conjecture; finite verification or short theoretical completion seems achievable in near term.

title: A Homogeneous Nullstellensatz for Joint Invariant Subspaces
plain_text: Let $f_1,,f_l,\, g in C x$. The following are equivalent: [(i)] for every complex Hilbert space $H$ and every tuple of bounded operators $A=(A_1,,A_d) in B(H)^d$, every joint invariant subspace of $f_1(A),,f_l(A)$ is invariant under $g(A)$; [(ii)] $g$ belongs to the unital $C$-algebra generated by $f_1,,f_l$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asks whether the finite-dimensional counterexample disappears in the infinite-dimensional operator setting; resolving it affects the noncommutative Nullstellensatz and links operator algebras with algebraic membership, a substantial subfield question.
viability_rationale: Infinite-dimensional setting removes decidability obstruction but a nontrivial gap (closure vs. span) and operator-algebra complications remain, requiring new techniques; not an immediate near-term resolution.
rationale: The infinite-dimensional analogue is explicitly proposed as a new conjecture in the paper and only discussed preliminarily; it is not proved or disproved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 507-516: "We therefore propose the following conjecture... for every complex Hilbert space ... every joint invariant subspace ... is invariant under g(...) iff g belongs to the unital C-algebra generated by f_i."

title: An assortment of problems in permutation patterns: unimodality, equivalence, derangements, and sorting
body_tex:

Every principal downset in the permutation pattern poset is rank-unimodal.
plain_text: Every principal downset in the permutation pattern poset is rank-unimodal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural unimodality conjecture in permutation patterns: a clear, potentially deep combinatorial problem with significant interest within the permutation-patterns community but specialized scope.
viability_rationale: Broad combinatorial unimodality conjecture with computational evidence but encompassing all principal downsets; historically hard and likely requires new techniques, so moderate chance of progress.
rationale: The principal-downset rank-unimodality conjecture is posed explicitly as an open problem in the paper; authors note it is unresolved in the permutation-pattern literature.
evidence_snippet: Lines 294–297: "Conjecture: Every principal downset in the permutation pattern poset is rank-unimodal." Context notes many unresolved unimodality questions.

viability_rationale: Broad, long-standing unimodality conjecture with mixed analogue results and no concrete reduction; community progress limited, so resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: The paper reproduces McNamara and Steingrímsson's conjecture and discusses analogous results but provides no proof or counterexample; it remains posed as open.
evidence_snippet: “McNamara and Steingrímsson posed the following conjecture... Every interval in the permutation pattern poset is rank-unimodal.” (Conjecture statement, lines 307–312)

title: An assortment of problems in permutation patterns: unimodality, equivalence, derangements, and sorting
body_tex:
\label{conj-layered-unimodal}
The principal downset of every composition is rank-unimodal in the subword order. Hence, the principal downset of every layered permutation is rank-unimodal.
plain_text: The principal downset of every composition is rank-unimodal in the subword order. Hence, the principal downset of every layered permutation is rank-unimodal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A narrower special case (compositions/layered permutations) of the general unimodality question; computationally verified to moderate size but limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Verified computationally up to length 34 and monotone cases proved, but the full layered/composition case remains open and part of larger unresolved conjectures; no clear closing route yet.
rationale: Stated explicitly as a conjecture; the author notes the general case remains open and only verifies cases up to length 34.
evidence_snippet: “The principal downset of every composition is rank-unimodal in the subword order... The general case, even for layered permutations, remains open.” (Conjecture and context, lines 411–418)

title: An assortment of problems in permutation patterns: unimodality, equivalence, derangements, and sorting
body_tex: The limiting proportion of derangements among $123$-avoiding permutations is equal to~$\nicefrac{9}{16}$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Numerically backed asymptotic proportion for 123-avoiding derangements; of clear interest to enumerative combinatorics but relatively narrow and empirically motivated.
viability_rationale: 123-avoiding permutations form a well-structured Catalan class with strong numerical evidence; analytic-combinatorics or bijective methods likely can obtain the asymptotic 9/16 constant, though technical work remains.
rationale: Presented as Pantone's conjecture based on numerical experimentation; the paper reports empirical support but no proof.
evidence_snippet: “Pantone ... conjecture that the limiting proportion is precisely 9/16. The limiting proportion of derangements among 123-avoiding permutations is equal to 9/16.” (Conjecture, lines 1181–1186)

title: Chebyshev polynomials and a refinement of the local residue/non-residue structure at a prime
body_tex: Every integer $a \not= 0, \pm 1, 2n^2+1$, and  not a Chebyshev squares is a Chebyshev primitive root mod $p$ for infinitely many $p$.
plain_text: Every integer $a = 0, 1, 2n^2+1$, and not a Chebyshev squares is a Chebyshev primitive root mod $p$ for infinitely many $p$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A direct analogue of Artin's primitive root conjecture for Chebyshev primitive roots; solving it would be a deep result in analytic number theory with clear structural and density consequences, mirroring Hooley-style difficulties.
viability_rationale: Direct analogue of Artin's primitive-root conjecture; no new decisive route given. Likely as hard as classical Artin, conditional results only; near-term full resolution unlikely.
rationale: The paper states this Chebyshev-Artin analogue as a conjecture, provides numerical data but does not prove or disprove it; it remains posed as an open problem.
evidence_snippet: Lines 289-295: authors state the Chebyshev analogue of Artin's conjecture, present numerical computations, and ask about density estimates and GRH bounds, without proof.

title: On cyclic invariants of the free associative algebra
body_tex:
\label{conjecture}
Every two minimal generating systems of an $S$-subalgebra of $(K\langle X_d\rangle,\circ)$ have the same number of elements of a given degree.
plain_text: Every two minimal generating systems of an $S$-subalgebra of $(K X_d,)$ have the same number of elements of a given degree.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural uniqueness statement about graded counts of minimal generators for S-subalgebras; meaningful to invariant theory and free algebra specialists but narrowly scoped to this algebraic framework.
viability_rationale: Only small-case evidence (C3) provided; problem is a recent, specialized structural question in noncommutative invariant theory. Plausible approachable but requires new representation/structural techniques, so moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: Authors explicitly pose the statement as a conjecture and note they do not know whether it holds in general; only a special case (d=3) is confirmed.
evidence_snippet: Lines 641-651: authors state they do not know the general fact, then Conjecture is given and Example confirms it only for the special case C_3.

title: The nonlinear Schrödinger equation with combined nonlinearities in 1D
plain_text: Let $u_0 in X$ or $H^1$ and for $b in R$ set $v_0 = e^ i b |x|^2 4 u_0$. Then (a) for $b>0$ the solution with the given initial condition $v_0$ exists globally and scatters; (b) for $b<0$ and sufficiently localized $u_0$, the solution with the initial condition $v_0$ blows up in finite time.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A dynamical dichotomy (global scattering for b>0, finite-time blow-up for b<0) extending large-b results to all b and relating to blow-up theory for 1D NLS with combined nonlinearities; significant within PDE dynamics.
viability_rationale: Authors prove scattering for large b and provide numerics; 1D setting and available pseudo-conformal/virial techniques make extension to all b>0 and blow-up for b<0 plausible, but general combined/unbounded nonlinearities leave technical gaps.
rationale: The authors state this as an explicit conjecture, provide numerical evidence and partial results but do not prove the full statement (scattering for all b>0 and blow-up for b<0).
evidence_snippet: Lines 521-533: authors say they expect scattering for any b>0, present numerical confirmation, then state Conjecture asserting global scattering for b>0 and blow-up for b<0.

title: Power monoids and their arithmetic: a survey

For each $l \in \mathbb N$, there exists an index $k \in \allowbreak \llb 0, l \rrb$ such that $\alpha_{0, l} \le \cdots \le \alpha_{k,l}$ and $\alpha_{k,l} \ge \cdots \ge \alpha_{l,l}$.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Unimodality of atom-count sequences links combinatorial and arithmetic structure of power monoids; meaningful within factorization theory but relatively narrow and likely approachable by combinatorial/probabilistic methods.
viability_rationale: New conjecture with experimental support and a partial result (holds for almost all l for H=ℕ). Non-classic, approachable combinatorial setting, but completing all l seems nontrivial.
rationale: The paper states the Unimodality Conjecture as open; only partial progress is cited (holds for almost all l for H=N), so the exact statement remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1071–1074: Conjecture stated. Lines 1082–1083: Aggarwal et al. show it holds for almost all l in the case H=ℕ (partial result).

title: Immersions with small normal curvature
body_tex:
\label{Conj:BiRic}
If $f: X^4\to \BB^N$ has $\fc(f) < \sqrt{\frac{12}{7}}$ then $g=f^*g_{\RR^N}$ is conformally equivalent to a metric of positive bi-Ricci curvature.
plain_text: If $f: X^4 to ^N$ has $(f) < 12 7 $ then $g=f^*g_ ^N $ is conformally equivalent to a metric of positive bi-Ricci curvature.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Connects an immersion curvature threshold to existence of positive bi-Ricci metrics in dimension four, with geometric and topological implications; central to curvature/immersion problems in the subfield.
viability_rationale: Technical 4D geometric-analytic conjecture; authors obtain sphere-case and sharp constants but extension to general ball appears difficult and methodologically nontrivial.
rationale: The BiRic conjecture is posed in the Remarks and Conjectures section and is not proved in the paper; only a special sphere-case is verified algebraically.
evidence_snippet: Lines 808–811: Conjecture stated. Line 811: Holds if the immersion lies on the unit sphere S^{N-1}, but general case remains difficult.

title: Immersions with small normal curvature
body_tex:
\label{conj:imo-ric}
For $X^n$ closed, if $f: X^n\to \BB^N$ has $\fc(f) < \sqrt{\frac 95}$ then $g=f^*g_{\RR^N}$ is conformally equivalent to a metric $\tilde g$ so that if $\tilde\lambda_1 \leq \dots \leq \tilde\lambda_4$ are the eigenvalues of $\widetilde\Ric$ at any point, then $\tilde \lambda_4 < \tilde\lambda_1+\tilde\lambda_2 + \tilde\lambda_3$.
plain_text: For $X^n$ closed, if $f: X^n to ^N$ has $(f) < 95$ then $g=f^*g_ ^N $ is conformally equivalent to a metric $ g$ so that if $_1 <= <= _4$ are the eigenvalues of $$ at any point, then $ _4 < _1+_2 + _3$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proposes a sharp eigenvalue inequality for conformally related Ricci tensors tied to immersion curvature bounds; technically significant for sharpness questions but narrower and more analytic.
viability_rationale: Specific conformal-Ricci eigenvalue condition tied to geometric immersion bounds; plausible but requires new analytic tools beyond current partial/special-case arguments.
rationale: The imo-ric conjecture is explicitly proposed and not proved; the authors pose it as an open problem and ask whether S^2×T^2 admits such a metric.
evidence_snippet: Lines 814–816: Conjecture stated. Line 817: Authors note it is an interesting (open) problem to determine if S^2×T^2 admits such a metric.

title: On the depth of depth-weighted trees
body_tex:
\label{conj:exp}
For every weight function $f$ satisfying assumption~\eqref{eq:ExpG} for some $c > 1$, there is $\nu=\nu(c)\in(0,1)$ such that 
\be 
\frac{d(T_n)}{n}\toas \nu.

title: Fano varieties with split tangent sheaf
body_tex:
\cite{Bea98}
\label{conjecturebeauville}
Let $X$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold such that $T_X = V_1 \oplus V_2$, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are {\em integrable} subbundles.
Let \holom{\mu}{\tilde{X}}{X} be the universal covering of $X$. Then there is a decomposition
$$
\tilde{X} \simeq X_1 \times X_2
$$
and the decomposition $T_X=V_1 \oplus V_2$ lifts to the canonical decomposition
$p_{X_1}^* T_{X_1} \oplus p_{X_2}^* T_{X_2}$.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold such that $T_X = V_1 V_2$, where $V_1$ and $V_2$ are integrable subbundles. Let X X be the universal covering of $X$. Then there is a decomposition $$ X X_1 x X_2 $$ and the decomposition $T_X=V_1 V_2$ lifts to the canonical decomposition $p_ X_1 ^* T_ X_1 p_ X_2 ^* T_ X_2 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Beauville's conjecture is a longstanding, central problem: integrable tangent splitting implies universal‑cover product decomposition, with wide consequences across complex/Kähler geometry and manifold structure theory.
viability_rationale: Longstanding (Beauville 1998) conjecture with substantial partial progress (projective/Fano cases, integrability results). Active research but full compact Kähler statement remains unresolved; modest chance of full resolution in five years.
rationale: Beauville's conjecture is stated as a well-known open problem; the paper proves special (Fano/mildly singular) cases but does not resolve the conjecture in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 437–446): If TX=V1⊕V2 with V1,V2 integrable, then universal cover splits as X1×X2 and the decomposition lifts to p_{X1}^*TX1⊕p_{X2}^*TX2.

title: Symmetry shifting for monoidal bicategories

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A higher-categorical generalization of Joyal–Street: proving equivalences between En-algebras and braided/symmetric pseudomonoids in Gray monoids would be foundational for higher monoidal category theory.
viability_rationale: Natural generalization of Joyal–Street; paper proves related results via ∞-operadic Additivity, so closure likely needs technical coherence/model-comparison work—plausible but nontrivial within five years.
rationale: Conjecture appears in the 'Future work' section and is not proved in the paper; results later are conditional on this conjecture holding.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1651-1659: Conjecture stated as future work. Lines 1671-1673: subsequent theorem assumes Conjecture~\ref{conj_higherEnint} holds.

title: Automorphisms of Kimura Hadamard Matrices
body_tex: Let $H$ be a KHM of order $4(2k)+4$. If $(R,S) \in \mathrm{Aut}(H)$ has prime order $p$, then $p=2$ or $p$ divides $k$.

interestingness_rationale: Restricts prime orders of automorphisms for Kimura Hadamard matrices; yields useful classification constraints but is a specialized structural claim limited to a narrow family.
viability_rationale: Concrete partial lemmas restrict prime orders (p≤k, permutation-block action) and many example data support it; remaining technical classification seems approachable by group/block analysis within a few years.
rationale: Presented in the paper's 'Open questions' and not proved or refuted; authors state examples only 'suggest' it. No proof or counterexample provided here.

title: A new problem related to Eulerian graphs
body_tex: Let $k$ be a natural number. The problem of determining whether a given subgraph $H$ with at most $k$ components of a finite graph $G$ is $S$-Eulerian (semi $S$-Eulerian) is polynomial in time.
plain_text: Let $k$ be a natural number. The problem of determining whether a given subgraph $H$ with at most $k$ components of a finite graph $G$ is $S$-Eulerian (semi $S$-Eulerian) is polynomial in time.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural fixed-k extension of the connected-case algorithmic result; useful for complexity classification but limited conceptual novelty or wider mathematical consequences beyond this specific problem.
viability_rationale: For fixed k, parameterized/DP or enumeration often yields polynomial algorithms. Paper proves k=1 linear and NP-complete in general, so bounded-component polynomiality is plausible and likely reachable within five years.
rationale: The author explicitly poses this as a conjecture (Section Introduction) and the paper does not prove or refute it; only special cases (connected H) and hardness for general H are proved.

title: $\mathcal L$-invariants and deep congruences between newforms

rationale: The authors formulate this global conjecture (Conjecture 3.1) and provide data/theoretical evidence but do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 391-405: Conjecture states existence and uniqueness of newform g with opposite Atkin–Lehner sign and specified p-adic valuation and congruence depth; presented as a conjecture.

title: Minimal Inversions in Integer Matrices of Fixed RSK Shape
body_tex:
\label{conj:hankel}
Let $\lambda$ be a partition with $n$ parts, and let 
$M=(m_{i,j})_{1\le i,j \le n} \in \mathcal{M}_\lambda$ 
be a minimal matrix of shape $\lambda$. Then $M$ is symmetric, that is,
\[
m_{i,j} = m_{j,i} \quad \text{for all } 1 \le i,j \le n.
\]
Moreover, every minimal matrix of shape $\lambda$ is Hankel: there exists a sequence of integers 
\[
s_2, s_3, \dots, s_{2n}
\]
such that
\[
m_{i,j} = s_{i+j}
\quad \text{for all } 1 \le i,j \le n.
\]
plain_text: Let $$ be a partition with $n$ parts, and let $M=(m_ i,j )_ 1 <= i,j <= n in M_$ be a minimal matrix of shape $$. Then $M$ is symmetric, that is, \[ m_ i,j = m_ j,i for all 1 <= i,j <= n. \] Moreover, every minimal matrix of shape $$ is Hankel: there exists a sequence of integers \[ s_2, s_3, , s_ 2n \] such that \[ m_ i,j = s_ i+j for all 1 <= i,j <= n. \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserting Hankel/symmetric structure for minimal RSK-shape matrices gives a strong structural simplification in algebraic combinatorics; meaningful within the RSK/Greene framework but primarily of specialist interest.
viability_rationale: Concrete combinatorial structural conjecture with numerical evidence and partial local results (closure under transpose, small-shape checks); likely approachable by combinatorial/RSK techniques, moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states Conjecture~\ref{conj:hankel} and later assumes it to prove Theorem~\ref{thm:char}; the conjecture is not proved in the text.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture~\ref{conj:hankel} stated (lines 150–165). Theorem~\ref{thm:char} later begins with 'Assume Conjecture~\ref{conj:hankel} holds' (lines 169–176).

title: A mirror deformation of Markov numbers
body_tex:
There exists a (possibly different) $q$-deformation $a_\ell(q) \in \ZZ_{\geq 0}[q]$ of $\ell$ such that, on the branch $(m_\ell,p_{n-1},p_n)$ with $\ell$ fixed, if we define the following $q$-Markov function
\[
\mathcal{M}_q^{\ell}(m_\ell,p_{n-1},p_n) = \dfrac{q^{d}M + q^{d+\varepsilon}P_n + q^{d-\varepsilon}P_{n-1}}{a_\ell p_np_{n-1}}
\]
where $d=\deg(p_{n+1})+\varepsilon$, and $\varepsilon=1$ if the branch is red and $\varepsilon=-1$ if the branch is blue, then $\mathcal{M}_q^{\ell}(m_\ell,p_{n-1},p_n)$ is constant equal to $[3]_q$.
plain_text: There exists a (possibly different) $q$-deformation $a_ ell (q) in _ >= 0 [q]$ of $ ell $ such that, on the branch $(m_ ell ,p_ n-1 ,p_n)$ with $ ell $ fixed, if we define the following $q$-Markov function \[ M_q^ ell (m_ ell ,p_ n-1 ,p_n) = q^ d M + q^ d+ P_n + q^ d- P_ n-1 a_ ell p_np_ n-1 \] where $d=(p_ n+1 )+$, and $=1$ if the branch is red and $=-1$ if the branch is blue, then $M_q^ ell (m_ ell ,p_ n-1 ,p_n)$ is constant equal to $[3]_q$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Nontrivial structural conjecture generalizing a q‑Markov invariant from Fibonacci/Pell branches to all fixed-ℓ branches; would clarify global behaviour of the mirror q‑deformation and its mutation dynamics within Markov/tree frameworks.
viability_rationale: Concrete algebraic identity with substantial computational evidence and a conjectural formula; likely amenable to explicit construction or reduction methods, so a resolution within ~5 years is plausible.

title: On the behavior of analytic torsion for twisted canonical bundles under degenerations

\label{conj:indep:rho}
If ${\rm Sing}\,X_{0}$ is isolated, $\xi={\mathcal O}_{X}$ and $n>1$, then $\varrho_{\pi, K_{X/S}(m)}$ is independent of the choice of 
an ample line bundle $H$ and $m>0$. Moreover, 
$$
\varrho_{\pi, K_{X/S}(m)}-\varrho_{\pi, K_{X/S}} = \dim \ker \square_{X_{0}}^{(0, n-1)} - h^{0,n-1}(X_{s}), 
\qquad
s\not=0.
$$ 
Here $\square_{X_{0}}^{(0,n-1)}$ is the Friedrichs extension of the Hodge-Kodaira Laplacian acting on the compactly supported 
smooth $(0,n-1)$-forms on $X_{0}\setminus{\rm Sing}\,X_{0}$.

viability_rationale: Extension of proven determinant and special-case results; methods (Takayama, L^2-metric analysis) give a credible path, but technical work remains to cover all q and higher direct images.
rationale: After proving related partial results (Proposition), the paper explicitly poses this Mumford-goodness statement as a conjecture without proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 4723–4726: "For any q>0, the Hermitian metric μ^* h_{L^2} on R^q f_* K_{Y/T}(F^* ξ) is good in the sense of Mumford."

title: Matching conditions for scattering solutions of scalar wave equations on extremal Reissner-Nordström spacetimes
body_tex:
\label{intro:conj:dhr}
		For the Einstein vacuum equations, given smooth scattering data settling down to a (possibly extremal) Kerr solution on $\hor$ and $\scri$ at a fast enough inverse polynomial decay rate, there exists a vacuum spacetime $(M, g)$ ``bounded by” $\hor$ and $\scri$, attaining the data, and that is regular away from $\hor$. However, for generic such data converging to a subextremal Kerr solution, the Christoffel symbols of the resulting metric fail to be locally square integrable near the horizon.
plain_text: For the Einstein vacuum equations, given smooth scattering data settling down to a (possibly extremal) Kerr solution on $$ and $$ at a fast enough inverse polynomial decay rate, there exists a vacuum spacetime $(M, g)$ ``bounded by” $$ and $$, attaining the data, and that is regular away from $$. However, for generic such data converging to a subextremal Kerr solution, the Christoffel symbols of the resulting metric fail to be locally square integrable near the horizon.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Core conjecture in mathematical general relativity about scattering regularity and generic horizon singularities; resolving it affects scattering theory, final-state constructions, and the possibility of smooth multi-black-hole spacetimes.
viability_rationale: Deep nonlinear Einstein scattering conjecture with some linear/conditional progress; full global construction and generic L^2 failure near subextremal horizons remains hard—unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: The paper cites this as a conjecture from Dafermos–Holzegel–Rodnianski and uses it as motivation; the authors do not prove or refute it here.
evidence_snippet: "\begin{conjecture}[\cite{dafermos_scattering_2024}] ... for generic such data converging to a subextremal Kerr solution, the Christoffel symbols ... fail to be locally square integrable near the horizon."

title: Matching conditions for scattering solutions of scalar wave equations on extremal Reissner-Nordström spacetimes

However, for generic data as above, the Christoffel symbols of the resulting metric will not be $C^N$ regular near the horizon.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extremal Einstein–Maxwell analogue asserting generic loss of C^N regularity at the horizon; important for constructing smooth extremal scattering solutions and for applicability of nonlinear matching techniques.
viability_rationale: Nonlinear Einstein–Maxwell extremal scattering claim; scalar-case techniques help but extending to full Kerr–Newman and proving generic C^N failure near the horizon is a difficult global problem, unlikely solved soon.
rationale: The authors state this extremal Kerr–Newman conjecture as their own open question (``We believe...''/``Conjecture'') and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: "\begin{conjecture}\label{intro:conj:extremal} For smooth scattering data ... there exists a solution ... However, for generic data ... the Christoffel symbols ... will not be $C^N$ regular near the horizon."

title: Kakeya conjecture and High-Rank Lattice von Neumann algebras
body_tex: \label{conj:Kakeya} For every $\varepsilon>0$, $\sup_{\delta > 0} \fonction_d(\delta)\delta^{-\varepsilon}=\infty$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A strong form of the Kakeya conjecture, central in harmonic analysis; here tied to L^p properties of high-rank lattice von Neumann algebras—resolution would have major cross-field consequences.
viability_rationale: Kakeya-type conjecture in higher dimensions is a longstanding, flagship harmonic-analysis problem; recent low-dimensional breakthroughs don't make full higher-dimensional resolution likely within five years.
rationale: This is a version of the Kakeya conjecture: proven in dimensions 2 and 3 but remains open in higher dimensions; the paper presents it as the classical open conjecture.

title: Two Dimensional Subtraction -- Transfer Games
body_tex:
\label{conjecture - main2}
    Suppose $0<y_2<2b$, $\gcd\{x_1,x_2\}=1$, and  $\gcd\{b,y_2\}=1$.  Suppose further that none of Corollary \ref{cor - vector elimination} or Propositions \ref{prop - Sporadic1}(i),\ref{prop - Sporadic1}(iii), \ref{prop - the trivial case generalized}, \ref{prop - sporadic2}, or \ref{prop - sporadic3} apply.     
    Then the  horizontal period of $L(b;x_1,0;x_2,y_2)$ is given by
    \[g(b,x_1,0,x_2,y_2) =\frac{2b(x_1+x_2)}{\gcd\{2b,y_2\}}.\]
plain_text: Suppose $0<y_2<2b$, $x_1,x_2=1$, and $b,y_2=1$. Suppose further that none of Corollary or Propositions (i),(iii), , , or apply. Then the horizontal period of $L(b;x_1,0;x_2,y_2)$ is given by \[g(b,x_1,0,x_2,y_2) =2b(x_1+x_2) 2b,y_2 .\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gives an exact horizontal-period formula for nim-values in a broad class of two-pile subtraction-transfer games; meaningful within combinatorial game theory but narrowly scoped and conditional on excluding sporadic elimination phenomena.
viability_rationale: Extensive computer verification (parameters ≤20) and many handled cases suggest plausibility. Main barrier is unpredictable “eventual vector elimination”; requires new combinatorial control. Moderate chance of resolution within five years, but nontrivial.
rationale: The paper explicitly states Conjecture \ref{conjecture - main2} and notes inability to prove it; no proof or counterexample appears in the paper.
evidence_snippet: “we currently have no idea...our inability to show when this does not happen is our main roadblock to proving Conjecture \ref{conjecture - main2}.” (lines 1325-1326)

title: Observability and Semiclassical Control for Schrödinger Equations on Non-compact Hyperbolic Surfaces
body_tex: Let $X$ be a $\Gamma$-covering of a compact hyperbolic surface $M$, $S \subset X$ be a nonempty, $\Gamma$-periodic, open subset. Then, for any $T>0$, there exists a constant $C=C(X,S,T)>0$ such that the observability inequality \eqref{observability} holds. Furthermore, denoted by $\Omega=\pi(S)$, then the constant $C$ in \eqref{observability} can be chosen such that it depends only on $M,\Omega$ and $T$.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a $$-covering of a compact hyperbolic surface $M$, $S subset X$ be a nonempty, $$-periodic, open subset. Then, for any $T>0$, there exists a constant $C=C(X,S,T)>0$ such that the observability inequality holds. Furthermore, denoted by $=(S)$, then the constant $C$ in can be chosen such that it depends only on $M,$ and $T$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Removing the type I restriction to obtain observability on general covers would extend semiclassical control and Bloch analysis to noncompact hyperbolic settings, addressing deep representation-theoretic and microlocal obstacles.
viability_rationale: Uniform semiclassical control is proved; remaining obstacles are representation-theoretic (generalized Bloch transform) and removing low-frequency errors for non-type I groups. Recent progress exists but technical gaps remain—moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: Authors propose this extension as a conjecture after proving a restricted (type I) case; they do not prove the general statement in the paper.
evidence_snippet: “By Theorem ... the observability inequality ... holds only for the type I group covering. Therefore, it is reasonable to propose the following conjecture:” (lines 394-396)

title: On the levels of rational regular orthogonal matrices for generalized cospectral graphs
body_tex: Let $G$ be a controllable graph and $p$ be an odd prime factor of $\det W(G)$. Let $d_{n-1}$ and $d_n$ be the last two invariant factors of $W(G)$. Then $v_p(L(G))\le \frac{1}{2}( v_p(d_{n})+v_p(d_{n-1}))$, and in particular,  $v_p(L(G))\le \frac{1}{2} v_p(\det W(G))$.
plain_text: Let $G$ be a controllable graph and $p$ be an odd prime factor of $ W(G)$. Let $d_ n-1 $ and $d_n$ be the last two invariant factors of $W(G)$. Then $v_p(L(G)) <= 1 2 ( v_p(d_ n )+v_p(d_ n-1 ))$, and in particular, $v_p(L(G)) <= 1 2 v_p( W(G))$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Refines p-adic valuation bounds for levels of rational regular orthogonal matrices using the last two invariant factors; technically useful for bounding generalized cospectral mates but largely specialized to spectral graph theory.
viability_rationale: Proof exists under a rank_p hypothesis improving prior work; removing that technical hypothesis and refining to last two invariant factors likely reachable by extending current methods, though p-adic/invariant-factor technicalities create moderate uncertainty.
rationale: The conjecture is posed at the paper's end as an open problem refining their results; the paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: “We end this paper by proposing a conjecture that the technical assumption on \rank_p W(G) ... Moreover, the upper bound ...” followed by the conjecture (lines 449-452).

title: The strong fractional choice number of triangle-free planar graphs
body_tex:
\label{conj-planar}
		There exists a constant integer $m$ such that every planar graph is $(5m-1,m)$-choosable.
plain_text: There exists a constant integer $m$ such that every planar graph is $(5m-1,m)$-choosable.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A prominent choosability conjecture in graph coloring: proving (5m-1,m)-choosability for all planar graphs would lower the strong fractional choice number below 5 and impact fundamental list-coloring theory.
viability_rationale: Open since 2017 with only Voigt's (5m,m) positive result and incremental negative lower bounds; key techniques fail for list coloring and no concrete finite reduction is evident, so near-term resolution seems unlikely.
rationale: The conjecture (Zhu 2017) is cited as an open problem; the paper proves related triangle-free results but does not resolve the planar conjecture.
evidence_snippet: “It remains unknown if ch^s_f(P) < 5, this would follow if the following conjecture holds: ... There exists a constant integer m such that every planar graph is (5m-1,m)-choosable.” (lines 189-193)

title: Steady-State Behavior of Constant-Stepsize Stochastic Approximation: Gaussian Approximation and Tail Bounds
body_tex:
\label{conj1:generalSGD}
Under Assumption \ref{A5} and \ref{A6}, there exists some $a > 0$ such that for all stepsize $\alpha \in (0,a)$, the sequence of random variables $\{X_k^{(\alpha)}\}_{k \geq 0}$ generated by the SGD iteration \eqref{SA2} converges in distribution to a random variable $X^{(\alpha)}$ as $k \to \infty$. Furthermore, there exists a constant $ C_{h} > 0$ such that $\sup_{\alpha \in(0,a)} \mathbb{E}|X^{(\alpha)} - x^*|^{3h} \leq C_{h}$.

Furthermore, there exists a constant $ C_ h > 0$ such that $_ in (0,a) E|X^ () - x^*|^ 3h <= C_ h $.

interestingness_rationale: An all-genus geometric definition equating open r‑spin potentials to integrable-hierarchy data; resolving would establish deep links between open Gromov–Witten theory and r‑KdV/integrable systems.

rationale: The paper states the g\ge2 formula as a conjecture; only g=0,1 are proved earlier, so the all‑genus g\ge2 statement remains open here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2884-2887: "Also for g\geq2 there exists a geometric definition... such that F^{x^r,o}_g = ..." (presented as Conjecture [BCT3])

title: Khovanov concordance minima and the (4,5) torus knot
body_tex:
\label{conj:globalsliceribbon}
Each concordance class has a global minimum with respect to $\leq$.
plain_text: Each concordance class has a global minimum with respect to $ <= $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A strong generalization asserting every concordance class has a global minimum; proving it would profoundly affect the structure of the ribbon partial order and concordance classification.
viability_rationale: This generalizes the long‑standing slice–ribbon conjecture; no concrete reduction or imminent attack is given. Extremely unlikely the full statement will be resolved within five years.
rationale: The stronger statement (every concordance class has a global minimum) is posed as a conjecture in the paper and is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 216-220: "Extending this to other concordance classes gives a stronger conjecture: Each concordance class has a global minimum with respect to \le."

title: Plethysm and orbit harmonics
plain_text: For positive integers $a >= b$, we have that \[(_ (b^a) ) subseteq (_ (a^b) ).\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A direct, stronger algebraic formulation implying Foulkes' conjecture (via module surjection); solving it would settle a major plethysm question in algebraic combinatorics and representation theory.
viability_rationale: Stronger form of Foulkes' conjecture (longstanding open problem). Only special cases (b=2) are proved here; no concrete finite reduction or imminent route is given, so full resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: The author explicitly poses this containment conjecture related to Foulkes' conjecture and only proves the special case b=2; the general statement remains unproved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 389-394: 'For positive integers a\ge b, we have that gr I(Π_{(b^a)}) \subseteq gr I(Π_{(a^b)}).' 'Currently, we can only solve the special case b=2.'

title: Plethysm and orbit harmonics
body_tex:
\label{conj:log-concavity}
    For integers $n>0$, both $\CC\Big[\xxxgraph{n}\Big]\Big/I(n)$ and $\CC\Big[\xxxgraph{n}\Big]\Big/J(n)$ are $\symm_n$-log-concave.
plain_text: For integers $n>0$, both $/I(n)$ and $/J(n)$ are $_n$-log-concave.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: An equivariant (S_n) log-concavity assertion for specific graded modules with computational support up to n<=20; interesting within algebraic combinatorics but narrower in scope and impact.
viability_rationale: Verified by computation for n≤20; problem is specific and admits concrete combinatorial/representation approaches, but requires a nontrivial global injection/structural proof—moderate chance of resolution in five years.
rationale: The paper formulates S_n‑log‑concavity for the listed quotients as an open conjecture, verified computationally for small n but not proved in general.
evidence_snippet: Lines 875-878: 'Conjecture: For integers n>0, both C[x_{(n)}]/I(n) and C[x_{(n)}]/J(n) are S_n‑log‑concave.' Verified by code for n\le20 only.

title: Plethysm and orbit harmonics
body_tex:
\label{conj:general-log-concavity}
    For $n\ge 9$, $R(\Pi_{n,m})$ is $\symm_n$-log-concave.
plain_text: For $n >= 9$, $R(_ n,m )$ is $_n$-log-concave.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Equivariant S_n-log-concavity is a meaningful structural property in algebraic combinatorics; computational evidence exists but the scope is mainly specialist and not obviously transformative beyond the subfield.
viability_rationale: Computer verification up to n≤16, failure only at n=7,8, and explicit module/monomial-basis structure provide concrete tools; remaining gap seems technical and plausibly closable within five years.
rationale: The paper states this as an explicit conjecture (verified by computer up to n≤16) but does not prove it; it's posed as an open problem in the conclusion.
evidence_snippet: Lines 875–883: authors verified symmetry-log-concavity for 9≤n≤16 by coding and state: 'For n≥9, R(Π_{n,m}) is S_n-log-concave.'

title: Knot contact homology as a planar limit of Chern-Simons theory
body_tex:
\label{conj:ev-injective}
The map $\ev$ is injective and $\Iq{L}$ is equal to the annihilator $\Iqp{L}$ of $\ev([L])$.
plain_text: The map $$ is injective and $L$ is equal to the annihilator $L$ of $([L])$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Injectivity of ev and identification of the recursion ideal would tightly connect HOMFLYPT recursions to evaluated invariants, yielding significant structural control in quantum/topological knot theory.
viability_rationale: Statement reduces to injectivity/annihilator properties of a quantum-torus module; partial holonomicity known but module-structure obstacles remain, making short-term resolution uncertain.
rationale: The paper formulates injectivity of ev and equality of ideals as Conjecture 1680–1682 and does not prove it; it remains an open assertion in the text.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1672–1682: definition of I_q(L) and ev followed by Conjecture: 'The map ev is injective and I_q(L) is equal to the annihilator I_q'(L) of ev([L]).'

title: Knot contact homology as a planar limit of Chern-Simons theory
plain_text: We have equalities $L=L$ and $I(L) = Au_L$ for all links $L$. That is, the augmentation ideal $Au_L$ is the classical limit of the ideal defining the recursion relations on antisymmetric HOMFLYPT polynomials. In particular, the vanishing set of the augmentation ideal $Au_L$ is Lagrangian in $(^ x )^ 2r $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Identifying the augmentation ideal as the classical limit of HOMFLYPT recursion ideals and yielding a Lagrangian augmentation variety is a major bridge between quantum invariants and symplectic/knot-contact geometry.
viability_rationale: Identifying augmentation ideal as classical limit is deep and relies on unresolved structural module facts; authors flag key obstacles, so a five-year resolution appears unlikely.
rationale: The paper explicitly states equality of the q-ideal, its classical limit, and Lagrangian property of augmentation variety as a conjecture (lines 2468–2472) and does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2468–2472: 'We have equalities I_q(L)=I_q'(L) and I(L)=Au_L for all links L... In particular, the vanishing set of the augmentation ideal Au_L is Lagrangian.'

title: Maximum capacity of Bartnik data and a generalization of static metrics
body_tex: Let $(M,g)$ be a maximal capacity extension of $(\Sigma, \gamma, H)$. Then $M$ has no minimal surfaces in its interior that enclose $\partial M$, and $\partial M$ is outward-minimizing.
plain_text: Let $(M,g)$ be a maximal capacity extension of $(, , H)$. Then $M$ has no minimal surfaces in its interior that enclose $ M$, and $ M$ is outward-minimizing.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A geometric regularity statement about maximal-capacity extensions is a natural, impactful conjecture in quasi-local GR/Bartnik-type problems, but its consequences are mainly within that specialist area.
viability_rationale: A concrete geometric-analytic claim with variational/minimal-surface flavor; analogous Bartnik progress exists, but technical PDE/geometry hurdles make a quick resolution only moderately likely.
rationale: Presented in the Questions and conjectures section as an open conjecture (lines 687–690); the paper offers intuition but no proof or counterexample.
evidence_snippet: Lines 687–690: 'Let (M,g) be a maximal capacity extension... Then M has no minimal surfaces in its interior that enclose ∂M, and ∂M is outward-minimizing.'

title: Half-plane non-coexistence without FKG
body_tex: Let $k \in \Z_{\geq 1}$.  Let $\mu$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic, finite-energy, $k$-dependent edge percolation measure on $\Z^2$.  Then the trichotomy of Theorem~\ref{thm:main} holds in the full plane.
plain_text: Let $k in _ >= 1 $. Let $$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic, finite-energy, $k$-dependent edge percolation measure on $^2$. Then the trichotomy of Theorem holds in the full plane.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends half-plane non-coexistence to k-dependent finite-energy translation-invariant measures in the full plane; important in percolation theory but primarily a specialist strengthening contingent on ruling out pathological long-range constructions.
viability_rationale: Full-plane extensions face known obstructions (spiral-like examples); partial domination/uniqueness results exist but new ideas appear necessary, so modest near-term solvability likelihood.
rationale: The authors explicitly state this k-dependent full-plane trichotomy as a conjecture in the concluding remarks and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: “Let k∈Z_{≥1}. Let μ be a translation-invariant, ergodic, finite-energy, k-dependent edge percolation measure on Z^2. Then the trichotomy of Theorem 1 holds in the full plane.”

title: Half-plane non-coexistence without FKG
body_tex: Let $\mu$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic percolation measure which almost surely has infinitely many infinite clusters. Then almost surely both $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu_{\hp}^*$ have infinitely many infinite clusters.
plain_text: Let $$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic percolation measure which almost surely has infinitely many infinite clusters. Then almost surely both $_ $ and $_ ^*$ have infinitely many infinite clusters.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts that infinite multiplicity of infinite clusters persists under half-plane marginalization; a natural structural claim in planar percolation with moderate impact, mainly of interest within probability theory.
viability_rationale: Plausible from topology/combinatorics and examples; authors provide relevant lemmas. Still could be delicate, but specialized arguments may yield a resolution within five years.
rationale: The paper conjectures that measures with infinitely many infinite clusters imply infinitely many half-plane clusters; this is posed as an open question and not proved here.
evidence_snippet: “Let μ be a translation-invariant, ergodic percolation measure which almost surely has infinitely many infinite clusters. Then almost surely both μ_{hp} and μ_{hp}^* have infinitely many infinite clusters.”

title: Twisted Pollicott--Ruelle resonances and zeta function at zero on surfaces
plain_text: Let $$ be a genus $G >= 2$ closed surface. There exists a negatively curved metric $g$ on $$ and a finite dimensional representation $ in Hom(_1(), GL_r()) $ for which $m(g,) != (2G-2)r$. If $$ is acyclic, i.e., $H^*(M,)=0$, then we always have $m(g,)=0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Specialist dynamical-systems conjecture about deviations from the generic resonant-dimension formula and Jordan-block phenomena; resolving it clarifies twisted Pollicott--Ruelle resonance structure and Fried-type relations on surfaces.
viability_rationale: Paper makes substantial progress on surfaces with explicit constructions and analytic tools; Jordan-block phenomena remain delicate, so a decisive resolution is plausible but not imminent within five years.
rationale: The authors explicitly state this as an open conjecture in the 'Further questions' section and do not prove or disprove either item in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 231-239: 'We are not aware... We make the following conjecture.' followed by the two-part conjecture about m(g,ρ).

title: Contention Resolution, With and Without a Global Clock
body_tex:
\label{conj:global-clock}
Consider the class of randomized acknowledgment-based protocols in the \GlobalClock{} model.
\begin{enumerate}
\item In the finite setting, there exists a 
        protocol with latency $O(n)$ with high probability against an adaptive adversary.
 
\item In the unbounded setting, there exists a 
protocol that utilizes the shared resource at some rate $\lambda$,\footnote{Here we have in mind the definition of utilization implicit in the analysis of~\cite{ChangJP19}.  Utilization $\lambda$ is achieved if (1) there is a potential function $\Phi$ such that waking up $n'$ parties increases $\Phi$ 
by at most $n'/\lambda$,  
(2) if $\Phi = \Omega(1)$ is sufficiently large, executing the protocol for another time step decreases $\Phi$ 
by $1+\epsilon$ in expectation, $\epsilon>0$, 
and (3) if $\Phi=O(1)$ is bounded, 
the number of 
still-unsuccessful parties left in the system is also $O(1)$.
This definition does not constrain the adversary, e.g., 
by limiting the wake-up rate to 
$\lambda$ over certain windows 
of time~\cite{BenderFHKL05}.} 
even against an adaptive adversary.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Consider the class of randomized acknowledgment-based protocols in the model. In the finite setting, there exists a protocol with latency $O(n)$ with high probability against an adaptive adversary. In the unbounded setting, there exists a protocol that utilizes the shared resource at some rate $$,Here we have in mind the definition of utilization implicit in the analysis of . Utilization $$ is achieved if (1) there is a potential function $$ such that waking up $n'$ parties increases $$ by at most $n'/$, (2) if $ = (1)$ is sufficiently large, executing the protocol for another time step decreases $$ by $1+$ in expectation, $>0$, and (3) if $=O(1)$ is bounded, the number of still-unsuccessful parties left in the system is also $O(1)$. This definition does not constrain the adversary, e.g., by limiting the wake-up rate to $$ over certain windows of time . even against an adaptive adversary.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Algorithmic conjecture predicting linear latency and nonzero utilization in a realistic GlobalClock model; resolving it would significantly advance contention-resolution theory and practical distributed protocols.
viability_rationale: Authors achieved near-linear bounds and propose concrete algorithmic improvements; no known impossibility and active community make an O(n) global-clock protocol plausibly achievable within five years.
rationale: The paper poses this as a conjecture (GlobalClock linear latency and utilization) after presenting partial results; it is not proved here and remains an open target.
evidence_snippet: Lines 339-361: authors state Conjecture 3.1 proposing O(n) latency in GlobalClock and utilization in the unbounded setting.

title: On plane rigidity matroids
body_tex: There are only finitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a symmetric abstract $2$-rigidity matroid $\M_n\neq \R_n$ in which $K_{3,3}$ is independent.
plain_text: There are only finitely many values of $n$ for which there exists a symmetric abstract $2$-rigidity matroid $_n != _n$ in which $K_ 3,3 $ is independent.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classifies occurrence of exotic symmetric 2‑rigidity matroids with K3,3 independent; resolves whether nonstandard examples persist for large n, impacting structural classification in planar rigidity/matroid theory.
viability_rationale: Concrete, finite-n examples known (n=6), checked for n=7; methods in paper nearly reduce possibilities. Problem is narrowly scoped and likely attackable with further combinatorial/matroid techniques.
rationale: The paper states this as an open conjecture (Section Discussion) and provides no proof or counterexample; only small n were checked.
evidence_snippet: “We are wondering if this distinction can occur for arbitrarily large n.” followed by: “There are only finitely many values of n for which there exists a symmetric abstract 2-rigidity matroid M_n ≠ R_n in which K_{3,3} is independent.” (lines 681–684)

title: Improved Universal Graphs for Trees
body_tex: The $\lim_{n\to \infty} \frac{s(n)}{n\ln n}$ exists.
plain_text: The $_ n to s(n) n n $ exists.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asymptotic existence of s(n)/(n ln n) fixes the leading constant for minimal edges in tree-universal graphs, answering a natural Chung–Graham asymptotic question with clear combinatorial significance.
viability_rationale: Authors tightened bounds substantially; existence of limit is natural and may follow from refined asymptotic embedding/degree analyses, but subtle oscillations could complicate a proof.
rationale: Authors pose existence of the limit lim_{n→∞} s(n)/(n ln n) as an explicit conjecture and only provide bounds, not a proof of the limit.
evidence_snippet: “The improved upper bound is one further step... A starting point could be to show the following statement... The limit exists.” “The lim_{n→∞} s(n)/(n ln n) exists.” (lines 99–103)

title: Residually finite groups that do not virtually have the unique product property
body_tex: Let $H$ be a finitely generated residually finite group. Then there exists a finitely generated residually finite group $G$ that persistently contains $H$.
plain_text: Let $H$ be a finitely generated residually finite group. Then there exists a finitely generated residually finite group $G$ that persistently contains $H$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A positive resolution gives a strong persistence construction in residually finite groups, with broad consequences for unique product/virtual diffuseness questions and subgroup embedding phenomena in geometric group theory.
viability_rationale: Broad, strong embedding statement in residually finite group theory; seems ambitious and likely requires major new constructions or counterexamples, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: Paper constructs a residually finite G containing Promislow's group but poses the general persistence-containment statement as an open conjecture without proof.
evidence_snippet: “We are curious... Perhaps the following holds: Let H be a finitely generated residually finite group. Then there exists a finitely generated residually finite group G that persistently contains H.” (lines 164–168)

title: A Note on the Complexity of Directed Clique
body_tex:
\label{con:main}
    \Ptourclique is \SPTWO-complete.
plain_text: is -complete.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classifying Ptourclique as Σ2^P-complete would settle the tournament directed-clique complexity and ties hardness to constructions of tournaments with large directed clique number, impacting complexity-theoretic understanding of ordering problems.
viability_rationale: Pdirclique is settled, but tournament case needs explicit tournaments with large directed clique number; constructing such examples or new reductions seems plausible but nontrivial within five years.
rationale: The authors explicitly conjecture that Ptourclique is Σ2^P-complete and do not prove it; they instead prove the directed-graph variant is Σ2^P-complete.
evidence_snippet: “We make the following conjecture... Ptourclique is Σ2^P-complete.” (lines 202–205). They note obstacles and then prove Pdirclique is Σ2^P-complete (lines 220–225).

title: A Note on the Complexity of Directed Clique
body_tex:
\label{con:lower_bound}
    There is an explicit construction of tournaments $T_1,T_2,\ldots$ such that $\norm{V(T_n)} = \Theta(n)$ and $\diomega(T_n) = \Omega(n^{1/100})$.
plain_text: There is an explicit construction of tournaments $T_1,T_2,$ such that $V(T_n) = (n)$ and $(T_n) = (n^ 1/100 )$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A polynomial lower bound on tournament directed clique number would bridge combinatorics and complexity, likely settling Ptourclique Σ2^P-hardness; significant within graph theory and complexity, not a mere technical extension.
viability_rationale: Only logarithmic lower bounds are known and authors report no constructions beyond log; large gap to √n upper bound and no concrete route presented, making a polynomial lower-bound construction unlikely within five years.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as an open conjecture (Conjecture 2) and does not prove or refute it; authors note lack of constructions and view it as unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 215–218: "We conjecture... There is an explicit construction of tournaments T_n ... with |V(T_n)| = Θ(n) and diomega(T_n) = Ω(n^{1/100})."

title: The Topology of $k$-Robust Clique Complexes in Grid-like Graphs
body_tex: For all $k\geq2,n\geq 1$, the $k$-robust clique complex $\Cliq{k}{G}$ of graph $G$ with a square sequence $C_4 \cong H_1 \subset  H_2 \subset \cdots \subset H_n \cong G$ is homotopy equivalent to a wedge sum of ($2k-3$)-dimensional spheres. Further, the number of spheres  is determined by a recurrence relation based on the homotopy type of $\Cliq{k}{H_{n-1}}$ and the specific attachment of the $n$-th square.
plain_text: For all $k2,n >= 1$, the $k$-robust clique complex $k G $ of graph $G$ with a square sequence $C_4 H_1 subset H_2 subset subset H_n G$ is homotopy equivalent to a wedge sum of ($2k-3$)-dimensional spheres. Further, the number of spheres is determined by a recurrence relation based on the homotopy type of $k H_ n-1 $ and the specific attachment of the $n$-th square.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extension of proven k=2,3 cases; would give a clean topological classification for a broad class of graphs, but remains a specialist structural conjecture with technical obstacles.
viability_rationale: Cases k=2,3 are proved and Alexander duality plus an inductive framework exist; remaining obstacles are technical (recursive step, corner gluing), suggesting plausible resolution with focused work.
rationale: The author explicitly poses this generalization as a conjecture and discusses technical obstacles, without proving the full statement for k>3.
evidence_snippet: Lines 234–237: "We propose the following generalization... For all k≥2,n≥1, the k-robust clique complex ... is homotopy equivalent to a wedge sum of (2k-3)-dimensional spheres."

title: On the excursion algebra
body_tex:
\label{conj:rat}
The action of $\on{Exc}(X,\sG)$ on $\on{Autom}$ is compatible with the rational structures.
plain_text: The action of $Exc(X,)$ on $Autom$ is compatible with the rational structures.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural arithmetic-Langlands assertion: extending rational-structure compatibility of the excursion algebra action from GL_n to arbitrary G clarifies deep arithmeticity properties, but is specialist rather than a field-wide flagship problem.
viability_rationale: Only proven for GL_n here; authors state no approach for arbitrary sG and proof methods are indirect (Drinfeld/comparison). Deep geometric Langlands obstacles remain, so near-term resolution unlikely.
rationale: The paper explicitly states the conjecture for general \sG, notes they do not know how to approach arbitrary \sG, and only proves the statement for \sG = GL_n, so the general claim remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 5182–5186 state the conjecture; lines 5188–5191 say they do not know how to approach arbitrary \sG; theorem at 5197–5199 proves only the GL_n case.

title: The Base Change Of Fundamental Group Schemes

Suppose for any $E in C_X$, $E_k K in C_ X_K $, then the following conditions are equivalent: For any $E in C_ X_K $, $p_*E in C_X$. The natural homomorphism $(C_ X_K ,x_K) (C_X,x)_K$ is an isomorphism.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical equivalence for base change under degree-p purely inseparable extensions; meaningful for specialists studying Tannakian fundamental group schemes in positive characteristic but relatively narrow in scope.
viability_rationale: Reduced to finite purely inseparable degree-p cases and built on existing Tannakian/finite-separable results; technical but accessible to specialists, making a near-term resolution plausible though nontrivial.
rationale: The authors present this equivalence as a conjecture in its own section and remark they do not know whether base change under finite purely inseparable extensions holds; no proof is given.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1653–1661 introduce the conjecture about purely inseparable extensions of degree p and state the equivalence; preceding text (1652–1653) explains the question remains unresolved.

title: Rational Points in Hyperbolic Regions and Multiplicative Diophantine Approximation on Manifolds
body_tex:
\label{conj: rel non-deg}
Let $\cL\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be an affine subspace satisfying Hypothesis D. Let $\cM\subset\cL$ be a smooth manifold which is non-degenerate when viewed as a manifold in $\mathbb{R}^{\dim \cL}\simeq \cL.$ Then the induced Lebesgue measure on $\cM$ is convergent Gallagher.

interestingness_rationale: Posits inheritance of the multiplicative convergent Gallagher property from Diophantine affine subspaces to non-degenerate submanifolds; significant within Diophantine approximation, linking affine and curved settings with broad implications.
viability_rationale: Authors have broad results and robust methods; conjectured inheritance from affine subspaces seems approachable with existing fibring and Diophantine techniques, making near-term progress likely.
rationale: The paper proves the flat subspace (affine L) case but poses the inheritance conjecture for non-degenerate manifolds inside L as an open problem; no proof is provided.
evidence_snippet: Lines 827–832 prove the affine subspace case; lines 841–843 state Conjecture (rel non-deg) for manifolds inside L as an open problem.

title: On the hyperreal dual Steenrod algebra

title: Golombic and Levine sequences
plain_text: Let $w$ be a homogeneous word. Then substate the golombic sequence $ w$ is either bounded or $_ n to _ n+1 w _nw\, * \,_ n-1 w = -1$, the Levine sequence $ w$ is either bounded or $_ n to _ n+1 w _nw\, * \,_ n-1 w = $. substate

interestingness_rationale: Generalises Mallows' hypothesis by predicting universal asymptotic ratios (φ−1 and Mallows' κ) for broad classes of golombic/Levine sequences; meaningful in combinatorial sequence asymptotics though limited outside the area.

title: Golombic and Levine sequences

title: Combinatorial structure of low degree rational curves on a smooth Hermitian surface
body_tex:

If $O={\rm Aut}(X_I)C_{F_J}$ then
                                       
             
                                                           
                                                                      
                                                                                                   
       
$$d=|\mathbb{P}^1(\mathbb{F}_{q^2})|\ {for}\ {all}\ q,$$ 
                               
that is, $\left(O,\{R_i\}_{i=0}^d\right)$ is of $d=(q^2+1)$-classes.

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts the association scheme arising from curve intersections on a Hermitian surface has q^2+1 classes; a natural counting/structural conjecture with combinatorial interest but specialized to this geometry.
viability_rationale: Observed in examples; resolving requires detailed intersection-multiplicity analysis on Hermitian surfaces—technical but amenable to finite-field geometry methods, giving moderate near-term chance.

where $a_i+a_{i+2}\geq a_{i+1}$ for every $i$, almost surely $\sum \beta_i=|\chi|$.\\

viability_rationale: Authors proved length 1–3 cases and equal-dimension case; conjecture is a natural extension. Progress suggests plausible methods can extend, but general n has nontrivial combinatorial obstacles, so moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture for arbitrary length and only proves special cases (length 1,2, some length 3, equal-dimension). It is not proved here.

title: A non-semisimple Witt class
body_tex:
\label{g2conj}
    Assume $l\ge 7$ and not divisible by 3.

    (1) We have
    $$\FP(\C(G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q))=6\frac{l^4}{(2\sin(\pi/l))^8(2\sin(2\pi/l))^2},$$
    and 
    $$\FP(T(\omega_1))=2[3]_l+1,\; \FP(T(\omega_2)=[5]_l+3[3]_l.$$

    (2) The category $\C(G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q)$ has stable Chevalley property.

    (3) The M\"uger center of the category $\C(G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q)$ is equivalent
    to $\Rep(S_3)$. The projective covers of the simple objects from $\Rep(S_3)$ are
    in the principal block; the highest weights of the corresponding tilting modules
    are in the alcoves $C_0, C_4, C_7$ (see Figure \ref{fig:subreg_cell_G2}).
plain_text: Assume $l >= 7$ and not divisible by 3. (1) We have $$((G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q))=6l^4 (2(/l))^8(2(2/l))^2 ,$$ and $$(T(_1))=2[3]_l+1,\; (T(_2)=[5]_l+3[3]_l.$$ (2) The category $(G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q)$ has stable Chevalley property. (3) The M\"uger center of the category $(G_2, G_2(a_1),l,q)$ is equivalent to $(S_3)$. The projective covers of the simple objects from $(S_3)$ are in the principal block; the highest weights of the corresponding tilting modules are in the alcoves $C_0, C_4, C_7$ (see Figure ).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Gives explicit Frobenius–Perron dimensions and structural properties for a family of non-semisimple G2 braided categories—central for classification and the Witt-group non-semisimple phenomena.
viability_rationale: Checked for l=7,11 in paper; finite-parameter, representation-theoretic structure suggests existing methods can likely be extended. Nontrivial technical work remains but community expertise makes a 5-year resolution plausible.
rationale: Conjecture g2conj (lines 1991–1997) is explicitly stated for general l≥7 undivisible by 3. The authors verified specific l (7,11) but did not prove the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1991–1997 and 2006–2009: Conjecture g2conj stated; authors checked it for l=7,11 but not proven in general.

title: A non-semisimple Witt class

\label{g2conjm}

and for $l\ge 15$
$$\FP(T(\omega_1))=[3]_l+[5]_l-1,\; \FP(T(\omega_2))=2[7]_l-[5]_l+[3]_l+2.$$

title: Hunting for Directed 2-Spiders
body_tex:

interestingness_rationale: A natural, tight extremal conjecture in directed graph theory about unavoidable (k,ℓ)-spiders; resolving it would clarify fundamental degree thresholds and structural embedding phenomena in directed graphs.
viability_rationale: k=2 case was just proved here; methods may extend to small k, but the full general k≥2 statement presents nontrivial combinatorial obstacles—moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The paper states the Giant Spider Conjecture for all k≥2 and explicitly says it is still open; the paper only proves the k=2 case, not the full conjecture.

viability_rationale: The PPT-squared conjecture is a long-standing, well-known open problem resolved only in low dimensions; many partial restricted results but no clear general-route to full resolution.

title: Solvability of meromorphic equations in elementary functions
body_tex:
\label{conj:any-solvable-tower-core}
    Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{C}$ be a connected open set and $f: X \to \mathbb{C}$ be a meromorphic elementary function. If the equation $f(x)=a$ is solvable in elementary functions, then there exists a finite sequence $g_1,g_2,\ldots,g_\ell$ of solvable functions of depth at most $1$, such that $f(x)=(g_1\circ g_2\circ\ldots\circ g_\ell)(x)\text{.}$
plain_text: Let $X subseteq C$ be a connected open set and $f: X to C$ be a meromorphic elementary function. If the equation $f(x)=a$ is solvable in elementary functions, then there exists a finite sequence $g_1,g_2,,g_ ell $ of solvable functions of depth at most $1$, such that $f(x)=(g_1 g_2 g_ ell )(x).$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Claims any elementarily solvable meromorphic inverse decomposes into depth-≤1 solvable factors—would give a useful structural classification in topological Galois/solvability theory beyond isolated examples.
viability_rationale: Authors give monodromy reductions and evidence, but completing a full structural decomposition requires classifying primitive solvable factors; nontrivial with no explicit closing route—plausible but not imminent.
rationale: The statement is presented as a concluding hypothesis based on modest computational evidence; the paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 572–577: “We conclude with the following hypothesis, based on modest computational evidence.” followed by the conjectural decomposition statement (lines 574–576).

title: Mock modularity of log Gromov--Witten Invariants: the mirror to $\mathbb{P}^2$
body_tex:
Let $\phi: Y \to B$ be an elliptic fibration and $D\subset Y$ a normal crossing divisor such that $\phi(D)$ is a divisor in $B$. Assume that the generic fiber of the restricted morphism $\phi|_{D} \colon D \to \phi(D)$ is either a cycle of $\PP^1$'s or a nodal elliptic curve. Then the generating series of logarithmic Gromov–Witten invariants of $(Y,D)$, 
\[ \sum_{d \geq 0} GW^{(Y,D)}_{g,v,\beta_0+dF} q^d \,, \]
where $v$ is a fixed contact order along $D$, $\beta_0 \in H_2(Y,\ZZ)$ is a fixed curve class, and $F$ denotes the fiber class, is a component of a (higher depth) vector-valued mock modular form, up to appropriate normalization in the power of $q$.
plain_text: Let $: Y to B$ be an elliptic fibration and $D subset Y$ a normal crossing divisor such that $(D)$ is a divisor in $B$. Assume that the generic fiber of the restricted morphism $|_ D D to (D)$ is either a cycle of $^1$'s or a nodal elliptic curve. Then the generating series of logarithmic Gromov–Witten invariants of $(Y,D)$, \[ _ d >= 0 GW^ (Y,D) _ g,v,_0+dF q^d \,, \] where $v$ is a fixed contact order along $D$, $_0 in H_2(Y,)$ is a fixed curve class, and $F$ denotes the fiber class, is a component of a (higher depth) vector-valued mock modular form, up to appropriate normalization in the power of $q$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Broad structural conjecture linking log Gromov–Witten invariants of elliptic fibrations to higher-depth vector-valued mock modularity; proven in a key example but its general truth would strongly impact enumerative geometry and connections to S-duality.
viability_rationale: Very general conjecture across elliptic fibrations; only special mirror cases proved. Deep links to Vafa–Witten/S-duality and mock modularity remain conceptual and technical, so full resolution within 5 years is possible but not likely.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this general mock-modularity conjecture (lines 180–184) but only proves a special case (mirror to P^2). The full general statement remains unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (lines 180–184) states series are vector-valued mock modular forms. The paper then notes Conjecture \ref{conj: mirror P2} is a special case and proves that special case (line 186).

title: Mock modularity of log Gromov--Witten Invariants: the mirror to $\mathbb{P}^2$
plain_text: Let $(Y,D)$ be the rational elliptic surface mirror to $^2$ as in . For any $r in _ > 0 $, the vector $(h^ GW _ r,c_1 ())_ -r< c_1 <= 0 $, with components the log Gromov--Witten generating series of $(Y,D)$ given in , is a vector-valued mock modular form of weight $-3 2 $ and depth $r$.

viability_rationale: Special mirror-to-P2 case reduced to Vafa–Witten modularity; proven for r≤3. Concrete reduction raises plausibility of extending to higher r, but significant new input on VW mock modularity is required.
rationale: The conjecture asserts mock modularity for all ranks r>0 on the mirror to P^2. The paper proves this only for 1 ≤ r ≤ 3 (Corollary), leaving the general r>3 case open.
evidence_snippet: Corollary (lines 553–556) proves the statement for 1 ≤ r ≤ 3; the full conjecture for all r>0 is then stated (lines 561–565) but not proved.
