title: Erdős--Turán Theorem and Eulerian Integers
body_tex:
\lb{conj01}
If $f\in\mathbb Z[x,y]$ is a two-variable polynomial that is not decomposable as $f(x,y)=g(x)h(y)$,
where $g,h\in\mathbb Z[x]$, then there exists a constant $c$ that depends only on the polynomial $f$, such that if
$\mc A,\ \mc B\subseteq\mathbb Z^+$ are finite sets and
$|\mc{A}|\geq|\mc{B}|\geq 2$, then
\[
\omega_{\mathbb N}(\prod_{a\in{\mc A},b\in{\mc B}}(f(a,b)))\geq{c\log|\mc A|}.
\]
plain_text: conj01 If $f in Z[x,y]$ is a two-variable polynomial that is not decomposable as $f(x,y)=g(x)h(y)$, where $g,h in Z[x]$, then there exists a constant $c$ that depends only on the polynomial $f$, such that if $ A,\ B subseteq Z^+$ are finite sets and $|A| >= |B| >= 2$, then \[ _ N (_ a in A ,b in B (f(a,b))) >= c| A| . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad Erdős–Turán-type lower bound for arbitrary nondecomposable bivariate polynomials would meaningfully generalize multiplicative prime-divisor results, but its impact is mostly within analytic/diophantine number theory.
viability_rationale: Authors prove a significant special case via Győry–Sárközy–Stewart and Vandermonde methods, but the conjecture’s full generality over all indecomposable f is broad and likely needs new deep input; moderate near-term solvability.
rationale: The authors state the general polynomial Erdős–Turán-type conjecture and explicitly note they could not prove it, only handling a special case (Theorem 3).
evidence_snippet: Lines 214-221: authors 'conjecture the following'. Lines 229-236: they state they could not prove full generality but proved a special-case theorem.

title: Fundamental groups of compact Kahler varieties with nef anti canonical bundle
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
Let $X$ be a compact normal K{\"a}hler variety, assume that there is an effective divisor $\Delta$ on $X$ such that $(X,\Delta)$ is a log canonical (lc) pair and the anti-log canonical divisor $-(K_X+\Delta)$ is nef. Then the fundamental group $\pi_1(X_{\reg})$ is almost Abelian.
plain_text: Let $X$ be a compact normal K \"a hler variety, assume that there is an effective divisor $$ on $X$ such that $(X,)$ is a log canonical (lc) pair and the anti-log canonical divisor $-(K_X+)$ is nef. Then the fundamental group $_1(X_ )$ is almost Abelian.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad extension of Păun's theorem to lc singular Kähler varieties; resolving it would significantly constrain fundamental groups and ties into Albanese surjectivity and major conjectures in complex geometry.
viability_rationale: Strong partial results and new analytic tools exist, but the general lc singular case ties into deep, long-standing structural problems; broad scope limits near-term solvability.
rationale: The authors explicitly present this statement as a conjecture for general lc compact normal Kähler varieties; the paper proves only special cases, not the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 266-269: "\begin{conjecture} ... Then the fundamental group \pi_1(X_{\reg}) is almost Abelian." Lines 271-275 note special cases are settled but the general case remains open.

title: Fundamental groups of compact Kahler varieties with nef anti canonical bundle

Suppose $$ is a Kahler current with $L^p$ volume measure for some $p>1$, the Ricci current of $$ is bounded below by $-$. $$ is smooth on a Zariski open subset $X^$ of $X$. Then the metric completion of the metric measure space $(X^, , ^n)$ is a non-collapsed RCD space homeomorphic to $X$.

title: The complexity of pinning simple multiloops
body_tex:
\label{conj:4-hard}
For a fixed orientable surface $\Sigma$, the problem $\mathsf{SimplePin}(\Sigma,4)$ is \textsf{NP}-complete.
plain_text: For a fixed orientable surface $$, the problem $SimplePin(,4)$ is NP-complete.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Pinpointing the exact strand threshold (s=4) for NP-completeness is a natural, central question linking surface topology and complexity theory and would sharply delineate the P/NP boundary in this subfield.
viability_rationale: They show P for s≤3 and NP-hardness for s≥20, but no concrete route to reduce to s=4; achieving the needed delicate gadget improvements appears challenging.
rationale: Authors conjecture NP-completeness for SimplePin(Σ,4) but only prove NP for s≤3 and NP-hardness for s≥20; the s=4 case remains unproven.
evidence_snippet: Statement: "For a fixed orientable surface Σ, the problem SimplePin(Σ,4) is NP-complete." (lines 4113–4116). Paper proves s≤3 in P and s≥20 NP-hard (discussion lines 4109–4116).

title: The complexity of pinning simple multiloops
body_tex:
For a non-orientable surface $\Sigma$ and $s\in \N$, what are the complexities of $\mathsf{Pin}(\Sigma, s)$ and $\mathsf{SimplePin}(\Sigma,s)$?

We conjecture that both are in \textsf{P} when $s\leq 3$ and \textsf{NP}-complete when $s\geq 4$.
plain_text: For a non-orientable surface $$ and $s in $, what are the complexities of $Pin(, s)$ and $SimplePin(,s)$? We conjecture that both are in P when $s <= 3$ and NP-complete when $s >= 4$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Analogous conjecture for non-orientable surfaces is a useful extension; resolving it clarifies how orientability affects complexity, but its impact is narrower and largely technical within geometric-topological complexity.
viability_rationale: Non-orientable cases lack the orientable-theory tools used; conjectured threshold mirrors orientable case but little progress indicated, so resolution in five years is uncertain.
rationale: The non-orientable surfaces complexity claim is posed as a conjecture; the paper does not prove these complexity thresholds for non-orientable surfaces.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: for non-orientable Σ, Pin and SimplePin are P when s≤3 and NP-complete when s≥4 (lines 4122–4126). Paper notes dependence on orientable-only theorems (line 4120).

title: The complexity of pinning simple multiloops

viability_rationale: PSPACE-completeness is plausible via Node Kayles analogies, but known reductions face open obstacles (planarity/max-degree constraints), so nontrivial barriers remain.

title: Unsplittable Transshipments
body_tex:
Given a~$b$-transshipment~$x$, one can efficiently compute an unsplittable~$b$-transshipment~$y$ such that
    \[x_a - d_{\max} \ \leq \ y_a \ \leq \ x_a + d_{\max} \quad \text{for all } a\in A.\]

interestingness_rationale: An arc-wise ±d_max conversion from fractional to unsplittable transshipments generalizes Dinitz–Garg–Goemans and would be a notable algorithmic advance in unsplittable flow/transshipment theory with practical routing implications.
viability_rationale: Natural algorithmic generalization of known SSUF results with possible constructive approaches, but achieving simultaneous per-arc upper and lower bounds is technically hard.
rationale: The conjecture appears in the conclusion as an open problem; the paper does not provide an algorithm achieving both arc-wise ±d_max bounds simultaneously.

title: Symmetry and localisation in causally constrained quantum operator dynamics

interestingness_rationale: A stability statement asserting generic restoration of linear (ballistic) light cones under local perturbations; central to thermalisation and operator-spreading universality in the subfield.

title: Constructing Koszul filtrations: existence and non-existence for G-quadratic algebras

Let $G$ be a reduced Gröbner basis of quadratic binomials each having terms of disjoint support. Then $S/(G)$ has a Koszul filtration. Moreover, if $(G)$ is toric then $S/(G)$ has a monomial Koszul filtration.
plain_text: Let $G$ be a reduced Gröbner basis of quadratic binomials each having terms of disjoint support. Then $S/(G)$ has a Koszul filtration. Moreover, if $(G)$ is toric then $S/(G)$ has a monomial Koszul filtration.

title: Isomorphism of cosymplectomorphism groups implies diffeomorphism of manifolds
body_tex:
Let $(M_1, g_1, J_1, \omega_1)$ and $(M_2, g_2, J_2, \omega_2)$ be compact Kähler manifolds. Suppose their groups of Kähler isometries (holomorphic isometries) are isomorphic as Lie groups via
		\[
		\Phi: \IsomKah(M_1) \;\stackrel{\text{Lie}}\cong\; \IsomKah(M_2),
		\]
		and assume both groups have positive dimension (i.e., are non-discrete). Then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biholomorphic. Moreover, if the isomorphism $\Phi$ is induced by an algebraic correspondence between the groups of isometries, then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric up to a constant scaling factor of the Kähler metrics.
plain_text: Let $(M_1, g_1, J_1, _1)$ and $(M_2, g_2, J_2, _2)$ be compact Kähler manifolds. Suppose their groups of Kähler isometries (holomorphic isometries) are isomorphic as Lie groups via \[ : (M_1) \; Lie \; (M_2), \] and assume both groups have positive dimension (i.e., are non-discrete). Then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are biholomorphic. Moreover, if the isomorphism $$ is induced by an algebraic correspondence between the groups of isometries, then $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric up to a constant scaling factor of the Kähler metrics.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A rigidity conjecture linking finite-dimensional Kähler isometry groups to biholomorphism/isometry; it sits centrally in Kähler geometry with clear structural consequences if true.
viability_rationale: Plausible but open: continuous symmetries constrain Kähler structure, yet full rigidity requires classifying manifolds with identical positive-dimensional isometry Lie groups. No concrete reduction given, so likely unresolved within five years.
rationale: The Kähler rigidity statement is presented as an open conjecture; the paper itself notes the full conjecture remains open and can fail without extra assumptions.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: isomorphic positive-dimension Kähler isometry groups imply biholomorphism (and possibly isometry); 'The full conjecture remains open and is false without additional structure.'

title: The Ising magnetisation field and the Gaussian free field

		\]
	corresponding to the \emph{imaginary Gaussian multiplicative chaos} with parameter $\alpha(g)=1/\sqrt{g}\in[1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)\subset[0, \sqrt{2}].$

title: Rigidity of proper holomorphic maps between balls with Hölder boundary regularity
body_tex:
Suppose $f: \Bb^m \rightarrow \Bb^M$ is a proper holomorphic map which extends to H\"older continuous map $\overline{\Bb^m} \rightarrow \overline{\Bb^M}$. If the image of the natural projection $\mathsf{G}_f \rightarrow \Aut(\Bb^m)$ contains a uniform lattice, then there exist $\phi_1 \in \Aut(\Bb^m)$ and $\phi_2 \in \Aut(\Bb^M)$ such that 
$$
\phi_2 \circ f \circ \phi_1(z) = (z,0)
$$
for all $z \in \Bb^m$.

title: Tilted Richardson Varieties
body_tex:
For a tilted Bruhat interval $[u, v]$, define the \emph{tilted Bruhat interval graph} as the directed graph with vertex set $[u, v]$ and an edge $w \xrightarrow{e_i - e_j} wt_{ij}$ whenever $w \prec wt_{ij}$ in the poset $[u, v]$. Here, recall that $w$ and $wt_{ij}$ are always comparable by \Cref{cor:tilted-bruhat-graph}. For any reflection ordering $\gamma$ of the set of positive roots $\Phi^+$, we have
\[R^\tilt_{u,v}(q)=q^{\frac{\ell(u,v)}{2}}\sum_{P:u\to v}\left(q^{\frac{1}{2}}-q^{-\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{\ell(P)},\]
where the sum is over all directed paths $P$ from $u$ to $v$ in the tilted Bruhat interval graph whose sequence of edge labels is strictly increasing with respect to $\gamma$ and $\ell(P)$ denotes the number of edges in the path $P$.

Here, recall that $w$ and $wt_ ij $ are always comparable by cor:tilted-bruhat-graph. For any reflection ordering $$ of the set of positive roots $^+$, we have \[R^_ u,v (q)=q^ ell (u,v) 2 _ P:u to v (q^ 1 2 -q^ -1 2 )^ ell (P) ,\] where the sum is over all directed paths $P$ from $u$ to $v$ in the tilted Bruhat interval graph whose sequence of edge labels is strictly increasing with respect to $$ and $ ell (P)$ denotes the number of edges in the path $P$.

evidence_snippet: Lines 3509–3513: Conjecture \label{conj:positive_part_closure} \overline{\cT_{u,v}^{>0}}=\cT_{u,v}^{\geq 0}. Authors note the classical strategy does not apply.

title: Tilted Richardson Varieties
body_tex: The CW-complex in \Cref{thm:CW-tilted} is regular.
plain_text: The CW-complex in thm:CW-tilted is regular.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Regularity of the CW-complex is a natural topological strengthening with clear geometric consequences for cell-closures; important locally but narrowly targeted to the tilted setting.
viability_rationale: Regularity likely tied to the closure statement and face-poset identification; dependent on resolving Conjecture 647. Nontrivial but plausible if closure is settled.
rationale: The regularity of the CW-complex is posed immediately as a conjecture after Theorem~thm:CW-tilted and is not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 3514–3516: "The CW-complex in \Cref{thm:CW-tilted} is regular." (stated as an open conjecture).

title: Strong local nondeterminism for stochastic time-fractional slow and fast diffusion equations
plain_text: The solution $u(t, x)$ satisfies (two-sided) strong local nondeterminism with respect to $t$ when $ in (0,1) cup (1,2)$ and when $=2$, $>1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Proving two-sided SLND in the remaining β-regimes would complete the probabilistic regularity picture and underpin sharp sample-path laws; significant within stochastic PDE analysis but confined to this class.
viability_rationale: Partial (one-sided and some two-sided) results are already proved; closing remaining regimes seems technical and plausible with new probabilistic/kernel estimates within five years.
rationale: Authors record two-sided strong local nondeterminism in the specified beta regimes as an open conjecture, remarking their methods yield only one-sided bounds and new ideas are needed.
evidence_snippet: Lines 873–878: Conjecture \label{Conj:SLND}: two-sided SLND holds when \beta\in(0,1)\cup(1,2) and when \beta=2, \gamma>1; preceding text says upgrading to two-sided requires new ideas.

title: Dead ends in square-free digit walks
body_tex:
There exists an infinite sequence of square-free integers
\[ \{N_0,N_1,N_2, N_3, \dots\} \]
and digits
$d_{k+1}\in\{0,1,\dots,9\}$ such that $N_{k+1}=10N_k+d_{k+1}$ for all $k\ge 0$.
plain_text: There exists an infinite sequence of square-free integers \[ N_0,N_1,N_2, N_3, \] and digits $d_ k+1 in 0,1,,9 $ such that $N_ k+1 =10N_k+d_ k+1 $ for all $k >= 0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural open problem about infinite square-free digit walks; probabilistic heuristics predict existence but proofs are absent. Resolving it would advance combinatorial/probabilistic number theory though with limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Paper gives exact dead-end density and formalized methods, yielding strong quantitative evidence but no explicit infinite walk; plausible progress possible, yet remaining constructive gap is nontrivial.
rationale: The paper treats this as an open problem and explicitly states no one has exhibited or proved existence of an infinite square-free digit walk.
evidence_snippet: "Despite its innocent appearance, this conjecture remains open. In particular, no one has exhibited an explicit infinite sequence ... nor has the existence of such a walk been established." (lines 245–247)

title: Almost all primes are partially regular
body_tex:
If $p$ is an odd prime, then we have
\[ A_p(\omega^{2k}) = 0 \quad \text{for all even } 2k \in \{2, 4, \dots, p-3\}. \]
plain_text: If $p$ is an odd prime, then we have \[ A_p(^ 2k ) = 0 for all even 2k in 2, 4, , p-3 . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The Kummer–Vandiver conjecture is a longstanding, central problem in algebraic number theory about cyclotomic class groups with deep consequences for K-theory, modular forms, and arithmetic; its resolution would be major.
viability_rationale: This is the classic Vandiver conjecture: century-old, widely studied. Density-one partial results don't close the full statement; no clear imminent route to complete resolution.
rationale: This is the full Kummer–Vandiver conjecture; the paper proves only a density-one partial/initial-range result, not the full statement, so the exact conjecture remains open.
evidence_snippet: "Kummer–Vandiver ... If p is an odd prime, then we have A_p(ω^{2k})=0 for all even 2k ..." followed by partial density-one results (lines 288–291, 298–306).

For $n sim U[N]$, the Kolmogorov distance between the random variable equation* ( n^2 + n + ) - N N equation* and the standard Gaussian $N(0, 1)$ is $O_ , , (_N)$ as $N$, where the implied constant may depend on $, $ and $$.

viability_rationale: Restricted to irreducible quadratics; partial methods and negative examples exist. Moderate chance of progress, but complex-analytic/sieve obstacles make a full quantitative bound in five years uncertain.

title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise

interestingness_rationale: Wise's conjecture links vanishing of the top L^2-Betti number to non-positive towers; central in geometric group theory with strong consequences (Whitehead, Howie), so resolving it has broad structural impact.
viability_rationale: Longstanding geometric-topology conjecture with deep consequences (Whitehead, Howie); known only in special cases, so near-term resolution appears unlikely.
rationale: Wise's conjecture is presented as an open conjecture in the paper; authors note partial results but do not resolve it in general.

title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
body_tex:
\label{con:Torsionfree_groups_are_Linnell_groups}
  Every torsionfree group is a  Linnell-group.
plain_text: Every torsionfree group is a Linnell-group.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Claim that every torsionfree group is a Linnell-group is a sweeping structural conjecture about existence/uniqueness of division rings for group algebras; a major field-level statement in noncommutative localization.
viability_rationale: Very general algebraic conjecture about all torsionfree groups being Linnell; broad scope and deep ring-theoretic obstacles make a five-year resolution unlikely.
rationale: The statement 'Every torsionfree group is a Linnell-group' is quoted as a conjecture from the literature and is not proved in this paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 781–785: 'The following conjecture is taken from ... Conjecture: Every torsionfree group is a Linnell-group.'

title: L^2-Betti numbers in prime characteristic and a conjecture of Wise
body_tex:
\label{con:Locally_indicibale_groups_are_Lewin_groups}
  Every locally indicable group is a Lewin group (and in particular a Hughes group).
plain_text: Every locally indicable group is a Lewin group (and in particular a Hughes group).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Locally indicable implies Lewin would guarantee universal division rings for a large class of groups; important for L^2-Betti theory and group-ring structure, but narrower than the full torsionfree/Linnell claim.
viability_rationale: Focused on locally indicable groups but demands existence of universal division rings; some partial results exist, yet the conjecture remains deep and not evidently close to resolution.
rationale: Paper cites Jaikin-Zapirain's conjecture that locally indicable groups are Lewin groups; it is stated as a conjecture and not resolved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2811–2816: 'Conjecture (Locally indicable groups are Lewin groups): Every locally indicable group is a Lewin group (and in particular a Hughes group).'

title: Basis Criteria for Extending Generalized Splines
body_tex:
Let \( (G, \beta) \) be an edge-labeled graph over a GCD domain \( R \), 
	and let \( F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n \in \hat{R}_{G} \) be splines.  
	If \( \{F_1, F_2, \dots, F_n\} \) forms an \( R \)-module basis of \( \hat{R}_{G} \),  
	then the determinant satisfies
	\[
	\lvert F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n \rvert = u \hat{Q}_{G}
	\quad \text{for some unit } u \in R.
	\]
plain_text: Let \( (G, ) \) be an edge-labeled graph over a GCD domain \( R \), and let \( F_1, F_2, , F_n in R_ G \) be splines. If \( F_1, F_2, , F_n \) forms an \( R \)-module basis of \( R_ G \), then the determinant satisfies \[ F_1, F_2, , F_n = u Q_ G for some unit u in R. \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A determinantal criterion characterizing freeness of extending generalized spline modules is a substantive algebraic‑combinatorial statement with clear geometric implications, but it is fairly specialized to spline theory.
viability_rationale: Authors prove special relatively-prime case; extending to arbitrary graphs over GCD domains is technical but concrete and within standard algebraic-combinatorial methods—moderate likelihood of resolution soon.
rationale: The authors state this determinantal criterion as a conjecture for arbitrary GCD-domain graphs; their paper proves a special case under pairwise relatively prime labels, not the full general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 178–186: "\begin{Conj} ... If \{F_1,\dots,F_n\} forms an R-module basis of \hat{R}_G, then |F_1,\dots,F_n| = u \hat{Q}_G for some unit u \in R. \end{Conj}"

title: The optimal chromatic bound for even-hole-free graphs without induced seven-vertex paths
body_tex: For every even-hole-free graph $G$, $\chi(G)\le \ceil{\frac{5}{4}\omega(G)}$.
plain_text: For every even-hole-free graph $G$, $(G) <= 5 4 (G) $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central χ-bounding conjecture for even-hole-free graphs; proving it would give the optimal chromatic bound, settle a natural structural question, and impact several lines of structural graph theory.
viability_rationale: Partial cases proved (P7-free, cap-even-hole-free) and new structural tools exist, but the full even-hole-free class remains broad and structurally challenging; significant global obstacles remain.
rationale: The paper states this as an explicit conjecture (Concluding Remarks) and only proves it for special subclasses (P7-free and cap-even-hole-free), so the general statement remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 6053-6058: 'we conjecture this should be the case. \begin{conjecture} For every even-hole-free graph G, χ(G) ≤ ⌈5/4 ω(G)⌉. \end{conjecture}'

title: Colored interlacing triangles and Genocchi medians
body_tex:
\label{conj:log_concavity}
	The coefficients of each polynomial $P_n(q)$ are log-concave:
	if $P_n(q) = \sum_{k} a_k(n) q^k$, then 
	we have $a_k(n)^2 \ge a_{k-1}(n) a_{k+1}(n)$ for all $n,k$.
	We have verified this for $n \le 9$.
plain_text: The coefficients of each polynomial $P_n(q)$ are log-concave: if $P_n(q) = _ k a_k(n) q^k$, then we have $a_k(n)^2 >= a_ k-1 (n) a_ k+1 (n)$ for all $n,k$. We have verified this for $n <= 9$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Log-concavity of coefficients for P_n(q) is a natural, nontrivial enumerative property (verified to n≤9); interesting within algebraic/combinatorial enumeration but limited in scope.
viability_rationale: Specific family with computational verification (n≤9) and structural links to LLT/Genocchi; plausible approaches (real-rootedness, combinatorial inequalities) exist but a general proof is nontrivial and uncertain within five years.
rationale: The authors pose log-concavity of coefficients of P_n(q) as a conjecture, verified only computationally for n ≤ 9; no proof is given in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1251-1256: 'Conjecture: The coefficients of each polynomial P_n(q) are log-concave... We have verified this for n ≤ 9.'

title: Colored interlacing triangles and Genocchi medians
plain_text: For each fixed $k >= 0$, and all $n >= n_0(k)$, the coefficient $a_k(n)$ of $q^k$ in $P_n(q)$ is a polynomial in $n$ of degree $k$. Specifically, we conjecture the following polynomials for the next four coefficients: equation split a_2(n)&=25n^2 - 49n - 116 2 , n >= 5, a_3(n)&=125n^3 + 15n^2 - 3104n + 1980 6 , n >= 7, a_4(n)&=625 n^4 + 2650 n^3 - 36877 n^2 - 31390 n + 244728 24 , n >= 9, a_5(n)&=3125 n^5 + 32500 n^4 - 290925 n^3 - 1585240 n^2 + 7120060 n + 5588400 120 , n >= 11. split equation
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Polynomiality in n of fixed-q coefficients with degree k is a meaningful structural regularity supported by computed data and explicit small-k formulas, but remains a specialist enumerative claim.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence (n≤15), explicit leading-term heuristic and bounded local-modification argument suggest fixed-k coefficients come from finite local patterns; transfer-matrix/finite-state methods likely yield proofs for each k within a few years.
rationale: The paper explicitly conjectures polynomiality in n of coefficients a_k(n) and gives formulas for a_2..a_5 based on computed data, but does not prove the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1309-1316 and 1317-1321: 'For each fixed k≥0 ... a_k(n) is a polynomial in n of degree k. Specifically, we conjecture ... a_2(n)=..., a_3(n)=..., a_4(n)=..., a_5(n)=...'

title: Colored interlacing triangles and Genocchi medians
body_tex:
\label{conj:leading_coefficient}
As polynomials in $n$, the expressions
$k!\ssp a_k(n)$
have integer coefficients for all $k \geq 0$,
and their leading coefficient is $5^k$.
plain_text: As polynomials in $n$, the expressions $k! a_k(n)$ have integer coefficients for all $k >= 0$, and their leading coefficient is $5^k$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Arithmetic/asymptotic statement (k! a_k(n) integral, leading coefficient 5^k) has clear combinatorial evidence and consequences for asymptotics; significant within the paper's enumerative framework.
viability_rationale: Strong local evidence: verified for small k, asymptotic/constructive lower bound gives 5^k leading term, and for fixed k the problem reduces to counting disjoint local defects—amenable to inclusion–exclusion/cluster methods.
rationale: The authors state as a conjecture that k! a_k(n) have integer coefficients with leading coefficient 5^k, and provide heuristic evidence but no proof.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1324-1330: 'Conjecture (Leading coefficient): As polynomials in n, k! a_k(n) have integer coefficients for all k≥0, and their leading coefficient is 5^k.'

title: Nearly Gorenstein normal graded rings
body_tex:
\label{b(R)<0} If $\chr(k) = 0$,  
$b(R)< 0$ and if $R$ has an isolated singularity, 
then $R$ is of strongly F-regular type.  
If, moreover,  $R$ is $\Q$-Gorenstein (that is, $r\; cl(K_R) = 0$ in $\Cl(R)$ 
for some positive integer $r$), then $R$ is log-terminal.
plain_text: If $(k) = 0$, $b(R)< 0$ and if $R$ has an isolated singularity, then $R$ is of strongly F-regular type. If, moreover, $R$ is $$-Gorenstein (that is, $r\; cl(K_R) = 0$ in $(R)$ for some positive integer $r$), then $R$ is log-terminal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates the new invariant b(R)<0 to strong F-regular type and log-terminality, a significant connection in singularity/MMP theory; proven in special cases, general resolution would be impactful.
viability_rationale: Deep link between characteristic-0 invariants and F-regular type; proved in dim 2 and special cases, but full higher-dimensional Q-Gorenstein/log-terminal claim is a hard singularity theory problem.
rationale: Authors pose Conjecture 2.3 asserting b(R)<0 implies strongly F-regular type (and log-terminal if Q-Gorenstein); they prove special cases (dim 2, F-pure) but not the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 509–514: Conjecture: if char(k)=0, b(R)<0 and R has isolated singularity then R is of strongly F-regular type; if Q-Gorenstein then R is log-terminal. Lines 516–523 note special-case proofs.

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
body_tex: Let $f: X \to S$ be a family from a normal $\Q$-factorial K\"ahler variety $X$ onto a smooth, connected, and relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $X_t$ have canonical singularities for all $t\in S$. Then $K_{X_0}$ is pseudo-effective if and only if $K_{X_t} $ are pseudo-effective for all $t \in S \setminus \{0\}$. (By Theorem \ref{Theorem Ou25}, this is equivalent to the statement that $X_0$ is uniruled if and only if $X_t$ are uniruled for all $t \in S \setminus \{0\}$.)
plain_text: Let $f: X to S$ be a family from a normal $$-factorial K\"ahler variety $X$ onto a smooth, connected, and relatively compact curve $S$. Assume that $X_t$ have canonical singularities for all $t in S$. Then $K_ X_0 $ is pseudo-effective if and only if $K_ X_t $ are pseudo-effective for all $t in S 0 $. (By Theorem , this is equivalent to the statement that $X_0$ is uniruled if and only if $X_t$ are uniruled for all $t in S 0 $.)
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts deformation stability of pseudo-effectivity/uniruledness for Kähler families without projectivity on the central fiber; leverages transcendental MMP advances and has notable consequences in complex geometry.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the statement when central fiber is projective and in dimension three; removing the projectivity assumption in general relies on broader transcendental MMP progress, so full resolution seems unlikely soon.
rationale: The paper proves the statement under additional hypotheses (projective central fiber or threefolds) and then explicitly proposes the more general deformation-stability conjecture without those assumptions, which remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 201–203 state the theorem with X_0 projective. Lines 211–213: "It is reasonable to propose the more general: \begin{conjecture} Let f:X→S... Then K_{X_0} is pseudo-effective iff K_{X_t} are pseudo-effective for all t≠0.\end{conjecture}"

title: On Pseudo-Effectivity and Volumes of Adjoint Classes in Kähler Families with Projective Central Fiber
plain_text: Let $f:X to S$ be a proper surjective morphism from a normal $Q$-factorial generalized K\"ahler pair $(X,B + )$ onto a smooth, connected, relatively compact curve $S$. Fix a point $0 in S$ and assume that the support of the boundary divisor $B$ does not contain the fiber $X_0$. Assume that the restriction to the central fiber $(X_0,B_0 + _0)$ is a generalized klt pair, such that $K_ X_0 +B_0 + _0$ is nef and big. Then $K_ X + B + _X $ is nef and big over $U$ for some open neighborhood $U subset S$ of $0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Deformation stability of nef-and-big adjoint classes from a central fiber; meaningful for Kähler families but largely a local extension and follows from the Transbpf conjecture.
viability_rationale: Conjecture reduces to Transcendental BPF; holds for threefolds and nd≤3. Progress on TransBPF would resolve it, so near-term solvability mirrors that parent conjecture.
rationale: The conjecture (deformation of nef-and-big adjoint classes) is proposed in full generality; the paper proves it under a projective-central-fiber hypothesis and in several special cases, but not in general.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If K_{X_0}+B_0+β_0 is nef and big then K_X+B+β_X is nef and big over a neighborhood. Proposition proves this when the central fiber is projective; general case left as conjecture.

title: Hodge theory of secant varieties
plain_text: Let $X$ be a compact pure-dimensional complex algebraic variety. Then $L_*(X)= IT _1 _*(X)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Relates Goresky–MacPherson L-class to the intersection Hirzebruch class at y=1: a structurally meaningful characteristic-class Hodge index statement with broad consequences for singular-variety topology and Hodge theory.
viability_rationale: Longstanding Cappell–Shaneson characteristic-class Hodge index conjecture; only special cases known and no clear route to a general proof, so general resolution within five years is unlikely.
rationale: The paper states this general Cappell–Shaneson characteristic-class Hodge index conjecture and only proves it for secant varieties; the full statement for all compact pure-dimensional varieties remains unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture stated (L_*(X)=IT_1_*(X)). Paper notes they will prove the conjecture for secant varieties ("We will use the above remark to prove Conjecture ... for secant varieties").

title: Clonoids over vector spaces
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:main}
Let $\algA$ and $\algB$ be finite modules. Then, there are only finitely many clonoids from $\algA$ to $\algB$, if and only if $\algA$ and $\algB$ are of coprime order.
plain_text: Let $$ and $$ be finite modules. Then, there are only finitely many clonoids from $$ to $$, if and only if $$ and $$ are of coprime order.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural classification conjecture in clonoid/minion theory: finiteness characterized by coprime module orders. Significant within universal algebra and CSP-related applications but limited to that domain.
viability_rationale: Significant partial results (vector spaces, distributive modules) and the necessary direction known; concrete reductions suggest a plausible, though nontrivial, near-term resolution.
rationale: The authors pose the finiteness iff coprime-orders conjecture for all finite modules; they prove many cases (vector spaces, others) but do not establish the full iff statement for all finite modules.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture 1.1: finiteness of clonoids iff module orders are coprime. Paper confirms it for vector-space source modules and other classes, but not in full generality.

title: Clonoids over vector spaces
body_tex:
\label{conjecture:strong}
Let $\algA$ be a finite abelian $p$-group, and let $\algR$ be a ring (considered as regular $\algR$-module), such that $p$ is invertible in $\algR$. Then, there is a $k\in \N$, such that $\mathcal O_{\algA,\algR}$ is uniformly generated by $k$-ary $(\algA,\algR)$-minors.
plain_text: Let $$ be a finite abelian $p$-group, and let $$ be a ring (considered as regular $$-module), such that $p$ is invertible in $$. Then, there is a $k in $, such that $ O_ , $ is uniformly generated by $k$-ary $(,)$-minors.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical uniform-generation statement for operations on finite abelian p-groups (p invertible in R). Implies the main conjecture; useful but specialist and method-driven.
viability_rationale: Authors reduced to explicit finite p-group cases; elementary abelian and cyclic groups handled. Remaining small p-group cases are concrete and technically focused, making near-term progress plausible.
rationale: The uniform generation-by-k-ary-minors conjecture for p-groups is presented in the conclusion and is not proved here; authors note partial results and pose specific open cases (e.g. Z_{p^2}×Z_p).
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (Section Conclusion): for finite abelian p-group A and ring R with p invertible, O_{A,R} uniformly generated by k-ary minors. Authors state this is conjectural and ask about Z_{p^2}×Z_p.

title: A note on exterior stability of finite time singularity formation for nonlinear wave equations

title: Stability of natural bundles on curves
body_tex:
\label{conj-main}
Let $f\colon X \to Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
plain_text: Let $f X to Y$ be a finite morphism between smooth irreducible projective curves, and let $V$ be a general vector bundle on $X$. Then $f_* V$ is stable if the genus of \(Y\) is at least \(2\) and semistable if the genus of \(Y\) is \(1\).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Beauville's conjecture is a longstanding, structurally meaningful prediction about stability of pushforwards linking Hurwitz spaces and moduli of bundles on curves; resolving it would have clear geometric consequences in algebraic geometry.
viability_rationale: Proved for the general member of any Hurwitz component, but Beauville's original assertion for every cover remains open with no explicit finite reduction—moderately unlikely to be solved within five years.
rationale: Paper proves the statement for a general morphism in any Hurwitz component but explicitly says Beauville's original conjecture (for every cover) remains open.
evidence_snippet: Remark: Beauville's original conjecture predicting the stability of the general pushforward for every cover remains open. (lines 1040-1042)

title: Spectral gap for Pollicott-Ruelle resonances on random coverings of Anosov surfaces
body_tex: \label{conj: uniform gap} Let $M$ be a closed Anosov surface. For any $\varepsilon>0$, with probability tending to $1$ as $n\to +\infty$, a uniformly random cover $M_n\to M$ has no new resonance in the half space $\{\operatorname{Re}(z)>-\frac{\gamma_0}{2}+\varepsilon\}$.
plain_text: Let $M$ be a closed Anosov surface. For any $>0$, with probability tending to $1$ as $n to +$, a uniformly random cover $M_n to M$ has no new resonance in the half space $ Re(z)>-_0 2 + $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A probabilistic spectral gap for Pollicott–Ruelle resonances on random Anosov covers links dynamics, random coverings, and spectral theory; it controls high-frequency resonances and impacts decay of correlations.
viability_rationale: Significant recent input (strong convergence theorems) plus the paper's partial results give a concrete route; plausible that the probabilistic uniform gap is resolved in the near term.
rationale: Conjecture is explicitly posed as an open conjecture in the paper; only special cases (e.g. hyperbolic surfaces) are known.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: 'For any ε>0, with probability tending to 1 as n→+∞, a uniformly random cover M_n→M has no new resonance in the half space Re(z)>-γ_0/2+ε.' (lines 245-247)

title: Spectral gap for Pollicott-Ruelle resonances on random coverings of Anosov surfaces
body_tex:
\label{conj: uniform asymptotic gap} For any $\varepsilon>0$, there is a constant $C_\varepsilon$ such that for any unitary flat bundle $\mathcal E \to SM$ of finite rank, the Ruelle operator $\mathbf X_{\mathcal E}$ has no resonance in 
		\[\mathcal S_\varepsilon:=\Big\{z\in\mathbb{C}, \ |\operatorname{Im}(z)|\ge C_\varepsilon \ \text{and} \ \operatorname{Re}(z)\ge -\frac{\gamma_0}{2}+\varepsilon\Big\}.\]
plain_text: For any $>0$, there is a constant $C_$ such that for any unitary flat bundle $ E to SM$ of finite rank, the Ruelle operator $ X_ E $ has no resonance in \[ S_:= z in C, \ |Im(z)| >= C_ \ and \ Re(z) >= -_0 2 + .\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A deterministic uniform asymptotic gap for Ruelle operators across all unitary flat bundles is a strong, technical microlocal/dynamical statement whose proof would yield uniform resolvent bounds and uniform mixing results.
viability_rationale: Authors believe it should follow by adapting known microlocal/semiclassical proofs (Nonnenmacher et al.); technical but feasible, giving a moderate chance of resolution within five years.
rationale: Paper presents this deterministic uniform asymptotic gap as an open conjecture to be investigated in future work; it is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: 'For any ε>0, there is C_ε such that ... the Ruelle operator has no resonance in S_ε.' 'Conjecture ... will be investigated in future work.' (lines 249-254)

title: Vigemers: on the number of $k$-mers sharing the same XOR-based minimizer
body_tex:
Let $g : \Sigma^m\to\Sigma^m$ be such that $g(a_1\cdots a_m) = g_1(a_1)\cdots g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i:\Sigma\to\Sigma$. Then, by defining the minimizer $\min_g(x)$ of a \kmer $x$ as its leftmost \mmer $w$ that minimizes $\texttt{rank}(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute
$\pi_k^g(w) = \left\lbrace x\in\Sigma^k : \min_g(x) = w\right\rbrace$
in $O(|\Sigma|\cdot km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.
plain_text: Let $g : ^m to ^m$ be such that $g(a_1 a_m) = g_1(a_1) g_m(a_m)$ where $g_i: to $. Then, by defining the minimizer $_g(x)$ of a $x$ as its leftmost $w$ that minimizes $rank(g(w))$ (for the lexicographical order), we can compute $_k^g(w) = x in ^k : _g(x) = w$ in $O(|| * km^2)$ time and $O(km)$ space by adapting the equations presented in this article.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A technical, method-specific extension to per-letter product maps g that broadens applicability but is primarily an algorithmic/local improvement with limited conceptual impact beyond the paper's framework.
viability_rationale: Direct extension of the paper's method; authors indicate a straightforward letter-by-letter adaptation. Remaining technicalities seem routine, so a resolution within five years is plausible.
rationale: The paper explicitly states this as a conjecture in the Conclusion and does not prove it; it's posed as an extension of their method and remains open here.

title: Phase transitions with bounded index: Parallels to De Giorgi's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:indimpends}
    Let $n\geq 3$, and let $u:\R^n\to[-1,1]$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ has finitely many ends, in the sense that there is some $R_0>0$ such that $\{u=0\}$ has finitely many connected components in $\R^n\setminus \overline{B_{R_0}}$.
plain_text: Let $n >= 3$, and let $u:^n to [-1,1]$ be a finite Morse index solution to the Allen--Cahn equation. Then, $u$ has finitely many ends, in the sense that there is some $R_0>0$ such that $ u=0 $ has finitely many connected components in $^n B_ R_0 $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A deep structural conjecture linking finite Morse index to finiteness of ends for Allen–Cahn solutions; resolving it impacts PDEs, geometric analysis and ties to De Giorgi/minimal surface problems, with broad consequences.
viability_rationale: Partial progress: n=2 is settled; this paper proves bounded-energy-density cases (n=4 and conditionally 4≤n≤7). Main gap is removing the bounded energy-density hypothesis and classifying stable solutions; active but nontrivial.
rationale: The finite-index implies finite-ends conjecture is stated as long-standing and not proved here in full; only n=2 is known and higher-dimensional cases remain open or conditional.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: finite Morse index solutions u:\R^n\to[-1,1] have finitely many ends. Paper notes n=2 is a theorem; higher n remain open/conditional.

title: Phase transitions with bounded index: Parallels to De Giorgi's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj:dPKW}
    If $u$ [a solution to Allen--Cahn in $\R^3$] has finite Morse index and $\nabla u(x)\neq 0$ outside a bounded set, then, outside a large ball, each level set of $u$ must have a finite number of components, each of them asymptotic either to a plane or to a catenoid. After a rotation of the coordinate system, all these components are graphs of functions of the same two variables.
plain_text: If $u$ [a solution to Allen--Cahn in $^3$] has finite Morse index and $ u(x) != 0$ outside a bounded set, then, outside a large ball, each level set of $u$ must have a finite number of components, each of them asymptotic either to a plane or to a catenoid. After a rotation of the coordinate system, all these components are graphs of functions of the same two variables.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Classifies finite‑index Allen–Cahn entire solutions in 3D by geometric ends (planes or catenoids). It's a central structural conjecture in PDE/geometric analysis with clear consequences for phase‑transition anatomy.
viability_rationale: Specific 3D classification is narrower than grand conjectures but remains delicate; paper proves related results under energy-density assumptions, giving partial progress but nontrivial technical gap likely remains.
rationale: The paper records the dPKW conjecture for R^3 but only claims to confirm the picture under an extra bounded energy density assumption; the exact statement (without that assumption) remains unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture recorded (Del Pino--Kowalczyk--Wei). The paper proves classification results assuming bounded energy density (Theorem for R^4: E(u,B_R) ≤ C R^3).

title: Special Lagrangians and Bridgeland stable objects beyond geometric stability conditions: the product case
body_tex:
{Joyce}}]\label{conj:Joyce}
Let $(M, \omega, \Omega)$ be a compact Calabi--Yau $n$-fold, where $\omega$ is a K\"ahler form and $\Omega$ is a holomorphic volume form. There exists a Bridgeland stability condition $\sigma=(Z,\cP)$ on the derived Fukaya category $\DFuk(M)$, whose objects are Lagrangian branes $\bL$, such that:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
    \item The central charge $Z$ is given by the composition:
    $$
    K_0\left(\DFuk(M)\right)\xrightarrow{\bL\mapsto[L]}H_n(M,\bZ)\xrightarrow{[L]\mapsto\int_{[L]}\Omega}\bC,
    $$
    where $L\subseteq M$ denotes the underlying Lagrangian of $\bL$.
    \item An object $\bL$ is $\sigma$-semistable if and only if its underlying Lagrangian admits a (possibly singular) special Lagrangian representative.
\end{enumerate}

title: On the Graphical $r$-Stirling Numbers of the First Kind for Specific Graph Families
body_tex:
\label{conj:r-monotone}
For any graph $G$ and fixed $k$, the $r$-restricted graphical Stirling numbers satisfy
\[
\st{G}{k}_1 \ge \st{G}{k}_2 \ge \dots \ge \st{G}{k}_k,
\]
i.e., the sequence decreases strictly as the size of the restricted set increases.
plain_text: For any graph $G$ and fixed $k$, the $r$-restricted graphical Stirling numbers satisfy \[ G k _1 >= G k _2 >= >= G k _k, \] i.e., the sequence decreases strictly as the size of the restricted set increases.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural monotonicity statement on r-restricted Stirling numbers; useful and plausible but largely a local combinatorial inequality without wide structural ramifications.
viability_rationale: Monotonic decrease as restricted set grows is combinatorially natural; likely quickly resolvable (proof or counterexample) since basic combinatorial arguments should settle it.
rationale: The monotonicity in the restricted parameter r is stated as a conjecture in the 'Further Work' section and is not established in the paper.

title: Comparison theorems for the extreme eigenvalues of a random symmetric matrix
body_tex:
\label{conj:nn}
Fix a subspace dimension $d$ and distortion parameters $\alpha = \beta \in (0,1)$.
There exists a construction of a random sparse matrix $\mtx{\Phi} : \R^n \to \R^k$
with at most $\zeta$ nonzero entries per column and with embedding dimension
$k$ that satisfy
\[
\zeta \leq \mathrm{Const} \cdot \alpha^{-1} \log d
\quad\text{and}\quad
k \leq \mathrm{Const} \cdot \alpha^{-2} \log d.
\]
For any fixed subspace of dimension $d$, with probability at least $1 - d^{-1}$,
this random sparse matrix $\mtx{\Phi}$ serves as a subspace embedding
with distortion parameters $\alpha, \beta$.
The constants are universal.

title: Joint distribution of leftmost digits in positional notation and Schanuels's conjecture
body_tex:
\label{conj2}
If $b_1,...,b_n$ are integers $\geq 3$
such that no pair of numbers in the set 
$\{\ln b_1,...,\ln b_n\}$ 
are rationaly dependent, then 
$\frac{1}{\ln b_1},...,\frac{1}{\ln b_n}$ 
are rationally independent.

rationale: The paper cites the 1987 Alavi–Malde–Schwenk–Erdős conjecture and only proves unimodality for two infinite families; the general statement for every forest remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: “In 1987 ... conjectured that the independence polynomial of every tree or forest is unimodal.” (conjecture stated; paper proves two families only)

title: Antimagic labelling of graphs with maximum degree $Δ(G) = n - 4$
body_tex: Every connected graph other than $K_2$ is antimagic.
plain_text: Every connected graph other than $K_2$ is antimagic.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Hartsfield–Ringel antimagic conjecture is a well‑known, long‑standing central problem in graph labelling; resolving it would settle a major structural question across graph theory.
viability_rationale: Long-standing Hartsfield–Ringel conjecture (1990). Only incremental progress (large-Δ cases) so far; full general statement remains widely open, making a complete resolution in five years unlikely.
rationale: The Hartsfield–Ringel conjecture (“Every connected graph other than K2 is antimagic”) is stated as the original open conjecture; this paper proves classes but does not settle it.
evidence_snippet: “Antimagic labelling ... with the following conjecture: Every connected graph other than K_2 is antimagic.” (stated as longstanding open problem)

title: Antimagic labelling of graphs with maximum degree $Δ(G) = n - 4$
body_tex: Let $k$ be a positive integer, and let $G$ be a connected graph with $\Delta(G) = n - k$. If $m \geq f(k)n$, for some positive function $f$, then $G$ is antimagic.
plain_text: Let $k$ be a positive integer, and let $G$ be a connected graph with $(G) = n - k$. If $m >= f(k)n$, for some positive function $f$, then $G$ is antimagic.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Existence of f(k) with m≥f(k)n guaranteeing antimagicness generalizes the k=4 result; useful for sparse/large‑degree families though technical and class‑specific in scope.
viability_rationale: Cases k≤4 are proved and Eccles gives large-average-degree bounds; authors' new framework likely extends with technical refinements and finite-case handling, making a resolution for each fixed k plausible within five years though not certain.
rationale: The general conjecture positing existence of f(k) with m≥f(k)n implies antimagic is posed in the conclusion; the paper establishes f(4)≤7 but does not resolve the general quantified statement.
evidence_snippet: “Let k be ... If m≥f(k)n, for some positive function f, then G is antimagic.” (conjecture); paper notes f(4)≤7 from their Theorem.

title: Acyclic sets and colorings in digraphs under restrictions on degrees and cycle lengths
plain_text: For every tournament $T$ there exists some $_T > 0$ such that for any $n$-vertex $T$-free simple digraph $D$, $(D) >= n^ _T $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A directed strengthening of Erdős–Hajnal: proving polynomial-size acyclic sets in T-free digraphs would have deep structural consequences in extremal and directed graph theory, and is a central, difficult problem.
viability_rationale: A deep strengthening of Erdős–Hajnal for tournaments; long-standing, authors admit no approach and only weak bounds; classic hard problem, unlikely to be solved in five years.
rationale: Paper explicitly proposes this strengthening of Erdős–Hajnal and does not prove it; only an upper bound on ε_T is given, so the exact statement remains unresolved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 426–433: Conjecture \'For every tournament T there exists ε_T>0 such that for any n-vertex T-free simple digraph D, vecα(D) ≥ n^{ε_T}\'. Theorem 441–443 gives only ε_T ≤ 2/t.

title: Acyclic sets and colorings in digraphs under restrictions on degrees and cycle lengths

viability_rationale: The directed-triangle special case remains open with only subpolynomial lower bounds and no available approach; meaningful gap persists, so short-term resolution is unlikely but somewhat more plausible than the general case.
rationale: The directed-triangle case is posed as a special open instance of the Strong EH conjecture; the authors state they have no means of approaching it and only prove a weaker bound.

viability_rationale: Specialized asymptotic with substantial partial results in the paper; plausible via method refinements (probabilistic/number-theoretic), but a nontrivial technical gap remains—moderate chance of resolution within five years.

title: Dual and double canonical bases of quantum groups

title: Locally Irregular Total Colorings of Graphs
body_tex:
\label{graph3}
Every connected graph $G \neq B$ that is locally irregular colorable satisfies $\mathrm{lir}(G)\leq 3$.
plain_text: Every connected graph $G != B$ that is locally irregular colorable satisfies $lir(G) <= 3$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Prominent conjecture in locally irregular edge-colorings; central to graph-decomposition theory with substantial partial progress and a single known exceptional bow-tie example; resolving it would clarify fundamental structural coloring limits.
viability_rationale: Strong partial progress: many graph classes resolved, only one concrete exception B known; but full general closure remains nontrivial across all graphs.
rationale: The Local Irregularity Conjecture (lir(G) ≤ 3 for connected G ≠ B) is stated but not proved in this paper; only partial results and the exceptional bow-tie example are discussed.
evidence_snippet: During their research a single cactus B satisfying lir(B)=4 was found. Sedlar and Škrekovski improved the Local Irregularity Conjecture: Every connected graph G ≠ B that is locally irregular colorable satisfies lir(G) ≤ 3.

title: Locally Irregular Total Colorings of Graphs
body_tex:
\label{main_conjecture}
    For every graph $G$, we have $\tlir(G) \leq 2$.
plain_text: For every graph $G$, we have $(G) <= 2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Total-version analogue of the local irregularity problem; actively studied with many class-wise confirmations (this paper included); a general proof would unify irregularity and total-coloring frameworks in graph theory.
viability_rationale: Total-version has new class-wise proofs and a global constant bound (220), yet the universal two-color statement remains broad and unresolved for general graphs.
rationale: The TLIR conjecture (tlir(G) ≤ 2) is posed and the paper proves it only for cacti, subcubic, split graphs and gives bounds elsewhere, so the general statement remains open.
evidence_snippet: In~\cite{Baudon2015} ... \begin{conjecture}\label{main_conjecture} For every graph G, we have \tlir(G) \leq 2. \end{conjecture}. One result: every cactus graph G has \tlir(G) \leq 2.

title: Rigidity and Gap Phenomena in the Sphere--Ball Correspondence

Assume that \[ |x^|^2\,|A(x)|^2 <= 3 2 \] for all $x in $. Then one of the following should hold: $$ is congruent to the equatorial three-disk $D^3$; $$ is congruent to an $O(2) x O(2)$-invariant free boundary minimal hypersurface; such examples are topologically homeomorphic to the solid torus $D^2 x S^1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A higher-dimensional curvature-pinching classification aiming to single out disk or O(2)xO(2)-invariant solid-torus models; addresses model selection among infinitely many symmetric examples and impacts gap/rigidity theory.
viability_rationale: High-dimensional pinching conjecture faces infinitely many known invariant examples; distinguishing the correct model globally appears delicate and currently unresolved.

evidence_snippet: Barbosa and Viana formulated the following conjecture... In the four-dimensional ball B^4 one expects... At present, the concrete uniqueness and existence problems in this higher-dimensional setting remain unresolved.

title: Toward the classification of strongly self-absorbing $\mathrm{C}^*$-dynamical systems of compact groups
body_tex:
\label{conjecture}Let $G$ be a compact group and $D$ be a strongly self-absorbing $G$-$\Cstar$-algebra that belongs to the $G$-equivariant bootstrap class $\mathcal{B}_G$. 
Then $D$ satisfies the localization condition with respect to every separable compact $G$-$\Cstar$-algebra $B$.
plain_text: Let $G$ be a compact group and $D$ be a strongly self-absorbing $G$-$$-algebra that belongs to the $G$-equivariant bootstrap class $B_G$. Then $D$ satisfies the localization condition with respect to every separable compact $G$-$$-algebra $B$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A structural classification conjecture in equivariant C*-dynamics; proving it yields classification and invariant-completeness results within an active operator-algebra subfield.
viability_rationale: Authors prove the conjecture for finite EPPO groups and give structural reductions; general compact groups (e.g. Lie groups) remain harder. Moderate chance but substantial technical gaps remain.
rationale: The paper states the conjecture for all compact groups and only resolves it for finite EPPO groups; the full general statement remains unproved in the article.
evidence_snippet: Main conjecture stated (lines 181-183). Abstract/intro: authors 'settle this conjecture for all finite EPPO groups' but not for arbitrary compact G.

body_tex:

Let $r\geq3$ be an integer. Every  $r$-connected $\{K_{1,r+1},K_{1,r+1}+e\}$-free  split graph of order at least $2r+7$ is Hamilton-connected.

rationale: Authors pose this general r-connected split-graph Hamilton-connectedness statement as a new open conjecture in 'Further study' and do not prove it.

interestingness_rationale: Technical generalization of large-genus Hurwitz asymptotics to arbitrary Riemann surfaces; mathematically substantive within enumerative geometry but narrowly focused to coefficient-vanishing phenomena.
viability_rationale: Paper proves special cases (Lemma via Frumkin-James-Roichman) and the statement is a finite, representation-theoretic combinatorial vanishing claim — plausible progress could finish remaining cases within five years.
rationale: The paper states Conjecture~\ref{cH11} in 'Further remarks' and does not prove it; only related special cases are established earlier. The statement remains posed as open.
