\label{conj: O min 2 oplus 2 on P3}
	$\Omega_{d[L]}$ is a Laurent polynomial in $q_0$, $q_1$, $q_2$ and $q_3$.
plain_text: $_ d[L] $ is a Laurent polynomial in $q_0$, $q_1$, $q_2$ and $q_3$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Polynomiality/Laurentness conjecture for Omega_{d[L]} in equivariant variables for Tot_{P3} O(-2)^2. Structurally useful for computing membrane indices; of clear specialist interest but limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Laurent-polynomial behavior verified numerically to degree ten and suggested by symmetry of vertex formula; plausible to be proved by combinatorial evaluation of the partition sums.
rationale: The Laurent-polynomial property for Ω_{d[L]} is conjectural and verified experimentally up to degree ten, not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1445–1453 and 1458: Conjecture stated; 'We checked that Conjecture holds up to degree ten numerically.'

title: Independence of tautological classes and cohomological stability for strata of differentials
body_tex:
\label{conj:upperbound}
Let $g \geq 2$ and let $\mu$ be any partition of $\ell(2g - 2)$ such that $m_i \neq -\ell$ for all $i$. Then the kernel of \eqref{t1} is non-trivial in degree $* = \lfloor g/3 \rfloor + 1$.
plain_text: Let $g >= 2$ and let $$ be any partition of $ ell (2g - 2)$ such that $m_i != - ell $ for all $i$. Then the kernel of is non-trivial in degree $* = g/3 + 1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts sharp degree of nontrivial tautological relations for strata of differentials, linking to known R^*(M_g) relations; clarifies cohomological structure of strata—meaningful within moduli theory.
viability_rationale: Reduction to a single explicit power-series coefficient, extensive computer verification for g≤30, and concrete relation sources (R^*(M_g)) give a plausible route; a uniform nonvanishing proof across all g remains the main obstacle.
rationale: Conjecture asserts nontrivial kernel in specific degree; paper proves it only up to g≤30 and reduces general case to nonvanishing of a coefficient, so conjecture remains open in general.
evidence_snippet: Lines 235–243 state the conjecture and Theorem 1.2 verifies it only for g≤30; general case left unresolved.

title: The Perimeter Winternitz Theorem in a Triangle
body_tex: The fraction of the perimeter represented by the shortest perimeter part cut by a variable line through the centroid of a compact planar convex set is a number between 3/10 and 1/2. The bound 3/10 is approached by scales of triangles approaching the 5-4-1 degenerate triangle and their medians corresponding to the middle sides. The bound 1/2 is achieved by each line through the centroid of a centrally symmetric convex set.
plain_text: The fraction of the perimeter represented by the shortest perimeter part cut by a variable line through the centroid of a compact planar convex set is a number between 3/10 and 1/2. The bound 3/10 is approached by scales of triangles approaching the 5-4-1 degenerate triangle and their medians corresponding to the middle sides. The bound 1/2 is achieved by each line through the centroid of a centrally symmetric convex set.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Natural extension of a triangle perimeter extremal result to all convex planar sets; an accessible but nontrivial problem in convex/extremal geometry with clear geometric interest.
viability_rationale: Authors proved the triangle case; extremizers plausibly triangles and reducible via polygonal approximation/compactness. Nontrivial but concrete finite-dimensional reductions seem attainable, so modest near-term solvability is plausible.
rationale: Paper proves the triangle case and explicitly conjectures extension to all compact planar convex sets; no proof provided for the general convex-case statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 379–383 state the Perimeter Winternitz Conjecture extending the 3/10 lower bound to all planar convex sets.

title: Cambrian lattices are fractionally Calabi-Yau via 2-cluster combinatorics
body_tex:
Let $A=kL$ be the incidence algebra of a lattice $L$.
Then $A$ is combinatorially Serre formal if and only if $A$ is periodic Serre formal.
plain_text: Let $A=kL$ be the incidence algebra of a lattice $L$. Then $A$ is combinatorially Serre formal if and only if $A$ is periodic Serre formal.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Links combinatorial and periodic Serre-formality for incidence algebras of lattices. Valuable for classification in representation theory; important to specialists though technically focused and lattice-specific.
viability_rationale: Small-case verification and distributive classification give encouraging structure, but a general equivalence across all lattices remains a nontrivial algebraic classification problem.
rationale: The appendix poses this equivalence conjecture for incidence algebras of lattices; authors verified small cases but did not prove the general equivalence in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1709-1713: "We pose the following conjecture: \begin{Conjecture} Let A=kL be the incidence algebra of a lattice L. Then A is combinatorially Serre formal if and only if A is periodic Serre formal. \end{Conjecture}"

title: Counting points in thin sets: A survey
body_tex:
\label{conj_affine_hypersurface}
 For any hypersurface $H \subset \Abb^n$ defined as the vanishing set of a  polynomial $F \in \Z[X_1,\ldots,X_n]$ of degree $d \geq 2$ with absolutely irreducible leading form, 
   \[ N_\aff(H,B) \ll_{n,d}  B^{\dimA H-1}s(B),\]
   for a ``small factor'' $s(B)$, which could take the form   $s(B)\ll_\ep B^\ep$ (for any $\ep>0$); or $s(B) \leq (\log B)^{\ga(n,d)}$ for some $\ga(n,d) > 0$; or $s(B) \leq 1$.
plain_text: For any hypersurface $H subset ^n$ defined as the vanishing set of a polynomial $F in $ of degree $d >= 2$ with absolutely irreducible leading form, \[ N_(H,B) _ n,d B^ H-1 s(B),\] for a ``small factor'' $s(B)$, which could take the form $s(B)_ B^$ (for any $>0$); or $s(B) <= ( B)^ (n,d) $ for some $(n,d) > 0$; or $s(B) <= 1$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Uniform affine-hypersurface bounds are crucial for deducing projective results and much progress exists, but the statement is narrower (notably unresolved for cubics), making it a meaningful specialist conjecture.
viability_rationale: Survey shows all homogeneous cases with d≠3 proved (recent breakthroughs); only homogeneous cubic case remains, with partial Salberger bounds. The gap is concrete and localized, but cubics are delicate—plausible but uncertain within 5 years.
rationale: The authors and cited works prove the uniform affine hypersurface conjecture in many cases (all homogeneous d≠3), but they explicitly state the homogeneous degree d=3 uniform case remains open.
evidence_snippet: “The Uniform Affine Dimension Growth Conjecture holds for homogeneous F with degree d ≠ 3... It remains an open problem ... for d=3.” (local context lines 863-869).

title: Orbit-Level Stretching in Cubic Fourier-Galerkin Navier-Stokes: Sharp Incidence, Spectral Decay, and a Continuation Criterion
body_tex:
\label{conj:spherical-incidence}
For the spherical truncation $\Lambda_N^{\mathrm{sph}}$,
\[
  S^{\mathrm{sph}}(N) = \Theta\!\bigl(N^3\,(\log N)^c\bigr)
\]
for some constant $c\ge 0$.
plain_text: For the spherical truncation $_N^ sph $, \[ S^ sph (N) = \!(N^3\,( N)^c) \] for some constant $c >= 0$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asymptotic form of spherical incidence counts impacts lattice-point geometry and spectral estimates for Galerkin truncations; interesting within this geometric-analytic niche but limited in scope.
viability_rationale: Depends on delicate lattice-point/cap-counting estimates (Bourgain–Rudnick style) for thin spherical shells; specialized but nontrivial. Partial techniques exist, so plausible but not imminent — moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The spherical-incidence formula is stated as a conjectured expectation in the paper and is not proved; authors present it as an open question.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: “For the spherical truncation ... S^{sph}(N)=Θ(N^3 (log N)^c) for some c≥0.” (local context lines 2195-2201).

title: Simplex Stratification and Phase Boundaries in the Partition Graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:triangular-threshold}
For every $r\ge 0$,
\[
\tau(r)=1+\frac{r(r+1)}{2}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Delta(n)=\max\Bigl\{r:\ 1+\frac{r(r+1)}{2}\le n\Bigr\}.
\]
plain_text: For every $r >= 0$, \[ (r)=1+r(r+1) 2 . \] Equivalently, \[ (n)= r:\ 1+r(r+1) 2 <= n . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Triangular-number threshold for first appearance of simplex layers in the partition graph is an elegant combinatorial pattern suggested by computations; interesting but a local, finite-structure combinatorial conjecture.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence up to r=7 and explicit local star/top capacity criteria plus staircase+one-cell candidate make a concrete, finite combinatorial proof plausible; remaining technical cases may still be nontrivial.
rationale: Computations up to n≤30 support the triangular threshold formula, but the global statement is left explicitly as a conjecture and open problem.
evidence_snippet: “The successive threshold gaps ... consistent with the triangular formula; these global statements are left as conjectures and open problems.” (local context lines 671-674, 686-685).

title: Simplex Stratification and Phase Boundaries in the Partition Graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:first-family}
For every $r\ge 2$, the first-occurrence layer
\[
L_r(\tau(r))
\]
consists exactly of the partitions obtained from the staircase partition $\delta_r$ by adding one
cell at an addable corner.
plain_text: For every $r >= 2$, the first-occurrence layer \[ L_r((r)) \] consists exactly of the partitions obtained from the staircase partition $_r$ by adding one cell at an addable corner.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A clear combinatorial structural claim about first-occurrence layers tied to staircase partitions; supported computationally but confined to partition-graph theory, giving meaningful specialist content with limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Concrete local capacity criteria and exhaustive computations up to r=7 give a plausible concrete route; nontrivial global combinatorial work remains but resolution within 5 years is moderately likely.
rationale: The paper presents this as an explicit conjecture supported by computations for n<=30 but does not prove it; authors state global statements remain conjectures and open problems.
evidence_snippet: Computations suggest a rigid threshold pattern ... while the corresponding global statements are left as conjectures and open problems. (Conjecture: For every r≥2, L_r(τ(r)) consists exactly of partitions from δ_r with one added cell.)

title: Simplex Stratification and Phase Boundaries in the Partition Graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:l1-antennas}
For every $n\ge 4$, the only vertices of local simplex dimension $1$ are the two antenna vertices
\[
(n),\qquad (1^n).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
L_1(n)=\{(n),(1^n)\}.
\]
plain_text: For every $n >= 4$, the only vertices of local simplex dimension $1$ are the two antenna vertices \[ (n), (1^n). \] Equivalently, \[ L_1(n)= (n),(1^n) . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A narrow, concrete characterization (L1 consists of the two antenna vertices) supported by data n≤30; valuable for the paper's stratification but mathematically local and technically specific.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence (n≤30) and an exact local characterization via star/top capacities give a concrete combinatorial route to a proof; moderate near-term solvability, though sporadic large-n counterexamples remain possible.
rationale: Authors observe data for 4≤n≤30 supporting the claim but explicitly state it as a conjecture and do not prove it in the paper.
evidence_snippet: One particularly stable feature is |L_1(n)|=2 (4≤n≤30), these two vertices are (n),(1^n). The data further suggest that for all n≥4 the only vertices of local simplex dimension 1 are the two antenna vertices. (Conjecture stated.)

title: Simplex Stratification and Phase Boundaries in the Partition Graph
body_tex:
\label{conj:framework-low-layers}
For every $n\ge 4$,
\[
\Fw_n\subseteq L_1(n)\cup L_2(n),
\qquad
\Fw_n\cap L_1(n)=\{(n),(1^n)\}.
\]
Equivalently, every non-antennal framework vertex has local simplex dimension exactly $2$.
plain_text: For every $n >= 4$, \[ _n subseteq L_1(n) cup L_2(n), _n cap L_1(n)= (n),(1^n) . \] Equivalently, every non-antennal framework vertex has local simplex dimension exactly $2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Structural claim that the framework lies in the two outermost layers, giving geometric insight into phase-boundaries; significant within this model but largely specialized to the partition-graph context.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence (n≤30) and an exact local star/top capacity characterization reduce the problem to forbidding certain local configurations; plausible combinatorial closure but nontrivial to prove for all n—moderate short-term chance.
rationale: The framework confinement statement is offered as a conjecture; computations support it but the authors explicitly note they do not have a proof.
evidence_snippet: Taken together, these observations suggest that the framework remains confined to the low-simplex part of the graph. ... Conjecture: For every n≥4, Fw_n⊆L_1(n)∪L_2(n), Fw_n∩L_1(n)={ (n),(1^n) }.

title: Extending partial edge-colorings of bounded size in Cartesian products of graphs
body_tex:
\label{basic} \cite{cartesian}
    If $G$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $k < \chi'(G)$ edges can be extended to a proper $\chi'(G)$-edge-coloring, then every precoloring of at most $k+1$ edges of $G \square K_2$ is extendable to a proper $(\chi'(G)+1)$-edge-coloring of $G \square K_2$.
plain_text: If $G$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $k < '(G)$ edges can be extended to a proper $'(G)$-edge-coloring, then every precoloring of at most $k+1$ edges of $G K_2$ is extendable to a proper $('(G)+1)$-edge-coloring of $G K_2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Casselgren's conjecture for Cartesian product with K2 generalizes hypercube extension results; a meaningful problem in edge-precoloring extension theory with solid combinatorial content but limited cross-field breadth.
viability_rationale: Paper settles numerous cases (trees, complete, complete bipartite, small Δ, triangle-free r-regular with k<Δ, subcubic Class 2). Remaining general higher-Δ/Class 2 cases look technical but plausibly resolvable within five years.
rationale: The Casselgren–Petros–Fufa basic conjecture is cited as motivation; the paper verifies many special cases but does not prove the conjecture in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If G has the extension property up to k<χ'(G), then every precoloring of at most k+1 edges of G□K_2 is extendable. Paper verifies trees, complete graphs, small Δ, and other special cases, but not the full conjecture.

title: Extending partial edge-colorings of bounded size in Cartesian products of graphs
body_tex:
\label{general}
    If $G$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $k < \chi'(G)$ edges can be extended to a proper $\chi'(G)$-edge-coloring and $H$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $l < \chi'(H)$ edges can be extended to a proper $\chi'(H)$-edge-coloring, then every precoloring of at most $k+l+1$ edges of $G \square H$ is extendable to a proper $(\chi'(G)+\chi'(H))$-edge-coloring of $G \square H$.
plain_text: If $G$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $k < '(G)$ edges can be extended to a proper $'(G)$-edge-coloring and $H$ is a graph that every precoloring of at most $l < '(H)$ edges can be extended to a proper $'(H)$-edge-coloring, then every precoloring of at most $k+l+1$ edges of $G H$ is extendable to a proper $('(G)+'(H))$-edge-coloring of $G H$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A broad generalization to arbitrary Cartesian products (G square H) that, if true, would resolve many extension questions; important within graph edge-coloring though specialized to this subfield.
viability_rationale: Significant partial progress for many graph classes (trees, even cycles, triangle-free r-regular, subcubic G with K2), but the general broad statement likely needs new techniques—moderate chance of resolution in five years.
rationale: The generalized Cartesian-product conjecture is proposed; the authors prove it in various restricted settings but do not claim a complete proof.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture (general): If G and H have extension properties up to k,l then any precoloring of at most k+l+1 edges of G□H is extendable. Stated as open; paper proves special cases only.

title: Extending partial edge-colorings of bounded size in Cartesian products of graphs
body_tex: Let $G$ be an $r$-regular, triangle-free graph. If any precoloring of at most $k < r$ edges of $G$ can be extended to a proper $\chi'(G)$-edge-coloring of $G$, then any precoloring of at most $k+n$ edges of $G \square K_{n,n}$ can be extended to a proper $(\chi'(G)+n)$-edge-coloring of $G \square K_{n,n}$.
plain_text: Let $G$ be an $r$-regular, triangle-free graph. If any precoloring of at most $k < r$ edges of $G$ can be extended to a proper $'(G)$-edge-coloring of $G$, then any precoloring of at most $k+n$ edges of $G K_ n,n $ can be extended to a proper $('(G)+n)$-edge-coloring of $G K_ n,n $.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A specific extension statement for r-regular triangle-free G and K_{n,n} linking to hypercube conjectures; interesting for implications but conditional and focused.
viability_rationale: Substantial partial results (many H, subcubic cases) indicate active progress, but essential triangle-free/regular assumptions and new techniques are required to handle K_{n,n}; plausible within 5 years but uncertain.
rationale: This K_{n,n}-variant is posed in concluding remarks as an open conjecture; the paper does not resolve it and suggests it would imply other open conjectures.
evidence_snippet: Concluding remarks: Conjecture: For r-regular triangle-free G, if precolorings of ≤k<r edges extend, then any precoloring of ≤k+n edges of G□K_{n,n} extends. Authors leave this as open, noting implications.

title: Extending partial edge-colorings of bounded size in Cartesian products of graphs
body_tex:
\cite{hypercube}
    If $n$ and $d$ are positive integers, and $\varphi$ is a proper edge-precoloring of $K_{n,n}^d$, with at most $nd - 1$ precolored edges, then $\varphi$ extends to a proper $nd$-edge-coloring of $K_{n,n}^d$.
plain_text: If $n$ and $d$ are positive integers, and $$ is a proper edge-precoloring of $K_ n,n ^d$, with at most $nd - 1$ precolored edges, then $$ extends to a proper $nd$-edge-coloring of $K_ n,n ^d$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Nontrivial edge-precoloring extension problem for Cartesian products/hypercube powers: meaningful within graph-coloring theory with structural consequences for hypercube cases, but limited broader mathematical reach.
viability_rationale: Specialized graph-coloring conjecture with partial progress in Cartesian products; active area but still requires new ideas to remove structural assumptions—moderate near-term chance.
rationale: Presented as a cited conjecture of Casselgren, Markström and Pham and not proved in this paper; authors state it would follow from their broader conjecture but do not resolve it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 722–726: "Establishing this conjecture ... would imply a solution to the following conjecture of Casselgren, Markström and Pham. ... If n and d are positive integers ... with at most nd-1 precolored edges, then φ extends ..."

title: Hyper-K\"ahler varieties: Lagrangian fibrations, atomic sheaves, and categories
plain_text: Let $M$ be a projective irreducible symplectic variety. Then $M_reg$ has finite fundamental group.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Central expectation in symplectic/irreducible symplectic variety theory; finiteness of pi1(M_reg) impacts quasi-étale covers and classification of singular hyper-Kähler varieties.
viability_rationale: Deep structural conjecture in irreducible symplectic varieties tied to Lagrangian fibration conjectures; nontrivial and plausibly resists resolution within five years.
rationale: Stated as a general expectation (Rapagnetta) and referenced as an open conjecture; authors do not claim a proof and discuss implications if true.
evidence_snippet: Lines 480–485: "We mention the following general expectation, which we learned from Rapagnetta... Let M be a projective irreducible symplectic variety. Then M_reg has finite fundamental group."

title: Hyper-K\"ahler varieties: Lagrangian fibrations, atomic sheaves, and categories
body_tex:
\label{Con:Atomic1Obs}
Let $E \in \Db(X)$ be a simple atomic object. Then $E$ is $1$-obstructed.
plain_text: Let $E in (X)$ be a simple atomic object. Then $E$ is $1$-obstructed.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A focused derived-category/atomic-object conjecture linking simplicity to 1-obstructedness; important for deformation theory of objects but specialized to categorical/hyper-Kähler contexts.
viability_rationale: Beckmann's equivalence between atomicity and 1-obstructedness is a concrete categorical statement; active research and structural theorems make near-term resolution plausible but nontrivial.
rationale: Attributed to Beckmann as a conjecture (Conjecture A & corollary), stated explicitly and not proved here; authors reference it as open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1352–1357: "Beckmann conjectures ... For simple atomic objects the reverse implication also holds. Conjecture (Beckmann): Let E in D^b(X) be a simple atomic object. Then E is 1-obstructed."

title: Hyper-K\"ahler varieties: Lagrangian fibrations, atomic sheaves, and categories
body_tex: Let $E$ be a stable atomic sheaf, then any nonzero element in the image of the obstruction map $\mathrm{obs}_E(\eta) \in \Ext^2(E,E)$ has the hard Lefschetz property for the Yoneda product on $\Ext^{\bullet}(E,E)$.
plain_text: Let $E$ be a stable atomic sheaf, then any nonzero element in the image of the obstruction map $obs_E() in ^2(E,E)$ has the hard Lefschetz property for the Yoneda product on $^ (E,E)$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Technical but meaningful for constructing symplectic moduli spaces: asserts hard Lefschetz for obstruction-image elements, primarily influential within moduli and atomic-sheaf theory.
viability_rationale: Hard Lefschetz property for obstruction-image elements is technical and tied to moduli smoothness; approachable but depends on deeper atomic/obstruction results.
rationale: Stated as Beckmann's conjecture in the text; presented as an open statement necessary for symplectic moduli constructions and not proved in the paper.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1634–1638: "To have a symplectic moduli space one should prove the following conjecture, which was put forward in [Beckmann]. Conjecture: Let E be a stable atomic sheaf, then any nonzero element ... has the hard Lefschetz property."

title: Hyper-K\"ahler varieties: Lagrangian fibrations, atomic sheaves, and categories
body_tex:
Let $E$ be a stable atomic vector bundle on a hyper-K\"ahler manifold. Then the Yoneda product
\[
\Ext^1(E,E) \times \Ext^1(E,E) \longrightarrow \Ext^2(E,E)
\]
is skew-symmetric.
plain_text: Let $E$ be a stable atomic vector bundle on a hyper-K\"ahler manifold. Then the Yoneda product \[ ^1(E,E) x ^1(E,E) ^2(E,E) \] is skew-symmetric.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A concrete structural claim (skew-symmetry of Yoneda product) for stable atomic bundles on hyper-Kähler manifolds; useful for smoothness arguments but fairly specialized.
viability_rationale: Skew-symmetry of the Yoneda product for stable atomic bundles is a difficult formality-type claim; progress likely requires new structural inputs—moderate-low near-term chance.
rationale: Explicitly presented as Beckmann's conjecture (difficult conjecture) needed for smoothness arguments; the paper does not prove it.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1673–1681: "The proof will be drastically simplified if one could establish the following difficult conjecture ... Conjecture (Beckmann): Let E be a stable atomic vector bundle on a hyper-Kähler manifold. Then the Yoneda product ... is skew-symmetric."

title: Anti-concentration of polynomials: $L^{p}$ balls and symmetric measures
body_tex:
\label{conj:l2bound}
	Let $d \in \N$. There exists a constant $C_d>0$, depending only on $d$, such that for every isotropic, log-concave and $H_n$-invariant measure $\mu$ on $\R^n$ and every degree-$d$ homogeneous polynomial $f:\R^n\to \R$,  
	$$\int\limits_{\R^n} f^2d\mu \geq C_d.$$
	Consequently, since homogeneous functions of odd degree are odd, in that case $$\mathrm{Var}_\mu(f) \geq C_d.$$
plain_text: Let $d in $. There exists a constant $C_d>0$, depending only on $d$, such that for every isotropic, log-concave and $H_n$-invariant measure $$ on $^n$ and every degree-$d$ homogeneous polynomial $f:^n to $, $$_ ^n f^2d >= C_d.$$ Consequently, since homogeneous functions of odd degree are odd, in that case $$Var_(f) >= C_d.$$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A uniform, dimension-free variance lower bound for degree-d homogeneous polynomials under all isotropic log-concave H_n-invariant measures would yield strong anti-concentration consequences; proven for d≤3, even-degree complexities make it challenging but central.
viability_rationale: Proved for d=2,3 and method extends partially; even-degree classification (d>=4) poses hard obstacles (many convex polynomials, NP-hard checks), so resolution within five years plausible but uncertain.
rationale: The paper states the L^2 lower-bound conjecture explicitly and notes they could not establish it uniformly (only d=2,3 cases treated); it is presented as an open question.
evidence_snippet: Lines 447–456, 463–466: authors state Conjecture \ref{conj:l2bound} and remark they could not establish it uniformly, giving computed C_2,C_3 and partial results.

title: Axial Morphology of the Partition Graph: Self-Conjugate Axis, Spine, and Concentration
body_tex:
For each invariant $I\in\mathcal I_{\loc}$, there exists a constant $s_I$ such that
\[
\rho_I^{\mathrm{sp}}(n)\le s_I
\]
for all sufficiently large axial $n$.
plain_text: For each invariant $I in I_ $, there exists a constant $s_I$ such that \[ _I^ sp (n) <= s_I \] for all sufficiently large axial $n$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserts uniform boundedness of axial/spinal concentration radii for local invariants of the partition graph. Computational evidence is limited; meaningful to combinatorics/partition-graph community but of narrow broader impact.
viability_rationale: Author gives computational evidence (n≤30) and structural lemmas; asymptotic boundedness remains unaddressed. Problem seems approachable by combinatorial/asymptotic partition analysis but lacks clear finite reduction.
rationale: The paper explicitly states it does not address the asymptotic validity of these bounded-concentration statements and poses them as conjectures for future work.
evidence_snippet: Line 773: "The present paper does not address their asymptotic validity." (Conjectures 757–771 are posed without proof.)

title: Contraction properties for holomorphic functions via isoperimetric stability on the Bergman ball
plain_text: Let $:[0,1] to R$ be a convex function. For every $p in (0,)$, $>n$, and every holomorphic function $f in A^p_(_n)$ normalized by \[ \|f\|_ A^p_ =1, \] one has equation* _ _n \!(|f(z)|^p (1-|z|^2)^)\,dv_g(z) \ <= \ _ _n \!((1-|z|^2)^)\,dv_g(z). equation*
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Convex-order extremizer conjecture in weighted Bergman spaces (f≡1). Nontrivial analytic/geometric statement linking isoperimetric stability and functional inequalities; central within complex analysis and Bergman-space extremal problems.
viability_rationale: Local perturbative validity proved and tied to isoperimetric stability; global convex-order inequality remains nontrivial. Partial analytic/geometric tools increase plausibility but full proof is still challenging.
rationale: The conjecture is cited from Nicola–Riccardi–Tilli and the paper proves only local (near f≡1) validity, not the full global statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 169–176 and 190–197: conjecture cited; authors prove it only "near f≡1" (perturbative regime), not in general.

title: The relative movable cone conjecture for K-trivial fibrations in varieties with well-clipped movable cones
plain_text: Let $f : (X,) to S$ be a klt $K$-trivial fiber space. There exists a rational polyhedral cone $$ which is a (weak) fundamental domain under the action of the group of relative automorphisms $(X/S,)$ preserving the boundary on \[^+(X/S) := Conv((X/S) cap N^1(X/S)_ ).\] There exists a rational polyhedral cone $'$ which is a (weak) fundamental domain under the action of the group of relative pseudoautomorphisms $(X/S,)$ preserving the boundary on \[^+(X/S) := Conv( (X/S) cap N^1(X/S)_ ).\]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The (relative) Kawamata–Morrison cone conjecture is a major birational-geometry prediction with deep consequences (finiteness of models, structure of movable/nef cones); central and long-standing in the field.
viability_rationale: A variant of the Kawamata–Morrison cone conjecture — a long-standing deep problem. Paper proves substantial special cases, but full relative conjecture in general is unlikely to be resolved within five years.
rationale: The conjecture is the general (weak) Kawamata–Morrison cone conjecture; the paper proves it for a specific class of fibrations but not in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Lines 213–214 and 271–275: paper proves the weak relative movable cone conjecture for certain fibrations, while the general conjecture "remains open in full generality."

title: The search for exotic knot traces
body_tex:
\label{conj:fibSQP}
    Fibered and strongly quasi-positive (or strongly quasi-negative) knots do not have friends.
plain_text: Fibered and strongly quasi-positive (or strongly quasi-negative) knots do not have friends.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Data-driven knot-theoretic conjecture that fibered strongly quasi-(un)positive knots have no friends. Empirically motivated and plausible, interesting within knot theory but limited external reach.
viability_rationale: Strong empirical absence of fibered strongly quasi-(anti)positive knots among computed friends motivates conjecture; plausible proofs may come from Floer/contact invariants but nontrivial obstructions remain.
rationale: Authors formulate this knot-theoretic conjecture based on census data; no proof is given and it is presented as a conjecture for future work.
evidence_snippet: Lines 194–198: "This motivates the following conjecture... Fibered and strongly quasi-positive ... knots do not have friends." No proof provided.

title: The search for exotic knot traces
plain_text: Let $K$ be a knot with unknotting number one. Then the following are equivalent. $K$ is a twisted Whitehead double. For every unknotting crossing $x$ of $K$, the Piccirillo friends $K_x$ and $K'_x$ are isotopic to $K$. There exists an unknotting crossing $x$ of $K$ such that the Piccirillo friends $K_x$ and $K'_x$ are isotopic to $K$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Characterizes when Piccirillo friends equal the original knot for unknotting-number-one knots (twisted Whitehead doubles). A neat classification problem with practical computational motivation, specialist in scope.
viability_rationale: Concrete, narrowly scoped statement supported by exhaustive computational census and structural Piccirillo analysis; likely attack via diagrammatic/topological classification and known invariants could settle it soon.
rationale: The Piccirillo friends equivalence is proposed as a conjecture motivated by computational evidence; the paper verifies only cases and does not prove the equivalence in general.
evidence_snippet: Lines 601–607 and 615–622: Proposition gives empirical evidence; Conjecture ℓ stated afterward without a general proof.

title: Single-letter one-way distillable entanglement for non-degradable states
body_tex:
\label{conj:spin-alignment}
Fix a probability distribution $\{p_{\vec x}\}_{\vec x\in\{0,1\}^n}$, the minimization of the von Neumann entropy 
\begin{equation}\label{eqn:spin von Neumann}
  \min_{\rho_{\vec x}} S \left(\sum_{\vec x\in\{0,1\}^n} p_{\vec x}\, \mc N_{\vec x}(\rho_{\vec x}) \right)
\end{equation}
has an optimal choice of input states $\{\rho_{\vec x}\}$ for which
\[
\rho_{\vec x}=\bigotimes_{i=1}^n \tau_{x_i}^{(i)},
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
\tau_0^{(i)}=|\psi_{\max}\rangle\langle\psi_{\max}|_{A_0^{(i)}},\quad
\tau_1^{(i)}=|\phi_{\max}\rangle\langle\phi_{\max}|_{A_1^{(i)}},
\]
where $\ket{\psi_{\max}}$ (resp.\ $\ket{\phi_{\max}}$) is any unit vector in the maximal-eigenspace of $\sigma_0$ (resp.\ $\sigma_1$), and $|\vec x|_0:=|\{i:\ x_i=0\}|$. Equivalently, each freely chosen spin is aligned with a maximal eigenvector of the corresponding fixed state.
plain_text: Fix a probability distribution $ p_ x _ x in 0,1 ^n $, the minimization of the von Neumann entropy equation _ _ x S (_ x in 0,1 ^n p_ x \, N_ x (_ x ) ) equation has an optimal choice of input states $ _ x $ for which \[ _ x =_ i=1 ^n _ x_i ^ (i) , _0^ (i) =|_ _ |_ A_0^ (i) , _1^ (i) =|_ _ |_ A_1^ (i) , \] where $ _ $ (resp.\ $ _ $) is any unit vector in the maximal-eigenspace of $_0$ (resp.\ $_1$), and $| x|_0:=| i:\ x_i=0 |$. Equivalently, each freely chosen spin is aligned with a maximal eigenvector of the corresponding fixed state.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Spin-alignment phenomenon predicting product-state optimality in entropy minimization; underlies additivity and single-letter formulas in quantum information, significant for channel-capacity and entropy optimization questions.
viability_rationale: Authors prove several special cases and perturbative results; the spin-alignment principle is structured and admits further tractable reductions, suggesting plausible resolution in coming years for many cases.
rationale: The spin-alignment phenomenon is posed as a conjecture; the paper proves several special cases but does not establish the general conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 970–974: conjecture stated; following text: "Here we provide the rigorous justifications for several special cases."

title: On corank 4 unitary representations of classical groups
plain_text: HJLLZ24 , see also Conjecture ] Let $G$ be a general connected reductive group defined over $F$. Assume that the local Arthur conjecture as in holds for every Levi subgroup $M$ of $G$. Then the following two sets are equal: $$_ A ^ (G) = _ u (G).$$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Unitary Dual Conjecture equating an Arthur-derived candidate set with the full unitary dual. Deep Langlands/representation-theory bridge with wide arithmetic and harmonic-analytic consequences; high field-level significance.
viability_rationale: Unitary dual conjecture is a broad, deep Langlands/Arthur-level classification problem. Recent verifications in low corank cases are valuable, but full general resolution is unlikely within five years.
rationale: The extracted conjecture is the general Unitary Dual Conjecture. The paper verifies it for corank 4 representations in specific classical groups but does not prove the full general conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 252–266 and 271–275: conjecture stated generally; authors verify it for corank 4 symplectic and split SO_9, not full generality.

title: A strengthened $(\infty, n)$-categorical pasting theorem
body_tex:
The \( \infty \)
bd category of diagrammatic \( (\infty, n) \)
bd categories is the localisation of \( 
Catinf{n} \) at the set of fundamental spines of molecules.
plain_text: The \( \) category of diagrammatic \( (, n) \) categories is the localisation of \( n \) at the set of fundamental spines of molecules.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Important for model comparison in higher category theory: showing the diagrammatic model is a localisation would clarify foundational equivalences between ∞-categorical presentations, relevant within the specialized community.
viability_rationale: Strong local progress: strict case and all molecules dimension ≤3 settled; builds on Campion's work. But full homotopical comparison for arbitrary n involves deep, subtle higher-categorical issues—moderate chance within five years.
rationale: The localisation statement is presented as an explicit conjecture in the paper and the authors state they will make progress but do not prove it here.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: The ∞-category of diagrammatic (∞,n)-categories is the localisation of nCat_∞ at the set of fundamental spines of molecules. The authors state they will make progress towards this conjecture.

title: Bollob\'as-Meir TSP Conjecture Holds Asymptotically
plain_text: For any finite set of points $X subseteq [0, 1]^k$, there exists a Hamiltonian cycle $H$ on $X$ with $S_k(H) <= 2 * k^ k / 2 $ if $k != 3$, and $S_3(H) <= 2^ 7/2 $ if $k = 3$. In other words, \[ = cases 2 * k^ k / 2 & for k != 3, 2^ 7/2 & for k = 3. cases \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A sharp universal constant for the geometric TSP in high dimensions is a meaningful problem in combinatorial geometry and optimization, but it is narrow in scope and largely specialist compared to field‑level flagship problems.
viability_rationale: Significant asymptotic progress and refined bounds exist; exact finite-k constants remain delicate. Active advances make partial or full resolution plausible but not highly likely in five years.
rationale: The paper disproves k=3 and proves asymptotic form (c_k = k^{k/2}(2+o_k(1))) but does not establish the exact equality for all k, so the original exact conjecture remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 252-256, 257-266: 'Bollobás and Meir conjectured SHC = 2·k^{k/2}. Case k=3 disproved; exact value unknown for any k>2.'

title: Moment inequalities for Chow polynomials of matroids and bounds on Chern numbers
body_tex:
\label{conj:coneofineq}
We conjecture that the inequality in Corollary \ref{cor:ineqfof}
\[
\sum_{J\subset\{1, \ldots, d\}}\prod_{i=1}^{m+1} (n_i-1)
           \left(\sum_{i=1}^{m+1}\frac{n_i(n_i-3)}{2}\right) N_J \;\leq\; 0
\]
is a nonnegative linear combination of the monotonicity inequalities established in Lemma~\ref{lem:elemonoineq}.
plain_text: We conjecture that the inequality in Corollary \[ _ J subset 1, , d _ i=1 ^ m+1 (n_i-1) (_ i=1 ^ m+1 n_i(n_i-3) 2 ) N_J \; <= \; 0 \] is a nonnegative linear combination of the monotonicity inequalities established in Lemma .

interestingness_rationale: Expressing a derived inequality as a nonnegative cone combination of known monotonicity inequalities is a natural structural simplification but is a fairly technical, local statement within matroid‑inequality manipulations.
viability_rationale: A finite, combinatorial linear-inequality claim verified computationally for moderate ranks; amenable to combinatorial/linear-algebraic proof techniques, so moderately likely to be resolved soon.
rationale: The authors pose this combinatorial decomposition conjecture, verify it by computer up to d≤19, but do not prove it in general, so it remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 903-910, 912-914: 'We conjecture that the inequality ... is a nonnegative linear combination ...' 'We verified Conjecture ... by computer for all ranks d+1 with d≤19.'

title: Individual-based stochastic model with unbounded growth, birth and death rates: a tightness result
body_tex:
We work under the assumptions of Theorem~\ref{theo:convergence} without Assumption~\ref{ass:finallejd}, but assume in addition that the sequence $(\mu_{0}^{K})_{K \in \mathbb{N}^{*}}$ converges in law towards $\mu_{0}^{*}$ in $(\mathcal{M}_{1+ \mathrm{Id}+\omega}(\mathbb{R}^{*}_{+}),\mathrm{w})$. Then, for all $T \geq 0$, the sequence $\bigg(\left(\mu^{K}_{t},R^{K}_{t}\right)_{t \in [0,T]}\bigg)_{K \in \mathbb{N}^{*}}$ is tight in $\mathbb{D}([0,T], (\mathcal{M}_{1+ \mathrm{Id} + \omega}(\mathbb{R}^{*}_{+}),\mathrm{w}) \times [0,R_{\max}])$.
\label{theo:final}
plain_text: We work under the assumptions of Theorem without Assumption , but assume in addition that the sequence $(_ 0 ^ K )_ K in N^ * $ converges in law towards $_ 0 ^ * $ in $(M_ 1+ Id+ (R^ * _ + ),w)$. Then, for all $T >= 0$, the sequence $((^ K _ t ,R^ K _ t )_ t in [0,T] )_ K in N^ * $ is tight in $D([0,T], (M_ 1+ Id + (R^ * _ + ),w) x [0,R_ ])$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Technical extension of convergence/tightness to a stronger Skorokhod topology for unbounded rates; valuable for rigorous mean-field limits in stochastic population models, but mainly of specialist probabilistic/PDE interest.
viability_rationale: Specialized extension of proven result; authors reduce it to deterministic regularity/finite integrability conditions and note concrete routes (bounded‑rate case, PDE methods). Remaining gap seems technical and plausibly closable within 5 years.
rationale: The paper states this tightness claim as a conjecture and only proves partial results and conditional propositions; the full conjecture is not proved here.
evidence_snippet: Lines 771-774: Conjecture states tightness in D([0,T],(M_{1+Id+ω},w)×[0,R_max]). Proposition and remarks give conditional/partial results, not a full proof.

title: Permutations with a fixed number of occurrences of a pattern: A case generalizing 231
body_tex:
Let $q\in \{4321, 4312\}$. Then there is a constant $K$ so that
     \[
     S_{n,r}(q) \sim KS_{n}(q).
     \]
     Let $q\in \{4123, 3412, 4231, 4213, 3142\}$. Then there is a constant $K$ so that
     \[
     S_{n,r}(q) \sim Kn^rS_{n}(q).
     \]
plain_text: Let $q in 4321, 4312 $. Then there is a constant $K$ so that \[ S_ n,r (q) sim KS_ n (q). \] Let $q in 4123, 3412, 4231, 4213, 3142 $. Then there is a constant $K$ so that \[ S_ n,r (q) sim Kn^rS_ n (q). \]

interestingness_rationale: Finishes an asymptotic classification for length-4 permutation patterns (Conway–Guttman); significant within permutation pattern enumeration and generating-function behavior, but limited scope.
viability_rationale: Most pattern cases already resolved by the authors and others; only one final pattern (4312) resists. Concentrated, well-studied combinatorial problem with substantial numeric evidence, reasonably solvable soon.
rationale: The Conway–Guttman conjecture as stated includes pattern 4312, which the authors note remains unsettled (they prove the other listed cases).
evidence_snippet: Lines 608-616: Conjecture listing patterns. Lines 619-621: authors confirm many cases but state none of their techniques applied to q=4312, which remains open.

title: The non-peripheral curve graph and divergence in big mapping class groups
plain_text: Suppose $$ is stable and $()$ is CB-generated. Then $()$ is either Gromov hyperbolic or it has infinite coarse rank.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A coarse-geometric dichotomy for big mapping class groups: hyperbolic vs infinite coarse rank. A structural, widely relevant conjecture in geometric group theory shaping classification of Map(Σ).
viability_rationale: Broad dichotomy in big mapping class groups; authors handle many regimes but gap (notably ζ=3) remains. Deep structural statement—unlikely to be fully settled within five years.
rationale: The dichotomy is posed as an open conjecture; the paper proves related results under zeta(Σ) bounds but does not prove the full conjecture.
evidence_snippet: Lines 540-543: 'Suppose Σ is stable and Map(Σ) is CB-generated. Then Map(Σ) is either Gromov hyperbolic or it has infinite coarse rank.' Presented as a conjecture.

title: Perfect divisibility of some bull-free graphs and its application
body_tex:

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Hoàng's conjecture that every P5-free graph is perfectly divisible: a natural structural graph-theory problem with consequences for χ-bounds, important within perfect-divisibility research.
viability_rationale: A recent broad graph-theory conjecture (P5-free → perfectly divisible). Progress exists on many subclasses (including bull-free), but full generality appears hard and long‑standing.
rationale: The statement 'Every P5-free graph is perfectly divisible' is cited as Hoàng's conjecture; the paper proves it only for bull-free graphs, not in full generality.
evidence_snippet: Lines 131-136 and 152-153: Conjecture stated. Paper notes these conjectures hold for bull-free graphs, not for all graphs.

title: Perfect divisibility of some bull-free graphs and its application

viability_rationale: Odd‑hole‑free perfect‑divisibility is a wide structural conjecture; substantial subclass progress exists, but full resolution seems unlikely in five years without major new techniques.
rationale: The 'Every odd hole-free graph is perfectly divisible' conjecture is presented from Hoàng and only verified here for bull-free graphs, not proven generally.
evidence_snippet: Lines 131-137 and 152-153: Conjecture given; authors state their results imply the conjectures hold for bull-free graphs, not the full class.

rationale: The fork-free graph conjecture is credited to Karthick et al.; the paper notes it remains open generally and proves it only for bull-free graphs.

title: The intrinsic approach to moduli theory
body_tex:
\label{conj:local-structure-charp} 
  Let $\cX$ be a quasi-separated algebraic stack, locally of finite type over a
  noetherian scheme, with affine automorphism groups.  If $x \in \cX$ is a
  closed point with reductive stabilizer, then there exists an étale morphism 
	$$(\Spec A/\GL_n, w) \to (\cX,x)$$ 
  inducing an isomorphism of automorphism groups at $w$.
plain_text: Let $$ be a quasi-separated algebraic stack, locally of finite type over a noetherian scheme, with affine automorphism groups. If $x in $ is a closed point with reductive stabilizer, then there exists an étale morphism $$( A/_n, w) to (,x)$$ inducing an isomorphism of automorphism groups at $w$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Seeks Local Structure Theorem in positive characteristic giving étale GL_n-quotient neighborhoods for points with reductive stabilizer; foundational for stacks and removing char 0 restrictions.
viability_rationale: A local-structure extension to positive characteristic has a concrete promising strategy (representation-theoretic vanishing) but key obstacles and limited evidence remain.
rationale: Stated explicitly as a conjecture about local structure in positive characteristic; authors note it is unknown and discuss lack of evidence and potential strategies, so it remains open.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: If x∈X is a closed point with reductive stabilizer, there exists an étale morphism (Spec A/GL_n,w)→(X,x) inducing automorphism isomorphism. 'It is not even known...'

title: Decomposing Centrally Symmetric Convex Polyhedral Surfaces into Parallelograms
body_tex: Let $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$, $\dots$, $\delta_N$ be $N$ positive numbers that sum up to $2\pi$. Then every surface in $\overline{\mathcal{M}_{2N}}(\delta_1, \delta_2, \dots, \delta_N)$ can be decomposed into at most $2\binom{2N-2}{2}$ parallelograms, and the decomposition is invariant under the antipodal map.
plain_text: Let $_1$, $_2$, $$, $_N$ be $N$ positive numbers that sum up to $2$. Then every surface in $ M_ 2N (_1, _2, , _N)$ can be decomposed into at most $22N-2 2 $ parallelograms, and the decomposition is invariant under the antipodal map.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: A natural higher-N extension of the authors' parallelogram decomposition results; interesting combinatorially and geometrically but largely a technical/generalization problem with limited broader impact.
viability_rationale: Proved for low N and authors outline extensible methods; technical but concrete obstacles suggest a reasonable chance of generalization within five years.
rationale: Authors proved the statement for small N and formulate the general decomposition bound as a conjecture for higher N, noting methods might extend but leaving it unproved.
evidence_snippet: Conjecture: For δ_1+...+δ_N=2π, every surface in closure M_{2N}(δ_i) can be decomposed into ≤2·C(2N-2,2) parallelograms, decomposition invariant under antipodal map. (Stated as future work.)

title: Motivic Cohomology and K-groups of varieties over higher local fields

title: Probability of super-regular matrices and MDS codes over finite fields
body_tex:

title: A sharp inequality between scalar curvature and the bottom spectrum on complete manifolds

For any manifold $M$ of dimension $m$, there is no complete metric of positive scalar curvature on $\torus^m \# M$.

title: Symplectic blenders near whiskered tori and persistence of saddle-center homoclinics
body_tex:
For every small $e>0$ there exists $\mu(e)$ for which
the orbit $L_1$ in the planar elliptic restricted three-body problem has a homoclinic orbit. Moreover,  there is a neighborhood $U$ of the curve $\mu=\mu(e)$ such that for each $(e,\mu) \in U$ the  time-$2\pi$ map of $R_{e,\mu}$  has a symplectic blender, and the orbit $L_1$ has a homoclinic orbit for a dense subset of $U$.
plain_text: For every small $e>0$ there exists $(e)$ for which the orbit $L_1$ in the planar elliptic restricted three-body problem has a homoclinic orbit. Moreover, there is a neighborhood $U$ of the curve $=(e)$ such that for each $(e,) in U$ the time-$2$ map of $R_ e, $ has a symplectic blender, and the orbit $L_1$ has a homoclinic orbit for a dense subset of $U$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Persistence of L1 homoclinics and creation of symplectic blenders in the elliptic three‑body problem is a significant dynamical conjecture connecting celestial mechanics and modern hyperbolic dynamics.

[Benjamini--Schramm limit]\label{conj:BS}
The sequence $(\Gamma_n)_{n\ge 1}$ converges in the
Benjamini--Schramm (local weak) sense \cite{BenjaminiSchramm2001, Lyons2005}
to the $d$-dimensional integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^d$,          
with its standard nearest-neighbour structure.
Consequently, the spectral measure of $\Gamma_n$ converges
weakly to the spectral measure of the Laplacian on $\mathbb{Z}^d$.
plain_text: [Benjamini--Schramm limit] The sequence $(_n)_ n >= 1 $ converges in the Benjamini--Schramm (local weak) sense to the $d$-dimensional integer lattice $Z^d$, with its standard nearest-neighbour structure. label: real_open_conjecture

Note that the leading term is always $n^2/12$ and the period appears as its denominator. Also note that we have equation* D(0) = D(1) = 0. equation* The corresponding generating function is given by equation H(x) (1 - x^ 12 )^3 , equation where multline* H(x) = x^ 32 + 2x^ 31 + 2x^ 30 + 4x^ 29 + 5x^ 28 + 5x^ 27 + 8x^ 26 + 9x^ 25 + 9x^ 24 + 13x^ 23 + 15x^ 22 + 15x^ 21 + 17x^ 20 + 17x^ 19 + 17x^ 18 + 17x^ 17 + 17x^ 16 + 17x^ 15 + 15x^ 14 + 15x^ 13 + 15x^ 12 + 11x^ 11 + 9x^ 10 + 9x^9 + 6x^8 + 5x^7 + 5x^6 + 3x^5 + 2x^4 + 2x^3 + x^2 multline* The polynomial $H(x)$ has the following properties: its coefficients are nonnegative and unimodal; its coefficients are neither symmetric nor weakly log concave; its roots are not necessarily on the unit circle (can be both inside and outside). The $H$-polynomial factors as equation* H(x) = x^2 (x + 1)^3 (x^2 + x + 1) (x^2 + 1)^2 (x^2 - x + 1)^3 (x^4 - x^2 + 1)^2 (x^7 + x^6 - x^3 + x + 1) equation* Hence, the generating function can be rewritten as equation* x^2 (x^7 + x^6 - x^3 + x + 1) (1-x)^3 (x^2 + 1) (x^2 + x + 1)^2 (x^4 - x^2 + 1) , equation*

title: Computation and sampling for Schubert specializations
body_tex:
\label{conj:delta_boundary}
As $n \to \infty$, the height function of a uniformly random
RBPD of size $n$ converges to a deterministic limit shape,
consisting of four frozen regions adjacent to the corners of the
domain (where the height function is linear)
and a liquid region (where it is curved),
separated by a deterministic \emph{arctic curve}.
The southeast arc of the RBPD arctic curve coincides with
the support of the singular component
of the Schubert permuton (\Cref{conj:permuton}).
plain_text: As $n to $, the height function of a uniformly random RBPD of size $n$ converges to a deterministic limit shape, consisting of four frozen regions adjacent to the corners of the domain (where the height function is linear) and a liquid region (where it is curved), separated by a deterministic arctic curve. The southeast arc of the RBPD arctic curve coincides with the support of the singular component of the Schubert permuton (conj:permuton).
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Establishing a deterministic limit shape and arctic curve for RBPDs ties combinatorial probability, tilings, and permuton limits; it has clear structural consequences within probabilistic combinatorics and integrable-models literature.
viability_rationale: Strong computational evidence and a conditional proposition; main gap is proving frozen-region existence with exponential convergence—plausible but technically nontrivial.
rationale: The paper states the limit-shape/arctic-curve statement as a conjecture and only proves a conditional coincidence result assuming existence/exponential frozen region, so the full conjecture remains unresolved.
evidence_snippet: After the conjecture the paper proves the coincidence assuming the frozen region exists with exponential rate of convergence (Prop. following Conjecture~\ref{conj:delta_boundary}).

title: Computation and sampling for Schubert specializations
body_tex:
\label{conj:layered_asymptotic}
The maximum of $\Upsilon_w$ over layered permutations has the 
same exponential growth rate as 
the maximum over the whole $S_n$, that is,
\begin{equation*}
	\lim_{n\to \infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \log_2 \,\max_{w \in S_n} \Upsilon_w
	\,=\,
	\lim_{n\to \infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \log_2 \,\max_{w \text{ layered}} \Upsilon_w
	\,\approx\, 0.29,
\end{equation*}
where the layered limit was computed in \cite{MoralesPakPanova2019}.
In other words, any potential improvement over layered permutations is subexponential in $n^2$.
plain_text: The maximum of $_w$ over layered permutations has the same exponential growth rate as the maximum over the whole $S_n$, that is, equation* _ n to 1 n^2 _2 \,_ w in S_n _w \,=\, _ n to 1 n^2 _2 \,_ w layered _w \, approx \, 0.29, equation* where the layered limit was computed in . In other words, any potential improvement over layered permutations is subexponential in $n^2$.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Determines the asymptotic source of Stanley's maximization problem for Schubert specializations; significant in algebraic combinatorics but narrowly focused on exponential-growth extremizers.
viability_rationale: Good computational support and local bounds; resolving the global asymptotic equality likely requires new combinatorial/variational methods but seems reachable.
rationale: The layered-asymptotic equality is posed as an expectation/conjecture; the paper offers supporting data and bounds but does not prove the exact limiting equality.
evidence_snippet: Section 'Bounds and asymptotics' states Conjecture~\ref{conj:layered_asymptotic}: the two n^{-2} log-limits over S_n and layered permutations are equal (~0.29).

title: Edge localization and Lifshitz tails for graphs with Ahlfors regular volume growth

For any random distribution of $V_\omega$, $H_\omega$ exhibits Anderson localization throughout its spectrum; that is, it has pure point spectrum with exponentially localized eigenfunctions forming a complete basis in $\ell^2(\mathbb{V})$.

\]

title: Enumeration of Factor Occurrences in $k$-Bonacci Words over an Infinite Alphabet
body_tex:
\label{conj:main}
For every fixed $k \ge 3$ and every non-empty factor
$B \sqsubseteq \mathbf{W}^{(k)}$, there exist an integer
$s(B) \ge 1$ and a polynomial $P_B(y) \in \Z[y]$ such that
\[
  C_B^{(k)}(y)
  = \frac{P_B(y)} 
         {(1 - y - y^2 - \cdots - y^{k-1})^{s(B)}}.
\]
Equivalently, the sequence
$\bigl(c^{(k)}(B;\, n)\bigr)_{n \in \N}$ satisfies a
linear recurrence whose characteristic polynomial divides a power
of
\[
  x^{k-1} - x^{k-2} - \cdots - x - 1.
\]
plain_text: For every fixed $k >= 3$ and every non-empty factor $B W^ (k) $, there exist an integer $s(B) >= 1$ and a polynomial $P_B(y) in $ such that \[ C_B^ (k) (y) = P_B(y) (1 - y - y^2 - - y^ k-1 )^ s(B) . \] Equivalently, the sequence $(c^ (k) (B;\, n))_ n in $ satisfies a linear recurrence whose characteristic polynomial divides a power of \[ x^ k-1 - x^ k-2 - - x - 1. \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Predicts uniform rational generating functions and (k-1)-step Fibonacci-type recurrences for factor occurrence counts—natural and useful in combinatorics on words but a fairly local, technical enumeration conjecture.
viability_rationale: Concrete combinatorial conjecture with substantial verified cases and structural proofs for many families; likely approachable with further combinatorial/automatic methods.
rationale: Given in the concluding remarks as a conjecture about generating functions/recurrences for factors; authors verify small cases computationally but do not prove it generally.
evidence_snippet: Lines 1751–1759 and 1775–1778: Conjecture \ref{conj:main} is stated and computational verification for small factors is reported.

title: Hamiltonicity of inhomogeneous random graphs
body_tex:
Suppose that $W:\Omega^2\to[0,1]$ is a graphon, and $k\ge 2$ is fixed. 
Then $\G(n,W)$ contains a.a.s.\ the $k$-th power of a Hamilton cycle if and only if the following three conditions are fulfilled:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
    \item $W$ is a connected graphon,
    \item we have $\lim_{\alpha\searrow 0}\frac{\mu(\SMALLDEG_\alpha(W))}\alpha=0$, and
    \item the only $K_{k+1}$-fractional cover of $W$ of $L^1$-norm at most $\frac1{k+1}$ is the constant-$\frac{1}{k+1}$ function.
\end{enumerate}
plain_text: Suppose that $W:^2 to [0,1]$ is a graphon, and $k >= 2$ is fixed. Then $(n,W)$ contains a.a.s.\ the $k$-th power of a Hamilton cycle if and only if the following three conditions are fulfilled: [label=()] $W$ is a connected graphon, we have $_ 0 (_(W))=0$, and the only $K_ k+1 $-fractional cover of $W$ of $L^1$-norm at most $1 k+1 $ is the constant-$1 k+1 $ function.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extends Hamiltonicity characterization to k-th powers via K_{k+1}-fractional covers; a substantive and deep extension connecting tiling/cover parameters in graphon theory to higher-order spanning structures.
viability_rationale: The homogeneous (Erdős–Rényi) k-th power Hamiltonicity problem is only partially resolved; the graphon generalization is strictly harder. Existing structural progress is helpful but gives no clear route to a full resolution within five years.
rationale: This k-th power Hamiltonicity criterion is presented as a conjecture generalizing the main theorem; the paper verifies only k=1 (and discusses obstacles), not the general k case.
evidence_snippet: "Hence, our conjecture, whose case k=1 corresponds to Theorem~\ref{thm:mainHC}, is as follows." followed by the k-th power conjecture. (lines 1224-1234)

title: Dynkin diagrams, generalized Nahm sums and 2d CFTs
plain_text: Let $X$ and $Y$ be Dynkin diagrams of type $ABCDEFGT$ with Cartan matrices $C(X)$ and $C(Y)$, respectively. Let $D(X)$ denote the diagonal matrix whose $j$-th diagonal entry is defined as the quotient $_j^2/^2$ of the square lengths of the simple roots, where $_j$ is the $j$-th simple root and $$ is a short root. We set equation A(X,Y)=C(X) C(Y)^ -1 and D(X,Y)=D(X) D(Y), equation where $$ is the Kronecker product of two matrices. We also set $$ C(X,Y)=-c(X,Y)/24, $$ where the central charge $c(X,Y)$ is given by equation c(X,Y)= tr(D(X,Y))h(X) h(X)+h(Y) , equation and $h(-)$ is the Coxeter number. Then $(A(X,Y),0,C(X,Y),D(X,Y))$ is a modular quadruple.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Broad generalization of Nahm's/modularity folklore to many Dynkin types linking generalized Nahm sums, Lie theory and 2d CFTs; resolving it would be a significant bridge across number theory, representation theory and physics.
viability_rationale: Generalizes the long-standing Nahm conjecture; many low-rank cases proven and Mizuno's extension helps, but full resolution reduces to extending deep results (e.g. Lee13) over infinite families, so unlikely within 5 years.
rationale: The modularity statement for generalized Nahm sums associated to Dynkin pairs is posed as a conjecture; the paper gives many verified cases but does not prove the general claim.
evidence_snippet: "Then \big(A(X,Y),0,C(X,Y),D(X,Y)\big) is a modular quadruple." (Conjecture statement) and "Conjecture \ref{Conj:main} is known to hold in many cases." (lines 150-163,181)

title: Existence of free boundaries for overdetermined value problems: Sharp conditions, regularity, and physical applications

\label{conj:symmetry}
For a generic non-constant function \(g\) on \(\partial C\) (with \(C\) not a ball), the solution \(\Omega\) of \(\mathcal{QS}(f,g)\) is not a dilation of \(C\). Moreover, the free boundary exhibits at least \(C^{1,\alpha}\) regularity but is not real-analytic unless \(g\) is constant.

viability_rationale: Conjecture holds in characteristic zero and several key cases; existing propositions and modular progress make a general proof plausible within a few years.

title: Geometric Properties of Level Sets for Domains under Geometric Normal Property
body_tex:
Let $G_{\Omega}(x,y)$ be the Green's function of $\Omega$. Then for $x \in \partial \Omega$ where the normal exists, we have:
\[
\frac{\partial G_{\Omega}}{\partial \nu}(x,y) \sim \frac{1}{\tau_{\Omega}(x)} \quad \text{as } y \to x \text{ along the normal}.
\]
This relation quantifies the singularity of the normal derivative near the boundary.
plain_text: Let $G_ (x,y)$ be the Green's function of $$. Then for $x in $ where the normal exists, we have: \[ G_ (x,y) sim 1 _ (x) as y to x along the normal. \] This relation quantifies the singularity of the normal derivative near the boundary.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Concrete potential-theoretic relation connecting boundary thickness to the Green function's normal derivative; meaningful analytic content with clear implications for singularity and boundary behavior.
viability_rationale: A concrete PDE/potential-theory boundary asymptotic for C-GNP domains; approachable with classical potential theory and boundary regularity tools, making a resolution plausible within five years.

title: The van der Waerden Simplicial Complex and its Lefschetz Properties
plain_text: Fix an integer $k >= 3$. If $k$ is odd, then there exists an $m$ such that $A( vdw (n,k))$ fails to have the WLP for $n >= m$. If $k$ is even, then $A( vdw (n,k))$ has the WLP for all $n > k$.
label: real_open_conjecture

[2026-03-05] For $x>0$, set
$$f_1(x)=\frac{\Gamma(x)^2}{2\Gamma(2x)}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_1'(k)&=-\frac32L_{-3}(2),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_1''(k)&=3L_{-3}(3)=\frac{4\pi^3}{27\sqrt3},
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_1'''(k)&=\frac34\left(2\pi^2L_{-3}(2)-27L_{-3}(4)\right).
\end{align}

$$f_3(k)=\frac{e^{\pi ik}\Gamma(k+1)^2}{\Gamma(2k+1)}.$$
Then

\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_3''(k)&=\frac{\pi^5}{25}i+\frac{8}{15}\pi^2\zeta(3)-\frac{52}5\zeta(5),
\\5\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_3^{(3)}(k)&=4\pi i(\pi^2\zeta(3)-39\zeta(5))-6(2\zeta(3)^2+3\zeta(6)).
\end{align}

[2026-03-07] For $x>1/2$, define
$$f_5(x):=\frac{(30x-11)\Gamma(x)^4}{4x(2x-1)\Gamma(2x)^2}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5'(k)&=-24\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5''(k)&=8(23\zeta(5)-2\zeta(2)\zeta(3)),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5^{(3)}(k)&=24\left(2\zeta(3)^2-49\zeta(6)\right),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5^{(4)}(k)&=96(177\zeta(7)-46\zeta(2)\zeta(5)-2\zeta(3)\zeta(4)),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5^{(5)}(k)&=640\left(9\zeta(3)\zeta(5)-3\zeta(2)\zeta(3)^2-12\zeta(5,3)-146\zeta(8)\right),
\end{align}
and
\begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\frac{\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_5^{(6)}(k)}{960}&=3001\zeta(9)+8\zeta(3)^3-6\zeta(3)\zeta(6)
\\&\ \ -138\zeta(4)\zeta(5)-1062\zeta(2)\zeta(7).
\end{aligned}\end{equation}
label: real_open_conjecture

$$ f_7(x):=\frac{(69x^2-40x+6)\Gamma(6x)\Gamma(x)^7}{18(6x-1)\Gamma(3x)^3\Gamma(2x)^2}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_7'(k)&=-12\zeta(3),
\\ \sum_{k=1}^\infty f_7''(k)&=28\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_7^{(3)}(k)&=72(7\zeta(5)-4\zeta(2)\zeta(3)).
\end{align}
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Contains further Gamma-derivative equalities to zeta values; interesting to experts in binomial/Gamma-sum evaluations but a focused technical contribution.
viability_rationale: Concrete, finite identities (base sum already proven). Likely reducible to hypergeometric/creative-telescoping, PSLQ and harmonic-sum techniques; technical but plausible to resolve within five years.
rationale: The identities for f_7 derivative sums are presented as conjectural in the paper and not proved here; thus the exact statements remain open in this source.

title: Various conjectural series identities
body_tex:
[2026-03-07] For $x>1/2$, we define
$$f_8(x):=\frac{e^{\pi ix}(410x^2-197x+24)\Gamma(2x)^3\Gamma(x)^2}{2(2x-1)\Gamma(4x)^2}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_8'(k)&=22\zeta(3)-i\frac{\pi^3}3,
\\ \sum_{k=1}^\infty f_8''(k)&=44\pi\zeta(3)i-174\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_8^{(3)}(k)&=4140\zeta(5)-1584\zeta(2)\zeta(3)-\frac{97}{15}\pi^5i,
\end{align}
and
$$\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_8^{(4)}(k)=24\left(374\zeta(3)^2-763\zeta(6)\right)+80\pi i\left(207\zeta(5)-11\pi^2\zeta(3)\right).$$
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Complex-valued Gamma-derivative sums producing mixed zeta and pi-imaginary terms; notable for combining transcendental constants and may illuminate functional/period relations in special-values theory.
viability_rationale: Concrete, highly explicit Gamma/Zeta equalities amenable to hypergeometric/creative-telescoping/WZ or modular methods; similar Sun identities have been resolved, but these are nontrivial—moderate chance of a proof within five years.
rationale: Written explicitly as a conjecture for f_8 and its derivative sums; the paper provides no proof, so these statements remain conjectural here.

title: Various conjectural series identities
body_tex:
[2026-03-07] For $x>1$, define
$$f_9(x)=\frac{(364x^2-227x+36)\Gamma(x)^6}{9(2x-1)\Gamma(3x)^2}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9'(k)&=-36\zeta(4),
\\ \sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9''(k)
&=24(17\zeta(5)-2\zeta(2)\zeta(3)),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9^{(3)}(k)&=12\left(16\zeta(3)^2-261\zeta(6)\right),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9^{(4)}(k)&=288\left(265\zeta(7)+2\zeta(3)\zeta(4)-102\zeta(2)\zeta(5)\right),
\\\frac1{2880}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9^{(5)}(k)&=16\zeta(3)\zeta(5)-99\zeta(8)-24\zeta(5,3)-8\zeta(2)\zeta(3)^2,
\end{align}
and
\begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\frac1{960}\sum_{k=1}^\infty f_9^{(6)}(k)=&\ 25849\zeta(9)+128\zeta(3)^3+30\zeta(3)\zeta(6)
\\&\ +918\zeta(4)\zeta(5)-14310\zeta(2)\zeta(7).
\end{aligned}\end{equation}
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Further high-order derivative identities yielding multiple zeta combinations; solid specialist content with potential to inform evaluation techniques but limited cross-field reach.
viability_rationale: Finite explicit hypergeometric-type identities; amenable to creative telescoping/WZ, PSLQ and known zeta relations. Many Sun series have been proved, but multiple-zeta terms and higher derivatives add technical hurdles—moderate near-term chance.
rationale: The paper lists f_9 derivative sum identities as conjectures; no proofs are provided here, so the exact claims remain open in this work.

title: Various conjectural series identities
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Involves complex Gamma-quotients and high-order derivatives giving precise zeta and pi terms; moderately interesting due to interplay of imaginary/pi contributions and MZV structure.
viability_rationale: The base series is already known (remark cites evaluation). Differentiating the hypergeometric/gamma representation yields polygamma/zeta terms; standard tools (creative telescoping, contour/hypergeometric methods) have resolved similar Sun identities, so plausible within 5 years.
rationale: f_{10} derivative identities are presented as conjectural in the paper and are not proved within this source, so they remain open here.

title: Various conjectural series identities
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Pairs of Gamma-based sums equated to zeta and pi powers; useful verifiable identities for specialists but fairly narrow in scope.
viability_rationale: Specific finite analytic identities; related non-derivative sums were recently proved (Au26). Standard hypergeometric/contour/telescoping techniques often apply, so resolution within five years is plausible but nontrivial.
rationale: g_1 and g_2 derivative sum identities are explicitly labeled as conjectures in the paper and are not resolved here; thus remain open in this work.

title: Various conjectural series identities
body_tex:
[2026-03-05] For $k>0$, define
$$Z(k):=\frac{(21k-8)\Gamma(k)^6}{8\Gamma(2k)^3}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z''(k)&=\frac{57}2\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z^{(3)}(k)&=18\pi^2\zeta(3)-324\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z^{(4)}(k)&=\frac{1959}2\zeta(6)-432\zeta(3)^2,
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z^{(5)}(k)&=19440\zeta(2)\zeta(5)-540\zeta(3)\zeta(4)-31860\zeta(7),
\end{align}
and
\begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z^{(6)}(k)=31104\zeta(5,3)+38880\zeta(2)\zeta(3)^2-77760\zeta(3)\zeta(5)-\frac{84807}4\zeta(8),
\end{equation}
Also,
\begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\frac{\sum_{k=1}^\infty Z^{(7)}(k)}{630}=&\ 6372\zeta(2)\zeta(7)-213\zeta(3)\zeta(6)-324\zeta(4)\zeta(5)
\\&\ -288\zeta(3)^3-8812\zeta(9).\end{aligned}\end{equation}
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Extensive list of high-order derivative/sum relations involving MZVs and classical zeta products; richer structure and ties to known WZ evaluations raise its specialist significance.
viability_rationale: Related lower-order identities were proved (Zeilberger, Au). These are explicit hypergeometric/WZ-type sums expressible in zeta/MZV values; specialists can likely settle many within five years, though higher-order MZV cases remain technically harder.
rationale: Z(k) derivative-sum identities are posed as conjectures in the paper; no proofs or citations of proofs are given here, so the exact formulas remain conjectural in this source.

title: Various conjectural series identities
body_tex:
[2026-03-05] For $x>0$, define
$$S(x):=\frac{2(145x^2-104x+18)\Gamma(2x)\Gamma(x)^4}{9\Gamma(3x)^2}.$$
Then
\begin{align}\sum_{k=1}^\infty S'(k)&=-16\zeta(3),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty S''(k)&=107\zeta(4),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty S^{(3)}(k)&=20\left(4\pi^2\zeta(3)-81\zeta(5)\right),
\\\sum_{k=1}^\infty S^{(4)}(k)&=21\left(415\zeta(6)-128\zeta(3)^2\right),
\end{align}
\begin{equation}\frac1{30}\sum_{k=1}^\infty S^{(5)}(k)=5400\zeta(2)\zeta(5)+272\zeta(3)\zeta(4)-10761\zeta(7),
\end{equation}
and
\begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\frac2{15}\sum_{k=1}^\infty S^{(6)}(k)=&\ 90665\zeta(8)-138240\zeta(3)\zeta(5)
\\&\ +44928\zeta(5,3)+53760\zeta(2)\zeta(3)^2,
\end{aligned}\end{equation}
 Also,
\begin{equation}\begin{aligned}\frac1{420}\sum_{k=1}^\infty S^{(7)}(k)=&\ 161415\zeta(2)\zeta(7)+2340\zeta(3)\zeta(6)+13770\zeta(4)\zeta(5)
\\&\ -6272\zeta(3)^3-284755\zeta(9).
\end{aligned}\end{equation}
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Concrete Gamma-derivative sums equated to zeta and multiple zeta values: nontrivial analytic identities with connections to special values, but largely a focused experimental-identity list of moderate structural reach.
viability_rationale: Many such binomial/hypergeometric series have been resolved by WZ/PSLQ/hypergeometric methods; prior similar Sun conjectures were proved, so substantial chance several (if not all) are settled within five years.
rationale: Stated explicitly as a conjecture in the paper and not proved; only related sums previously confirmed are noted, but these specific equalities remain conjectural.
evidence_snippet: Lines 771–789: "\begin{conjecture} [2026-03-05] ... Then \sum_{k=1}^\infty S'(k) = -16\zeta(3), ..." (presented as Conjecture).

title: Sparse counting lemma for $K_4$
body_tex:
\label{conj: count} 
Let $H$ be a fixed graph. For any $\beta > 0$ and $\delta>0$, there exist constants $\eps > 0$, $C>0$, and~$n_0 >0$ such that for all $m \geq Cn^{2-1/m_2(H)}$ and~$n\geq n_0$, we have
\[ 
|\F(H,n,m,\delta) \cap \G(H, n, m, \eps)| \leq \beta^m {n^2 \choose m}^{|E(H)|}.
\]
plain_text: Let $H$ be a fixed graph. For any $ > 0$ and $>0$, there exist constants $ > 0$, $C>0$, and $n_0 >0$ such that for all $m >= Cn^ 2-1/m_2(H) $ and $n >= n_0$, we have \[ |(H,n,m,) cap (H, n, m, )| <= ^m n^2 m ^ |E(H)| . \]
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The sparse counting lemma is a central open problem in sparse regularity/random graph theory; resolving it for all H would have broad structural and probabilistic consequences beyond the current K4 advance.
viability_rationale: General sparse counting lemma is widely open; recent breakthroughs (KLR, K3 known, K4 progress) give momentum but full general H remains difficult.
rationale: The counting lemma is posed as a general conjecture for every fixed graph H; the paper proves only the H=K4 case, so the full general statement remains open.
evidence_snippet: Lines 188–193: "\begin{conjecture}[Counting Lemma]... Let H be a fixed graph. For any \beta>0 and \delta>0 ..." (stated as conjecture).

title: The boundary of K-moduli of prime Fano threefolds of genus twelve
body_tex:
\label{conj:Fano-K3-inj}
For each ADE K3 surface $(S,L)\in \cF_{22}$, there exists a unique Gorenstein canonical Fano threefold $X$ admitting a $\bQ$-Gorenstein smoothing to $V_{22}$ such that $S \in |-K_X|$ and $L = -K_X|_S$. Moreover, the forgetful map
\[
\cP^{\plt} \longrightarrow \cF_{22}, \qquad (X,S) \longmapsto (S, -K_X|_S),
\]
is an isomorphism.
plain_text: For each ADE K3 surface $(S,L) in _ 22 $, there exists a unique Gorenstein canonical Fano threefold $X$ admitting a $$-Gorenstein smoothing to $V_ 22 $ such that $S in |-K_X|$ and $L = -K_X|_S$. Moreover, the forgetful map \[ ^ _ 22 , (X,S) (S, -K_X|_S), \] is an isomorphism.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Asserting a bijective reconstruction between ADE K3 surfaces and Gorenstein canonical V22 degenerations has substantial moduli-theoretic consequences, advancing Mukai-style relations between Fano threefolds and K3 geometry.
viability_rationale: Deep moduli/K‑stability problem with strong partial results (proper, surjective, birational map); full injectivity/isomorphism likely hard and may need new techniques.
rationale: This Fano–K3 injectivity conjecture is proposed in the paper and not proved; authors note results supporting it but leave it unresolved.
evidence_snippet: Lines 2877–2885: "\begin{conj} For each ADE K3 surface ... there exists a unique Gorenstein canonical Fano threefold X ... Moreover, the forgetful map ... is an isomorphism."

body_tex:
\label{conj:qG-smoothing}
The Gorenstein canonical Fano threefolds constructed for the above seven Noether--Lefschetz divisors are of Picard rank 1 and admit $\bQ$-Gorenstein smoothing to $V_{22}$.

title: Analytic rank-one elliptic curves over function fields and their rank over certain ring class fields
body_tex:
\label{Larsen}
    Let $A/k$ be an abelian variety over a finitely generated infinite field $k$. Let $G$ be a topologically finitely generated subgroup of $G_k := \Gal(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)$. Then the rank of $A$ over the fixed subfield $(k^{\mathrm{sep}})^G$ of $k^{\mathrm{sep}}$ under $G$ is infinite.
plain_text: Let $A/k$ be an abelian variety over a finitely generated infinite field $k$. Let $G$ be a topologically finitely generated subgroup of $G_k := (k^ sep /k)$. Then the rank of $A$ over the fixed subfield $(k^ sep )^G$ of $k^ sep $ under $G$ is infinite.
label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: Larsen's conjecture on infinite rank over fixed subfields is a long-standing, deep arithmetic statement with wide implications for ranks of abelian varieties and field-extension phenomena.
viability_rationale: Larsen's conjecture is broad and longstanding; only special cases are known. Major breakthroughs would be required to resolve it in five years.
rationale: Larsen's conjecture is stated as a prominent open conjecture; the paper proves special cases but does not settle the general statement.
evidence_snippet: Lines 157–160: "A prominent conjecture ... was formulated by M. Larsen: \begin{conj} Let A/k be an abelian variety... Then the rank ... is infinite. \end{conj}"

title: Seymour-tight orientations
body_tex:
\label{conj:Seymour}
    Every orientation $G$ contains at least one vertex $v$ such that $|\NGi{2}{G}{v}| \geq |\NGi{1}{G}{v}|$.

viability_rationale: Seymour's second neighbourhood conjecture is a long‑standing combinatorial problem with many partial results but no clear imminent route to a full proof.

title: Seymour-tight orientations
body_tex:
\label{conjecture cayley}
    Every Seymour Cayley orientation can be constructed by taking (possibly repeated) lexicographic products of empty graphs, the $k$-th power of a directed cycles, and regular tournaments.

rationale: Authors pose this classification-of-Seymour-Cayley-orientations as an explicit conjecture after proving the abelian case; they discuss approaches but give no proof.

title: Randomstrasse101: Open Problems of 2025
body_tex:
There exist universal constants $C>0$ and $0<\beta<1$ such that, for any $M>1$ and $A\in\mathbb{R}^{2M-1\times M}$ a matrix for which any subset of $M$ rows spans $\mathbb{R}^M$,

title: The Andersen-Masbaum-Ueno conjecture for the derived subgroup of the Johnson kernel
body_tex: (AMU conjecture \cite{AMU06}) Let $\Sigma_{g,n}$ be a compact oriented surface and $f\in \mathrm{Mod}(\Sigma_{g,n}).$ Then $f$ has a pseudo-Anosov part if and only if $\rho_r(f)$ has infinite order for all large enough odd $r\geq 3.$

label: real_open_conjecture
interestingness_rationale: The AMU conjecture links Nielsen–Thurston dynamics and quantum WRT representations; central to quantum topology with deep structural consequences for mapping class groups and TQFT.
viability_rationale: AMU is well-known and difficult; many partial results exist but full general conjecture seems unlikely to be settled across all surfaces/levels within five years.

title: On flat shadow boundaries from point light sources and the characterization of ellipsoids
body_tex:

Let $K,S\subset\mathbb R^n$, $n\geq 3$, be convex bodies and let $S\subset\Int K$. Suppose that for every hyperplane $H$ supporting $S$, the section $K\cap H$ is centrally symmetric. Then $K$ is an ellipsoid.
